The Flood will commence in the next verse; before it does, the closure-of-protection is made personally. Note the divine-name shift: the same verse calls God Elohim (in the commanding) and YHWH (in the closing) — the chapter is moving between the two names in close proximity, as the documentary hypothesis would predict from a redacted P + J seam, and as the chapter's own grammar absorbs without comment.

17
The deluge was on the land forty days; the waters increased and lifted up the ark, and it rose above the land.
And the flood was forty days upon the earth; and the waters increased, and bare up the ark, and it was lifted up above the earth.
וַֽיְהִ֧י הַמַּבּ֛וּל אַרְבָּעִ֥ים י֖וֹם עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַיִּרְבּ֣וּ הַמַּ֗יִם וַיִּשְׂאוּ֙ אֶת־הַתֵּבָ֔ה וַתָּ֖רָם מֵעַ֥ל הָאָֽרֶץ׃
18
The waters prevailed and increased greatly on the land, and the ark went on the face of the waters.
And the waters prevailed, and increased greatly upon the earth; and the ark went upon the face of the waters.
וַיִּגְבְּר֥וּ הַמַּ֛יִם וַיִּרְבּ֥וּ מְאֹ֖ד עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַתֵּ֥לֶךְ הַתֵּבָ֖ה עַל־פְּנֵ֥י הַמָּֽיִם׃
19
The waters prevailed more and more upon the land, and all the high mountains under all the skies were covered.
And the waters prevailed exceedingly upon the earth; and all the high mountains that were under the whole heaven were covered.
וְהַמַּ֗יִם גָּֽבְר֛וּ מְאֹ֥ד מְאֹ֖ד עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַיְכֻסּ֗וּ כָּל־הֶֽהָרִים֙ הַגְּבֹהִ֔ים אֲשֶׁר־תַּ֖חַת כָּל־הַשָּׁמָֽיִם׃
20
The waters prevailed fifteen cubits upward, and the mountains were covered.
Fifteen cubits upward did the waters prevail; and the mountains were covered.
חֲמֵ֨שׁ עֶשְׂרֵ֤ה אַמָּה֙ מִלְמַ֔עְלָה גָּבְר֖וּ הַמָּ֑יִם וַיְכֻסּ֖וּ הֶהָרִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

A draft of fifteen cubits. The ark's height was thirty cubits (6:15). The water rises fifteen cubits above the highest mountains. Rashi reads the symmetry: the ark sat in the water at half its height — fifteen cubits submerged, fifteen above the surface — and floated exactly clearing the highest peaks below. The mountains pass under the ark with the precise clearance of its draft. The chapter does not comment on its own arithmetic; the geometry is left for the reader to assemble.

21
All flesh that moved on the land perished — birds, cattle, wild animals, every swarming creature that swarms on the land — and every human.
And all flesh died that moved upon the earth, both birds, and cattle, and beasts, and every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth, and every man:
וַיִּגְוַ֞ע כָּל־בָּשָׂ֣ר ׀ הָרֹמֵ֣שׂ עַל־הָאָ֗רֶץ בָּע֤וֹף וּבַבְּהֵמָה֙ וּבַ֣חַיָּ֔ה וּבְכָל־הַשֶּׁ֖רֶץ הַשֹּׁרֵ֣ץ עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וְכֹ֖ל הָאָדָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

The verb of quiet death. Vayigwa — expired — is distinct from the generic verb for dying. The same verb is used in the Hebrew Bible for the patriarchs' deaths: Abraham expired and was gathered to his people (Gen 25:8); Isaac expired (35:29); Jacob expired (49:33). In each case the register is full-of-years, peaceful, gathered-to-kin. Here the same verb is applied at mass scale: vayigwa kol basar — all flesh expired. The chapter chooses a vocabulary of peaceful expiration to report a violent mass extinction. The narrative refuses spectacle; it lists categories (birds, cattle, wild animals, swarmers, humans) in the order of 1:20–26, and reports that all of them have expired.

22
Everything in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life, of all that was on the dry ground, died.
all in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life, of all that was on the dry land, died.
כֹּ֡ל אֲשֶׁר֩ נִשְׁמַת־ר֨וּחַ חַיִּ֜ים בְּאַפָּ֗יו מִכֹּ֛ל אֲשֶׁ֥ר בֶּחָֽרָבָ֖ה מֵֽתוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The reversal of 2:7. The phrase נִשְׁמַת רוּחַ חַיִּים nishmat ruach chayim unusually doubles the breath-vocabulary, pairing neshamah with ruach. Compare 2:7, where YHWH-Elohim breathed nishmat chayim (breath of life) into the human's nostrils, and the human became a living being. Here in 7:22 the same vocabulary is used in reverse: everything in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life — died. The animation-by-breath of 2:7 is undone by the un-breath of 7:22. The chapter is reversing creation's specific act of inspiration.

23
He wiped out every living thing on the face of the ground — from human to cattle, to creeping thing, to bird of the skies — they were wiped out from the land. Only Noah remained, and those with him in the ark.
And every living thing was destroyed that was upon the face of the ground, both man, and cattle, and creeping things, and birds of the heavens; and they were destroyed from the earth: and Noah only was left, and they that were with him in the ark.
וַיִּ֜מַח אֶֽת־כָּל־הַיְק֣וּם ׀ אֲשֶׁ֣ר ׀ עַל־פְּנֵ֣י הָֽאֲדָמָ֗ה מֵאָדָ֤ם עַד־בְּהֵמָה֙ עַד־רֶ֙מֶשׂ֙ וְעַד־ע֣וֹף הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וַיִּמָּח֖וּ מִן־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַיִשָּׁ֧אֶר אַךְ־נֹ֛חַ וַֽאֲשֶׁ֥ר אִתּ֖וֹ בַּתֵּבָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

The remnant. The verse closes the chapter with the word that will become a major theological term: vayisha'er ach Noach — only Noah remained. The verb שָׁאַר sha'ar (here in the niphal: was left, remained) is the lexical family of שְׁאֵרִית she'erit — the remnant — the technical term in later prophetic literature for the surviving faithful few preserved through national catastrophe (Isa 10:20–22, Mic 2:12, Jer 31:7, Zeph 3:13). Genesis 7:23 stands at the origin of the Hebrew Bible's remnant-theology: a faithful core preserved through total destruction.

The verse also closes the chapter's wiping-vocabulary: the verb machah — wipe, erase, blot out — appears twice in active and passive: vayyimach ('he wiped') and vayyimmachu ('they were wiped'). The wiping that v4 announced is completed here. The chapter ends with the verb of erasure achieved, and the remnant clause attached.

The restrictive adverb אַךְ ach ('only, just') frames the verse with its key image: from total population to only Noah. The thread of preservation has narrowed to a single household in a sealed wooden box on top of an ocean that has covered the highest mountains.

24
The waters prevailed upon the land one hundred and fifty days.
And the waters prevailed upon the earth a hundred and fifty days.
וַיִּגְבְּר֥וּ הַמַּ֖יִם עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ חֲמִשִּׁ֥ים וּמְאַ֖ת יֽוֹם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 24

The chapter ends suspended. No release, no recession, no dove yet — just the duration. Chamishim u-meat yom — 150 days of prevailing waters. The chapter ends with the picture: a single floating ark on a sea over a buried world, day 150 of the prevailing. The next chapter opens with vayyizkor Elohim et Noach — and the Elohim remembered Noah. The 150 days are the longest stretch of the Flood narrative without a single divine action recorded. The chapter lets the silence stand.

Chapter 8

Genesis 8 — The waters recede, the altar built

1
The Elohim remembered Noah, and every wild animal and every domesticated animal that was with him in the ark; and the Elohim caused a breath to pass over the land, and the waters subsided.
And God remembered Noah, and all the beasts, and all the cattle that were with him in the ark: and God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters assuaged;
וַיִּזְכֹּ֤ר אֱלֹהִים֙ אֶת־נֹ֔חַ וְאֵ֤ת כָּל־הַֽחַיָּה֙ וְאֶת־כָּל־הַבְּהֵמָ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר אִתּ֖וֹ בַּתֵּבָ֑ה וַיַּעֲבֵ֨ר אֱלֹהִ֥ים ר֙וּחַ֙ עַל־הָאָ֔רֶץ וַיָּשֹׁ֖כּוּ הַמָּֽיִם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

Two echoes of Genesis 1 in a single verse.

First, vayyizkor — the Elohim remembered. After 150 days of prevailing waters at the close of chapter 7, with no divine action recorded across that stretch, the chapter opens with a turning verb. The same verb will mark each major covenantal turning point in the Hebrew Bible: 'YHWH remembered Sarah' (Gen 21:1), 'the Elohim remembered Rachel' (30:22), 'the Elohim remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob' at the start of the Exodus (Ex 2:24). In every case, remembering names the moment when divine attention returns to the preserved-and-waiting individual after a long stretch. The chapter is doing a literary thing it will do again at every covenantal hinge.

Second, vayya'aver Elohim ruach al ha-aretz — the Elohim caused a breath to pass over the land. Compare Gen 1:2: ruach Elohim merachefet al p'nei ha-mayim — the breath of the Elohim was hovering over the face of the waters. Same noun (ruach), same agents, the same waters being acted on. The chapter is rebooting Genesis 1's grammar. The un-creation of the Flood is being reversed into a re-creation, with the same divine ruach serving as the medium. What hovered over the waters at the beginning now passes over the land at the resumption.

2
The fountains of the deep and the floodgates of the skies were closed, and the rain from the skies was held back.
the fountains also of the deep and the windows of heaven were stopped, and the rain from heaven was restrained;
וַיִּסָּֽכְרוּ֙ מַעְיְנֹ֣ת תְּה֔וֹם וַֽאֲרֻבֹּ֖ת הַשָּׁמָ֑יִם וַיִּכָּלֵ֥א הַגֶּ֖שֶׁם מִן־הַשָּׁמָֽיִם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The reversal reversed. The two cosmic openings of 7:11 — the fountains of the great deep and the floodgates of the skies — are now closed. The verbs are passive (niphal): vayyissakhru ('were closed', from סָכַר sakar — to shut, stop up) and vayyikkale ('was held back', from כָּלָא kala — to restrain, withhold). The chapter is undoing 7:11 with mirror-grammar. The raqia of Genesis 1, breached in 7:11, is now resealed; the lower waters of Genesis 1, released in 7:11, are now contained. The cosmological collapse of chapter 7 is structurally reversed in two verbs of chapter 8.

3
The waters receded from upon the land, going and returning; and at the end of one hundred and fifty days the waters had diminished.
and the waters returned from off the earth continually: and after the end of a hundred and fifty days the waters decreased.
וַיָּשֻׁ֧בוּ הַמַּ֛יִם מֵעַ֥ל הָאָ֖רֶץ הָל֣וֹךְ וָשׁ֑וֹב וַיַּחְסְר֣וּ הַמַּ֔יִם מִקְצֵ֕ה חֲמִשִּׁ֥ים וּמְאַ֖ת יֽוֹם׃
4
In the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, the ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat.
And the ark rested in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, upon the mountains of Ararat.
וַתָּ֤נַח הַתֵּבָה֙ בַּחֹ֣דֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִ֔י בְּשִׁבְעָה־עָשָׂ֥ר י֖וֹם לַחֹ֑דֶשׁ עַ֖ל הָרֵ֥י אֲרָרָֽט׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

The ark rests. The verb תָּנַח tanach is from נוּחַ nuach — the same root as Noah's name (5:29). The ark of Noah comes-to-rest on the mountains of Ararat. The chapter's name-and-verb wordplay is structural: the resting one (Noach) is in a resting (tanach) vessel; the rest happens exactly when the divine ruach has done its work over the waters.

The date — seventh month, seventeenth day — is precisely five months (150 days) after the Flood began (7:11: second month, seventeenth day). The chapter is keeping a calendar; the durations are not symbolic durations only but counted days. The five months equal 150 days only if each month is exactly 30 days — implying the chapter uses a schematic 30-day-month, 360-day-year calendar (the same calendar later used in 1 Enoch's astronomical book and in priestly liturgical reckoning). The chapter's calendrical care is precise enough to surface its own calendrical assumptions.

Harei Ararat — mountains of Ararat, plural. The Hebrew specifies a mountainous region (Urartu, around Lake Van in eastern Anatolia / Armenia), not a single peak. The traditional identification of Mount Ararat (Ağrı Dağı in Turkey, 5,137m) became fixed in late antique and medieval Christian tradition; the Hebrew is broader.

5
The waters continued to recede until the tenth month; in the tenth month, on the first day of the month, the tops of the mountains appeared.
And the waters decreased continually until the tenth month: in the tenth month, on the first day of the month, were the tops of the mountains seen.
וְהַמַּ֗יִם הָיוּ֙ הָל֣וֹךְ וְחָס֔וֹר עַ֖ד הַחֹ֣דֶשׁ הָֽעֲשִׂירִ֑י בָּֽעֲשִׂירִי֙ בְּאֶחָ֣ד לַחֹ֔דֶשׁ נִרְא֖וּ רָאשֵׁ֥י הֶֽהָרִֽים׃
6
At the end of forty days, Noah opened the window of the ark which he had made.
And it came to pass at the end of forty days, that Noah opened the window of the ark which he had made:
וַֽיְהִ֕י מִקֵּ֖ץ אַרְבָּעִ֣ים י֑וֹם וַיִּפְתַּ֣ח נֹ֔חַ אֶת־חַלּ֥וֹן הַתֵּבָ֖ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר עָשָֽׂה׃
7
He sent out the raven, and it went out, going to and fro, until the waters dried up from the land.
and he sent forth a raven, and it went forth to and fro, until the waters were dried up from off the earth.
וַיְשַׁלַּ֖ח אֶת־הָֽעֹרֵ֑ב וַיֵּצֵ֤א יָצוֹא֙ וָשׁ֔וֹב עַד־יְבֹ֥שֶׁת הַמַּ֖יִם מֵעַ֥ל הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The raven first. Noah sends out ha-orev — the raven — and it leaves and returns repeatedly without reporting back conclusively. The Mesopotamian flood narratives (Gilgamesh Tablet XI, Atrahasis) have a different bird-sequence: Utnapishtim sends a dove, then a swallow, then a raven — and the raven does not return, signaling the waters have receded. Genesis has reversed the order: raven first, dove second, and the dove's behavior is what tells Noah the land has dried.

The raven's going to and fro (יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב yatzo va-shov, an infinitive-absolute construction) is iterative — the bird shuttles without settling. The raven is unclean (by later Levitical categorization, Lev 11:15) and a carrion-eater; the chapter's logic is that the raven could subsist on floating bodies and emerging carcasses, requiring no settled landing. The dove, by contrast, will return because there is nowhere clean yet to alight. The two birds' contrasting behaviors are not arbitrary — they reflect each species' ecological capacity.

8
Then he sent out the dove from him, to see whether the waters had lightened from the face of the ground.
And he sent forth a dove from him, to see if the waters were abated from off the face of the ground;
וַיְשַׁלַּ֥ח אֶת־הַיּוֹנָ֖ה מֵאִתּ֑וֹ לִרְאוֹת֙ הֲקַ֣לּוּ הַמַּ֔יִם מֵעַ֖ל פְּנֵ֥י הָֽאֲדָמָֽה׃
9
But the dove found no resting place for the sole of her foot, and she returned to him to the ark, for the waters were on the face of all the land. He put out his hand and took her, and brought her to him into the ark.
but the dove found no rest for the sole of her foot, and she returned unto him to the ark; for the waters were on the face of the whole earth: and he put forth his hand, and took her, and brought her in unto him into the ark.
וְלֹֽא־מָצְאָה֩ הַיּוֹנָ֨ה מָנ֜וֹחַ לְכַף־רַגְלָ֗הּ וַתָּ֤שָׁב אֵלָיו֙ אֶל־הַתֵּבָ֔ה כִּי־מַ֖יִם עַל־פְּנֵ֣י כָל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַיִּשְׁלַ֤ח יָדוֹ֙ וַיִּקָּחֶ֔הָ וַיָּבֵ֥א אֹתָ֛הּ אֵלָ֖יו אֶל־הַתֵּבָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The resting-place vocabulary. Manoach l-khaf-raglah — a resting-place for the sole of her foot. The noun מָנוֹחַ manoach is again from the root נוּחַ nuach — rest, settle. The chapter's Noah / Noach / tanach / manoach wordplay continues: the resting one (Noach) sends the dove to look for a resting-place (manoach), and the dove cannot find one — yet. Noah gently takes her (וַיִּקָּחֶהָ vayyiqqachehah) — the verbal tenderness is striking after the chapter's mass-extinction vocabulary; put out his hand (vayyishlach yado) and brought her in. The interaction is small-scale, intimate, and named in domestic verbs.

10
He waited another seven days, and again sent the dove out from the ark.
And he stayed yet other seven days; and again he sent forth the dove out of the ark;
וַיָּ֣חֶל ע֔וֹד שִׁבְעַ֥ת יָמִ֖ים אֲחֵרִ֑ים וַיֹּ֛סֶף שַׁלַּ֥ח אֶת־הַיּוֹנָ֖ה מִן־הַתֵּבָֽה׃
11
The dove came to him at evening time — and behold, a fresh olive leaf in her mouth! Noah knew that the waters had lightened from upon the land.
and the dove came in to him at eventide; and, lo, in her mouth an olive-leaf plucked off: so Noah knew that the waters were abated from off the earth.
וַתָּבֹ֨א אֵלָ֤יו הַיּוֹנָה֙ לְעֵ֣ת עֶ֔רֶב וְהִנֵּ֥ה עֲלֵה־זַ֖יִת טָרָ֣ף בְּפִ֑יהָ וַיֵּ֣דַע נֹ֔חַ כִּי־קַ֥לּוּ הַמַּ֖יִם מֵעַ֥ל הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

The chapter's iconic image. Two verbal precisions and a botanical note.

The verb טָרַף taraf has two senses: plucked (active — the dove plucked it) or fresh / newly-broken-off (stative passive — the leaf was new). Modern translations favor fresh: the leaf is new growth, evidence that vegetation has resumed on the land. The chapter's evidence is botanical: there is now a re-emerging biosphere.

The choice of olive is precise. The olive tree (Olea europaea) is one of the most flood-resistant and quickly-regenerating Mediterranean species, capable of resprouting from roots after extended submersion. ANE-Mediterranean farmers knew this; the olive was the tree one would expect to see leafing first. The chapter's botany is observational, not arbitrary.

The image — yonah carrying a fresh olive leaf — will become one of the Hebrew Bible's most-reproduced icons. Christian art will make the dove the Holy Spirit; modern peace iconography will use the olive branch as its sign. The chapter does neither — it simply records evening, dove, olive, knowledge. The afterlife of the image dwarfs its modest verse.

12
He waited another seven days, and sent out the dove; and she did not return to him again.
And he stayed yet other seven days, and sent forth the dove; and she returned not again unto him any more.
וַיִּיָּ֣חֶל ע֔וֹד שִׁבְעַ֥ת יָמִ֖ים אֲחֵרִ֑ים וַיְשַׁלַּח֙ אֶת־הַיּוֹנָ֔ה וְלֹֽא־יָסְפָ֥ה שׁוּב־אֵלָ֖יו עֽוֹד׃
13
In the six hundred and first year, on the first day of the first month, the waters dried up from on the land. Noah removed the covering of the ark and looked — and behold, the face of the ground had dried.
And it came to pass in the six hundred and first year, in the first month, the first day of the month, the waters were dried up from off the earth: and Noah removed the covering of the ark, and looked, and, behold, the face of the ground was dried.
וַֽ֠יְהִי בְּאַחַ֨ת וְשֵׁשׁ־מֵא֜וֹת שָׁנָ֗ה בָּֽרִאשׁוֹן֙ בְּאֶחָ֣ד לַחֹ֔דֶשׁ חָֽרְב֥וּ הַמַּ֖יִם מֵעַ֣ל הָאָ֑רֶץ וַיָּ֤סַר נֹ֙חַ֙ אֶת־מִכְסֵ֣ה הַתֵּבָ֔ה וַיַּ֕רְא וְהִנֵּ֥ה חָֽרְב֖וּ פְּנֵ֥י הָֽאֲדָמָֽה׃
14
In the second month, on the twenty-seventh day of the month, the land was dry.
And in the second month, on the seven and twentieth day of the month, was the earth dry.
וּבַחֹ֙דֶשׁ֙ הַשֵּׁנִ֔י בְּשִׁבְעָ֧ה וְעֶשְׂרִ֛ים י֖וֹם לַחֹ֑דֶשׁ יָבְשָׁ֖ה הָאָֽרֶץ׃ (ס)
15
The Elohim spoke to Noah, saying,
And God spake unto Noah, saying,
וַיְדַבֵּ֥ר אֱלֹהִ֖ים אֶל־נֹ֥חַ לֵאמֹֽר׃
16
"Come out of the ark — you, your wife, your sons, and your sons' wives with you.
Go forth from the ark, thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons` wives with thee.
צֵ֖א מִן־הַתֵּבָ֑ה אַתָּ֕ה וְאִשְׁתְּךָ֛ וּבָנֶ֥יךָ וּנְשֵֽׁי־בָנֶ֖יךָ אִתָּֽךְ׃
17
Bring out with you every living thing that is with you, of all flesh — birds, cattle, every creeping thing that creeps upon the land — that they may swarm on the land, and be fruitful and multiply on the land."
Bring forth with thee every living thing that is with thee of all flesh, both birds, and cattle, and every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth; that they may breed abundantly in the earth, and be fruitful, and multiply upon the earth.
כָּל־הַחַיָּ֨ה אֲשֶֽׁר־אִתְּךָ֜ מִכָּל־בָּשָׂ֗ר בָּע֧וֹף וּבַבְּהֵמָ֛ה וּבְכָל־הָרֶ֛מֶשׂ הָרֹמֵ֥שׂ עַל־הָאָ֖רֶץ הוצא [הַיְצֵ֣א] אִתָּ֑ךְ וְשָֽׁרְצ֣וּ בָאָ֔רֶץ וּפָר֥וּ וְרָב֖וּ עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

The creation blessing repeated. Sharetzu ... peru u-rvu — swarm ... be fruitful and multiply — is the explicit verbal echo of the creation blessings of Gen 1:22 (to the sea-creatures: 'be fruitful and multiply, and fill the waters') and 1:28 (to humans: 'be fruitful and multiply, and fill the land'). The Flood erased; the disembarkation re-mandates. The chapter's re-creation grammar is exact: the same blessing-vocabulary that named the original creation's fertility-mandate is repeated to the post-Flood remnant. The species-categories Noah brought through the Flood are now told to do again what they were told to do before — fill the categories with kind.

18
Noah came out — his sons, his wife, and his sons' wives with him.
And Noah went forth, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons` wives with him:
וַיֵּ֖צֵא־נֹ֑חַ וּבָנָ֛יו וְאִשְׁתּ֥וֹ וּנְשֵֽׁי־בָנָ֖יו אִתּֽוֹ׃
19
Every living thing, every creeping thing, and every bird — everything that moves upon the land — went out from the ark by their families.
every beast, every creeping thing, and every bird, whatsoever moveth upon the earth, after their families, went forth out of the ark.
כָּל־הַֽחַיָּ֗ה כָּל־הָרֶ֙מֶשׂ֙ וְכָל־הָע֔וֹף כֹּ֖ל רוֹמֵ֣שׂ עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹ֣תֵיהֶ֔ם יָצְא֖וּ מִן־הַתֵּבָֽה׃
20
Noah built an altar to YHWH; and he took some of every clean animal and of every clean bird, and offered whole burnt offerings on the altar.
And Noah builded an altar unto Jehovah, and took of every clean beast, and of every clean bird, and offered burnt-offerings on the altar.
וַיִּ֥בֶן נֹ֛חַ מִזְבֵּ֖חַ לַֽיהוָ֑ה וַיִּקַּ֞ח מִכֹּ֣ל ׀ הַבְּהֵמָ֣ה הַטְּהוֹרָ֗ה וּמִכֹּל֙ הָע֣וֹף הַטָּהֹ֔ר וַיַּ֥עַל עֹלֹ֖ת בַּמִּזְבֵּֽחַ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

Two firsts in one verse. First mizbeach (altar) and first olah (whole burnt offering) in the Hebrew Bible. Cain and Hevel's earlier offerings (4:3–4) were minchot — gifts/grain-offerings — and were brought without a recorded altar. Here in 8:20 the cultic vocabulary that will run through Leviticus and Numbers makes its founding appearance: built altar, slaughtered offering, ascending smoke. The chapter places the institution of formal altar-and-burnt-offering at the post-Flood threshold — before circumcision (chapter 17), before the priesthood (Exodus 28), before the tabernacle (Exodus 25), but the cultic vocabulary they will all use is here.

The surplus from the seven-pair instruction (7:2) is what makes the offering possible — Noah has extras of the clean animals beyond breeding pairs, available for sacrifice. The chapter's earlier numerical instruction was the practical-cultic preparation for this moment. The two textual moves bind: 7:2 made room for surplus; 8:20 uses it.

21
YHWH smelled the soothing aroma, and YHWH said in his heart, "I will not again curse the ground because of the human, for the inclination of the heart of the human is evil from his youth; and I will not again strike down every living thing as I have done.
And Jehovah smelled the sweet savor; and Jehovah said in his heart, I will not again curse the ground any more for man`s sake, for that the imagination of man`s heart is evil from his youth; neither will I again smite any more everything living, as I have done.
וַיָּ֣רַח יְהוָה֮ אֶת־רֵ֣יחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ֒ וַיֹּ֨אמֶר יְהוָ֜ה אֶל־לִבּ֗וֹ לֹֽא־אֹ֠סִף לְקַלֵּ֨ל ע֤וֹד אֶת־הָֽאֲדָמָה֙ בַּעֲב֣וּר הָֽאָדָ֔ם כִּ֠י יֵ֣צֶר לֵ֧ב הָאָדָ֛ם רַ֖ע מִנְּעֻרָ֑יו וְלֹֽא־אֹסִ֥ף ע֛וֹד לְהַכּ֥וֹת אֶת־כָּל־חַ֖י כַּֽאֲשֶׁ֥ר עָשִֽׂיתִי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

The chapter's keystone verse — three load-bearing claims.

First, vayyarach YHWH et reach hannichoach — YHWH smelled the soothing aroma. The Hebrew is anthropopathic and concrete: YHWH smells the rising smoke. The ANE parallel is unmistakable. In the Atrahasis epic and Gilgamesh Tablet XI, after the flood, Utnapishtim builds an altar and offers sacrifice, and the gods smell the sweet savor and gather around it like flies — because they have been starved during the flood (no human sacrifices reached them while humanity was drowned). Genesis's grammar of the smelling is exactly that of the Mesopotamian parallel, but Genesis's theology of it is different: the gods of Atrahasis are hungry, the YHWH of Genesis is moved-to-resolve. The verb and image are shared with the broader ANE flood traditions; the consequence drawn from the smelling differs.

Second, lo osif l-qallel od et ha-adamah ba-avur ha-adam — I will not again curse the ground because of the human. The chapter delivers on the hope Lamech named in 5:29: the ground which YHWH cursed (a callback to 3:17, arurah ha-adamah ba-avurecha) is now not-going-to-be-cursed-again. Note the verb-shift: 3:17 used אָרַר arar (the formal curse-pronouncement verb); 8:21 uses קָלַל qalel (a lighter curse-verb). The commitment is to not adding curses, not to removing the formal arar of 3:17. The post-Flood condition is the curse stays operative; no new curses will be added.

Third, ki yetzer lev ha-adam ra mi-neurav — for the inclination of the heart of the human is evil from his youth. The same diagnosis given as the reason for the Flood (6:5: every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil all day long) is now given as the reason for not repeating the Flood. The exact same observation has produced opposite divine decisions. The rabbinic tradition (Rashi) reads the inversion as theological breakthrough: pre-Flood, human evil was the reason to destroy; post-Flood, recognition that human evil is constitutional (mi-neurav — from youth, present from the outset, structural rather than acquired) makes destruction-as-policy futile. Destruction did not solve the problem of human evil, because the problem is in the yetzer — in the inclining-faculty itself. The chapter's theology of divine restraint: rather than respond to human evil with cyclic destruction, the divine commits to continuity and lets the evil-prone humans persist. The Flood was the response that did not work; non-destruction is the response that follows.

22
While the land yet endures, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night — these shall not cease."
While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease.
עֹ֖ד כָּל־יְמֵ֣י הָאָ֑רֶץ זֶ֡רַע וְ֠קָצִיר וְקֹ֨ר וָחֹ֜ם וְקַ֧יִץ וָחֹ֛רֶף וְי֥וֹם וָלַ֖יְלָה לֹ֥א יִשְׁבֹּֽתוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

Four pairs name the restored order. Zera v'qatzir (seed and harvest — agricultural cycle), qor va-chom (cold and heat — thermal cycle), qayitz va-choref (summer and winter — seasonal cycle), yom va-laylah (day and night — diurnal cycle). The chapter closes with eight words that re-establish what the Flood had threatened.

Note the verb לֹא יִשְׁבֹּתוּ lo yishbotu — they shall not cease. The root is שָׁבַת shabat — the root behind shabbat, the cessation. The cycles will not sabbath. The chapter is using the cessation-vocabulary in the negative: the rhythms that organize life will not stop, will not rest, will not cease. The Sabbath cycle (Gen 2:2–3) is, in Hebrew theology, the cessation that gives shape to the others; here at the post-Flood threshold the other cycles are promised non-cessation. The Sabbath cessation continues to structure the week, but the larger seasonal, diurnal, and agricultural rhythms continue without interruption. The chapter closes on the most basic guarantee the Hebrew Bible offers: the world's rhythms are pinned in place against another collapse like the Flood.

Chapter 9

Genesis 9 — The covenant, the bow in the cloud, and Noah's vineyard

1
The Elohim blessed Noah and his sons, and said to them: be fruitful and multiply and fill the land.
And God blessed Noah and his sons, and said unto them, Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth.
וַיְבָ֣רֶךְ אֱלֹהִ֔ים אֶת־נֹ֖חַ וְאֶת־בָּנָ֑יו וַיֹּ֧אמֶר לָהֶ֛ם פְּר֥וּ וּרְב֖וּ וּמִלְא֥וּ אֶת־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The Genesis 1 blessing, re-issued. Word-for-word, this is the blessing of 1:28 — peru u-revu u-mil'u et ha-aretz — spoken now to the survivors at the start of the second human age. The chapter is doing the structural thing it has been doing since chapter 8: rebooting Genesis 1's grammar onto the post-Flood world. Adam and his wife received this blessing as the first humans; Noah and his sons receive it again as the second-first humans. The Flood narrative has positioned Noah as a second Adam — same blessing, same mandate.

What is missing is also revealing. Genesis 1:28 included kivshuha (subdue it) and redu (rule over). Here those verbs are dropped — only the demographic command (be fruitful, multiply, fill) is preserved. The dominion mandate is being reissued separately in v2 with new and harsher vocabulary.

2
The dread of you and the terror of you shall be upon every living thing of the land, and upon every bird of the skies — upon everything that the soil teems with, and upon all the fish of the sea: into your hand they are given.
And the fear of you and the dread of you shall be upon every beast of the earth, and upon every bird of the heavens; With all wherewith the ground teemeth, and all the fishes of the sea, into your hand are they delivered.
וּמוֹרַאֲכֶ֤ם וְחִתְּכֶם֙ יִֽהְיֶ֔ה עַ֚ל כָּל־חַיַּ֣ת הָאָ֔רֶץ וְעַ֖ל כָּל־ע֣וֹף הַשָּׁמָ֑יִם בְּכֹל֩ אֲשֶׁ֨ר תִּרְמֹ֧שׂ הָֽאֲדָמָ֛ה וּֽבְכָל־דְּגֵ֥י הַיָּ֖ם בְּיֶדְכֶ֥ם נִתָּֽנוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The dominion mandate, re-issued in fear-vocabulary. Where Genesis 1:28 told the first humans to rule (radah) and subdue (kavash) the animals — verbs of governance and ordering — the post-Flood mandate uses mora (dread) and chittah (terror). These are war-vocabulary words. Mora appears in Deuteronomy and the conquest narratives for the dread that falls on Israel's enemies (Deut 11:25, 'no man shall be able to stand before you; YHWH your Elohim will lay the dread of you'). Chittah (from חתת chatat — to be shattered, broken) names the way enemies collapse in fear (Job 6:21).

The rhetorical move is precise. Before the Flood, animal and human relations are pictured under the verbs radah and kavash — verbs that have a meaning-range from 'rule' (functional) to 'tread down' (forceful), but which name a relationship. After the Flood, the relationship is named by the psychological state the animals will live in: dread, terror. The next verse explains why.

3
Every moving thing that lives shall be yours for food; as the green plants, I have given you everything.
Every moving thing that liveth shall be food for you; As the green herb have I given you all.
כָּל־רֶ֙מֶשׂ֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר הוּא־חַ֔י לָכֶ֥ם יִהְיֶ֖ה לְאָכְלָ֑ה כְּיֶ֣רֶק עֵ֔שֶׂב נָתַ֥תִּי לָכֶ֖ם אֶת־כֹּֽל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

Meat enters the human diet. This verse is the precise inverse of 1:29–30. There, the first humans were given every herb yielding seed and every tree with seed-bearing fruit — and only that — as food; the animals likewise received only the green plants. The Edenic and antediluvian diet, on the chapter's own account, is vegetarian.

Here, post-Flood, the dietary permission is expanded: kol remes asher hu-chai lakhem yihyeh l'akhlah — every moving thing that lives shall be yours for food. The comparative phrase k-yereq esev (as the green plants) makes the analogy explicit: meat is now given on the same footing that vegetation was given in 1:29. Two ages of human diet, with the Flood as the hinge.

The chapter's editorial logic seems to be: the violent world of chapter 6 (where 'all flesh had corrupted its way upon the land,' 6:12) is being acknowledged. Pure herbivory was the Edenic ideal; in the post-Flood, post-violence world, the chapter concedes meat-eating — but with the immediate restrictions of vv. 4–6.

4
Only — flesh with its life, its blood, you shall not eat.
But flesh with the life thereof, [which is] the blood thereof, shall ye not eat.
אַךְ־בָּשָׂ֕ר בְּנַפְשׁ֥וֹ דָמ֖וֹ לֹ֥א תֹאכֵֽלוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

The blood-life equation. The Hebrew is densely compressed: basar b'nafsho damo lo tokhelu — literally flesh in-its-nefesh its-blood, you shall not eat. The blood is in apposition to nefesh (life-soul, breath-self); the chapter is asserting an identification, not a separate rule. The blood IS the nefesh, the carrier of the life-force.

This is the first explicit dietary prohibition in the Torah. It is repeated and elaborated in Leviticus 17:10–14 ('the nefesh of the flesh is in the blood'), Deuteronomy 12:23 ('the blood is the nefesh, and you shall not eat the nefesh with the flesh'), and underlies all kosher slaughter regulations (shechitah's central concern is the complete drainage of blood). The chapter is establishing the principle that grounds an entire later legal tradition.

The theological logic, on the chapter's terms: meat is permitted, but the life-force in the meat must be returned (poured out, drained) and not consumed. To consume the blood would be to consume the nefesh of the animal — a kind of category-violation that the chapter prohibits even as it concedes the meat itself.

5
And further — your own blood, for your lives, I will demand; from the hand of every living thing I will demand it, and from the hand of the human, from the hand of each man's brother, I will demand the life of the human.
And surely your blood, [the blood] of your lives, will I require; At the hand of every beast will I require it. And at the hand of man, even at the hand of every man`s brother, will I require the life of man.
וְאַ֨ךְ אֶת־דִּמְכֶ֤ם לְנַפְשֹֽׁתֵיכֶם֙ אֶדְרֹ֔שׁ מִיַּ֥ד כָּל־חַיָּ֖ה אֶדְרְשֶׁ֑נּוּ וּמִיַּ֣ד הָֽאָדָ֗ם מִיַּד֙ אִ֣ישׁ אָחִ֔יו אֶדְרֹ֖שׁ אֶת־נֶ֥פֶשׁ הָֽאָדָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

The reckoning for human blood. The verb darash (to seek, inquire, demand) is the chapter's strongest word for divine accountability. The construction edrosh — edreshennu — edrosh (three repetitions in one verse) gives the line its weight: I will demand, I will demand it, I will demand. The triple emphasis pivots from animal predation (animals that kill humans will be held to account, v5a) to human violence (humans who kill humans will be held to account, v5b).

The phrase mi-yad ish achiv — from the hand of each man's brother — is a deliberate echo of chapter 4. Cain killed Hevel his brother and YHWH said the blood of your brother cries out to me from the ground (4:10). The chapter is invoking that scene by name. The first-recorded fratricide was met then with a curse and a mark; now, after the Flood that the human violence of chapter 6 helped trigger, the chapter installs a standing divine demand against brother-killing-brother.

6
One who sheds the blood of the human — by the human his blood shall be shed; for in the image of the Elohim, they made the human.
Whoso sheddeth man`s blood, by man shall his blood be shed: For in the image of God made he man.
שֹׁפֵךְ֙ דַּ֣ם הָֽאָדָ֔ם בָּֽאָדָ֖ם דָּמ֣וֹ יִשָּׁפֵ֑ךְ כִּ֚י בְּצֶ֣לֶם אֱלֹהִ֔ים עָשָׂ֖ה אֶת־הָאָדָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

Murder is a wound to the Elohim's likeness. The verse is structured as a tight chiasm: shofech / dam / ha-adam / ba-adam / damo / yishshafekh — sheds / blood / of-the-human / by-the-human / his-blood / shall-be-shed. Six words mirroring around their center, with the act and its consequence locked in formal symmetry.

The grounding clause is the load-bearing one for the chapter's anthropology: ki b'tselem Elohim asah et ha-adam — for in the image of the Elohim, he made the human. This is the third invocation of tselem Elohim in Genesis (1:26–27, 5:1, now 9:6) and the only one that draws a legal consequence. Killing a human is now framed as an injury to the resemblance the human carries.

On the WoH frame: the tselem and demut of 1:26 are read as physical resemblance, not metaphor. Here that reading does not weaken — it strengthens — the prohibition. To kill a human is to destroy a body that physically resembles the Elohim who made it. The chapter is not arguing from a 'dignity' that is symbolic or abstract; it is arguing from a literal resemblance that grounds a literal claim. The post-Flood legal order is being founded on the fact that humans look like the Elohim.

7
As for you — be fruitful and multiply, teem upon the land, and multiply in it.
And you, be ye fruitful, and multiply; Bring forth abundantly in the earth, and multiply therein.
וְאַתֶּ֖ם פְּר֣וּ וּרְב֑וּ שִׁרְצ֥וּ בָאָ֖רֶץ וּרְבוּ־בָֽהּ׃
8
The Elohim said to Noah and to his sons with him, saying:
And God spake unto Noah, and to his sons with him, saying,
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֱלֹהִים֙ אֶל־נֹ֔חַ וְאֶל־בָּנָ֥יו אִתּ֖וֹ לֵאמֹֽר׃
9
As for me — here I am, establishing my covenant with you, and with your offspring after you,
And I, behold, I establish my covenant with you, and with your seed after you;
וַאֲנִ֕י הִנְנִ֥י מֵקִ֛ים אֶת־בְּרִיתִ֖י אִתְּכֶ֑ם וְאֶֽת־זַרְעֲכֶ֖ם אַֽחֲרֵיכֶֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The first explicit covenant in the Torah. Brit was first promised to Noah in 6:18 (and I will establish my brit with you); here it is enacted. The verb is meqim — establishing, from קוּם qum (to rise, stand). A brit is not declared; it is raised up between parties, like a structure being set in place.

What is novel is the scope. The chapter will widen it in v10: this brit is not only with humans but with every living nefesh aboard the ark — birds, cattle, every wild animal. The chapter is establishing the first universal cosmic covenant, addressed to all life. The later Abrahamic covenant (chapters 15, 17) and the Sinaitic covenant (Exodus 19–24) will both be narrower in scope: a single family, a single people. The Noahic covenant is the canopy under which the rest sit.

10
and with every living being that is with you — among the birds, among the cattle, and among every wild creature of the land that is with you; all of them that came out of the ark, every living thing of the land.
and with every living creature that is with you, the birds, the cattle, and every beast of the earth with you. Of all that go out of the ark, even every beast of the earth.
וְאֵ֨ת כָּל־נֶ֤פֶשׁ הַֽחַיָּה֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר אִתְּכֶ֔ם בָּע֧וֹף בַּבְּהֵמָ֛ה וּֽבְכָל־חַיַּ֥ת הָאָ֖רֶץ אִתְּכֶ֑ם מִכֹּל֙ יֹצְאֵ֣י הַתֵּבָ֔ה לְכֹ֖ל חַיַּ֥ת הָאָֽרֶץ׃
11
I will establish my covenant with you: never again shall all flesh be cut off by the waters of a flood; never again shall there be a flood to ruin the land.
And I will establish my covenant with you; neither shall all flesh be cut off any more by the waters of the flood; neither shall there any more be a flood to destroy the earth.
וַהֲקִמֹתִ֤י אֶת־בְּרִיתִי֙ אִתְּכֶ֔ם וְלֹֽא־יִכָּרֵ֧ת כָּל־בָּשָׂ֛ר ע֖וֹד מִמֵּ֣י הַמַּבּ֑וּל וְלֹֽא־יִהְיֶ֥ה ע֛וֹד מַבּ֖וּל לְשַׁחֵ֥ת הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

The 'never again' written into the brit. In 8:21 the never-again promise was internal (YHWH said in his heart, an inner resolve). Here in 9:11 it is externalized as a public covenant clause with witnesses. The structure of chapters 8–9 is: first the private divine resolution, then the public divine ratification. The chapter is staging the post-Flood promise twice — first as inward decision, then as outward covenant — and pairing them with the soothing-aroma offering (8:20–21) and the bow-in-the-cloud sign (9:13).

The verb yikkaret (shall be cut off) is the technical verb for treaty-violation consequences in the ancient Near East. The chapter is using treaty-vocabulary. The brit is structured like a treaty: parties named, terms specified, sign affixed, witnesses called.

12
The Elohim said: this is the sign of the covenant that I am giving between me and you, and between every living being that is with you, for the generations of forever:
And God said, This is the token of the covenant which I make between me and you and every living creature that is with you, for perpetual generations:
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֗ים זֹ֤את אֽוֹת־הַבְּרִית֙ אֲשֶׁר־אֲנִ֣י נֹתֵ֗ן בֵּינִי֙ וּבֵ֣ינֵיכֶ֔ם וּבֵ֛ין כָּל־נֶ֥פֶשׁ חַיָּ֖ה אֲשֶׁ֣ר אִתְּכֶ֑ם לְדֹרֹ֖ת עוֹלָֽם׃
13
My bow I have placed in the cloud, and it shall be a sign of covenant between me and the land.
I do set my bow in the cloud, and it shall be for a token of a covenant between me and the earth.
אֶת־קַשְׁתִּ֕י נָתַ֖תִּי בֶּֽעָנָ֑ן וְהָֽיְתָה֙ לְא֣וֹת בְּרִ֔ית בֵּינִ֖י וּבֵ֥ין הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

The bow that hangs in the cloud. The Hebrew noun is קֶשֶׁת qeshet — and elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible it almost exclusively means war-bow, the weapon. Qeshet is the bow of the warrior (Gen 27:3, 48:22), the bow of YHWH as divine warrior (Hab 3:9, 'you uncovered your bow'), the bow that is broken in eschatological peace texts (Hos 1:5, 2:18, Zech 9:10). The vocabulary is martial.

The chapter's choice to call the rainbow qeshet is not casual. The same noun that names the weapon that humans use against each other (cf. 5:5's darosh-edrosh logic) and that the divine warrior wields against enemies is now declared hung up, placed in the cloud, pointed (in the iconography of an unstrung bow held against a cloudy sky) away from the land and toward the heavens. The Elohim is unstringing the weapon.

On the WoH frame: the chapter is preserving an extraordinary image. The post-Flood promise is signed not with an abstract symbol but with an inverted weapon. The Elohim, having used cosmic violence on humanity once, hangs up the bow as a continuous public sign that the violence is over.

Comparative note: similar imagery appears in Mesopotamian flood traditions. In Atrahasis and the Gilgamesh XI flood, the goddess raises her necklace of lapis lazuli (the rainbow) and swears never again to forget the destruction. The motif of a celestial colored arc as a post-flood promise-token is shared; the Hebrew chapter has reshaped the lapis necklace into an unstrung war-bow.

14
Whenever I bring a cloud over the land, the bow shall be seen in the cloud.
And it shall come to pass, when I bring a cloud over the earth, that the bow shall be seen in the cloud,
וְהָיָ֕ה בְּעַֽנְנִ֥י עָנָ֖ן עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וְנִרְאֲתָ֥ה הַקֶּ֖שֶׁת בֶּעָנָֽן׃
15
And I will remember my covenant which is between me and you and every living being among all flesh; the waters shall never again become a flood to ruin all flesh.
and I will remember my covenant, which is between me and you and every living creature of all flesh; and the waters shall no more become a flood to destroy all flesh.
וְזָכַרְתִּ֣י אֶת־בְּרִיתִ֗י אֲשֶׁ֤ר בֵּינִי֙ וּבֵ֣ינֵיכֶ֔ם וּבֵ֛ין כָּל־נֶ֥פֶשׁ חַיָּ֖ה בְּכָל־בָּשָׂ֑ר וְלֹֽא־יִֽהְיֶ֨ה ע֤וֹד הַמַּ֙יִם֙ לְמַבּ֔וּל לְשַׁחֵ֖ת כָּל־בָּשָֽׂר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 15

The Elohim binds the Elohim's own memory. The verb is zakharti — the same root as 8:1's vayyizkor Elohim et Noach (the Elohim remembered Noah). At the start of the Flood-reversal, divine memory was the act that turned the waters back. Here that same memory is built into the covenant as the function of the sign. The bow is not a memory aid for humans (humans do not need a reminder that they are alive); it is a memory aid the Elohim establishes for the Elohim. Whenever a cloud comes, the bow appears, and the Elohim's covenant-memory is triggered.

The chapter has now established the architecture of biblical covenant memory: a sign is given (natatti — v13), the sign is seen (nirathah — v14), the covenant is remembered (vezakharti — v15). Three verbs, three steps. The same architecture will recur at Sinai (the law on tablets-of-stone as covenant-memory) and in the prophets ('I have remembered my covenant').

16
The bow shall be in the cloud, and I will see it — to remember the everlasting covenant between the Elohim and every living being among all flesh that is upon the land.
And the bow shall be in the cloud; and I will look upon it, that I may remember the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of all flesh that is upon the earth.
וְהָיְתָ֥ה הַקֶּ֖שֶׁת בֶּֽעָנָ֑ן וּרְאִיתִ֗יהָ לִזְכֹּר֙ בְּרִ֣ית עוֹלָ֔ם בֵּ֣ין אֱלֹהִ֔ים וּבֵין֙ כָּל־נֶ֣פֶשׁ חַיָּ֔ה בְּכָל־בָּשָׂ֖ר אֲשֶׁ֥ר עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
17
The Elohim said to Noah: this is the sign of the covenant which I have established between me and all flesh that is upon the land.
And God said unto Noah, This is the token of the covenant which I have established between me and all flesh that is upon the earth.
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֖ים אֶל־נֹ֑חַ זֹ֤את אֽוֹת־הַבְּרִית֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר הֲקִמֹ֔תִי בֵּינִ֕י וּבֵ֥ין כָּל־בָּשָׂ֖ר אֲשֶׁ֥ר עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
18
The sons of Noah who came out of the ark were Shem and Ham and Yapheth; and Ham — he is the father of Canaan.
And the sons of Noah, that went forth from the ark, were Shem, and Ham, and Japheth: and Ham is the father of Canaan.
וַיִּֽהְי֣וּ בְנֵי־נֹ֗חַ הַיֹּֽצְאִים֙ מִן־הַתֵּבָ֔ה שֵׁ֖ם וְחָ֣ם וָיָ֑פֶת וְחָ֕ם ה֖וּא אֲבִ֥י כְנָֽעַן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

The narrator pre-frames the curse. Note the timing: the chapter names Ham's three brothers, then immediately re-identifies Ham as the father of Canaan — before any incident has been narrated. The chapter is editorially priming the reader for v25 (the curse on Canaan, not on Ham himself). The phrase Cham hu avi Kena'an is repeated in v22, bookending the incident. The chapter is making sure the reader can connect the post-Flood family to the later Israelite-Canaanite political geography: the curse that falls in this scene will explain the conquest narratives later in the Torah.

19
These three were the sons of Noah, and from these the whole land was scattered.
These three were the sons of Noah: and of these was the whole earth overspread.
שְׁלֹשָׁ֥ה אֵ֖לֶּה בְּנֵי־נֹ֑חַ וּמֵאֵ֖לֶּה נָֽפְצָ֥ה כָל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
20
Noah, a man of the soil, began — he planted a vineyard.
And Noah began to be a husbandman, and planted a vineyard:
וַיָּ֥חֶל נֹ֖חַ אִ֣ישׁ הָֽאֲדָמָ֑ה וַיִּטַּ֖ע כָּֽרֶם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

Noah the second Adam, planting. The phrase ish ha-adamah — man of the soil — echoes 2:5's ein adam la'avod et ha-adamah (there was no human to till the soil) and 4:2's Qayin hayah oved adamah (Cain was a tiller of soil). Noah is being named as a continuation of the soil-cultivation line. The first human was placed in a garden; the first human's first son was a soil-tiller; the second-first human's first recorded post-Flood act is planting a vineyard. The chapter is tracking an evolving agricultural identity: garden → field → vineyard.

Vayyachel — he began. Some rabbinic readings (Bereshit Rabbah 36) heard the verb as vayyachel from חלל chalal (to profane, to make common) — Noah profaned. The Hebrew is ambiguous; the chapter does not commit. But the wordplay sits at the start of the scene, and the events of v21 will retroactively color the verb.

21
He drank of the wine and became drunk, and uncovered himself within his tent.
and he drank of the wine, and was drunken. And he was uncovered within his tent.
וַיֵּ֥שְׁתְּ מִן־הַיַּ֖יִן וַיִּשְׁכָּ֑ר וַיִּתְגַּ֖ל בְּת֥וֹךְ אָהֳלֹֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

The first drunkenness, the first uncovering. Three perfect-consecutive verbs in tight sequence — vayyesht (and he drank), vayyishkar (and he became drunk), vayyitgal (and he uncovered himself). The chapter does not condemn the wine, the drink, or the drunkenness directly; it narrates the sequence as fact and then narrates what happens next.

Vayyitgal — from גָּלָה galah, the same root as Galilee and galut (exile, uncovering). The reflexive form (hithpa'el) is rare and pointed: he uncovered himself. The next verse (v22) speaks of Ham seeing — but the chapter narrates Noah unmaking his own cover first. The vulnerability is self-induced and accidental, not assault.

The scene rhymes with chapter 3. There: a piece of fruit, a knowing, a discovered nakedness, a covering with fig leaves. Here: a vine's fruit (wine), a stupor, a self-uncovering, a covering with a garment. The chapter is doing a second-Adam pattern in miniature — Noah's vineyard scene as the post-Flood echo of Eden.

22
Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father, and told his two brothers outside.
And Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father, and told his two brethren without.
וַיַּ֗רְא חָ֚ם אֲבִ֣י כְנַ֔עַן אֵ֖ת עֶרְוַ֣ת אָבִ֑יו וַיַּגֵּ֥ד לִשְׁנֵֽי־אֶחָ֖יו בַּחֽוּץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The contested verse. The Hebrew is short, and that brevity is part of the interpretive problem. Vayyar Cham et ervat aviv — Ham saw the nakedness of his father. Vayyaged li-shenei echav ba-chutz — and he told his two brothers outside.

Three readings have circulated through the history of interpretation:

  1. Plain reading: Ham literally saw his father's naked body and gossiped about it to his brothers. The transgression is the seeing-and-telling — the violation of Noah's dignity, especially as compared with the brothers' careful aversion in v23. This is the reading most consistent with the chapter's own next-verse demonstration of the right response.

  2. Euphemism for sexual assault: The phrase ervat aviv (the nakedness of one's father) functions as a sexual idiom elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible (Lev 18:7–8, 20:11), where it can name sexual contact with the father's wife. Some readers (already in late antique Jewish tradition, e.g. b. Sanhedrin 70a) infer that something worse than a glance occurred. The chapter's text does not say so; the legal idiom is later.

  3. Euphemism for paternal castration: A minority reading (also in Sanhedrin 70a) extrapolates that Ham injured Noah in some way that prevented him from fathering a fourth son. The text gives no support beyond the heavy curse that follows.

The chapter itself is silent on which reading is correct. What the chapter does commit to is that whatever happened, it was sufficient to warrant the curse on Canaan in v25.

The re-identification of Ham as avi Kena'an (the father of Canaan) is repeated here for the third time in five verses (vv. 18, 22, 25). The chapter is hammering the genealogical link. Whoever Ham was, what is being set up is the etiology of the people Canaan — the indigenous population of the land that Israel would later occupy. The narrative is doing the political work of explaining a future conquest by placing its origin in a primal family scene.

23
Shem and Yapheth took the garment and laid it on the shoulders of the two of them, and walked backward, and covered the nakedness of their father; their faces were backward, and the nakedness of their father they did not see.
And Shem and Japheth took a garment, and laid it upon both their shoulders, and went backward, and covered the nakedness of their father. And their faces were backward, and they saw not their father`s nakedness.
וַיִּקַּח֩ שֵׁ֨ם וָיֶ֜פֶת אֶת־הַשִּׂמְלָ֗ה וַיָּשִׂ֙ימוּ֙ עַל־שְׁכֶ֣ם שְׁנֵיהֶ֔ם וַיֵּֽלְכוּ֙ אֲחֹ֣רַנִּ֔ית וַיְכַסּ֕וּ אֵ֖ת עֶרְוַ֣ת אֲבִיהֶ֑ם וּפְנֵיהֶם֙ אֲחֹ֣רַנִּ֔ית וְעֶרְוַ֥ת אֲבִיהֶ֖ם לֹ֥א רָאֽוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

Ostentatious carefulness as moral demonstration. The chapter spends a long verse on a small action. The brothers (i) took a garment, (ii) placed it on the shoulders of both of them together, (iii) walked backward into the tent, (iv) covered their father's nakedness without turning, (v) kept their faces backward, and (vi) did not see. Six clauses for one act of covering.

The verbal contrast with v22 is the point. Ham saw (vayyar) and told (vayyaged). Shem and Yapheth placed (vayyasimu), walked (vayelchu), covered (vaykhasu), and did not see (lo ra'u). The chapter is using verb-architecture to do moral commentary without commentary: one brother accumulates verbs of exposure, two brothers accumulate verbs of covering. The narrator's evaluation is in the syntax.

24
Noah awoke from his wine, and knew what his youngest son had done to him.
And Noah awoke from his wine, and knew what his youngest son had done unto him.
וַיִּ֥יקֶץ נֹ֖חַ מִיֵּינ֑וֹ וַיֵּ֕דַע אֵ֛ת אֲשֶׁר־עָ֥שָׂה־ל֖וֹ בְּנ֥וֹ הַקָּטָֽן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 24

'His youngest son' — bno ha-qatan. The phrase has produced an old textual puzzle. The chapter has been listing Noah's sons as Shem, Ham, Yapheth (vv. 18, 19, 23) — that ordering places Ham as the middle son, not the youngest. Either (a) the listing order is by prominence rather than birth order, with Ham actually the youngest; or (b) the phrase bno ha-qatan is doing other work (the diminutive qatan can mean 'small' in dignity, not only in age, in some idioms). The Hebrew is not unambiguous. The chapter does not stop to resolve.

Vayyeda et asher asah lo — and he knew what he had done to him. The verb yada (to know) — the same verb that organized Eden in 2:9 (the tree of the knowing) and the recognition of nakedness in 3:7 (and they knew that they were naked). Noah waking from wine into knowing-what-his-son-did continues the chapter's Eden-rhyme. The first knowing led to the first hiding; the second knowing leads to the first curse-by-a-human.

25
He said: cursed be Canaan; a slave of slaves shall he be to his brothers.
And he said, Cursed be Canaan; A servant of servants shall he be unto his brethren.
וַיֹּ֖אמֶר אָר֣וּר כְּנָ֑עַן עֶ֥בֶד עֲבָדִ֖ים יִֽהְיֶ֥ה לְאֶחָֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

The curse skips the offender. Ham is the one who saw and told. Canaan is the one who is cursed. The chapter does not explain the skip, and the silence has been heavily theologized across the history of interpretation — none of it grounded in the text. What the text gives is the brute political function: the people Canaan, whose territory Israel will later occupy, are being preassigned to slave-status to Shem's line (the Israelite line) and Yapheth's line.

Eved avadim — slave of slaves. The grammatical construct (construct of intensification, like holy of holies, song of songs) is superlative: the lowest possible status. The chapter is installing the political grammar of conquest in a primal-family curse.

The verse has a brutal reception history: in colonial-era Christian readings, the curse-on-Canaan was racialized and repurposed to justify the African slave trade (the so-called 'curse of Ham', stripped of its actual textual referent to Canaan, the people of the southern Levant). The text is not racial; it is regional and political. But the misreading was load-bearing for centuries of racial-religious ideology, and the historical violence is real. WoH commentary will not minimize that reception, and will not minimize the chapter's own ethnocentric framing either: the curse-of-Canaan formula was the Torah's first attempt to give a divinely-sanctioned origin story to its conflicts with neighbors.

26
He said: blessed be YHWH, Elohim of Shem; and let Canaan be a slave to him.
And he said, Blessed be Jehovah, the God of Shem; And let Canaan be his servant.
וַיֹּ֕אמֶר בָּר֥וּךְ יְהֹוָ֖ה אֱלֹ֣הֵי שֵׁ֑ם וִיהִ֥י כְנַ֖עַן עֶ֥בֶד לָֽמוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 26

YHWH is identified as the elohim of Shem. This is the first time in the Torah that YHWH is identified by relationship to a specific human line. Before this, YHWH has been the Elohim of the humans (collectively); here YHWH is the elohei Shem — the Elohim of Shem. The pattern will repeat: Elohei Avraham (Elohim of Abraham), Elohei Yitzchaq (Elohim of Isaac), Elohei Yisrael (Elohim of Israel). The chapter is opening the genealogical bracket inside which the rest of the Torah's covenant theology will operate. The future Israelite line is being demarcated, here, in a blessing on Shem.

27
May the Elohim enlarge Yapheth; and may he dwell in the tents of Shem; and let Canaan be a slave to him.
God enlarge Japheth, And let him dwell in the tents of Shem; And let Canaan be his servant.
יַ֤פְתְּ אֱלֹהִים֙ לְיֶ֔פֶת וְיִשְׁכֹּ֖ן בְּאָֽהֳלֵי־שֵׁ֑ם וִיהִ֥י כְנַ֖עַן עֶ֥בֶד לָֽמוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 27

The name-pun. Yapht Elohim l-Yephet — may the Elohim enlarge for Yapheth. The verb yapht (from פתה patah — to be wide, to open up, to enlarge) is the same root as the name Yephet itself. The chapter is folding a wish into a name: may the wide one widen. The same naming-by-pun pattern recurs throughout Genesis (Adam from adamah, Hevel from hevel, Yitzchaq from tzachaq, Yisrael from sarah).

Yishkon b'oholei Shem — may he dwell in the tents of Shem. The clause is grammatically ambiguous: does he refer back to Yapheth (Yapheth will dwell in Shem's tents — a peaceful coexistence reading), or does it refer to the Elohim (the Elohim will dwell in Shem's tents — an early sanctuary-theology reading)? Rabbinic readers split. The chapter does not commit. Either way the structural point is that Shem's tents become the privileged location.

28
Noah lived after the Flood three hundred years and fifty years.
And Noah lived after the flood three hundred and fifty years.
וַֽיְחִי־נֹ֖חַ אַחַ֣ר הַמַּבּ֑וּל שְׁלֹ֤שׁ מֵאוֹת֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וַֽחֲמִשִּׁ֖ים שָׁנָֽה׃
29
All the days of Noah were nine hundred years and fifty years, and he died.
And all the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years: And he died.
וַיִּֽהְיוּ֙ כָּל־יְמֵי־נֹ֔חַ תְּשַׁ֤ע מֵאוֹת֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וַחֲמִשִּׁ֖ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיָּמֹֽת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 29

The last antediluvian-scale lifespan. Noah lives 950 years total — longer than every patriarch in chapter 5 except Methuselah (969) and Yared (962), and 19 years longer than Adam (930). His lifespan straddles the Flood: 600 years before, 350 years after. The chapter closes the second half of his life in a single verse, mirroring the closing formulae of chapter 5 (and all his days were X years, and he died).

Noah is the last person in the Torah to live near a millennium. After him, the lifespans contract sharply: Shem lives 600 (11:11), Arpachshad 438 (11:13), and the curve descends until Abraham at 175 (25:7) and Moses at 120 (Deut 34:7). The chapter is setting up the post-Flood lifespan-decay arc that will run for the next two chapters and shape all later patriarchal accounts.

The 120-year ceiling announced in 6:3 does not bind here yet — Noah and his immediate descendants still live far beyond it. The chapter's calendar of life-lengths is a slow descent across generations, not an instant cap.

Chapter 10

Genesis 10 — The Table of Nations

1
These are the begettings of the sons of Noah — Shem, Ham, and Yapheth; sons were born to them after the deluge.
Now these are the generations of the sons of Noah, [namely], of Shem, Ham, and Japheth: and unto them were sons born after the flood.
וְאֵ֙לֶּה֙ תּוֹלְדֹ֣ת בְּנֵי־נֹ֔חַ שֵׁ֖ם חָ֣ם וָיָ֑פֶת וַיִּוָּלְד֥וּ לָהֶ֛ם בָּנִ֖ים אַחַ֥ר הַמַּבּֽוּל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The toledot formula resumes. This is the fourth toledot in Genesis (after 2:4 of the skies and the land, 5:1 of Adam, 6:9 of Noah), and the first to organize an ethnographic catalogue rather than a personal genealogy. The chapter's structure mirrors the Flood-clean-slate: after the Flood, the human family branches into the three lines that will populate the post-Flood world. Acharei ha-mabbul — after the deluge — names the chronological frame: everything in this chapter unfolds in the new era.

2
The sons of Yapheth: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Yavan, Tuval, Meshech, and Tiras.
The sons of Japheth: Gomer, and Magog, and Madai, and Javan, and Tubal, and Meshech, and Tiras.
בְּנֵ֣י יֶ֔פֶת גֹּ֣מֶר וּמָג֔וֹג וּמָדַ֖י וְיָוָ֣ן וְתֻבָ֑ל וּמֶ֖שֶׁךְ וְתִירָֽס׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The Japhethite line — the peoples of the northern and western rim. The names map onto the Mediterranean and Black Sea horizon of the Hebrew Bible's geographic imagination: Gomer with the Cimmerians (Akkadian Gimirrai); Magog a region north of the Caucasus (Ezek 38–39's eschatological adversary); Madai the Medes (Iran); Yavan the Greeks (Ionia); Tuval and Meshech in eastern Anatolia (Tabal and Mushki in Assyrian sources); Tiras possibly the Tyrsenoi (Etruscans) or Thracians. The chapter places the Japhethites in the lands that Israel later encounters as trade partners, distant rivals, or apocalyptic threats — but rarely as direct neighbors.

3
The sons of Gomer: Ashkenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah.
And the sons of Gomer: Ashkenaz, and Riphath, and Togarmah.
וּבְנֵ֖י גֹּ֑מֶר אַשְׁכֲּנַ֥ז וְרִיפַ֖ת וְתֹגַרְמָֽה׃
4
The sons of Yavan: Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim.
And the sons of Javan: Elishah, and Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim.
וּבְנֵ֥י יָוָ֖ן אֱלִישָׁ֣ה וְתַרְשִׁ֑ישׁ כִּתִּ֖ים וְדֹדָנִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

Greek-world toponyms. Elishah is generally identified with Cyprus (Alashiya in Late Bronze Age sources). Tarshish is the Hebrew Bible's far-western trading horizon — variously identified with Tartessos (southern Spain), Sardinia, or a generic 'farthest west' label. Kittim is Cyprus or coastal Greece in its earliest usage, later extended to refer to Romans and other western powers in later prophetic and Qumran literature. Dodanim is read as Rodanim in some manuscripts (Rhodes); the daleth/resh swap is a common scribal variant in Hebrew.

5
From these the coastlands of the nations were separated into their lands, each by his tongue, by their families, in their nations.
Of these were the isles of the nations divided in their lands, every one after his tongue, after their families, in their nations.
מֵ֠אֵלֶּה נִפְרְד֞וּ אִיֵּ֤י הַגּוֹיִם֙ בְּאַרְצֹתָ֔ם אִ֖ישׁ לִלְשֹׁנ֑וֹ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָ֖ם בְּגוֹיֵהֶֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

The summary formula for the Japhethite branch. Iyei ha-goyim — coastlands of the nations — names the Japhethites as the maritime, language-distinct peoples of the rim. The formula has four dimensions: lands (geographic), tongues (linguistic), families (kinship), nations (political). The same four dimensions recur in the summary formulae for the Hamite line (v20) and the Shemite line (v31). The fact that the language-distinction is already in place here, before the Babel narrative of chapter 11, is one of the chapter's structural seams: the Table of Nations presents linguistic diversity as a present reality; chapter 11 explains its origin. The two are different sources stitched together; the redactor preserved both. The result is a chapter that says each by his tongue before the chapter that says how they came to have different tongues.

6
The sons of Ham: Kush, Mitsrayim, Put, and Canaan.
And the sons of Ham: Cush, and Mizraim, and Put, and Canaan.
וּבְנֵ֖י חָ֑ם כּ֥וּשׁ וּמִצְרַ֖יִם וּפ֥וּט וּכְנָֽעַן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

The Hamite line — the southern and southwestern peoples. Kush is the Nile-valley region south of Egypt (Nubia / modern Sudan); Mitsrayim is the dual-form name for Egypt (literally 'the two Mitsrayims' — Upper and Lower Egypt, the dual reflecting the political duality of the Egyptian state in the Hebrew Bible's view); Put is Libya or the eastern African coast; Canaan is the land Israel will later occupy. The Hamites are framed as the peoples Israel will encounter on its southern borders and within its eventual territory.

7
The sons of Kush: Seba, Havilah, Sabtah, Raamah, and Sabtecha; and the sons of Raamah: Sheba and Dedan.
And the sons of Cush: Seba, and Havilah, and Sabtah, and Raamah, and Sabteca; and the sons of Raamah: Sheba, and Dedan.
וּבְנֵ֣י כ֔וּשׁ סְבָא֙ וַֽחֲוִילָ֔ה וְסַבְתָּ֥ה וְרַעְמָ֖ה וְסַבְתְּכָ֑א וּבְנֵ֥י רַעְמָ֖ה שְׁבָ֥א וּדְדָֽן׃
8
And Kush fathered Nimrod — he was the first to be a mighty one in the land.
And Cush begat Nimrod: he began to be a mighty one in the earth.
וְכ֖וּשׁ יָלַ֣ד אֶת־נִמְרֹ֑ד ה֣וּא הֵחֵ֔ל לִֽהְי֥וֹת גִּבֹּ֖ר בָּאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

The Nimrod parenthesis opens. The chapter pauses its enumeration to introduce a single named figure across five verses (v8–12). Hu hechel lihyot gibbor ba-aretz — he was the first to be a mighty one in the land — uses the same noun גִּבּוֹר gibbor that named the antediluvian gibborim anshei ha-shem of 6:4 (the offspring of the bnei ha-elohim with the daughters of the human). The post-Flood world has its first gibbor, and the chapter names him: Nimrod. The verbal echo across 6:4 and 10:8 is unmistakable — the chapter is asserting that the gibbor-class did not end with the Flood. How that survives the wipe-out of 7:23 is the same problem the Nephilim raise in Numbers 13:33, and the chapter does not resolve it.

9
He was a mighty hunter before YHWH; therefore it is said: 'Like Nimrod, a mighty hunter before YHWH.'
He was a mighty hunter before Jehovah: wherefore it is said, Like Nimrod a mighty hunter before Jehovah.
הֽוּא־הָיָ֥ה גִבֹּֽר־צַ֖יִד לִפְנֵ֣י יְהוָ֑ה עַל־כֵּן֙ יֵֽאָמַ֔ר כְּנִמְרֹ֛ד גִּבּ֥וֹר צַ֖יִד לִפְנֵ֥י יְהוָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

A proverb embedded in the narrative. The chapter cites a popular saying — like Nimrod, a mighty hunter before YHWH — as already in circulation in the period the chapter is composed. The text is reporting a folk-tradition about Nimrod, not constructing one. // The preposition לִפְנֵי lifnei ('before, in the presence of') is grammatically neutral — it can mean 'in the favor of' (positive), 'in defiance of' (adversarial), or simply 'in the sight of' (witnessed by). Jewish tradition has read it as adversarial (Nimrod the rebel-against-YHWH, builder of Babel); Christian tradition has often read it as positive (Nimrod the divinely-empowered hunter). The Hebrew settles neither. The chapter places Nimrod visible to YHWH without taking sides on the relationship's polarity. // Tzayid — hunter — names a specialized warrior-class skill. In ANE iconography, royal lion-hunting was the signature image of kingship (Assyrian palace reliefs; the Standard of Ur). Nimrod the gibbor-tzayid is the Hebrew Bible's compressed image of the founder-king.

10
The beginning of his kingdom was Babel, and Erech, and Akkad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar.
And the beginning of his kingdom was Babel, and Erech, and Accad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar.
וַתְּהִ֨י רֵאשִׁ֤ית מַמְלַכְתּוֹ֙ בָּבֶ֔ל וְאֶ֖רֶךְ וְאַכַּ֣ד וְכַלְנֵ֑ה בְּאֶ֖רֶץ שִׁנְעָֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

The founding of Mesopotamian civilization, named. The chapter attributes the four foundational southern-Mesopotamian cities to a single founder — Nimrod. Babel (Bavel in Hebrew) — the future Babylon, whose ziggurat will be the subject of chapter 11. Erech — Sumerian Uruk, the great walled city of the third-millennium-BCE Sumerian world; archaeologically the largest city of its era. Akkad — the capital of Sargon's third-millennium empire, the first political entity to project unified rule across all of Mesopotamia; its location is archaeologically unknown but textually well-attested. Calneh — uncertain identification; possibly Nippur (the Sumerian religious center) or an unidentified site. // Eretz Shinar — the land of Shinar — is the chapter's standard Hebrew term for the alluvial plain of southern Mesopotamia (Sumer + Akkad). It returns in 11:2 as the location of Babel-tower construction, in 14:1 (Amraphel king of Shinar), and in later prophetic literature (Isa 11:11, Zech 5:11, Dan 1:2) where it carries an exilic register. // The Wheel of Heaven lens reads this verse with attention: the chapter is naming Mesopotamia as the cradle of post-Flood civilization, attributing the founding to a gibbor whose lineage traces (via Cush) to Ham. The first city-founder of the post-Flood world is, on the Hebrew's terms, a mighty one in the same vocabulary as the antediluvian gibborim anshei ha-shem. The connection between human technological-political ambition and the gibbor-class is being drawn at the chapter's textual surface.

11
From that land Asshur went out, and built Nineveh, and Rehoboth-Ir, and Calah.
Out of that land he went forth into Assyria, and builded Nineveh, and Rehoboth-ir, and Calah,
מִן־הָאָ֥רֶץ הַהִ֖וא יָצָ֣א אַשּׁ֑וּר וַיִּ֙בֶן֙ אֶת־נִ֣ינְוֵ֔ה וְאֶת־רְחֹבֹ֥ת עִ֖יר וְאֶת־כָּֽלַח׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

The Mesopotamian-civilization founding extends north. The verse is grammatically ambiguous about who Asshur is: (a) Asshur the person (a son of Shem, named in v22) went out from Shinar and founded the northern Mesopotamian cities, OR (b) reading the same consonants as a verbal phrase, Nimrod went out to Assyria and built those cities (this is the marginal Jewish reading: 'from that land he went out to Assyria'). The plain reading is (a), with Asshur the founder of northern Mesopotamian civilization paralleling Nimrod's founding of southern Mesopotamian civilization. // Nineveh is the future Assyrian capital (modern Mosul region). Rehoboth-Ir (literally 'broad-places city') and Calah (Kalhu, modern Nimrud — named for Nimrod in traditional etymology) are the other major Assyrian cities. The chapter is compressing the entire ANE city-founding tradition into two figures: Nimrod for the south, Asshur for the north.

12
And Resen, between Nineveh and Calah — that is the great city.
and Resen between Nineveh and Calah (the same is the great city).
וְֽאֶת־רֶ֔סֶן בֵּ֥ין נִֽינְוֵ֖ה וּבֵ֣ין כָּ֑לַח הִ֖וא הָעִ֥יר הַגְּדֹלָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

Ha-ir ha-gedolah — the great city. The phrase has a sticky referent: grammatically it can attach to Resen (the immediately preceding city) or to Calah, or it can be read as a summary descriptor of the whole Nineveh-Rehoboth-Calah-Resen complex (the 'great city' as the larger metropolitan area). The phrase echoes Jonah 1:2, 3:2, 4:11, where the great city is unambiguously Nineveh. The Genesis chapter probably uses the phrase in the larger-complex sense; Jonah will later use it to name Nineveh specifically. The Nimrod parenthesis closes here.

13
Mitsrayim fathered the Ludim, the Anamim, the Lehabim, and the Naphtuhim.
And Mizraim begat Ludim, and Anamim, and Lehabim, and Naphtuhim,
וּמִצְרַ֡יִם יָלַ֞ד אֶת־לוּדִ֧ים וְאֶת־עֲנָמִ֛ים וְאֶת־לְהָבִ֖ים וְאֶת־נַפְתֻּחִֽים׃
14
and the Patrusim, and the Casluhim — from whom the Pelishtim went out — and the Caphtorim.
and Pathrusim, and Casluhim (whence went forth the Philistines), and Caphtorim.
וְֽאֶת־פַּתְרֻסִ֞ים וְאֶת־כַּסְלֻחִ֗ים אֲשֶׁ֨ר יָצְא֥וּ מִשָּׁ֛ם פְּלִשְׁתִּ֖ים וְאֶת־כַּפְתֹּרִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

The Philistines come from where? The parenthesis אֲשֶׁר יָצְאוּ מִשָּׁם פְּלִשְׁתִּים — from whom the Pelishtim went out — places the Philistines as descendants of the Casluhim. But the prophetic tradition (Jer 47:4, Amos 9:7) and the consensus modern reading (matching Egyptian and Aegean archaeological evidence) trace the Philistines from Caphtor (Crete / the Aegean), not from the Casluhim. The parenthesis may be a scribal misplacement: it should attach to the Caphtorim that immediately follow. The textual problem is well-attested in the manuscript tradition. // Either way, the chapter is locating the Philistines (the Iron Age I newcomers who would dominate Israel's south-coast throughout the Judges and early monarchy period) within the Hamite-Egyptian sphere — which is geographically and culturally accurate to the Sea-Peoples-origin model that contemporary archaeology supports.

15
Canaan fathered Sidon his firstborn, and Heth.
And Canaan begat Sidon his first-born, and Heth,
וּכְנַ֗עַן יָלַ֛ד אֶת־צִידֹ֥ן בְּכֹר֖וֹ וְאֶת־חֵֽת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 15

The Canaanite line. Tsidon (Sidon) is the Phoenician city on the Lebanese coast — the chapter names it as Canaan's firstborn, locating the Phoenician civilization as the senior Canaanite descendant. Cheth is the Hittites — though the biblical Hittites are likely a distinct group (Anatolian sub-population residing in Canaan) from the Anatolian Hittite Empire.

16
and the Jebusite, the Amorite, and the Girgashite,
and the Jebusite, and the Amorite, and the Girgashite,
וְאֶת־הַיְבוּסִי֙ וְאֶת־הָ֣אֱמֹרִ֔י וְאֵ֖ת הַגִּרְגָּשִֽׁי׃
17
and the Hivite, the Arkite, and the Sinite,
and the Hivite, and the Arkite, and the Sinite,
וְאֶת־הַֽחִוִּ֥י וְאֶת־הַֽעַרְקִ֖י וְאֶת־הַסִּינִֽי׃
18
and the Arvadite, the Zemarite, and the Hamathite. Afterward the families of the Canaanite were scattered.
and the Arvadite, and the Zemarite, and the Hamathite: and afterward were the families of the Canaanite spread abroad.
וְאֶת־הָֽאַרְוָדִ֥י וְאֶת־הַצְּמָרִ֖י וְאֶת־הַֽחֲמָתִ֑י וְאַחַ֣ר נָפֹ֔צוּ מִשְׁפְּח֖וֹת הַֽכְּנַעֲנִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

Naphotzu — they were scattered. The verb is the same as in 11:8–9 (YHWH scattered the people from Babel) and in 9:19 (the scattering from Noah's sons). The Canaanite peoples are placed by the chapter as a scattered cluster, with no single political center — the geographic spread to be detailed in v19.

19
The territory of the Canaanite extended from Sidon, in the direction of Gerar, as far as Gaza, in the direction of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboim, as far as Lasha.
And the border of the Canaanite was from Sidon, as thou goest toward Gerar, unto Gaza; as thou goest toward Sodom and Gomorrah and Admah and Zeboiim, unto Lasha.
וַֽיְהִ֞י גְּב֤וּל הַֽכְּנַעֲנִי֙ מִצִּידֹ֔ן בֹּאֲכָ֥ה גְרָ֖רָה עַד־עַזָּ֑ה בֹּאֲכָ֞ה סְדֹ֧מָה וַעֲמֹרָ֛ה וְאַדְמָ֥ה וּצְבֹיִ֖ם עַד־לָֽשַׁע׃
Translation Commentary Verse 19

The geography of Canaan, prospectively. The chapter is sketching the area Israel will later occupy as a single political-ethnographic zone, defining its boundaries by the major coastal city (Sidon in the north), the southern coastal-plain cities (Gerar and Gaza), the cities of the plain (Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboim — destroyed in Gen 19), and an eastern limit (Lasha, location uncertain). The verse is one of the chapter's clearer cases where the post-Flood ethnographic frame includes information that only makes sense in light of later narrative — the chapter is composed in awareness of Sodom-Gomorrah's later destruction and Israel's later occupation.

20
These are the sons of Ham, by their families, by their tongues, in their lands, in their nations.
These are the sons of Ham, after their families, after their tongues, in their lands, in their nations.
אֵ֣לֶּה בְנֵי־חָ֔ם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָ֖ם לִלְשֹֽׁנֹתָ֑ם בְּאַרְצֹתָ֖ם בְּגוֹיֵהֶֽם׃
21
To Shem also there were born — he the father of all the sons of Eber, the elder brother of Yapheth.
And unto Shem, the father of all the children of Eber, the elder brother of Japheth, to him also were children born.
וּלְשֵׁ֥ם יֻלַּ֖ד גַּם־ה֑וּא אֲבִי֙ כָּל־בְּנֵי־עֵ֔בֶר אֲחִ֖י יֶ֥פֶת הַגָּדֽוֹל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

Shem is identified as the father of all the sons of Eber. The verse anticipates v24–25, where Eber appears as Shem's great-grandson. The early identification flags Eber's prospective importance: עֵבֶר Eber is the eponymous ancestor of the Ivri (Hebrew) people — the etymology is widely (though not universally) accepted in modern scholarship. The chapter is opening the Shemite branch by naming, four generations ahead, the figure from whom Israel's own self-designation will derive. // Achi Yepheth ha-gadol — the elder brother of Yapheth. The Hebrew grammar is ambiguous about whether ha-gadol (the elder/great) modifies Yepheth (making Yapheth the elder brother) or Shem (making Shem the elder); the standard reading is the former. The chapter is positioning Yapheth as the eldest of Noah's three sons, even though the listing order (Shem, Ham, Yapheth) has placed Shem first throughout. The ordering is by prominence (Shem-line is Israel's), not by birth order.

22
The sons of Shem: Elam, Asshur, Arpachshad, Lud, and Aram.
The sons of Shem: Elam, and Asshur, and Arpachshad, and Lud, and Aram.
בְּנֵ֥י שֵׁ֖ם עֵילָ֣ם וְאַשּׁ֑וּר וְאַרְפַּכְשַׁ֖ד וְל֥וּד וַֽאֲרָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The Shemite line — the peoples of the eastern and northeastern arc. Elam is the powerful eastern neighbor of Mesopotamia (southwestern Iran; the capital Susa). Asshur is the Assyrian people (the name is identical to the founder of v11 — possibly a doublet, possibly two distinct figures sharing the name). Arpachshad is the ancestor through whom the line will pass to Eber, Peleg, and ultimately Abraham. Lud is variously identified (Lydia in Anatolia, or a Semitic Lud). Aram is the Aramean peoples of upper Mesopotamia and Syria — Israel's later northern cousins in language and culture.

23
The sons of Aram: Uz, Hul, Gether, and Mash.
And the sons of Aram: Uz, and Hul, and Gether, and Mash.
וּבְנֵ֖י אֲרָ֑ם ע֥וּץ וְח֖וּל וְגֶ֥תֶר וָמַֽשׁ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

Uz is the homeland of Job (Job 1:1, 'in the land of Uz'). The Aramean placement of Uz is one of the data-points by which Job is read as a non-Israelite text the Hebrew Bible nevertheless preserved as canonical.

24
Arpachshad fathered Shelach, and Shelach fathered Eber.
And Arpachshad begat Shelah; and Shelah begat Eber.
וְאַרְפַּכְשַׁ֖ד יָלַ֣ד אֶת־שָׁ֑לַח וְשֶׁ֖לַח יָלַ֥ד אֶת־עֵֽבֶר׃
25
To Eber were born two sons; the name of the one was Peleg, for in his days the land was divided; and his brother's name was Yoqtan.
And unto Eber were born two sons: The name of the one was Peleg. For in his days was the earth divided. And his brother`s name was Joktan.
וּלְעֵ֥בֶר יֻלַּ֖ד שְׁנֵ֣י בָנִ֑ים שֵׁ֣ם הָֽאֶחָ֞ד פֶּ֗לֶג כִּ֤י בְיָמָיו֙ נִפְלְגָ֣ה הָאָ֔רֶץ וְשֵׁ֥ם אָחִ֖יו יָקְטָֽן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

The first explicit dating-by-event in Genesis 10. Peleg — פֶּלֶג — is from the root פלג palag 'to divide, split'. The chapter glosses the name etiologically: ki b'yamav niflega ha-aretz — for in his days the land was divided. // What division? Three readings: // (1) The linguistic division of Babel (Gen 11). The chapter places Peleg in the genealogy just before the Babel narrative; the etiology pairs his name with the scattering-of-languages event. This is the standard ancient and modern interpretation. // (2) A geological / continental division — the reading favored by some modern creationist and ancient-astronaut traditions: that the splitting of continents (sometimes proposed as a post-Flood event) is what is meant. There is no support for this reading in the Hebrew lexicon or in the chapter's own self-frame. // (3) A political-territorial division — the division of post-Flood lands among the descendants of Noah, the very catalogue the chapter is providing. The verb is used elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible for the division of inherited land (Job 38:25). // The chapter does not specify which division is meant. The most contextually defensible reading is (1), the linguistic-political scattering; (3) is supplementary. (2) is interpretive and reads back from later cosmological frames. The translation preserves the bare 'the land was divided' and lets the commentary surface the spectrum.

26
Yoqtan fathered Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, and Yerach.
And Joktan begat Almodad, and Sheleph, and Hazarmaveth, and Jerah,
וְיָקְטָ֣ן יָלַ֔ד אֶת־אַלְמוֹדָ֖ד וְאֶת־שָׁ֑לֶף וְאֶת־חֲצַרְמָ֖וֶת וְאֶת־יָֽרַח׃
27
Hadoram, Uzal, and Diqlah,
and Hadoram, and Uzal, and Diklah,
וְאֶת־הֲדוֹרָ֥ם וְאֶת־אוּזָ֖ל וְאֶת־דִּקְלָֽה׃
28
Obal, Abimael, and Sheba,
and Obal, and Abimael, and Sheba,
וְאֶת־עוֹבָ֥ל וְאֶת־אֲבִֽימָאֵ֖ל וְאֶת־שְׁבָֽא׃
29
Ophir, Havilah, and Yobab — all these were the sons of Yoqtan.
and Ophir, and Havilah, and Jobab: all these were the sons of Joktan.
וְאֶת־אוֹפִ֥ר וְאֶת־חֲוִילָ֖ה וְאֶת־יוֹבָ֑ב כָּל־אֵ֖לֶּה בְּנֵ֥י יָקְטָֽן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 29

The Yoqtanite line is the Arabian-peninsula branch. Several of the names map onto South Arabian and Yemenite locations — Hazarmaveth (v26) is the Hadramawt region of Yemen; Sheba is the famous spice-trade kingdom (Saba); Ophir is the proverbial Hebrew Bible source of gold (1 Kgs 9:28, 10:11). The Shemite line forks at Eber into two branches: Peleg's line carries forward toward Abraham; Yoqtan's line populates Arabia.

30
Their dwelling extended from Mesha in the direction of Sephar — the mountain of the east.
And their dwelling was from Mesha, as thou goest toward Sephar, the mountain of the east.
וַֽיְהִ֥י מוֹשָׁבָ֖ם מִמֵּשָׁ֑א בֹּאֲכָ֥ה סְפָ֖רָה הַ֥ר הַקֶּֽדֶם׃
31
These are the sons of Shem, by their families, by their tongues, in their lands, by their nations.
These are the sons of Shem, after their families, after their tongues, in their lands, after their nations.
אֵ֣לֶּה בְנֵי־שֵׁ֔ם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָ֖ם לִלְשֹׁנֹתָ֑ם בְּאַרְצֹתָ֖ם לְגוֹיֵהֶֽם׃
32
These are the families of the sons of Noah, by their begettings, in their nations; and from these the nations were separated on the land after the deluge.
These are the families of the sons of Noah, after their generations, in their nations: and of these were the nations divided in the earth after the flood.
אֵ֣לֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹ֧ת בְּנֵי־נֹ֛חַ לְתוֹלְדֹתָ֖ם בְּגוֹיֵהֶ֑ם וּמֵאֵ֜לֶּה נִפְרְד֧וּ הַגּוֹיִ֛ם בָּאָ֖רֶץ אַחַ֥ר הַמַּבּֽוּל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 32
