The closing summary frames the whole chapter as a single ethnographic act. Mishpechot bnei Noach l'toledotam b'goyehem — the families of the sons of Noah, by their begettings, in their nations. The four organizing dimensions of the chapter (family, lineage, geography, polity) are listed in the closing verse, sealing the catalogue. // The verb niphredu — were separated — runs through the chapter: at v5 (the Japhethite coastlands), implicit in v18 (the Canaanites naphotzu scattered), and now in v32 as the universal closing claim. The chapter's keyword is separation — the post-Flood world is the separated world. Babel in chapter 11 will explain why.

Chapter 11

Genesis 11 — Babel and the line to Abram

1
The whole land was of one lip and one set of words.
And the whole earth was of one language and of one speech.
וַֽיְהִ֥י כָל־הָאָ֖רֶץ שָׂפָ֣ה אֶחָ֑ת וּדְבָרִ֖ים אֲחָדִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The opening claim is doubled. Safa achat — one lip — and devarim achadim — one set of words. The two phrases name distinct dimensions of linguistic unity. Safa — שָׂפָה — is literally the speech-organ ('lip'); the metonymy for language is well-attested in Hebrew (cf. Isa 19:18 'the lip of Canaan' = the Canaanite language). Devarim — דְּבָרִים — is words / matters / things. The doubled claim is: the whole land had not only a shared phonology and grammar (one lip) but a shared semantic stock (one set of words / matters). // The verse stands in tension with 10:5, 10:20, 10:31, which described the Japhethites, Hamites, and Shemites as already differentiated by tongues (lashon). The chapter is opening a parallel timeline — or the redactor has preserved two distinct traditions about linguistic origins, with the Table of Nations chapter and the Babel chapter belonging to different source-strands. The standard scholarly attribution: chapter 10 is the Priestly (P) ethnography; chapter 11:1–9 is the Yahwist (J) etiology. The redactor placed them side-by-side and left the seam visible.

2
As they journeyed from the east, they found a valley in the land of Shinar, and they settled there.
And it came to pass, as they journeyed east, that they found a plain in the land of Shinar; and they dwelt there.
וַֽיְהִ֖י בְּנָסְעָ֣ם מִקֶּ֑דֶם וַֽיִּמְצְא֥וּ בִקְעָ֛ה בְּאֶ֥רֶץ שִׁנְעָ֖ר וַיֵּ֥שְׁבוּ שָֽׁם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

Eastward, or from the east? מִקֶּדֶם miqedem has the same ambiguity here as in 2:8 (where the garden was planted miqedem): it can mean from the east (the people are coming westward from a more easterly origin) or eastward (they are journeying in an eastward direction). The standard reading is from the east — the post-Flood humans, descending from Noah's ark on the mountains of Ararat (8:4) in the Armenian highlands, are migrating south and west into the Mesopotamian alluvium. // Biqah b'eretz Shinar — a valley in the land of Shinar. The same Shinar named in 10:10 (the location of Nimrod's Babel, Erech, Akkad, Calneh). The chapter is geographically continuous with chapter 10's Nimrod parenthesis: the same southern-Mesopotamian alluvial plain, the same proto-civilization context. Biqah — valley, plain — names the flat alluvium itself; the contrast with Levantine hill-country is implicit.

3
They said each to his neighbor, "Come, let us make bricks, and burn them in fire." The brick was for them as stone, and the bitumen was for them as mortar.
And they said one to another, Come, let us make brick, and burn them thoroughly. And they had brick for stone, and slime had they for mortar.
וַיֹּאמְר֞וּ אִ֣ישׁ אֶל־רֵעֵ֗הוּ הָ֚בָה נִלְבְּנָ֣ה לְבֵנִ֔ים וְנִשְׂרְפָ֖ה לִשְׂרֵפָ֑ה וַתְּהִ֨י לָהֶ֤ם הַלְּבֵנָה֙ לְאָ֔בֶן וְהַ֣חֵמָ֔ר הָיָ֥ה לָהֶ֖ם לַחֹֽמֶר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

The first human cohortative. Havah nilbenah — come, let us make bricks. Havah (an interjection, 'come!') plus the first-person plural cohortative nilbenah. The verb is built on the same noun l'venah (brick); the wordplay is nilbenah l'venim — let us brick bricks. The doubled construction repeats in the next clause: nisrefah lisrefah — let us burn (them) with a burning. // The chapter is opening on a HUMAN cohortative — let us... — that will be echoed structurally three times: here (v3), in the city-and-tower decision (v4 havah nivneh), and then mirrored from the divine side (v7 havah neredah). The cohortative becomes the chapter's rhetorical signature, and the chapter ends by showing the divine cohortative answering the human ones. // Ha-l'venah l'aven v'ha-chemar laya lahem la-chomer — the brick was for them as stone, and the bitumen as mortar. The aside is technological-archaeological: it notes that the southern Mesopotamian builders, lacking stone (the alluvium has none), substituted kiln-fired clay brick with bitumen mortar. This is empirically accurate to the construction methods of southern Mesopotamian ziggurats, attested archaeologically and in Mesopotamian texts. The chapter is technically literate about the construction methods of the world it describes.

4
They said, "Come, let us build for ourselves a city, and a tower with its top in the skies; and let us make for ourselves a name, lest we be scattered upon the face of the whole land."
And they said, Come, let us build us a city, and a tower, whose top [may reach] unto heaven, and let us make us a name; lest we be scattered abroad upon the face of the whole earth.
וַיֹּאמְר֞וּ הָ֣בָה ׀ נִבְנֶה־לָּ֣נוּ עִ֗יר וּמִגְדָּל֙ וְרֹאשׁ֣וֹ בַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וְנַֽעֲשֶׂה־לָּ֖נוּ שֵׁ֑ם פֶּן־נָפ֖וּץ עַל־פְּנֵ֥י כָל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

The chapter's keystone verse — three intentions stated in one breath. // First, havah nivneh lanu ir umigdal — come, let us build for ourselves a city and a tower. The pair ir (city) and migdal (tower) names the political-architectural project. Migdal is the ziggurat — the stepped temple-platform of Mesopotamian religious architecture, exemplified by the great ziggurat of Etemenanki at Babylon (the Esagila complex), the most famous of which was rebuilt by Nebuchadnezzar II in the 6th c. BCE. The chapter is reading the ziggurat-tradition, not inventing it. // Second, v'rosho bashamayim — with its top in the skies. Rosho (its head/top) reaching bashamayim (in the skies). The phrase is not necessarily a literal claim that the tower will physically reach the heavens; rosho bashamayim is a stock-phrase for very tall (cf. Deut 1:28, 9:1, of fortified cities 'whose walls reach up to the skies'). But in this chapter the phrase carries more than hyperbole: the ziggurat's function was to be a stairway between earth and the divine realm — the Mesopotamian theology of the ziggurat is that the deity descended onto the platform-shrine at its summit, and that human worship ascended through it. Rosho bashamayim is naming the ziggurat's aspiration, not just its dimension. // Third, v'na'aseh lanu shem — and let us make for ourselves a name. This is the chapter's most lens-charged phrase. The cohortative naase — let us make — is the same form as Genesis 1:26's naase adam — let us make humankind. There it was the Elohim speaking; here it is humans. The chapter is staging humans assuming the divine cohortative register — the naase adam of creation is mirrored by the naase lanu shem of the post-Flood project. The verbal parallel is exact, and the chapter is silent about whether to read this as imitation, presumption, or aspiration. // Shem — name — in Hebrew Bible idiom is not just an identifying label but renown, reputation, lasting memory (cf. 2 Sam 7:9, of YHWH making David's shem great). The phrase anshei ha-shem — men of name — was applied in 6:4 to the antediluvian gibborim; now in 11:4 the post-Flood humans propose to make for themselves a name. The chapter is connecting the human ambition at Babel to the gibborim-class register the Flood was supposed to have ended. // Pen-naputz al-pnei kol ha-aretz — lest we be scattered upon the face of the whole land. The motive is given as fear of dispersion. The verb putz (to scatter, disperse) will return in v8 (vayyafetz YHWH otam) — what they feared is what happens, brought about by the very God they were trying to overshoot.

5
YHWH came down to see the city and the tower which the sons of the human had built.
And Jehovah came down to see the city and the tower, which the children of men builded.
וַיֵּ֣רֶד יְהוָ֔ה לִרְאֹ֥ת אֶת־הָעִ֖יר וְאֶת־הַמִּגְדָּ֑ל אֲשֶׁ֥ר בָּנ֖וּ בְּנֵ֥י הָאָדָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

Vayered YHWH — YHWH came down. The verb is יָרַד yarad — descend — used here with YHWH as subject. The anthropomorphic register is plain: YHWH is positioned above, the city-and-tower is below, and YHWH descends to see it. The descent is the inverse of the tower's ascent: the humans build upward (with its top in the skies); YHWH comes down to inspect. The verbal pairing is the chapter's structural irony — the tower designed to reach the divine realm prompts the divine being to descend, with results the humans did not intend. // The descent-language will recur in v7 (havah neredah — let us go down) when YHWH speaks in the divine cohortative. The chapter has two descents: v5 (singular YHWH yarad — sees) and v7 (plural neredah — acts). // Bnei ha-adam — the sons of the human / sons of humankind. The phrase emphasizes the lineage: these are descendants of the adam of Genesis 2–3. The chapter is connecting Babel-builders to the Edenic origin — they are doing what humans do, building what humans build, asserting what humans assert.

6
YHWH said, "Behold — one people and one tongue for all of them! And this is what they begin to do — and now, nothing will be withheld from them which they have schemed to do."
And Jehovah said, Behold, they are one people, and they have all one language; and this is what they begin to do: and now nothing will be withholden from them, which they purpose to do.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר יְהוָ֗ה הֵ֣ן עַ֤ם אֶחָד֙ וְשָׂפָ֤ה אַחַת֙ לְכֻלָּ֔ם וְזֶ֖ה הַחִלָּ֣ם לַעֲשׂ֑וֹת וְעַתָּה֙ לֹֽא־יִבָּצֵ֣ר מֵהֶ֔ם כֹּ֛ל אֲשֶׁ֥ר יָזְמ֖וּ לַֽעֲשֽׂוֹת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

The chapter's load-bearing claim. YHWH's assessment has three components. // First, am echad v'safa achat l'kulam — one people and one tongue for all of them. The unity is named twice — one people, one tongue. The verse is acknowledging that unified human coordination is what enables what follows. // Second, zeh hachilam la'asot — this is what they begin to do. The verb הֵחֵל hechel — began — is the same verb that opened the Nimrod parenthesis (hu hechel lihyot gibbor, 10:8). The chapter is reading the Babel project as the beginning of unified human technological-political ambition, not the end of it. // Third, the keystone clause: v'atah lo yibatzer mehem kol asher yazmu la'asot — and now, nothing will be withheld from them which they have schemed to do. The verb בָּצַר batsar — to restrain, hold back, make inaccessible — appears in the passive niphal (yibatzer): nothing will be made inaccessible to them. The clause is YHWH's plain claim that human coordination at this scale produces unlimited capacity. The verb yazmu (from zamam, 'devise, scheme, plan') is neutral-to-negative in its semantic range — it covers planning generally but is often used of premeditation in legal contexts. // The Wheel of Heaven reading hears this verse with unusual clarity. The divine assessment is not that what the humans are doing is metaphysically wrong (no curse-language is used, no judicial register); it is that what they are doing is technologically open-ended. The response in v7 will be a deliberate intervention to break the coordination — not a punishment but a cap on capacity. The chapter is staging a strategic divine response to human power-aggregation. The lens reads this against the canon's framing of the Elohim as a senior civilization that has reasons to manage human technological progress.

7
"Come, let us go down and confuse their tongue there, so that they may not understand each other's tongue."
Come, let us go down, and there confound their language, that they may not understand one another`s speech.
הָ֚בָה נֵֽרְדָ֔ה וְנָבְלָ֥ה שָׁ֖ם שְׂפָתָ֑ם אֲשֶׁר֙ לֹ֣א יִשְׁמְע֔וּ אִ֖ישׁ שְׂפַ֥ת רֵעֵֽהוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The third explicit divine cohortative in Genesis. Havah neredah v'navlah — come, let us go down, and let us confuse. // The first was 1:26 — naase adam b'tsalmenu kidmutenu (let us make humankind in our image, in our likeness) — at the creation of humankind. The second was 3:22 — hen ha-adam haya k'achad mimennu lada'at tov vara (behold, the human has become like one of us, knowing good and bad) — after the Tree-eating. The third is 11:7. All three are first-person plural speech with divine subject. // The pattern across the three: at 1:26 the divine plural makes humankind; at 3:22 the divine plural recognizes that humankind has become like the divine plural in a specific capacity; at 11:7 the divine plural intervenes to cap what humankind can do together. The Genesis primaeval-history arc, on the chapter's textual surface, is the story of a divine-plural class managing the emergence of a human-collective whose capacity is approaching theirs. The chapter does not editorialize on this reading; it preserves the grammatical plural and lets the reader draw the structural pattern. // The cohortative havah neredah — come, let us go down — is the mirror of the humans' havah nilbenah (v3) and havah nivneh (v4). The chapter has stacked four cohortatives in nine verses: two human (let us brick / let us build), two divine (let us go down / let us confuse). The verbal symmetry is the chapter's most precise structural feature. The humans' cohortative-projects produce a divine cohortative-response. The chapter is composed with a careful sense of rhetorical match. // V'navlah sham sefatam — let us confuse their tongue there. The verb בָּלַל balal — mix, confound, confuse — is the etymological root of Babel (v9). The intervention is linguistic: not destroying the city, not killing the builders, but breaking the coordination capability by fragmenting the shared language. The response is targeted: it removes the specific affordance that made the project possible.

8
YHWH scattered them from there upon the face of the whole land, and they ceased to build the city.
So Jehovah scattered them abroad from thence upon the face of all the earth: and they left off building the city.
וַיָּ֨פֶץ יְהוָ֥ה אֹתָ֛ם מִשָּׁ֖ם עַל־פְּנֵ֣י כָל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַֽיַּחְדְּל֖וּ לִבְנֹ֥ת הָעִֽיר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

Their fear is fulfilled by the divine action. The humans had said in v4: pen-naputz al-pnei kol ha-aretz — lest we be scattered upon the face of the whole land. The verse now reports: vayyafetz YHWH otam ... al-pnei kol ha-aretz — YHWH scattered them ... upon the face of the whole land. The exact phrase the humans had used in their fear-clause is now used in the action-clause, with YHWH as the subject of the verb the humans had used in the passive. // The verb is the same root (פוץ putz); the humans feared the dispersal that the divine action then produced. The chapter is reading the Babel project as motivated by the fear of dispersion, and the divine response as enacting exactly what the project was designed to prevent. // Vayyachdelu livnot ha-ir — and they ceased to build the city. The verb chadal (cease) is the gentlest possible language for project-stop: not destroyed, not punished, not killed. The construction simply halts when the linguistic coordination fails. The chapter is technically accurate about the social mechanism: large-scale construction projects collapse when communication breaks down. The divine intervention is engineering-grade: minimal, targeted, sufficient.

9
Therefore its name was called Babel, because there YHWH confounded the tongue of the whole land; and from there YHWH scattered them upon the face of the whole land.
Therefore was the name of it called Babel; because Jehovah did there confound the language of all the earth: and from thence did Jehovah scatter them abroad upon the face of all the earth.
עַל־כֵּ֞ן קָרָ֤א שְׁמָהּ֙ בָּבֶ֔ל כִּי־שָׁ֛ם בָּלַ֥ל יְהוָ֖ה שְׂפַ֣ת כָּל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וּמִשָּׁם֙ הֱפִיצָ֣ם יְהוָ֔ה עַל־פְּנֵ֖י כָּל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The etymology and the closing summary. Babel — בָּבֶל — and balal — בָּלַל — share a triconsonantal root structure (b-b-l / b-l-l) close enough for the chapter to assert an etymological pun. The actual etymology is contested: the Hebrew Babel most likely derives from the Akkadian Bab-ilim ('gate of the gods') — the same name that gives English Babylon. The chapter is offering a folk etymology that recodes the city's name from gate of the gods (the Mesopotamian self-naming) into the place of confusion (the Hebrew Bible's reframing). The pun is theological re-naming: the Mesopotamian city of divine access is recast as the city of divine refusal of access. // The chapter closes the Babel narrative and pivots to the Shem-genealogy that will land at Abraham. The reader is to keep two things in view: (1) the divine assessment that human coordination at scale was approaching divine capacity, and (2) the divine response of fragmentation. The line that will run from v10 down to v26 — Shem to Abram — is the new divine project after the cap was imposed. The chapter's structural logic: humanity en masse could not be permitted to continue at Babel; the next chapter will start the covenant-line of Abraham. The two are sequential narrative moves.

10
These are the begettings of Shem. Shem was one hundred years old when he fathered Arpachshad, two years after the deluge.
These are the generations of Shem. Shem was a hundred years old, and begat Arpachshad two years after the flood.
אֵ֚לֶּה תּוֹלְדֹ֣ת שֵׁ֔ם שֵׁ֚ם בֶּן־מְאַ֣ת שָׁנָ֔ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־אַרְפַּכְשָׁ֑ד שְׁנָתַ֖יִם אַחַ֥ר הַמַּבּֽוּל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

The toledot of Shem opens. This is the fifth toledot in Genesis (after 2:4 of the skies, 5:1 of Adam, 6:9 of Noah, 10:1 of Noah's sons). The genealogy that follows is the structural counterpart to chapter 5: ten generations, each with the formula X lived Y years, fathered Z; and X lived W years after fathering Z, and fathered sons and daughters. The pattern is identical to chapter 5; what differs is the length of lives — the post-Flood lifespans drop steadily across the ten generations from ~600 (Shem) down to ~205 (Terah, v32). // Shenatayim achar ha-mabbul — two years after the deluge. The dating-by-Flood-event continues the chronological grid of chapter 5–9; it also closes the chronological loop: the Flood is the anchor-event, with all subsequent dates measured from it.

11
Shem lived after he fathered Arpachshad five hundred years, and fathered sons and daughters.
and Shem lived after he begat Arpachshad five hundred years, and begat sons and daughters.
וַֽיְחִי־שֵׁ֗ם אַֽחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־אַרְפַּכְשָׁ֔ד חֲמֵ֥שׁ מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃ (ס)
12
Arpachshad lived thirty-five years and fathered Shelach.
And Arpachshad lived five and thirty years, and begat Shelah.
וְאַרְפַּכְשַׁ֣ד חַ֔י חָמֵ֥שׁ וּשְׁלֹשִׁ֖ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־שָֽׁלַח׃
13
Arpachshad lived after he fathered Shelach four hundred and three years, and fathered sons and daughters.
and Arpachshad lived after he begat Shelah four hundred and three years, and begat sons and daughters.
וַֽיְחִ֣י אַרְפַּכְשַׁ֗ד אַֽחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־שֶׁ֔לַח שָׁלֹ֣שׁ שָׁנִ֔ים וְאַרְבַּ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃ (ס)
14
Shelach lived thirty years and fathered Eber.
And Shelah lived thirty years, and begat Eber:
וְשֶׁ֥לַח חַ֖י שְׁלֹשִׁ֣ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־עֵֽבֶר׃
15
Shelach lived after he fathered Eber four hundred and three years, and fathered sons and daughters.
and Shelah lived after he begat Eber four hundred and three years, and begat sons and daughters.
וַֽיְחִי־שֶׁ֗לַח אַחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־עֵ֔בֶר שָׁלֹ֣שׁ שָׁנִ֔ים וְאַרְבַּ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃ (ס)
16
Eber lived thirty-four years and fathered Peleg.
And Eber lived four and thirty years, and begat Peleg:
וַֽיְחִי־עֵ֕בֶר אַרְבַּ֥ע וּשְׁלֹשִׁ֖ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־פָּֽלֶג׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

Eber, the eponymous Hebrew ancestor. עֵבֶר Eber is the etymological source most commonly proposed for עִבְרִי Ivri (Hebrew), the gentilic that Abraham will be called in 14:13. The chapter places Eber in the central position of the Shem-genealogy: four generations after Shem, with Abraham coming four generations after Eber. The genealogy is structured to make Eber the symbolic midpoint between the post-Flood patriarch and the covenant-founder.

17
Eber lived after he fathered Peleg four hundred and thirty years, and fathered sons and daughters.
and Eber lived after he begat Peleg four hundred and thirty years, and begat sons and daughters.
וַֽיְחִי־עֵ֗בֶר אַחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־פֶּ֔לֶג שְׁלֹשִׁ֣ים שָׁנָ֔ה וְאַרְבַּ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃ (ס)
18
Peleg lived thirty years and fathered Reu.
And Peleg lived thirty years, and begat Reu:
וַֽיְחִי־פֶ֖לֶג שְׁלֹשִׁ֣ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־רְעֽוּ׃
19
Peleg lived after he fathered Reu two hundred and nine years, and fathered sons and daughters.
and Peleg lived after he begat Reu two hundred and nine years, and begat sons and daughters.
וַֽיְחִי־פֶ֗לֶג אַחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־רְע֔וּ תֵּ֥שַׁע שָׁנִ֖ים וּמָאתַ֣יִם שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 19

The lifespans drop. Peleg's total lifespan (239 years) is less than half of his great-grandfather Shem's (600 years). The compression of post-Flood lives — already attested in chapter 5 with Adam's 930 and Methuselah's 969 — is accelerating in chapter 11. By Terah (v32, 205 years), the antediluvian-scale lifespans are gone. The chapter does not comment on the compression. The patriarchal narratives that follow (Abraham 175, Isaac 180, Jacob 147) will continue the decline.

20
Reu lived thirty-two years and fathered Serug.
And Reu lived two and thirty years, and begat Serug:
וַיְחִ֣י רְע֔וּ שְׁתַּ֥יִם וּשְׁלֹשִׁ֖ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־שְׂרֽוּג׃
21
Reu lived after he fathered Serug two hundred and seven years, and fathered sons and daughters.
and Reu lived after he begat Serug two hundred and seven years, and begat sons and daughters.
וַיְחִ֣י רְע֗וּ אַחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־שְׂר֔וּג שֶׁ֥בַע שָׁנִ֖ים וּמָאתַ֣יִם שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃ (ס)
22
Serug lived thirty years and fathered Nachor.
And Serug lived thirty years, and begat Nahor:
וַיְחִ֥י שְׂר֖וּג שְׁלֹשִׁ֣ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־נָחֽוֹר׃
23
Serug lived after he fathered Nachor two hundred years, and fathered sons and daughters.
and Serug lived after he begat Nahor two hundred years, and begat sons and daughters.
וַיְחִ֣י שְׂר֗וּג אַחֲרֵ֛י הוֹלִיד֥וֹ אֶת־נָח֖וֹר מָאתַ֣יִם שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃ (ס)
24
Nachor lived twenty-nine years and fathered Terah.
And Nahor lived nine and twenty years, and begat Terah:
וַיְחִ֣י נָח֔וֹר תֵּ֥שַׁע וְעֶשְׂרִ֖ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־תָּֽרַח׃
25
Nachor lived after he fathered Terah one hundred and nineteen years, and fathered sons and daughters.
and Nahor lived after he begat Terah a hundred and nineteen years, and begat sons and daughters.
וַיְחִ֣י נָח֗וֹר אַחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־תֶּ֔רַח תְּשַֽׁע־עֶשְׂרֵ֥ה שָׁנָ֖ה וּמְאַ֣ת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃ (ס)
26
Terah lived seventy years and fathered Abram, Nachor, and Haran.
And Terah lived seventy years, and begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran.
וַֽיְחִי־תֶ֖רַח שִׁבְעִ֣ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֙וֹלֶד֙ אֶת־אַבְרָ֔ם אֶת־נָח֖וֹר וְאֶת־הָרָֽן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 26

The decade-genealogy lands at the threshold. The ten-generation sequence from Shem to Abram parallels the ten-generation sequence from Adam to Noah in chapter 5. The structural parallel is deliberate: each ten-generation arc anchors a turning point — Noah for the Flood, Abram for the covenant. // Abram enters Genesis here, the first explicit mention. The reader who has been following the toledot-arc since chapter 5 has been waiting for this moment for two chapters. The naming triad — Abram, Nachor, Haran — places Abram first by prominence (not necessarily by birth-order; vayyoled et X et Y v'et Z is the standard naming-by-importance pattern, as with Shem-Ham-Yepheth).

27
These are the begettings of Terah. Terah fathered Abram, Nachor, and Haran; and Haran fathered Lot.
Now these are the generations of Terah. Terah begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran. And Haran begat Lot.
וְאֵ֙לֶּה֙ תּוֹלְדֹ֣ת תֶּ֔רַח תֶּ֚רַח הוֹלִ֣יד אֶת־אַבְרָ֔ם אֶת־נָח֖וֹר וְאֶת־הָרָ֑ן וְהָרָ֖ן הוֹלִ֥יד אֶת־לֽוֹט׃
Translation Commentary Verse 27

The sixth toledot in Genesis opens. The chapter has now stacked three toledot-formulae in two chapters (10:1 Noah's sons; 11:10 Shem; 11:27 Terah), each narrowing the focus: from the post-Flood world to the Shemite line to the household of Terah. The narrowing prepares the move from cosmic-ethnographic scope to family-narrative scope that will run through chapters 12–50.

28
Haran died in the presence of Terah his father, in the land of his birth, in Ur of the Kasdim.
And Haran died before his father Terah in the land of his nativity, in Ur of the Chaldees.
וַיָּ֣מָת הָרָ֔ן עַל־פְּנֵ֖י תֶּ֣רַח אָבִ֑יו בְּאֶ֥רֶץ מוֹלַדְתּ֖וֹ בְּא֥וּר כַּשְׂדִּֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 28

The first recorded child-predeceasing-parent in Genesis. Vayyamat Haran al-pnei Terah aviv — Haran died in the presence of Terah his father. The phrase al-pnei (literally 'upon the face of') in the context of death means during the lifetime of — Haran predeceased his father, the reverse of the patriarchal-norm in which the father dies first. The narrative aside marks the death as out-of-order. // Ur Kasdim — Ur of the Kasdim. Ur was the great Sumerian city of southern Mesopotamia, capital of the Third Dynasty of Ur (ca. 2100 BCE), excavated by Leonard Woolley in the 1920s. The qualifier Kasdim (Chaldeans) is anachronistic: the Chaldeans are a first-millennium-BCE Aramean people who came to dominate Babylonia in the Neo-Babylonian period. Ur Kasdim is therefore a chronologically-confused identifier — the city of Ur but with its later-conquering people's name attached. Modern scholarly debate has proposed alternative identifications (a northern Mesopotamian Ur near Haran, rather than the famous southern Ur) precisely to avoid the Kasdim anachronism. The chapter is naming the family's place of origin in Mesopotamia, with vocabulary that mixes geographic eras. The Wheel of Heaven lens reads Abram's family as Mesopotamian in origin — the same alluvial plain that hosted Nimrod's Babel — preparing the reader for chapter 12's move out of that civilization.

29
Abram and Nachor took for themselves wives. The name of Abram's wife was Sarai, and the name of Nachor's wife was Milkah, daughter of Haran, the father of Milkah and the father of Yiskah.
And Abram and Nahor took them wives: The name of Abram`s wife was Sarai; and the name of Nahor`s wife, Milcah, the daughter of Haran, the father of Milcah, and the father of Iscah.
וַיִּקַּ֨ח אַבְרָ֧ם וְנָח֛וֹר לָהֶ֖ם נָשִׁ֑ים שֵׁ֤ם אֵֽשֶׁת־אַבְרָם֙ שָׂרָ֔י וְשֵׁ֤ם אֵֽשֶׁת־נָחוֹר֙ מִלְכָּ֔ה בַּת־הָרָ֥ן אֲבִֽי־מִלְכָּ֖ה וַֽאֲבִ֥י יִסְכָּֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 29

The brides are named — and one is identified. Sarai's lineage is not given; Milkah's is (she is Haran's daughter, niece to her husband Nachor — an intra-family marriage consistent with patriarchal-period norms). Yiskah is named only here in the Hebrew Bible; rabbinic tradition identifies her with Sarai (no textual evidence), or as a separate sister. The brevity is one of the chapter's textual cruces — the chapter leaves the relationship unresolved. // The naming of the wives anticipates Genesis 12+: Sarai will be central to the Abraham narrative; Milkah will be referenced again only as Rebekah's grandmother (24:15). The chapter is setting up the patriarchal family network through which the covenant will be transmitted.

30
Sarai was barren — she had no child.
And Sarai was barren; She had no child.
וַתְּהִ֥י שָׂרַ֖י עֲקָרָ֑ה אֵ֥ין לָ֖הּ וָלָֽד׃
Translation Commentary Verse 30

The chapter's quiet pivot. Six words in Hebrew set up the entire Abraham-Sarah cycle that will run through chapters 12–25. Aqarah — barren — names the condition that will be the load-bearing constraint of the next ten chapters: the covenant-line passes through a couple unable to produce children. The whole drama of the Abraham narrative — the divine promise of offspring, the years of waiting, the Hagar and Ishmael episode, the laughter of Sarah, the late birth of Isaac — turns on this single verse. // The redundant ein lah valad — she had no child — drives the point home: barrenness is the asserted fact, not just delayed conception. The chapter is foregrounding the obstacle the next narrative will overcome.

31
Terah took Abram his son, Lot the son of Haran his grandson, and Sarai his daughter-in-law, the wife of his son Abram; and they went out with them from Ur of the Kasdim to go to the land of Canaan, and they came to Haran and settled there.
And Terah took Abram his son, and Lot the son of Haran, his son`s son, and Sarai his daughter-in-law, his son Abram`s wife; and they went forth with them from Ur of the Chaldees, to go into the land of Canaan; and they came unto Haran, and dwelt there.
וַיִּקַּ֨ח תֶּ֜רַח אֶת־אַבְרָ֣ם בְּנ֗וֹ וְאֶת־ל֤וֹט בֶּן־הָרָן֙ בֶּן־בְּנ֔וֹ וְאֵת֙ שָׂרַ֣י כַּלָּת֔וֹ אֵ֖שֶׁת אַבְרָ֣ם בְּנ֑וֹ וַיֵּצְא֨וּ אִתָּ֜ם מֵא֣וּר כַּשְׂדִּ֗ים לָלֶ֙כֶת֙ אַ֣רְצָה כְּנַ֔עַן וַיָּבֹ֥אוּ עַד־חָרָ֖ן וַיֵּ֥שְׁבוּ שָֽׁם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 31

The unfinished journey. Terah, not Abram, is the verse's grammatical subject — vayyiqach Terah (Terah took). The family's exit from Ur is at Terah's initiative; Abram is the passenger of his father's migration. The destination is artzah Kena'an — toward the land of Canaan — the same Canaan whose boundaries chapter 10:19 sketched in anticipation. // But they do not arrive. Vayavo'u ad-Charan vayyeshvu sham — they came as far as Haran and settled there. Haran (חָרָן) is a major northern Mesopotamian city (the site near modern Harran in southeastern Turkey), an important Bronze and Iron Age trading center on the Aramaean / Mesopotamian frontier. The family stops short of Canaan; Terah will die in Haran (v32). The migration toward Canaan is interrupted. // Chapter 12 will open with YHWH's call to Abram to go from your land, from your kindred, from your father's house, to the land that I will show you (12:1) — and Abram will complete the migration his father had begun and abandoned. The Wheel of Heaven lens reads the seam: the journey toward Canaan begins under Terah's initiative without divine prompting, stalls at Haran, and resumes under explicit divine call. The covenant-line is positioned as completing what the family-line could not.

32
The days of Terah were two hundred and five years, and Terah died in Haran.
And the days of Terah were two hundred and five years: and Terah died in Haran.
וַיִּהְי֣וּ יְמֵי־תֶ֔רַח חָמֵ֥שׁ שָׁנִ֖ים וּמָאתַ֣יִם שָׁנָ֑ה וַיָּ֥מָת תֶּ֖רַח בְּחָרָֽן׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 32

The primaeval-history arc closes. Terah's 205-year lifespan is the chapter's closing data-point; his death in Haran is the chapter's closing event. The primaeval-history block of Genesis 1–11 ends here — at a death, in a transitional city, with a stalled migration. The next chapter opens with the divine call that will turn the family's incomplete journey into the foundational journey of the patriarchal narrative. // The transition from primaeval history to patriarchal narrative is structurally clean: Genesis 1–11 has moved from cosmos (ch 1) to humanity (chs 2–3) to family (chs 4–5) to the cosmic reset (chs 6–9) to the ethnographic catalogue (ch 10) to the Babel cap (ch 11:1–9) to the genealogy (11:10–26) to the household-narrative threshold (11:27–32). Each successive section narrows the scope. Chapter 12 will narrow further: from Terah's whole household to a single called man — Abram — and a single destination — the land YHWH will show him.

Chapter 12

Genesis 12 — The call of Abram and the descent to Mitsrayim

1
YHWH said to Abram, "Go forth from your land, from your kindred, and from your father's house, to the land that I will show you.
Now Jehovah said unto Abram, Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred, and from thy father`s house, unto the land that I will show thee:
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר יְהוָה֙ אֶל־אַבְרָ֔ם לֶךְ־לְךָ֛ מֵאַרְצְךָ֥ וּמִמּֽוֹלַדְתְּךָ֖ וּמִבֵּ֣ית אָבִ֑יךָ אֶל־הָאָ֖רֶץ אֲשֶׁ֥ר אַרְאֶֽךָּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The chapter opens the patriarchal narratives with a single verb whose grammar resists single-English rendering. Lekh-lekha — לֶךְ־לְךָ — is the qal imperative of halakh ('go') followed by a lamed-prefixed second-person pronoun. The construction is the so-called ethical dative (or dative of advantage / dative of interest): the lamed + pronoun adds a reflexive overtone — go for yourself, go on your own account, or in the most expansive scholarly reading, go to your-self (i.e., into your own becoming). // Three readings circulate. (a) The classical philological position treats the lamed as redundant intensification — Hebrew imperative idiom occasionally takes a dative pronoun without independent semantic load (cf. qum-lakh, shuv-lakh). On this reading the standard ASV 'get thee out' captures the sense. (b) Rashi, drawing on rabbinic tradition (Genesis Rabbah 39:9), reads the lamed as l'ha'natah'kha v'l'tovat'kha — for your benefit and for your good — the journey is divinely-prescribed Abrahamic enrichment. (c) The modern critical consensus (Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary, 1989; Alter, The Five Books of Moses, 2004; Hamilton, NICOT, 1990) reads the lamed as a genuine ethical dative carrying a reflexive-advantageous nuance that resists smoothing — the journey is for yourself, the new identity is toward yourself. // The phrase recurs only once more in the Hebrew Bible: Gen 22:2, the aqedah, where YHWH commands Abraham lekh-lekha el eretz ha-Moriah — go forth to the land of Moriah — to offer Isaac. The doubled use forms a deliberate inclusio across the Abraham cycle: the call that opens it (12:1, leave father's house) and the call that closes it (22:2, offer the son who is the future) are marked with the same idiom. Sarna and Alter both emphasize the rhetorical bracket. // The WoH translation reads 'Go forth' — intensified over bare 'go', neutral on the ethical-dative philological question, leaving the inclusio with 22:2 to be marked at that verse. The reflexive-advantageous resonance is left for commentary.

2
I will make of you a great nation, and I will bless you, and I will make your name great; and you shall be a blessing.
and I will make of thee a great nation, and I will bless thee, and make they name great; and be thou a blessing;
וְאֶֽעֶשְׂךָ֙ לְג֣וֹי גָּד֔וֹל וַאֲבָ֣רֶכְךָ֔ וַאֲגַדְּלָ֖ה שְׁמֶ֑ךָ וֶהְיֵ֖ה בְּרָכָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The promise has four clauses in escalating scope. I will make of you a great nation — agadellekha l'goy gadol — promises numeric and political mass; I will bless you — avarekhekha — promises personal divine favor; I will make your name great — agadelah shemekha — promises lasting reputation; and you shall be a blessing — vehyeh berakhah — turns Abram from recipient to source. The final clause is the structural pivot: the blessing flowing toward Abram in the first three clauses now flows through him in the fourth, setting up the universal-scope promise of v3. // The phrase agadelah shemekha — I will make your name great — directly answers and reverses the Babel-builders' naase lanu shem of 11:4 (let us make for ourselves a name). What humanity tried to seize collectively at Babel through architecture and coordination, YHWH now grants to one individual through covenantal call. The chapter is deliberately staging the contrast: the shem-ambition that ended in dispersion (11:8) reappears as the shem-promise that opens the covenant-line.

3
I will bless those who bless you, and the one who curses you I will curse; and in you all the families of the ground shall be blessed.
and I will bless them that bless thee, and him that curseth thee will I curse: and in thee shall all the families of the earth be blessed.
וַאֲבָֽרֲכָה֙ מְבָ֣רְכֶ֔יךָ וּמְקַלֶּלְךָ֖ אָאֹ֑ר וְנִבְרְכ֣וּ בְךָ֔ כֹּ֖ל מִשְׁפְּחֹ֥ת הָאֲדָמָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

The chapter's load-bearing verse — the universal-scope promise that makes Genesis 12 the literary opening of the covenant arc. Two philological questions sit at its center. // First, the curse-verbs. The verse uses two distinct verbs for cursing in immediate parallel: m'qallel'kha a'or — the one cursing you (qillel) I will curse (arar). Qillel (from q-l-l, 'be slight, treat as trifling') names a contemptuous diminishment — to curse in the sense of belittle or speak slightingly of. Arar (a-r-r) names a stronger juridical curse — the cursing that excludes from blessing-flow (the same root used at 3:14, 3:17, 4:11, 9:25). The verse is not redundant: a mild contempt directed at Abram triggers a juridical exclusion from YHWH. Sarna (JPS, 1989) and Alter (2004) both flag the verb asymmetry; the WoH translation preserves it as 'curse' for both, with the lexical detail surfaced here. // Second, and more contested: v'nivrakhu vekha kol mishpechot ha-adamah. The verb nivrakhu is niphal at this verse — passive: shall be blessed. But the parallel formulae at Gen 22:18 and 26:4 are pointed hithpael — middle / reflexive: shall bless themselves. The Masoretic Text itself preserves both forms across the five iterations of the promise: niphal at 12:3, 18:18, 28:14; hithpael at 22:18, 26:4. // Three positions on what the verse means. (a) The traditional Christian reading, going back to the LXX eneulogēthēsontai and confirmed by Paul's citation in Galatians 3:8 ("in you shall all the nations be blessed"), reads the verb as straightforwardly passive across all five occurrences. The MT pointing at 12:3 (niphal) supports this reading at this verse. (b) The modern critical consensus (Speiser, Anchor Bible Genesis, 1964; Sarna, JPS, 1989; Westermann, Genesis 12–36, Hermeneia, 1985; NJPS) reads the original idiom as middle/reflexive across all five verses: by you all the families of the ground shall bless themselves — i.e., they shall invoke Abram's name as a paradigm of blessing (cf. Gen 48:20, by you shall Israel bless, saying, 'Elohim make you as Ephraim and Manasseh'). On this reading the niphal pointings at 12:3 / 18:18 / 28:14 are Masoretic harmonization toward the theologically richer passive sense, against an original consistent hithpael. (c) Wenham (WBC, 1994) and Hamilton (NICOT, 1990) split the difference, suggesting the niphal/hithpael distinction was meaningful for the redactor and should be preserved verse-by-verse — passive here, middle at 22:18. // The Pauline reception is decisive for the Christian translation tradition. Galatians 3:8 quotes the verse as eneulogēthēsontai en soi panta ta ethnē — in you shall all the nations be blessed — and reads the passive as the textual foundation of the gospel-to-the-gentiles. ASV, RSV, NRSV, and most English translations in the Christian tradition follow Paul. NJPS, the JPS Tanakh, and Alter follow the modern critical reading and render middle. // The WoH translation reads passive at this verse, matching the MT niphal pointing here. The choice is local: the passive matches the pointed text at 12:3 specifically, and the middle/reflexive will be the rendering at 22:18 and 26:4 where the MT itself points hithpael. The verse-by-verse approach preserves the MT's own variation rather than smoothing toward either pole. Commentary at 22:18 will mark the seam.

4
Abram went as YHWH had spoken to him, and Lot went with him; and Abram was seventy-five years old when he went out from Haran.
So Abram went, as Jehovah had spoken unto him; and Lot went with him: and Abram was seventy and five years old when he departed out of Haran.
וַיֵּ֣לֶךְ אַבְרָ֗ם כַּאֲשֶׁ֨ר דִּבֶּ֤ר אֵלָיו֙ יְהוָ֔ה וַיֵּ֥לֶךְ אִתּ֖וֹ ל֑וֹט וְאַבְרָ֗ם בֶּן־חָמֵ֤שׁ שָׁנִים֙ וְשִׁבְעִ֣ים שָׁנָ֔ה בְּצֵאת֖וֹ מֵחָרָֽן׃
5
Abram took Sarai his wife, and Lot his brother's son, and all the substance that they had gathered, and the souls that they had made in Haran; and they went out to go to the land of Canaan, and to the land of Canaan they came.
And Abram took Sarai his wife, and Lot his brother`s son, and all their substance that they had gathered, and the souls that they had gotten in Haran; and they went forth to go into the land of Canaan; and into the land of Canaan they came.
וַיִּקַּ֣ח אַבְרָם֩ אֶת־שָׂרַ֨י אִשְׁתּ֜וֹ וְאֶת־ל֣וֹט בֶּן־אָחִ֗יו וְאֶת־כָּל־רְכוּשָׁם֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר רָכָ֔שׁוּ וְאֶת־הַנֶּ֖פֶשׁ אֲשֶׁר־עָשׂ֣וּ בְחָרָ֑ן וַיֵּצְא֗וּ לָלֶ֙כֶת֙ אַ֣רְצָה כְּנַ֔עַן וַיָּבֹ֖אוּ אַ֥רְצָה כְּנָֽעַן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

The unusual phrase ha-nefesh asher asu b-Charan — the souls that they had made in Haran — has no exact idiomatic parallel in the Hebrew Bible. Asah (make / do) does not standardly take nefesh (soul / person) as object. The verse forces a decision and three readings circulate. // (a) The proselyte reading — Rashi (following Sifrei Deuteronomy 32 and Onkelos's targum de'sha'abidu l'oraita, 'those whom they made subject to the Torah') reads asu as converted, made into adherents. On this reading Abram and Sarai conducted a missionary enterprise in Haran and brought converts with them. The reading has deep traditional roots but no philological support outside the rabbinic-targumic tradition. // (b) The slaves-acquired reading — Speiser (Anchor Bible Genesis, 1964), Sarna (JPS, 1989), and Westermann (Genesis 12–36, 1985) read asu as a variant of qanah (acquire), parallel to the rekhush asher rakhashu (substance that they had gathered) of the immediately preceding clause. The nefesh here are household slaves and dependents acquired by purchase in Haran. The parallelism with rekhush rakhashu is structurally tight. // (c) The dependents / household reading — a softer version of (b): the nefesh are the broader household — slaves, dependents, attached persons — without specifying the mode of acquisition. NJPS renders 'the persons that they had acquired in Haran'; NRSV 'the persons whom they had acquired in Haran'. // The literal Hebrew asu (made) is unusual and the WoH translation preserves the oddness as 'the souls that they had made' — matching ASV's 'gotten' in keeping the literal verb, leaving the proselyte/slaves question for the reader. The chapter's later content (vv 16, 20 — male and female slaves listed among Abram's holdings) suggests the household-economy reading is the one the chapter assumes, but the verse-form itself declines to disambiguate.

6
Abram passed through the land to the place of Shechem, to the oak of Moreh. And the Canaanite was then in the land.
And Abram passed through the land unto the place of Shechem, unto the oak of Moreh. And the Canaanite was then in the land.
וַיַּעֲבֹ֤ר אַבְרָם֙ בָּאָ֔רֶץ עַ֚ד מְק֣וֹם שְׁכֶ֔ם עַ֖ד אֵל֣וֹן מוֹרֶ֑ה וְהַֽכְּנַעֲנִ֖י אָ֥ז בָּאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

Abram's first stop in the promised land is a sacred tree at a name-bearing oracle site. Elon Moreh — אֵלוֹן מוֹרֶה — is two lexically loaded words. // First, elon. The botanical identification is contested in Hebrew lexicography. Elon (אֵלוֹן), elah (אֵלָה), and alon (אַלּוֹן) are three related forms that can refer to oak, terebinth, or large-tree-of-sacred-significance generally. BDB groups all three under 'oak / terebinth'; HALOT distinguishes alon (oak) from elah/elon (terebinth) with less than full confidence. The LXX renders δρῦς (oak) here. Modern scholarly translations vary: ASV 'oak', NJPS 'terebinth', NRSV 'oak'. The WoH translation reads 'oak' with ASV — the tree-species precision is not load-bearing for the verse. // Second, Moreh. The proper-name form derives from the root y-r-h, 'to throw, point, teach, give oracle / torah'. Moreh is the hiphil participle: teacher, one who instructs, oracle-giver. The word retains its semantic transparency throughout the Hebrew Bible — moreh appears in Isa 9:14, Isa 30:20, Hab 2:18, Prov 5:13, Job 36:22 as a common noun meaning 'teacher / instructor'. The 'oak of Moreh' is therefore plausibly read as 'oak of the Teacher / oak of the Oracle-Giver' — a sacred-tree site associated with prophetic / divinatory consultation. // The sacred-tree pattern in Genesis and the patriarchal corpus is consistent. Elon Moreh recurs at Deut 11:30 (the blessing-and-curse ceremony on Gerizim and Ebal happens etzel elonei Moreh). Jacob buries the foreign gods under the oak which was at Shechem (Gen 35:4) — the same place. Deborah is buried under the oak below Bethel called Allon Bakhut (Gen 35:8). Joshua sets up a stone under the oak that was by the sanctuary of YHWH at Shechem (Josh 24:26). Judges 6:11 places the angelic call of Gideon under the oak which was in Ophrah. Judges 9:6, 37 names elon mutzav and elon m'on'nim (the oak of the diviners) at Shechem. // The pattern is unmistakable: trees at Shechem and elsewhere function as theophany-and-oracle sites in the patriarchal and pre-monarchic narratives. The verse here is naming the spot where the first patriarchal theophany (v 7) will immediately follow — and a sacred-tree-oracle context is exactly what the next verse will deliver. // V'ha-Kena'ani az ba-aretz — and the Canaanite was then in the land — is the chapter's first parenthetical historical aside, marking the temporal seam between the narrative present (Abram's day) and the redactor's later vantage when the Canaanites were no longer 'in the land' as the dominant population. The aside discloses the redactor's perspective without resolving the dating question.

7
YHWH appeared to Abram and said, "To your seed I will give this land." And he built there an altar to YHWH who had appeared to him.
And Jehovah appeared unto Abram, and said, Unto thy seed will I give this land: and there builded he an altar unto Jehovah, who appeared unto him.
וַיֵּרָ֤א יְהוָה֙ אֶל־אַבְרָ֔ם וַיֹּ֕אמֶר לְזַ֨רְעֲךָ֔ אֶתֵּ֖ן אֶת־הָאָ֣רֶץ הַזֹּ֑את וַיִּ֤בֶן שָׁם֙ מִזְבֵּ֔חַ לַיהוָ֖ה הַנִּרְאֶ֥ה אֵלָֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The first theophany to a patriarch — and the nuclear form of the land-promise that will be elaborated across the patriarchal cycle. Two glossary-grade items in one verse. // First, va-yera YHWH el-Avram. The verb is yera, the niphal of r-a-h (see), conjugated as 'was seen / appeared / let himself be seen / made himself visible'. The niphal is mediopassive: it can be read as YHWH being passively seen by Abram (the receptive sense) or as YHWH actively causing himself to be seen (the reflexive-causative sense). The standard English 'appeared' smooths the niphal into a colorless intransitive — appeared in English suggests sudden visibility without specifying its mode. The Hebrew preserves the question of whether the seeing originates with the seer or with the seen. // The theophany-formula va-yera YHWH recurs across the patriarchal narratives: to Abram at 17:1 (the covenant of circumcision), to Abraham at 18:1 (the visit at Mamre — where the appearance is explicitly embodied as three men), to Isaac at 26:2 and 26:24 (Beersheba), to Jacob at 35:9 (return from Paddan-Aram). The formula bookends every major covenantal moment in the Abraham–Isaac–Jacob arc. The chapter is introducing the patriarchal-theophany pattern with this verse; the mode of the seeing is left unspecified here and only becomes explicitly embodied at 18:1. // The chapter does not specify the mode of seeing. The Wheel of Heaven lens here is measured: chapters 1–11 have established a context in which elohim are a plural senior class that interact with humans through embodied descent (the vayered YHWH of 11:5 and 11:7) and through audible speech (4:6, 6:13). The first patriarchal theophany at 12:7 fits the same register — a localized presence that becomes visible, speaks, and gives a promise. The chapter does not editorialize. // Second, l'zarakha eten et-ha-aretz ha-zot. Seven Hebrew words constitute the nuclear form of the land-promise. Zera (seed) — the agricultural-biological term for offspring / posterity / line — will be the chapter's recurring designator for Abram's progeny across the patriarchal arc. The promise-formula will be expanded at 13:14–17 (with quadrant directions and innumerable-as-dust imagery), 15:7–21 (with the cut-covenant ritual and the explicit Euphrates-to-the-river-of-Egypt boundaries), 17:8 (as 'an everlasting possession'), 22:17 (with the seashore-sand imagery), 26:3 (to Isaac), 28:13 (to Jacob), and elsewhere. Verse 12:7 is the bare nucleus: to your seed I will give this land. // The WoH translation preserves seed over the modern smoothings 'offspring' (NIV) and 'descendants' (RSV, NRSV), matching ASV. The agricultural metaphor — seed-in-ground, growth, multiplication — is the patriarchal cycle's load-bearing image and will recur through the chapter and corpus. // The altar. Vayyiven sham mizbeach la-YHWH ha-nire'eh elav — he built there an altar to YHWH who had appeared to him. The altar-building marks the site as a cult-place: the spot of the theophany is now spatially designated. The chapter is establishing the patriarchal pattern of cult-site-foundation, which will continue at Bethel (v 8) and Hebron (13:18). The first patriarchal altar is under the oak at Shechem — the sacred-tree-and-altar combination that the later cult would not maintain in the same form.

8
He moved on from there to the hill country east of Bethel and pitched his tent, with Bethel on the west and Ai on the east; and he built there an altar to YHWH and called on the name of YHWH.
And he removed from thence unto the mountain on the east of Beth-el, and pitched his tent, having Beth-el on the west, and Ai on the east: and there he builded an altar unto Jehovah, and called upon the name of Jehovah.
וַיַּעְתֵּ֨ק מִשָּׁ֜ם הָהָ֗רָה מִקֶּ֛דֶם לְבֵֽית־אֵ֖ל וַיֵּ֣ט אָהֳלֹ֑ה בֵּֽית־אֵ֤ל מִיָּם֙ וְהָעַ֣י מִקֶּ֔דֶם וַיִּֽבֶן־שָׁ֤ם מִזְבֵּ֙חַ֙ לַֽיהוָ֔ה וַיִּקְרָ֖א בְּשֵׁ֥ם יְהוָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

The second altar in two verses, and the introduction of the qara b'shem YHWH formula in the patriarchal corpus. // Vayyiqra b'shem YHWH — and he called on the name of YHWH — is one of the Hebrew Bible's stable formulae for cult-site invocation. The phrase qara b'shem YHWH (literally call with/in the name of YHWH) covers a semantic range from worship-invocation through proclamation of the name through ritual naming. The phrase was first introduced at Gen 4:26, in the closing notice of the Seth-Enosh genealogy: az huchal liqro b'shem YHWH — then it was begun to call on the name of YHWH — marking the antediluvian origin of YHWH-cult. // The patriarchal recurrences mark altar-sites along the Abrahamic and Isaac itineraries: here at 12:8 (the hill country between Bethel and Ai); 13:4 (return to the same altar after the descent to Mitsrayim — the chapter is structurally framing the Mitsrayim episode between two callings-on-the-name at Bethel); 21:33 (Abraham at Beersheba, after planting a tamarisk); 26:25 (Isaac at Beersheba). The verb is the patriarchal altar-formula. // The chapter is establishing Abram as the resumption of the Seth-Enosh YHWH-cult tradition — the one whose line had been broken by Babel and the dispersion. The first thing Abram does on entering the promised land is to re-found the worship that had begun at Enosh's generation and been interrupted by the post-Flood human ambition. // The geographic detail Beth-El miyam v'ha-Ai miqedem (Bethel on the sea-side, Ai on the east) names the precise topographic position with respect to the two later-important towns. Bethel will be Jacob's theophany site (Gen 28); Ai will be the site of Israel's first conquest setback (Josh 7–8). The chapter is geographically dense with future-narrative resonance.

9
Abram journeyed on, going and journeying toward the Negev.
And Abram journeyed, going on still toward the South.
וַיִּסַּ֣ע אַבְרָ֔ם הָל֥וֹךְ וְנָס֖וֹעַ הַנֶּֽגְבָּה׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The doubled infinitive-absolute construction. Halokh v'naso'a — going and journeying — pairs two infinitives-absolute to mark continuous, progressive motion: 'going on continuously', 'kept journeying'. The Hebrew idiom intensifies what a single verb would say. ASV: 'going on still toward the South'. // The Negev (הַנֶּגֶב, the south / the dry-country) is the semi-arid southern region of Canaan, between the central hill country and the Sinai. The chapter is positioning Abram in the southern Negev just before the famine of v 10 will push him further south into Egypt — the geographic sequence is Shechem → Bethel/Ai → Negev → Mitsrayim, a steady southward progression that will pivot at Mitsrayim and return.

10
There was a famine in the land, and Abram went down to Mitsrayim to sojourn there, for the famine was heavy in the land.
And there was a famine in the land: and Abram went down into Egypt to sojourn there; for the famine was sore in the land.
וַיְהִ֥י רָעָ֖ב בָּאָ֑רֶץ וַיֵּ֨רֶד אַבְרָ֤ם מִצְרַ֙יְמָה֙ לָג֣וּר שָׁ֔ם כִּֽי־כָבֵ֥ד הָרָעָ֖ב בָּאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

The chapter pivots into the wife-sister type-scene. Vayered Avram Mitsraymah — Abram went down to Mitsrayim. // The verb yarad (descend / go down) is the Hebrew Bible's standard geographic register for movement to Egypt. The Levant is topologically up relative to the Nile delta — both in elevation (Canaan's hill country is hundreds of meters above sea level; the eastern Delta is at or near sea level) and in Hebrew cardinal-orientation conventions (south is down). The verbal pattern yarad ... Mitsraymah is consistent across the corpus: Joseph is brought down to Egypt (Gen 39:1); Jacob goes down to Mitsrayim (Gen 46:3); the credal recapitulation in Deut 26:5 opens with Arami oved avi vayered Mitsraymah — my father was a wandering Aramean, and he went down to Mitsrayim. The Exodus reverses the descent: Israel comes up (aliyah) out of Mitsrayim. // The verbal pattern is also a thematic pattern. Going down to Mitsrayim in the patriarchal corpus is structurally the move into peril-with-deliverance: famine drives the descent (here at 12:10, again at 42–47 for Jacob's household), foreign-court complications follow (Pharaoh here, Potiphar's house and the imprisonment for Joseph), and divine intervention restores. The Abram-going-down at 12:10 is the first instance of a pattern that will end in the Exodus. // Robert Alter, The Art of Biblical Narrative (1981, expanded 2011), is the canonical scholarly treatment of the wife-sister sequence as a type-scene. Three instances appear in Genesis: 12:10–20 (Abram, Sarai, Pharaoh), 20:1–18 (Abraham, Sarah, Abimelech of Gerar), 26:1–11 (Isaac, Rebekah, Abimelech of Gerar). All three share the same narrative skeleton — patriarch travels to foreign land under duress; pre-empts the danger of foreign king seeing his wife by passing her off as his sister; wife is taken into foreign king's house; YHWH intervenes; king discovers the deception and returns the wife with rebuke. The repetition with variation is the diagnostic feature of biblical type-scene composition. // The classical source-critical reading (Gunkel; Speiser, 1964) treats the three as variant traditions preserved by different sources (12:10–20 = J; 20 = E; 26 = J again). The modern literary-critical reading (Alter; Sternberg, The Poetics of Biblical Narrative, 1985) treats them as deliberate narrative repetition — the same scene played three times to develop character, contrast patriarchs, and trace ethical maturation across the cycle. The two readings are not mutually exclusive. // The chapter is here opening the first instance of one of the corpus's most studied type-scenes. The remaining verses (11–20) will play it out.

11
As he drew near to enter Mitsrayim, he said to Sarai his wife, "Behold now, I know that you are a woman beautiful of appearance.
And it came to pass, when he was come near to enter into Egypt, that he said unto Sarai his wife, Behold now, I know that thou art a fair woman to look upon:
וַיְהִ֕י כַּאֲשֶׁ֥ר הִקְרִ֖יב לָב֣וֹא מִצְרָ֑יְמָה וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ אֶל־שָׂרַ֣י אִשְׁתּ֔וֹ הִנֵּה־נָ֣א יָדַ֔עְתִּי כִּ֛י אִשָּׁ֥ה יְפַת־מַרְאֶ֖ה אָֽתְּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

Ishah yefat-mar'eh — אִשָּׁה יְפַת־מַרְאֶה — a woman beautiful of appearance. The phrase is a stock formula in biblical narrative for the dangerously attractive female figure. // The formula's recurrences across the Hebrew Bible track the cases where a woman's beauty becomes consequential: Rebekah at Gen 26:7 (tovat mar'eh, 'good of appearance' — applied within the wife-sister type-scene's second instance); Rachel at Gen 29:17 (yefat to'ar v'yefat mar'eh, the doubled form 'beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance' — Jacob's love at first sight); Joseph at Gen 39:6 (yefeh to'ar v'yefeh mar'eh, the same doubled form in masculine — making Joseph one of only two men in the Hebrew Bible explicitly described with the beauty-formula; the other is David at 1 Sam 16:12); Abigail at 1 Sam 25:3 (tovat sekhel v'yefat to'ar, intelligence-and-beauty); Tamar daughter of Absalom at 2 Sam 14:27 (ishah yefat mar'eh); Bathsheba at 2 Sam 11:2 (tovat mar'eh me'od — the seeing-from-the-roof scene); Abishag the Shunammite at 1 Kgs 1:3–4 (na'arah yafah ... yafah ad-me'od); Vashti at Esther 1:11 (tovat mar'eh); Esther at Esth 2:7 (yefat to'ar v'tovat mar'eh). // The formula functions in the narrative as a plot-trigger: it signals that the woman's beauty is about to drive consequential events. Sarai's beauty here will drive the Pharaoh-episode; Rebekah's at 26:7 will drive the Abimelech-episode; Rachel's will drive Jacob's fourteen years of labor; Bathsheba's will drive the David-Uriah crisis; Esther's will drive the deliverance of the diaspora. The Hebrew Bible's storytelling reserves the beauty-formula for cases where beauty becomes a vector of narrative force. // The WoH translation preserves the formulaic structure a woman beautiful of appearance — over ASV's 'a fair woman to look upon' (archaic) and modern 'a beautiful woman' (which dissolves the formula). The intratextual signal is preserved.

12
And it shall come to pass that when the Mitsrim see you, they will say, 'This is his wife,' and they will kill me and let you live.
and it will come to pass, when the Egyptians shall see thee, that they will say, This is his wife: and they will kill me, but they will save thee alive.
וְהָיָ֗ה כִּֽי־יִרְא֤וּ אֹתָךְ֙ הַמִּצְרִ֔ים וְאָמְר֖וּ אִשְׁתּ֣וֹ זֹ֑את וְהָרְג֥וּ אֹתִ֖י וְאֹתָ֥ךְ יְחַיּֽוּ׃
13
Say, please, that you are my sister, so that it may go well with me on your account, and my soul shall live because of you.
Say, I pray thee, thou art my sister; that it may be well with me for thy sake, and that my soul may live because of thee.
אִמְרִי־נָ֖א אֲחֹ֣תִי אָ֑תְּ לְמַ֙עַן֙ יִֽיטַב־לִ֣י בַעֲבוּרֵ֔ךְ וְחָיְתָ֥ה נַפְשִׁ֖י בִּגְלָלֵֽךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

The chapter's most ethically debated verse. Imri-na achoti at — say, please, that you are my sister. The strategy: have Sarai claim to be Abram's sister rather than his wife, so that a Pharaonic appropriation of her (which Abram is treating as inevitable given her beauty) will not require killing the husband. // Three readings of Abram's strategy circulate. // (a) The morally-compromised reading (Speiser, 1964; Sarna, JPS, 1989; Westermann, 1985; Hamilton, NICOT, 1990; Alter, 2004 — the modern critical mainstream). Abram is lying to save his own life by exposing Sarai to sexual appropriation. The story is included in the patriarchal cycle precisely because it is morally ambiguous — the patriarchs of Genesis are not idealized; the narrative records their lapses (Abram and Hagar in ch 16; Jacob's deception of Isaac in 27; Judah and Tamar in 38; Joseph's brothers' fratricidal plot in 37). The verse-form's na (politeness particle) and l'ma'an yitav-li (so that it may go well with me) make Abram's self-interest explicit. The Pharaoh-episode that follows will deliver an implicit narrative judgment: Pharaoh rebukes Abram in v 18 (what is this you have done to me?) for the deception, and YHWH afflicts Pharaoh's house only because of what Abram has set in motion. // (b) The apologetic-rabbinic reading (Genesis Rabbah 41:2; the targumim; Rashi). Abram's claim is technically true — Sarai is Abram's half-sister (per Gen 20:12, where Abraham tells Abimelech she is indeed my sister, the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother). The strategy is a partial truth deployed under duress, not a full lie. On this reading Abram's deception is mitigated. // (c) The defensive-tactical reading (some modern apologetic commentators; less common in critical scholarship). Abram is acting within the constraints of a foreign court system in which marriage protections did not extend to traveling foreigners. The sister-designation activates kinship-negotiation protocols that would not have applied to a foreign couple presented as married, where the husband would simply be removed. On this reading the strategy is risky but pragmatic, and the moral weight falls on the foreign system that creates the constraint. // The Sarai-as-half-sister claim of 20:12 is itself complicated — the verse appears only in the second wife-sister episode and may be apologetic redactional material aimed at exactly the problem the chapter creates. Critical scholarship is divided on whether 20:12 should be read as authentic Abrahamic genealogy or as later harmonization. // The chapter at 12:13 does not editorialize on the strategy. The verbs yitav-li (it may go well with me) and chaytah nafshi (my soul shall live) are stated baldly in Abram's voice. The narrative judgment, such as it is, will come through Pharaoh's rebuke in v 18 and through the parallel re-tellings at chs 20 and 26 — each of which the redactor chose to preserve.

14
When Abram entered Mitsrayim, the Mitsrim saw the woman, that she was very beautiful.
And it came to pass, that, when Abram was come into Egypt, the Egyptians beheld the woman that she was very fair.
וַיְהִ֕י כְּב֥וֹא אַבְרָ֖ם מִצְרָ֑יְמָה וַיִּרְא֤וּ הַמִּצְרִים֙ אֶת־הָ֣אִשָּׁ֔ה כִּֽי־יָפָ֥ה הִ֖וא מְאֹֽד׃
15
The officers of Pharaoh saw her and praised her to Pharaoh, and the woman was taken into the house of Pharaoh.
And the princes of Pharaoh saw her, and praised her to Pharaoh: and the woman was taken into Pharaoh`s house.
וַיִּרְא֤וּ אֹתָהּ֙ שָׂרֵ֣י פַרְעֹ֔ה וַיְהַֽלְל֥וּ אֹתָ֖הּ אֶל־פַּרְעֹ֑ה וַתֻּקַּ֥ח הָאִשָּׁ֖ה בֵּ֥ית פַּרְעֹֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 15

Sarei Par'oh — שָׂרֵי פַרְעֹה — the officers of Pharaoh (plural of sar, prince / official / commander). The Pharaonic court structure assumed by the narrative is familiar from the Joseph cycle (chs 39–41) and the Exodus (Ex 1:11): a hierarchy of sarim who serve the king and conduct his affairs of state. The verse moves with brisk procedural language — the officers see, then praise, then the woman is taken. The grammatical passive vatuqqach (was taken) keeps the agency diffuse: Sarai is taken into the royal household without the verse naming who exactly does the taking, in keeping with the court-procedural register.

16
And he treated Abram well on her account; and he had sheep and cattle and male donkeys and male slaves and female slaves and female donkeys and camels.
And he dealt well with Abram for her sake: and he had sheep, and oxen, and he-asses, and men-servants, and maid-servants, and she-asses, and camels.
וּלְאַבְרָ֥ם הֵיטִ֖יב בַּעֲבוּרָ֑הּ וַֽיְהִי־ל֤וֹ צֹאן־וּבָקָר֙ וַחֲמֹרִ֔ים וַעֲבָדִים֙ וּשְׁפָחֹ֔ת וַאֲתֹנֹ֖ת וּגְמַלִּֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

The camels at the end of the gift-list are the patriarchal narratives' most-cited anachronism. The seven-item inventory is otherwise plausible for second-millennium-BCE Levantine pastoralism: tzon (sheep / small cattle), baqar (cattle / oxen), chamorim (male donkeys), avadim (male slaves), shfachot (female slaves), atonot (female donkeys), g'mallim (camels). The first six fit the Bronze Age economy without controversy. The seventh does not. // The archaeological consensus on camel domestication in the southern Levant places the appearance of the domesticated camel as a working pack animal between roughly 1000 and 900 BCE — the early Iron Age, well into the period of the Israelite monarchy. The decisive recent study is Sapir-Hen and Ben-Yosef, 'The Introduction of Domestic Camels to the Southern Levant: Evidence from the Aravah Valley', Tel Aviv 40 (2013): 277–285 — a stratigraphic analysis of camel bones in the Aravah copper-mining sites that pins the introduction to the late tenth century BCE. Earlier reviews of the evidence (Köhler-Rollefson, 'Camels and Camel Pastoralism in Arabia', Biblical Archaeologist 56/4, 1993) reached the same conclusion from different evidence. Finkelstein and Silberman, The Bible Unearthed (2001), made the Iron Age dating widely known in popular scholarship. // The patriarchal narratives are conventionally placed in the Middle Bronze Age (ca. 2000–1500 BCE) or, on some chronologies, the Late Bronze Age (ca. 1500–1200 BCE). Both placements are centuries earlier than the documented arrival of the domesticated camel as a Levantine working animal. The camels at Gen 12:16 (and at 24:10–11, 30:43, 31:17, 32:7, 37:25) are therefore widely read as redactional anachronism — markers of the post-monarchic period in which the narratives reached final form, projected backward onto an earlier setting. // The standard scholarly inference is that the patriarchal narratives were composed or finalized in a period when camel pastoralism was familiar (the late monarchic or post-exilic redactor would have known camels as part of the standard pastoral inventory) and the camel was projected backward as a generic luxury-trade-animal. The four wife-sister episodes' mentions of camels (here at 12:16; at 24:10–11 — the most prominent, with ten camels in Abraham's servant's mission to Aram-Naharaim; at 30:43; at 37:25) are concentrated in J/E material, consistent with later compositional dating. // The WoH translation renders the inventory item straight as 'camels' — the editorial question is for commentary, not for translation. The text-as-received names camels; the historical-critical question is when the text-as-received reached this form, and what that implies about its compositional dating.

17
YHWH afflicted Pharaoh and his house with great plagues on account of Sarai, the wife of Abram.
And Jehovah plagued Pharaoh and his house with great plagues because of Sarai, Abram`s wife.
וַיְנַגַּ֨ע יְהוָ֧ה ׀ אֶת־פַּרְעֹ֛ה נְגָעִ֥ים גְּדֹלִ֖ים וְאֶת־בֵּית֑וֹ עַל־דְּבַ֥ר שָׂרַ֖י אֵ֥שֶׁת אַבְרָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

The first plague-on-a-Pharaoh in the Hebrew Bible, and the verse most often cited in scholarship on Genesis 12 as proto-Exodus typology. // Vaynagga YHWH et-Par'oh n'ga'im g'dolim — YHWH afflicted Pharaoh with great plagues. The verb naga (n-g-' qal) means touch, strike, afflict, smite. The cognate noun nega (plague, blow, stroke, affliction-mark) is the technical Hebrew term for divinely-sent affliction across the Pentateuch. Leviticus 13–14 uses nega extensively for the ritual-impurity category of skin-disease (the so-called tzara'at of 'leprosy'). Deuteronomy 24:8 uses nega ha-tzara'at in the same sense. The root carries a specifically cultic-juridical connotation: the plague is not natural illness but divinely-imposed mark. // The chapter's typological link to the Exodus is structurally precise. The Exodus narrative uses the same root naga / nega for the divine plagues against Egypt: most decisively at Exodus 11:1, in YHWH's announcement of the tenth plague to Moses: od nega echad avi al-Par'oh v'al-Mitsrayim — one more plague (nega) I will bring upon Pharaoh and upon Mitsrayim. The same noun, the same construction with al (upon), the same subject (YHWH), the same object (Pharaoh and Mitsrayim). The verbal echo across the two passages is one of the patriarchal corpus's most discussed structural parallels. // The Abram-Pharaoh / Moses-Pharaoh parallel structure can be tabulated cleanly: a famine drives Israel down to Mitsrayim → Pharaoh oppresses (here by taking Sarai; in Exodus by enslavement) → YHWH afflicts Pharaoh with plagues (n'ga'im g'dolim / n'ga'im) → Pharaoh summons the Hebrew patriarch / prophet (v 18 here; Exod 12:31 there) → Pharaoh expels the Hebrews (v 20 here; Exod 12:33 there) → the Hebrews depart with great substance (vv 16, 20 — the gift-list; Exod 12:35–36 — the spoiling of the Egyptians). The structural parallel is extensive enough that critical scholarship (Garbini, History and Ideology in Ancient Israel, 1988; Crüsemann; Sarna's JPS commentary on Genesis 12) treats Gen 12:10–20 as a deliberate prefiguration of the Exodus, composed or redacted in light of the later narrative. // The two readings of the parallel in critical scholarship are: (a) typological redaction — Gen 12:10–20 was composed late, modeled on the Exodus narrative, and inserted into the Abrahamic cycle to establish Abram as proto-Moses and the patriarchal descent-and-return as proto-Exodus; (b) convergent type-scene — both narratives draw on a common Mesopotamian or Egyptian topos of the foreign-court appropriation-and-deliverance, with no necessary direction of dependence. Most modern critical scholars (Westermann, Sarna, Alter) favor (a). // The verse's al-d'var Sarai eshet Avram (on account of Sarai the wife of Abram) makes the causal link explicit: the plagues are YHWH's response specifically to the Pharaonic appropriation of Sarai. The chapter is silent about what made the link visible to Pharaoh — the next verse will reveal that Pharaoh somehow knows the cause.

18
Pharaoh called Abram and said, "What is this you have done to me? Why did you not tell me that she is your wife?
And Pharaoh called Abram, and said, What is this that thou hast done unto me? why didst thou not tell me that she was thy wife?
וַיִּקְרָ֤א פַרְעֹה֙ לְאַבְרָ֔ם וַיֹּ֕אמֶר מַה־זֹּ֖את עָשִׂ֣יתָ לִּ֑י לָ֚מָּה לֹא־הִגַּ֣דְתָּ לִּ֔י כִּ֥י אִשְׁתְּךָ֖ הִֽוא׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

Pharaoh's rebuke delivers the chapter's implicit moral judgment on Abram's strategy. Mah-zot asita li — what is this you have done to me? — uses the same construction (mah-zot asita) that YHWH addressed to Eve at Gen 3:13 (mah-zot asit, 'what is this you have done?'). The rebuke-formula is consistent across the corpus: the more powerful party confronts the less powerful with the consequences of a hidden act. Here the formula's polarity is striking — Pharaoh is addressing Abram in the register that elsewhere belongs to YHWH addressing humans. The chapter is staging a foreign king as the agent of moral truth-telling against the patriarch. The narrative judgment lands on Abram from the unexpected direction. // Lamah lo higgad'ta li ki ishtekha hi — why did you not tell me that she is your wife? — assumes a moral framework in which appropriating another man's wife is wrong and Abram's concealment is culpable. Pharaoh is invoking a standard the verse takes for granted, without exposition. The rebuke is brief and pointed.

19
Why did you say, 'She is my sister,' so that I took her for myself as a wife? Now then, here is your wife — take her and go.
why saidst thou, She is my sister, so that I took her to be my wife? now therefore behold thy wife, take her, and go thy way.
לָמָ֤ה אָמַ֙רְתָּ֙ אֲחֹ֣תִי הִ֔וא וָאֶקַּ֥ח אֹתָ֛הּ לִ֖י לְאִשָּׁ֑ה וְעַתָּ֕ה הִנֵּ֥ה אִשְׁתְּךָ֖ קַ֥ח וָלֵֽךְ׃
20
Pharaoh commanded men concerning him, and they sent him away with his wife and all that he had.
And Pharaoh gave men charge concerning him: and they brought him on the way, and his wife, and all that he had.
וַיְצַ֥ו עָלָ֛יו פַּרְעֹ֖ה אֲנָשִׁ֑ים וַֽיְשַׁלְּח֥וּ אֹת֛וֹ וְאֶת־אִשְׁתּ֖וֹ וְאֶת־כָּל־אֲשֶׁר־לֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

The chapter closes with the verb that will close the Exodus. Vay'shal'chu oto — and they sent him away. // The verb shalach in the piel stem (shillach) is the Hebrew Bible's standard term for expulsion / sending-out / dismissal of persons. The piel intensifies the qal shalach (send) toward forced removal: not merely send but send off, dismiss, expel, set free. The same piel verb governs the keystone Exodus moments: // Exodus 6:1 — YHWH's promise to Moses that Pharaoh will send Israel out: b'yad chazaqah y'shalchem u-v'yad chazaqah y'gar'shem me-artzo — with a strong hand he will send them out (y'shalchem), and with a strong hand he will drive them out from his land. // Exodus 11:1 — YHWH's announcement of the tenth plague: acharei khen y'shalach et'khem mi-zeh, k'shalcho kalah garesh y'garesh et'khem mi-zeh — after that he will send you out (y'shalach) from here; when he sends you out completely (k'shalcho kalah), he will utterly drive you out from here. // Exodus 12:33 — the immediate aftermath of the tenth plague: vatechezaq Mitsrayim al-ha-am l'maher l'shalcham min ha-aretz — the Mitsrim were urgent upon the people, to send them out (l'shalcham) from the land in haste. // The verbal link is exact. The Abram-expulsion of 12:20 is verbally identical to the Israel-expulsion of Exod 12:33 — the same piel of sh-l-ch, the same context of Pharaonic dismissal under divine pressure, the same outcome of departure with great substance. The chapter is verbally closing its proto-Exodus structure with the verb that will verbally open the Exodus's final scene. // The structural parallel discussed in scholarship (Sarna, JPS, 1989; Garbini; Crüsemann; the wider Pentateuchal-criticism literature) extends to the detail of vay'shal'chu oto v'et-ishto v'et-kol-asher-lo (sent him with his wife and all that he had), which closely parallels the Exodus departure-of-Israel-with-spoils. The chapter ends with Abram-and-household leaving Mitsrayim wealthier than they arrived, escorted out under royal command — the prefiguration of what Israel will do at the Exodus.

Chapter 13

Genesis 13 — The return from Mitsrayim and the separation from Lot

1
Abram went up from Mitsrayim, he and his wife and all that he had, and Lot with him, toward the Negev.
And Abram went up out of Egypt, he, and his wife, and all that he had, and Lot with him, into the South.
וַיַּעַל֩ אַבְרָ֨ם מִמִּצְרַ֜יִם ה֠וּא וְאִשְׁתּ֧וֹ וְכָל־אֲשֶׁר־ל֛וֹ וְל֥וֹט עִמּ֖וֹ הַנֶּֽגְבָּה׃
2
And Abram was very heavy in cattle, in silver, and in gold.
And Abram was very rich in cattle, in silver, and in gold.
וְאַבְרָ֖ם כָּבֵ֣ד מְאֹ֑ד בַּמִּקְנֶ֕ה בַּכֶּ֖סֶף וּבַזָּהָֽב׃
3
He went on his journeys from the Negev as far as Bethel, to the place where his tent had been at the beginning, between Bethel and Ai,
And he went on his journeys from the South even to Beth-el, unto the place where his tent had been at the beginning, between Beth-el and Ai,
וַיֵּ֙לֶךְ֙ לְמַסָּעָ֔יו מִנֶּ֖גֶב וְעַד־בֵּֽית־אֵ֑ל עַד־הַמָּק֗וֹם אֲשֶׁר־הָ֨יָה שָׁ֤ם אהלה [אָֽהֳלוֹ֙] בַּתְּחִלָּ֔ה בֵּ֥ין בֵּֽית־אֵ֖ל וּבֵ֥ין הָעָֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

A ketiv/qere divergence sits at the center of the verse. The consonantal text (ketiv) reads אהלה — without a final mater lectionis — which the Masoretes pointed in the margin (qere) as אָהֳלוֹ, oholo, 'his tent' (masculine-singular noun + 3ms pronominal suffix). The qere is the standard masculine-singular construct-with-suffix form and matches the consistent Genesis idiom (cf. Gen 12:8, 18:1, 18:10, 26:25, 31:25). Two minority readings of the unpointed ketiv circulate: as a defectively-written oholah (feminine, 'her tent' — Sarai's tent), or as an archaic spelling of the same masculine form preserved without the conventional waw. The Masoretes' qere is the consensus reading and the WoH translation follows it ('his tent'), matching ASV, NJPS, and NRSV.

4
to the place of the altar that he had made there at the first; and Abram called there on the name of YHWH.
unto the place of the altar, which he had made there at the first: and there Abram called on the name of Jehovah.
אֶל־מְקוֹם֙ הַמִּזְבֵּ֔חַ אֲשֶׁר־עָ֥שָׂה שָׁ֖ם בָּרִאשֹׁנָ֑ה וַיִּקְרָ֥א שָׁ֛ם אַבְרָ֖ם בְּשֵׁ֥ם יְהוָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

The Bethel-altar return is the structural close of the Mitsrayim descent-and-return frame. The chapter is deliberately bracketing the famine-and-descent episode of 12:10–20 between two altar-callings at Bethel — the inaugural altar of 12:8 (qara b-shem YHWH there for the first time on the land) and this restored altar at the same site. The phrase barishonah (at the first / formerly) is the chapter's own backward gesture: Abram is returning not just to a region but to a specific cult-place and resuming an interrupted relationship. The qara b-shem YHWH formula carries the antediluvian Seth-Enosh cult-resumption claim across the patriarchal narratives; the glossary entry tracks the formula's recurrences.

5
And Lot also, who went with Abram, had flocks and herds and tents.
And Lot also, who went with Abram, had flocks, and herds, and tents.
וְגַם־לְל֔וֹט הַהֹלֵ֖ךְ אֶת־אַבְרָ֑ם הָיָ֥ה צֹאן־וּבָקָ֖ר וְאֹהָלִֽים׃
6
And the land was not able to bear them, that they might dwell together; for their substance was great, and they could not dwell together.
And the land was not able to bear them, that they might dwell together: for their substance was great, so that they could not dwell together.
וְלֹא־נָשָׂ֥א אֹתָ֛ם הָאָ֖רֶץ לָשֶׁ֣בֶת יַחְדָּ֑ו כִּֽי־הָיָ֤ה רְכוּשָׁם֙ רָ֔ב וְלֹ֥א יָֽכְל֖וּ לָשֶׁ֥בֶת יַחְדָּֽו׃
7
And there was strife between the herders of Abram's cattle and the herders of Lot's cattle. And the Canaanite and the Perizzite were then dwelling in the land.
And there was a strife between the herdsmen of Abram`s cattle and the herdsmen of Lot`s cattle: and the Canaanite and the Perizzite dwelt then in the land.
וַֽיְהִי־רִ֗יב בֵּ֚ין רֹעֵ֣י מִקְנֵֽה־אַבְרָ֔ם וּבֵ֖ין רֹעֵ֣י מִקְנֵה־ל֑וֹט וְהַֽכְּנַעֲנִי֙ וְהַפְּרִזִּ֔י אָ֖ז יֹשֵׁ֥ב בָּאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The verse closes with the chapter's most-cited Documentary-Hypothesis flag. V-ha-Kna'ani v-ha-Prizi az yoshev ba-aretz — 'and the Canaanite and the Perizzite were then dwelling in the land' — is a temporal-perspective parenthetical that foregrounds a redactional vantage later than the events described. The participial yoshev (dwelling) with the temporal adverb az (then) reads as a narrator's aside to a reader for whom the Canaanites and Perizzites are no longer the dominant population: the parenthetical only does work if it informs the audience of a state of affairs they would not have assumed by default. // The verse pairs with the simpler parallel at Gen 12:6 — v-ha-Kna'ani az ba-aretz — which uses the Canaanite alone. The expanded inventory here at 13:7 doubles it with the Perizzite, anticipating the longer ethnographic lists that will appear at 15:18–21 and 34:30 and across Deuteronomy and Joshua. The doubling has been read as either a redactional expansion of the 12:6 form (so Westermann, Genesis 12–36, Hermeneia 1985) or as a context-specific shift naming the two peoples Abram and Lot's herders would have been in direct competition with for grazing-rights in the hill country (so Wenham, WBC Genesis 1–15, 1987, p. 297). // The Documentary Hypothesis reading treats both notices (12:6 and 13:7) as J-stratum parentheticals from a post-conquest perspective.
