The reading is mainstream in critical scholarship (Wellhausen, Gunkel, von Rad, Speiser) but not universally accepted; Cassuto and Kitchen have argued that the parentheticals are compatible with a pre-conquest narrator describing the ethnographic situation of the patriarchs' own day, where the Canaanite-Perizzite presence was the salient feature of land-use competition. The WoH translation preserves the parenthetical's temporal force ('were then dwelling') without taking a definitive source-critical position; the redactional-seam reading is well-attested but the dating question remains open.

8
Abram said to Lot, "Let there be no strife, please, between me and you, and between my herders and your herders; for we are men who are brothers.
And Abram said unto Lot, Let there be no strife, I pray thee, between me and thee, and between my herdsmen and thy herdsmen; for we are brethren.
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר אַבְרָ֜ם אֶל־ל֗וֹט אַל־נָ֨א תְהִ֤י מְרִיבָה֙ בֵּינִ֣י וּבֵינֶ֔יךָ וּבֵ֥ין רֹעַ֖י וּבֵ֣ין רֹעֶ֑יךָ כִּֽי־אֲנָשִׁ֥ים אַחִ֖ים אֲנָֽחְנוּ׃
9
Is not the whole land before you? Separate yourself, please, from me. If you go to the left, then I will go to the right; and if you go to the right, then I will go to the left."
Is not the whole land before thee? separate thyself, I pray thee, from me. If [thou wilt take] the left hand, then I will go to the right. Or if [thou take] the right hand, then I will go to the left.
הֲלֹ֤א כָל־הָאָ֙רֶץ֙ לְפָנֶ֔יךָ הִפָּ֥רֶד נָ֖א מֵעָלָ֑י אִם־הַשְּׂמֹ֣אל וְאֵימִ֔נָה וְאִם־הַיָּמִ֖ין וְאַשְׂמְאִֽילָה׃
10
Lot lifted up his eyes and saw all the plain of the Jordan, that it was well watered everywhere — before YHWH destroyed Sodom and Amorah — like the garden of YHWH, like the land of Mitsrayim, as you go toward Tsoar.
And Lot lifted up his eyes, and beheld all the Plain of the Jordan, that it was well watered every where, before Jehovah destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah, like the garden of Jehovah, like the land of Egypt, as thou goest unto Zoar.
וַיִּשָּׂא־ל֣וֹט אֶת־עֵינָ֗יו וַיַּרְא֙ אֶת־כָּל־כִּכַּ֣ר הַיַּרְדֵּ֔ן כִּ֥י כֻלָּ֖הּ מַשְׁקֶ֑ה לִפְנֵ֣י ׀ שַׁחֵ֣ת יְהוָ֗ה אֶת־סְדֹם֙ וְאֶת־עֲמֹרָ֔ה כְּגַן־יְהוָה֙ כְּאֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרַ֔יִם בֹּאֲכָ֖ה צֹֽעַר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

The verse contains the chapter's most consequential inter-textual move. K-gan YHWH k-eretz Mitsrayim — 'like the garden of YHWH, like the land of Mitsrayim' — is a doubled simile that yokes two of the Hebrew Bible's most freighted geographical referents to the pre-destruction plain of the Jordan. // First, gan YHWH. The phrase is a lexical back-reference to gan b-Eden of Gen 2:8 — the only other 'garden' associated with YHWH-Elohim in the primeval narratives. The reading is mainstream: Westermann (Genesis 12–36, 1985, p. 178), Sarna (JPS Genesis, 1989, p. 100), Hamilton (NICOT Genesis 1–17, 1990, p. 393), and Alter (Five Books of Moses, 2004, p. 65) all flag the Eden allusion. The phrase frames the Jordan plain as a second Eden — a primeval-fertility analogue — and sets up the narrative engine that the next several chapters will run on: Lot has chosen the Eden-like region precisely when YHWH is about to destroy it. The narrator's parenthetical lifnei shachet YHWH et-Sodom v-et-Amorah ('before YHWH destroyed Sodom and Amorah') makes the typology explicit by name. // The Eden-loss / Sodom-destruction parallel that Genesis stages across chs 2–3 and chs 18–19 is one of the corpus's clearest type-scene pairings: a fertile divinely-favored region, a human action that violates its terms, expulsion-and-destruction, and a flaming sword (Gen 3:24) / fire-and-sulfur from the skies (Gen 19:24). The reading is critical-consensus and pre-dates the modern literary-critical turn (von Rad, Genesis, 1972 ET, p. 173). // Second, eretz Mitsrayim. The Nile-delta comparison stages a different resonance: the Jordan plain is a Nile-grade agricultural prototype, irrigated and produce-rich, the model the wilderness generation will remember nostalgically (Num 11:5, we remember the fish we ate in Mitsrayim freely, the cucumbers and the melons and the leeks and the onions and the garlic). Lot is choosing on Mitsrayim-grade criteria — the same evaluative frame that drew Abram down to Egypt in the famine of 12:10 — and the chapter is structurally rhyming his choice with Abram's earlier descent. // The WoH translation preserves the doubled simile literally ('like the garden of YHWH, like the land of Mitsrayim'), matching ASV. The Eden back-reference and the Sodom destruction-cycle typology are tracked by the gan-yhwh-eden-back-reference glossary entry.

11
Lot chose for himself all the plain of the Jordan, and Lot journeyed eastward; and they separated themselves the one from the other.
So Lot chose him all the Plain of the Jordan; and Lot journeyed east: and they separated themselves the one from the other.
וַיִּבְחַר־ל֣וֹ ל֗וֹט אֵ֚ת כָּל־כִּכַּ֣ר הַיַּרְדֵּ֔ן וַיִּסַּ֥ע ל֖וֹט מִקֶּ֑דֶם וַיִּפָּ֣רְד֔וּ אִ֖ישׁ מֵעַ֥ל אָחִֽיו׃
12
Abram dwelt in the land of Canaan, and Lot dwelt in the cities of the plain and pitched his tent as far as Sodom.
Abram dwelt in the land of Canaan, and Lot dwelt in the cities of the Plain, and moved his tent as far as Sodom.
אַבְרָ֖ם יָשַׁ֣ב בְּאֶֽרֶץ־כְּנָ֑עַן וְל֗וֹט יָשַׁב֙ בְּעָרֵ֣י הַכִּכָּ֔ר וַיֶּאֱהַ֖ל עַד־סְדֹֽם׃
13
And the men of Sodom were wicked and sinners against YHWH exceedingly.
Now the men of Sodom were wicked and sinners against Jehovah exceedingly.
וְאַנְשֵׁ֣י סְדֹ֔ם רָעִ֖ים וְחַטָּאִ֑ים לַיהוָ֖ה מְאֹֽד׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

The verse is a narrator-editorial intrusion that foreshadows chs 18–19. Two features make the assertion rhetorically pointed. // First, the doubled epithet ra'im v-chata'im — 'wicked and sinners'. Ra'im (from r-a-a, 'be bad / evil') names the moral-quality term: the men were bad. Chata'im (from ch-t-a, 'to miss the mark, to fail in obligation, to sin') names the relational-juridical term: they had failed against someone. The pairing collapses character and action — they were both the kind of people and the doers of the kind of acts. // Second, the dative l-YHWH. Hebrew has two standard ways to mark the offended party of a sin: neged (against, adversative — cf. 1 Sam 12:23, 'far be it from me that I should sin against [neged] YHWH') and l- + name (dative — cf. Gen 39:9, v-chatati l-Elohim, 'and I would have sinned against Elohim'; Exod 10:16, 'I have sinned against [l-] YHWH your Elohim'; Num 32:23, u-vatem chata'tem la-YHWH, 'and you have sinned against YHWH'). The l- + divine name with chata'im is grammatically standard for 'sinners against [someone]', so the construction itself is unremarkable. Speiser (Anchor Bible Genesis, 1964, p. 99) and HALOT both treat it as the normal Hebrew idiom. // What is more loaded is the me'od intensifier at the verse's end. The flat-assertion register — 'were wicked and sinners against YHWH exceedingly' — pairs the verse with the corpus's other narrator-editorial intrusions of moral judgment: Gen 6:5 (v-yar YHWH ki rabbah ra'at ha-adam ba-aretz v-khol-yetzer machshvot libo raq ra kol-ha-yom, 'YHWH saw that the wickedness of humanity was great in the earth, and every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually') and Gen 6:11–12 (v-tishachet ha-aretz lifnei ha-Elohim, 'and the earth was corrupt before the Elohim'). The verse is doing scene-setting work for the destruction of chs 18–19, framing it as a judgment on a community whose wickedness has been editorially established in advance. The WoH translation reads 'sinners against YHWH exceedingly', matching ASV; the dative-l construction is preserved without flagging a separate glossary entry.

14
And YHWH said to Abram, after Lot had separated himself from him, "Lift up now your eyes and look from the place where you are, northward and southward and eastward and westward;
And Jehovah said unto Abram, after that Lot was separated from him, Lift up now thine eyes, and look from the place where thou art, northward and southward and eastward and westward:
וַֽיהוָ֞ה אָמַ֣ר אֶל־אַבְרָ֗ם אַחֲרֵי֙ הִפָּֽרֶד־ל֣וֹט מֵֽעִמּ֔וֹ שָׂ֣א נָ֤א עֵינֶ֙יךָ֙ וּרְאֵ֔ה מִן־הַמָּק֖וֹם אֲשֶׁר־אַתָּ֣ה שָׁ֑ם צָפֹ֥נָה וָנֶ֖גְבָּה וָקֵ֥דְמָה וָיָֽמָּה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

The verse uses the va-YHWH amar formula — and YHWH said — rather than the patriarchal-theophany formula va-yera YHWH of 12:7 and 17:1. The distinction is meaningful: at 12:7 and 18:1 YHWH was seen / appeared (niphal of r-a-h); here YHWH simply speaks without the mode of seeing being specified. The chapter is staging the address as an auditory revelation rather than a visual theophany — the sa eynekha imperative that follows asks Abram to lift his own eyes, not to look upon a visible YHWH. // The phrase sa-na eynekha u-re'eh — 'lift up, please, your eyes and look' — is the inaugural occurrence of the nasa eynayim (lift one's eyes) formula in the patriarchal corpus. The formula recurs at Gen 18:2 (Abraham sees the three visitors at Mamre), Gen 22:4 (Abraham sees the place of the binding from afar), Gen 22:13 (Abraham sees the ram), Gen 24:63 (Isaac at evening), Gen 31:10 and 31:12 (Jacob's dream-vision), Gen 33:1 and 33:5 (Jacob sees Esau and the family), Gen 37:25 (Joseph's brothers see the caravan), Gen 43:29 (Joseph sees Benjamin). The formula is the standard Genesis seeing-formula, often introducing a visionary or recognitional perception. Westermann (Genesis 12–36, 1985, p. 180) notes the formulaic character; Alter (2004) flags the consistent use across the patriarchal arc. The WoH translation preserves the verb-phrase 'lift up your eyes' across all occurrences; the glossary entry tracks the full recurrence-set.

15
for all the land that you see, to you I will give it, and to your seed forever.
for all the land which thou seest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed for ever.
כִּ֧י אֶת־כָּל־הָאָ֛רֶץ אֲשֶׁר־אַתָּ֥ה רֹאֶ֖ה לְךָ֣ אֶתְּנֶ֑נָּה וּֽלְזַרְעֲךָ֖ עַד־עוֹלָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 15

The verse reissues and expands the seed-land promise of 12:7. The 12:7 formula (l-zarakha eten et-ha-aretz ha-zot) is here doubled into a to you + to your seed binary, and the new modifier ad-olam ('forever / for an indefinite duration') is added. The expansion is the second iteration in the Genesis covenantal chain that will run through 15:7-21 (cut-covenant ritual), 17:7-8 (brit olam), 22:17 (the doubled stars-and-sand image), 26:3-4 (to Isaac), 28:13-14 (to Jacob), and 35:11-12. The zera-eretz-promise glossary entry tracks the full chain.

16
And I will make your seed as the dust of the earth, so that if a man can count the dust of the earth, then your seed also shall be counted.
And I will make thy seed as the dust of the earth: So that if a man can number the dust of the earth, then may thy seed also be numbered.
וְשַׂמְתִּ֥י אֶֽת־זַרְעֲךָ֖ כַּעֲפַ֣ר הָאָ֑רֶץ אֲשֶׁ֣ר ׀ אִם־יוּכַ֣ל אִ֗ישׁ לִמְנוֹת֙ אֶת־עֲפַ֣ר הָאָ֔רֶץ גַּֽם־זַרְעֲךָ֖ יִמָּנֶֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

The seed-like-dust comparison is the first of three patriarchal seed-comparisons. The three are dust of the earth (Gen 13:16, here), stars of the heavens (Gen 15:5), and sand on the seashore (Gen 22:17, paired with the stars). The triad spans a deliberate cosmographic distribution: dust (earth, immanent, lowly), stars (sky, transcendent, height), sand (shore, the threshold between dry land and sea). Sarna (JPS Genesis, 1989, p. 100) and Alter (2004, p. 66) both treat the three as a deliberate set; Hamilton (NICOT Genesis 1–17, 1990, p. 396) reads the dust-image specifically as recalling Gen 2:7 and 3:19 — the afar min ha-adamah from which humanity was shaped and to which it returns. The dust-comparison here is therefore not just a multiplicity-image but a creation-recall: the patriarch's seed will share the substance of the original adamic forming-material. // The WoH translation preserves 'as the dust of the earth' (matching ASV); the patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad glossary entry tracks the three comparisons across the corpus.

17
Arise, walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it; for to you I will give it."
Arise, walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it; for unto thee will I give it.
ק֚וּם הִתְהַלֵּ֣ךְ בָּאָ֔רֶץ לְאָרְכָּ֖הּ וּלְרָחְבָּ֑הּ כִּ֥י לְךָ֖ אֶתְּנֶֽנָּה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

The verse names a juridical-symbolic act. Qum hit'hallekh ba-aretz l-orkah ul-rochbah — 'arise, walk through the land in its length and in its breadth' — uses the hithpael of h-l-k (walk-about, traverse-iteratively) with ba-aretz (in the land). The construction has been read across the modern critical tradition as a covenantal taking-possession by traversal: the patriarch's walking the boundaries of the land is itself a legal act of taking title to it. // The reading goes back to David Daube's Studies in Biblical Law (1947), §3 — comparing the rite to the Roman usucapio and to the Anglo-Saxon seisin-by-perambulation — and has been adopted by Speiser (Anchor Bible Genesis, 1964, p. 100), Sarna (JPS Genesis, 1989, p. 101), and Wenham (WBC Genesis 1–15, 1987, p. 299). The German biblical-law tradition discusses the same rite under the term Begehungsritus (boundary-walking ceremony). The parallel formulae at Josh 1:3 (kol-makom asher tidrokh kaf-raglekhem bo lakhem n'tatiyv, 'every place where the sole of your foot shall tread, to you have I given it') and Josh 18:8 (qumu v-hit'halku ba-aretz v-kitvu otah, 'arise, walk through the land and describe it') name the same juridical-symbolic act in the conquest setting. Deut 11:24 reiterates the foot-treading possession-formula. // The WoH translation preserves 'walk through the land', matching ASV; the covenant-walking reading is tracked by the hithallech-ba-aretz-covenant-walking glossary entry. The reading is held as inferred — the juridical-act framing is the modern scholarly synthesis rather than an explicit statement in the text.

18
Abram moved his tent, and came and dwelt by the oaks of Mamre, which are in Hebron, and built there an altar to YHWH.
And Abram moved his tent, and came and dwelt by the oaks of Mamre, which are in Hebron, and built there an altar unto Jehovah.
וַיֶּאֱהַ֣ל אַבְרָ֗ם וַיָּבֹ֛א וַיֵּ֛שֶׁב בְּאֵלֹנֵ֥י מַמְרֵ֖א אֲשֶׁ֣ר בְּחֶבְר֑וֹן וַיִּֽבֶן־שָׁ֥ם מִזְבֵּ֖חַ לַֽיהוָֽה׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 18

The chapter closes by naming a sacred-tree site that will become a recurring locus across the Abraham cycle. Elonei Mamre — 'the oaks of Mamre' (plural construct, אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא) — is morphologically and topographically distinct from the elon Moreh (singular construct) of Gen 12:6. The Moreh site is at Shechem in the central hill country and names a tree-by-its-oracle-function (moreh = teacher / oracle-giver); the Mamre site is near Hebron in the southern hill country and names a tree-grove by its human owner — Mamre the Amorite, named explicitly at Gen 14:13 and 14:24 as one of Abram's three Amorite allies in the war of the four kings. // The two sacred-tree sites are parallels but not the same entry. Both fit the patriarchal sacred-tree pattern that also runs through Gen 35:4 (Shechem oak), Gen 35:8 (Allon Bakhut at Bethel), Josh 24:26 (Shechem sanctuary oak), Judg 6:11 (Ophrah oak), and Judg 9:6, 37 (Shechem diviners' oak); both are theophany-and-altar locations. But the proper names track different historical persons / places, and the elon-moreh glossary entry from 12:6 does not extend to here. // The Mamre site is forward-loaded in the narrative. Abram will dwell there during the war of the four kings (Gen 14:13); the three-visitors theophany of Gen 18:1 (va-yera elav YHWH b-elonei Mamre) happens here; the cave of Machpelah purchased by Abraham as the patriarchal-matriarchal burial site (Gen 23:17, 19) is in the field of Ephron near Mamre; the burial-narratives of Sarah (23:19), Abraham (25:9), Isaac and Rebekah (49:31), and Jacob (50:13) all return to Mamre. The chapter's closing notice quietly seeds the geographical anchor of the rest of the cycle. // On the botanical question (oak vs. terebinth): BDB, HALOT, and the Hebrew lexica do not securely distinguish elon (אֵלוֹן) and elah (אֵלָה); LXX renders δρῦς (oak); ASV and NRSV translate 'oaks', NJPS 'terebinths'. The WoH translation follows ASV with 'oaks of Mamre', consistent with the 12:6 'oak of Moreh' rendering. The species precision is not load-bearing.

Chapter 14

Genesis 14 — The war of the four kings, Melchizedek, and Abram's refusal of the spoils

1
And it came to pass in the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Kedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goyim,
And it came to pass in the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim,
וַיְהִ֗י בִּימֵי֙ אַמְרָפֶ֣ל מֶֽלֶךְ־שִׁנְעָ֔ר אַרְי֖וֹךְ מֶ֣לֶךְ אֶלָּסָ֑ר כְּדָרְלָעֹ֙מֶר֙ מֶ֣לֶךְ עֵילָ֔ם וְתִדְעָ֖ל מֶ֥לֶךְ גּוֹיִֽם׃
2
that they made war with Bera king of Sodom, and with Birsha king of Amorah, Shinab king of Admah, and Shemeber king of Tzeboim, and the king of Bela (the same is Tsoar).
that they made war with Bera king of Sodom, and with Birsha king of Gomorrah, Shinab king of Admah, and Shemeber king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (the same is Zoar).
עָשׂ֣וּ מִלְחָמָ֗ה אֶת־בֶּ֙רַע֙ מֶ֣לֶךְ סְדֹ֔ם וְאֶת־בִּרְשַׁ֖ע מֶ֣לֶךְ עֲמֹרָ֑ה שִׁנְאָ֣ב ׀ מֶ֣לֶךְ אַדְמָ֗ה וְשֶׁמְאֵ֙בֶר֙ מֶ֣לֶךְ צביים [צְבוֹיִ֔ים] וּמֶ֥לֶךְ בֶּ֖לַע הִיא־צֹֽעַר׃
3
All these joined together at the Valley of Siddim (the same is the Salt Sea).
All these joined together in the vale of Siddim (the same is the Salt Sea).
כָּל־אֵ֙לֶּה֙ חָֽבְר֔וּ אֶל־עֵ֖מֶק הַשִּׂדִּ֑ים ה֖וּא יָ֥ם הַמֶּֽלַח׃
4
Twelve years they served Kedorlaomer, and in the thirteenth year they rebelled.
Twelve years they served Chedorlaomer, and in the thirteenth year they rebelled.
שְׁתֵּ֤ים עֶשְׂרֵה֙ שָׁנָ֔ה עָבְד֖וּ אֶת־כְּדָרְלָעֹ֑מֶר וּשְׁלֹשׁ־עֶשְׂרֵ֥ה שָׁנָ֖ה מָרָֽדוּ׃
5
And in the fourteenth year Kedorlaomer came, and the kings who were with him, and struck the Refa'im in Ashteroth-karnaim, and the Zuzim in Ham, and the Emim in Shaveh-kiryathaim,
And in the fourteenth year came Chedorlaomer, and the kings that were with him, and smote the Rephaim in Ashteroth-karnaim, and the Zuzim in Ham, and the Emim in Shaveh-kiriathaim,
וּבְאַרְבַּע֩ עֶשְׂרֵ֨ה שָׁנָ֜ה בָּ֣א כְדָרְלָעֹ֗מֶר וְהַמְּלָכִים֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר אִתּ֔וֹ וַיַּכּ֤וּ אֶת־רְפָאִים֙ בְּעַשְׁתְּרֹ֣ת קַרְנַ֔יִם וְאֶת־הַזּוּזִ֖ים בְּהָ֑ם וְאֵת֙ הָֽאֵימִ֔ים בְּשָׁוֵ֖ה קִרְיָתָֽיִם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

The list of pre-Israelite peoples in this verse stands at one end of a corpus-wide giant-peoples chain. The three names — Refa'im (רְפָאִים) in Ashteroth-Karnaim, Zuzim (הַזּוּזִים) in Ham, Emim (הָאֵימִים) in Shaveh-Kiryathaim — recur with explicit giant-equation in Deuteronomy. Deut 2:10-11 identifies the Emim as 'a great people, numerous and tall as the Anaqim' and equates them with the Refa'im. Deut 2:20-21 identifies the Trans-Jordanian Zuzim (under the Ammonite name Zamzumim) as 'a great people, numerous and tall as the Anaqim, counted as Refa'im'. Deut 3:11 names Og king of Bashan as 'the last of the Refa'im', with his iron bedstead nine cubits long (roughly 13.5 feet). The Deuteronomist's framing is direct: these are a pre-Israelite, giant-stature population.

The chain runs further. Num 13:33 — the spies' report from Canaan — explicitly identifies the bnei Anaq with the Nefilim of Genesis 6:1-4: 'and there we saw the Nephilim, the sons of Anaq, from the Nephilim'. The Hebrew Bible's own narrative connective tissue therefore links Gen 14:5 → Num 13:33 → Deut 2-3 → back to the antediluvian Nephilim of Gen 6.

The modern critical commentaries (Westermann, Genesis 12-36, 1985, pp. 195-196; Sarna, JPS Genesis, 1989, p. 105; Wenham, WBC Genesis 1-15, 1987, pp. 311-312; Hamilton, NICOT Genesis 1-17, 1990, p. 403) treat the giant-stature reading as the mainstream understanding of the Refa'im in the patriarchal narratives; a second philological track in BDB and HALOT registers a homonymous Refa'im meaning 'shades / dead' (Isa 14:9, 26:14; Ps 88:11) with Ugaritic rpʾum as the cognate background. The two senses are distinguished here; the Gen 14:5 use is the giant-peoples sense.

The WoH translation preserves the bare proper-name transcriptions ('Refa'im', 'Zuzim', 'Emim'), matching ASV, without parenthetical 'giants' glosses. The cross-corpus chain lives in the glossary apparatus.

The place-names are themselves informative: Ashteroth-Karnaim is the two-horned Astarte-cult site in Bashan; Shaveh-Kiryathaim is a Moabite-region site (cf. Num 32:37, Josh 13:19); Ham is the obscure third site, likely in central Trans-Jordan.

6
and the Horites in their Mount Seir, as far as El-paran, which is by the wilderness.
and the Horites in their mount Seir, unto Elparan, which is by the wilderness.
וְאֶת־הַחֹרִ֖י בְּהַרְרָ֣ם שֵׂעִ֑יר עַ֚ד אֵ֣יל פָּארָ֔ן אֲשֶׁ֖ר עַל־הַמִּדְבָּֽר׃
7
And they turned back and came to En-mishpat (the same is Kadesh), and struck all the country of the Amalekites, and also the Amorite who dwelt in Hazazon-tamar.
And they returned, and came to En-mishpat (the same is Kadesh), and smote all the country of the Amalekites, and also the Amorites, that dwelt in Hazazon-tamar.
וַ֠יָּשֻׁבוּ וַיָּבֹ֜אוּ אֶל־עֵ֤ין מִשְׁפָּט֙ הִ֣וא קָדֵ֔שׁ וַיַּכּ֕וּ אֶֽת־כָּל־שְׂדֵ֖ה הָעֲמָלֵקִ֑י וְגַם֙ אֶת־הָ֣אֱמֹרִ֔י הַיֹּשֵׁ֖ב בְּחַֽצְצֹ֥ן תָּמָֽר׃
8
And the king of Sodom went out, and the king of Amorah, and the king of Admah, and the king of Tzeboim, and the king of Bela (the same is Tsoar); and they set the battle in array against them in the Valley of Siddim;
And there went out the king of Sodom, and the king of Gomorrah, and the king of Admah, and the king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (the same is Zoar); and they set the battle in array against them in the vale of Siddim;
וַיֵּצֵ֨א מֶֽלֶךְ־סְדֹ֜ם וּמֶ֣לֶךְ עֲמֹרָ֗ה וּמֶ֤לֶךְ אַדְמָה֙ וּמֶ֣לֶךְ צביים [צְבוֹיִ֔ם] וּמֶ֥לֶךְ בֶּ֖לַע הִוא־צֹ֑עַר וַיַּֽעַרְכ֤וּ אִתָּם֙ מִלְחָמָ֔ה בְּעֵ֖מֶק הַשִּׂדִּֽים׃
9
against Kedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goyim, and Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar — four kings against the five.
against Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim, and Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar; four kings against the five.
אֵ֣ת כְּדָרְלָעֹ֜מֶר מֶ֣לֶךְ עֵילָ֗ם וְתִדְעָל֙ מֶ֣לֶךְ גּוֹיִ֔ם וְאַמְרָפֶל֙ מֶ֣לֶךְ שִׁנְעָ֔ר וְאַרְי֖וֹךְ מֶ֣לֶךְ אֶלָּסָ֑ר אַרְבָּעָ֥ה מְלָכִ֖ים אֶת־הַחֲמִשָּֽׁה׃
10
Now the Valley of Siddim was full of bitumen pits; and the kings of Sodom and Amorah fled, and they fell there, and those who remained fled to the mountain.
Now the vale of Siddim was full of slime pits; and the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, and they fell there, and they that remained fled to the mountain.
וְעֵ֣מֶק הַשִׂדִּ֗ים בֶּֽאֱרֹ֤ת בֶּאֱרֹת֙ חֵמָ֔ר וַיָּנֻ֛סוּ מֶֽלֶךְ־סְדֹ֥ם וַעֲמֹרָ֖ה וַיִּפְּלוּ־שָׁ֑מָּה וְהַנִּשְׁאָרִ֖ים הֶ֥רָה נָּֽסוּ׃
11
And they took all the goods of Sodom and Amorah, and all their food, and went their way.
And they took all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah, and all their victuals, and went their way.
וַ֠יִּקְחוּ אֶת־כָּל־רְכֻ֨שׁ סְדֹ֧ם וַעֲמֹרָ֛ה וְאֶת־כָּל־אָכְלָ֖ם וַיֵּלֵֽכוּ׃
12
And they took Lot, Abram's brother's son, who dwelt in Sodom, and his goods, and departed.
And they took Lot, Abram`s brother`s son, who dwelt in Sodom, and his goods, and departed.
וַיִּקְח֨וּ אֶת־ל֧וֹט וְאֶת־רְכֻשׁ֛וֹ בֶּן־אֲחִ֥י אַבְרָ֖ם וַיֵּלֵ֑כוּ וְה֥וּא יֹשֵׁ֖ב בִּסְדֹֽם׃
13
And the fugitive came and told Abram the Hebrew. Now he dwelt by the oaks of Mamre the Amorite, brother of Eshkol and brother of Aner; and these were confederate with Abram.
And there came one that had escaped, and told Abram the Hebrew: now he dwelt by the oaks of Mamre, the Amorite, brother of Eshcol, and brother of Aner; and these were confederate with Abram.
וַיָּבֹא֙ הַפָּלִ֔יט וַיַּגֵּ֖ד לְאַבְרָ֣ם הָעִבְרִ֑י וְהוּא֩ שֹׁכֵ֨ן בְּאֵֽלֹנֵ֜י מַמְרֵ֣א הָאֱמֹרִ֗י אֲחִ֤י אֶשְׁכֹּל֙ וַאֲחִ֣י עָנֵ֔ר וְהֵ֖ם בַּעֲלֵ֥י בְרִית־אַבְרָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

This is the first occurrence of the gentilic Ivri — 'Hebrew' — in the Hebrew Bible. Avram ha-Ivri (אַבְרָם הָעִבְרִי) is a load-bearing identification, and the etymology of the term is one of the long-standing disputes in modern critical scholarship. Three readings circulate, none of them settled.

The eponymous-ancestor reading derives Ivri from Eber (עֵבֶר), the great-grandson of Shem. The Genesis editor's own genealogical apparatus supports this derivation: Gen 10:21 singles out Shem as 'father of all the sons of Eber', and Gen 10:24-25 + 11:14-17 trace the line Shem → Arpachshad → Shelah → Eber → ... → Terah → Abram. The gentilic Ivri would form from Eber by the regular qatil-pattern (Sarna, JPS Genesis, 1989, p. 107; Cassuto, From Noah to Abraham, 1964, p. 201).

The habiru / apiru social-class reading connects Ivri to the Akkadian ḫabiru (variant ʿapiru) attested across the second-millennium Near Eastern corpus — the Amarna letters (especially Abdi-Heba's Jerusalem letters EA 285-290), Mari, Nuzi, Hittite treaties, and Egyptian administrative texts. The term designates a marginal social class of stateless, displaced persons — brigands, mercenaries, hired labor, refugees. Greenberg (The Hab/piru, 1955), Bottéro (Le problème des Habiru, 1954), and Na'aman (JNES 1986) work the philological-historical case. On this reading, 'Abram the Ivri' would be a social designation — Abram the marginal-outsider — consistent with the patriarchal nomadic-stateless context.

The eber / Trans-Euphrates reading derives Ivri from eber (עֵבֶר, 'beyond / across'), pointing to Abram's Trans-Euphratine origin. Joshua 24:2-3 explicitly names the patriarchal origin b-ever ha-nahar (across the River). The LXX at Gen 14:13 reads περάτης ('one-from-across'), explicitly preserving this etymology. Speiser (Anchor Bible Genesis, 1964, pp. 102-103) and Westermann (Genesis 12-36, 1985, pp. 195-196) discuss it.

The three readings are not strictly incompatible; Greenberg and Rainey (1995) propose a layered diachrony in which an inherited social-class term (habiru) was eponymized through the genealogical figure of Eber and accommodated to the geographical eber reading.

The corpus distribution of Ivri is itself instructive. Within Genesis, the term recurs in the Joseph narrative (39:14, 39:17, 40:15, 41:12, 43:32) and is consistently used by foreigners about Israelites, or by Israelites to foreigners — never as Israelites' self-designation among themselves. The pattern persists into Exodus. The gentilic functions as an outsider's designation, which is consistent with the habiru social-class background.

The WoH translation renders 'the Hebrew', matching ASV / NRSV / NJPS consensus, without committing the English to any one of the three etymologies.

14
And when Abram heard that his brother was taken captive, he led forth his trained men, born in his house, three hundred and eighteen, and pursued as far as Dan.
And when Abram heard that his brother was taken captive, he led forth his trained men, born in his house, three hundred and eighteen, and pursued as far as Dan.
וַיִּשְׁמַ֣ע אַבְרָ֔ם כִּ֥י נִשְׁבָּ֖ה אָחִ֑יו וַיָּ֨רֶק אֶת־חֲנִיכָ֜יו יְלִידֵ֣י בֵית֗וֹ שְׁמֹנָ֤ה עָשָׂר֙ וּשְׁלֹ֣שׁ מֵא֔וֹת וַיִּרְדֹּ֖ף עַד־דָּֽן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

The noun chanikh (חָנִיךְ) is a hapax legomenon — it occurs only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. The verbal root ch-n-k is well-attested with the basic semantic range dedicate / inaugurate / train-up (Prov 22:6 chanokh la-na'ar, 'train up a child'; Deut 20:5 chanakh, 'dedicate' a house; the noun chanukkah used of altar-dedication at Num 7:10-11, the Second Temple dedication at Ezra 6:16, and post-biblically the Hasmonean rededication). But the precise sense of the noun chanikh — applied here to Abram's household militia — cannot be settled from internal Hebrew evidence alone.

The Egyptian ḥnk parallel is the strongest comparative anchor. Middle Kingdom Egyptian execration texts and administrative documents use ḥnk of retainers / dependents / liegemen of an Asiatic chieftain — armed household-bondsmen owed to a tribal-patriarchal figure. Albright (1949), Lambdin (1953), and the modern commentaries (Sarna 1989, p. 108; Wenham 1987, p. 313; Hamilton 1990, p. 404) cite the Egyptian parallel. On this reading, chanikhav y'lidei beito ('his chanikhs, born in his house') describes armed household-retainers — domestically-born, not purchased — drawn from Abram's extended household. NJPS and Alter both read 'retainers'; ASV / NRSV / KJV read 'trained men'.

The number 318 has attracted a parallel post-biblical interpretive tradition: the gematria-Eliezer reading. The Talmudic homily at b. Nedarim 32a (also b. Bava Batra 16a) and Bereshit Rabbah 43:2 note that the numerical value of Eliezer (אֱלִיעֶזֶר) — Abraham's chief servant named at Gen 15:2 — sums to 318: aleph(1) + lamed(30) + yod(10) + ayin(70) + zayin(7) + resh(200). The rabbinic reading homiletically equates the 318 with Eliezer himself. This is DOWNSTREAM-RECEPTION: post-biblical Tannaitic / Amoraic exegesis, not the literal sense of the verse.

The literal sense is the plain-sense reading: 318 trained members of Abram's household militia, drawn from the y-lidei beito — those domestically born, distinct from purchased slaves (cf. the formal distinction at Gen 17:12-13, 17:23, 17:27). The number is large enough to constitute a credible raiding party against four kings' rear-guard and small enough to be plausibly one patriarch's retinue.

The WoH translation renders 'his trained men, born in his house, three hundred and eighteen', matching ASV.

15
And he divided himself against them by night, he and his servants, and struck them, and pursued them as far as Hobah, which is on the left hand of Damascus.
And he divided himself against them by night, he and his servants, and smote them, and pursued them unto Hobah, which is on the left hand of Damascus.
וַיֵּחָלֵ֨ק עֲלֵיהֶ֧ם ׀ לַ֛יְלָה ה֥וּא וַעֲבָדָ֖יו וַיַּכֵּ֑ם וַֽיִּרְדְּפֵם֙ עַד־חוֹבָ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר מִשְּׂמֹ֖אל לְדַמָּֽשֶׂק׃
16
And he brought back all the goods, and also brought back his brother Lot and his goods, and the women also, and the people.
And he brought back all the goods, and also brought back his brother Lot, and his goods, and the women also, and the people.
וַיָּ֕שֶׁב אֵ֖ת כָּל־הָרְכֻ֑שׁ וְגַם֩ אֶת־ל֨וֹט אָחִ֤יו וּרְכֻשׁוֹ֙ הֵשִׁ֔יב וְגַ֥ם אֶת־הַנָּשִׁ֖ים וְאֶת־הָעָֽם׃
17
And the king of Sodom went out to meet him, after his return from the smiting of Kedorlaomer and the kings who were with him, at the Valley of Shaveh (the same is the King's Valley).
And the king of Sodom went out to meet him, after his return from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer and the kings that were with him, at the vale of Shaveh (the same is the King`s Vale).
וַיֵּצֵ֣א מֶֽלֶךְ־סְדֹם֮ לִקְרָאתוֹ֒ אַחֲרֵ֣י שׁוּב֗וֹ מֵֽהַכּוֹת֙ אֶת־כְּדָרלָעֹ֔מֶר וְאֶת־הַמְּלָכִ֖ים אֲשֶׁ֣ר אִתּ֑וֹ אֶל־עֵ֣מֶק שָׁוֵ֔ה ה֖וּא עֵ֥מֶק הַמֶּֽלֶךְ׃
18
And Melchizedek king of Shalem brought out bread and wine; and he was priest of El Elyon.
And Melchizedek king of Salem brought forth bread and wine: and he was priest of God Most High.
וּמַלְכִּי־צֶ֙דֶק֙ מֶ֣לֶךְ שָׁלֵ֔ם הוֹצִ֖יא לֶ֣חֶם וָיָ֑יִן וְה֥וּא כֹהֵ֖ן לְאֵ֥ל עֶלְיֽוֹן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

This verse is the chapter's keystone — the most theologically and historically loaded eight Hebrew words in the Abraham cycle. Four lexical decisions converge here, and each carries its own scholarly apparatus.

Malki-Tzedek (מַלְכִּי־צֶדֶק) is a theophoric compound: malki (1cs-suffixed construct, 'my king is') + Tzedek (a divine name attested across West-Semitic). Tzedek appears at Ugarit as the deity paired with Mishor (Tzedek and Mishor, 'righteousness' and 'uprightness', members of the Ugaritic divine assembly KTU 1.123); in Phoenician material (Philo of Byblos citing Sanchuniathon names Sydyk as the father of the Dioscuri); and crucially in pre-Israelite Jerusalem itself, in the Jebusite king-name Adoni-Tzedek (Josh 10:1, 10:3 — my lord is Tzedek). The Adoni-Tzedek parallel is decisive: theophoric Jerusalem king-names compounded with Tzedek are attested at both the patriarchal stratum (Melchizedek) and the conquest stratum (Adoni-Tzedek), suggesting Tzedek was the local Jerusalem high-god name in the pre-Israelite period. Westermann (1985, pp. 203-205), Sarna (1989, p. 109), Wenham (1987, p. 316), Hamilton (1990, p. 409), and Cross (Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic, 1973, pp. 207-209) all read the name theophorically. Hebrews 7:2 famously offers the alternative honorific reading — king of righteousness — which dominated the Christian and traditional Jewish reception.

Melekh Shalem (מֶלֶךְ שָׁלֵם) is identified with Jerusalem in the canonical tradition. The Hebrew Bible itself anchors the identification: Ps 76:3 explicitly equates Shalem with Zion — his shelter was in Shalem, and his dwelling-place in Zion. The Qumran 1QapGen (Genesis Apocryphon, col. 22) glosses l'Shalem hi Yerushalem, 'Shalem which is Jerusalem'. Josephus, Targum Onkelos, and Hebrews 7:1-2 all assume the identification. The pre-Israelite city-name Urusalim is attested in the Egyptian Execration Texts (19th-18th c. BCE) and the Amarna letters (Abdi-Heba's correspondence, 14th c. BCE), with the Shalim-element possibly preserving the West-Semitic dusk-deity paired with Shahar in the Ugaritic Shaḥar and Shalim text (KTU 1.23). A minority modern view treats Shalem as an independent site near Shechem, but the Ps 76:3 parallelism is decisive against it.

Kohen (כֹּהֵן) is the first occurrence of priest in the Hebrew Bible. Three structural facts: the inaugural priest is non-Israelite, pre-Aaronic and pre-Sinaitic (the Aaronic order is not established until Exod 28-29 / Lev 8), and king-priest in a single office. The lexeme has West-Semitic cognates (Phoenician khn, Aramaic kahna, Arabic kāhin with semantic specialization to 'seer'); Melchizedek's priesthood is intelligible in the West-Semitic religious context, not a distinctively Israelite institution. The collocation kohen l-El Elyon — priest of El Elyon, not of YHWH — preserves the pre-Israelite character of the figure; the YHWH-identification happens only at v 22, in Abram's mouth, by apposition.

El Elyon (אֵל עֶלְיוֹן) is the divine name introduced here and is the chapter's most discussed lexical site. The compound joins El — the shared West-Semitic high-god name (Ugaritic ʾIl, the chief of the divine assembly, the bull-El, the father of years) — with the epithet Elyon ('highest, uppermost'). Elyon is itself attested as an independent divine name in West-Semitic material: Philo of Byblos cites Sanchuniathon naming Elioun / Hypsistos as a distinct deity, the father of Ouranos and Ge in the Phoenician theogony. The patriarchal-religion reconstruction developed by Alt (The God of the Fathers, 1929) and Cross (1973, pp. 13-75) reads the El-compounds across the patriarchal narratives — El Shaddai, El Olam, El Bethel, El Elohei Yisra'el, El Elyon — as preserving an earlier stratum of patriarchal religion in which the patriarchs worshipped El at named sanctuary-sites, with the YHWH-name harmonization a later editorial layer. The reading is mainstream modern critical scholarship.

On the Deut 32:8 divine-council material (the most lens-relevant Hebrew Bible passage on Elyon): the verse reads when Elyon allotted the nations as their inheritance... he established the borders of the peoples according to the number of the [variant: sons of Israel / sons of Elohim]. The MT reads bnei Yisra'el; the Qumran 4QDeut^j and 4QDeut^q read bnei Elohim (sons of Elohim — divine beings, the divine council); LXX reads aggelōn theou (angels of God), reflecting an underlying bnei Elohim. The DSS / LXX reading is the modern scholarly consensus as the original (Skehan, BASOR 1959; Tov, Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible, 3rd ed. 2012; Tigay, JPS Deuteronomy, 1996, Excursus 31; NRSV and NJPS both adopt it). The verse recovers a divine-council picture: Elyon is the high-god who allots the peoples to the divine sons, with YHWH receiving Jacob/Israel as his portion (v 9). This places Elyon and YHWH in a hierarchical-distinct relation in one stratum of the corpus — and is precisely the framework the Gen 14:22 appositional construction works to harmonize.

The lens-relevant material — the West-Semitic-pantheon framework, the Deut 32:8 divine-council reading, the El-YHWH harmonization — is mainstream modern critical scholarship (Cross 1973, Smith The Origins of Biblical Monotheism 2001), not a Wheel-of-Heaven-distinctive claim. The WoH lens reads through this scholarly framework; the framework itself supplies the layered-divine-council picture.

DOWNSTREAM-RECEPTION (flagged, not preempted): Two major post-biblical reception streams attach to vv 18-20.

(1) The Qumran 11QMelchizedek (11Q13), ca. mid-1st-century BCE, makes Melchizedek a heavenly Elohim-figure / divine-council member executing eschatological judgment. The scroll applies Ps 82 — Elohim stands in the divine assembly, among the elohim he gives judgment — to Melchizedek, and reads the Isaiah 61 jubilee-release as the eschatological release Melchizedek will execute. The Melchizedek of 11Q13 is a quasi-angelic redeemer-judge, not a Canaanite king of Salem. Horton (The Melchizedek Tradition, 1976), Kobelski (Melchizedek and Melchireša', 1981), and Mason (You Are a Priest Forever, 2008) treat the scroll in detail.

(2) The Christian Hebrews 5-7 reception builds an entire Christology of pre-Aaronic, pre-Levitical, eternal priesthood on the silence of Genesis about Melchizedek's lineage and death. Heb 7:3 characterizes him as apatōr, amētōr, agenealogētos — without father, without mother, without genealogy — turning the narrative silence into a typological warrant for the eternal-priesthood Christological claim about Jesus.

Both receptions post-date the Hebrew text by centuries. Neither is permitted to color the translated English; both are scholarly-historical facts about how this verse was later read.

The WoH translation renders 'And Melchizedek king of Shalem brought out bread and wine; and he was priest of El Elyon', matching ASV at every divergence point except the preservation of the Hebrew place-name 'Shalem' (rather than the Vulgate-routed 'Salem') and the bare transliterated divine name 'El Elyon' (rather than 'God Most High'). The transliterations preserve the West-Semitic character of the lexicon; the apparatus carries the rest.

19
And he blessed him and said, "Blessed be Abram of El Elyon, creator of heaven and earth;
And he blessed him, and said, Blessed be Abram of God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth:
וַֽיְבָרְכֵ֖הוּ וַיֹּאמַ֑ר בָּר֤וּךְ אַבְרָם֙ לְאֵ֣ל עֶלְי֔וֹן קֹנֵ֖ה שָׁמַ֥יִם וָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 19

The epithet koneh shamayim va-aretz is an inherited West-Semitic high-god title — and its presence here is one of the most directly attested cross-Semitic philological links in the Hebrew Bible.

The Hebrew verb-root q-n-h has a lexical duality: in dominant Biblical Hebrew usage it means acquire / possess / buy (Gen 4:1, 25:10, Exod 21:2, and the noun miqneh); in an older, more poetic register it means create / produce / beget. The create-sense is preserved at Deut 32:6 halo hu avikha qanekha ('is he not your father who created you'), at Ps 139:13 ki atah qanita khilyotai ('for you created my inward parts' — a womb-formation context), and at Prov 8:22 YHWH qanani reshit darko (the personified Wisdom's origin — and the verse over which the entire 4th-century Arian-Athanasian Christological dispute turned).

The cross-Semitic parallels are decisive for the create-sense in divine-epithet contexts. The Ugaritic high-god El bears the epithet qny ʾrṣ — creator of the earth. The Phoenician Karatepe inscription (KAI 26, 8th c. BCE) contains the divine-name compound ʾl qn ʾrṣ — El, creator of the earth — the closest comparative cognate to the Hebrew Gen 14 formula (identical form, same divine-name pairing). The Hittite-Hurrian Elkunirsa myth preserves the same divine epithet as a personal name (Elkunirsa = ʾEl-qōnē-ʾaraṣ). The 2nd-millennium West-Semitic qny ʾrṣ epithet is well-attested as a stable, transmissible high-god title attached to El, and in every attested cross-Semitic instance the meaning is unambiguously create, not possess.

Westermann (1985, p. 204), Sarna (1989, p. 109), Wenham (1987, p. 317), Hamilton (1990, p. 410), Cross (1973, pp. 15-18, 50-52), and Smith (The Origins of Biblical Monotheism, 2001) all treat the epithet as inherited West-Semitic. The modern English translation tradition splits on which Hebrew sense — possess or create — best preserves the inherited West-Semitic meaning: ASV / KJV / RSV read 'possessor'; NRSV / NJPS / NIV / Alter / Speiser read 'maker / creator'.

The WoH translation reads 'creator of heaven and earth', departing from ASV in favor of the modern critical consensus. The choice rests on the cross-Semitic epithet evidence: in the specific El qn ʾrṣ high-god-epithet context that Gen 14:19, 22 inherits, the West-Semitic comparators preserve only the create-sense. Rendering 'creator' makes the inherited West-Semitic epithet-pattern visible in English and matches NJPS / NRSV. The dominant Biblical Hebrew possess-sense of q-n-h is preserved in the glossary apparatus for the lexical layer.

20
and blessed be El Elyon, who has delivered your enemies into your hand." And he gave him a tithe of all.
and blessed be God Most High, who hath delivered thine enemies into thy hand. And he gave him a tenth of all.
וּבָרוּךְ֙ אֵ֣ל עֶלְי֔וֹן אֲשֶׁר־מִגֵּ֥ן צָרֶ֖יךָ בְּיָדֶ֑ךָ וַיִּתֶּן־ל֥וֹ מַעֲשֵׂ֖ר מִכֹּֽל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

Ma'aser (מַעֲשֵׂר) — this is the first occurrence of tithe in the Hebrew Bible. The first-occurrence locus is structurally load-bearing: the institution of the tithe is established at a pre-Sinai, pre-Mosaic, pre-Aaronic moment, by a patriarch giving a tenth of war-spoils to a non-Israelite priest of El Elyon.

The grammatical direction of the giving — va-yiten lo ma'aser mi-kol, and he gave him a tithe of all — is technically ambiguous about who gives to whom. The consensus reading (Hebrews 7:2 makes it explicit; the patristic, rabbinic, and modern critical traditions concur) is that Abram gives the tithe to Melchizedek, matching the ancient Near Eastern priest-king/petitioner pattern: Melchizedek blesses Abram (vv 19-20a) and Abram responds with a tithe (v 20b). The WoH translation follows the consensus reading.

The developed tithe-legislation is post-Sinai. Lev 27:30-33 establishes the holiness-code tithe; Num 18:21-32 gives the Levitical tithe in lieu of land; Deut 12:6, 11, 17 and Deut 14:22-29 develop the ma'aser sheni (second tithe) and the festival-meal tithe; Deut 26:12 institutes the triennial tithe for the Levite, sojourner, orphan, and widow. The Genesis 14:20 instance stands as the aetiological precedent for all of this.

Gen 28:22 — Jacob's tithe at Bethel is the structural parallel: after the Bethel theophany, Jacob vows v-kol asher titen-li aser a-asrenu lakh ('and of all that you give me, I will surely give the tenth to you'). The two pre-Sinai tithe-instances — Abram at Gen 14:20 (to Melchizedek-El-Elyon) and Jacob at Gen 28:22 (to YHWH at Bethel) — together establish the patriarchal-stratum tithe institution (Sarna 1989, pp. 109, 200; Wenham 1987, pp. 317-318).

The ANE comparative context is wide: Mesopotamian ešrû / ešretu tithes from the third millennium onward, Ugaritic mʿšr in the economic texts, Egyptian temple-tithes, and the Phoenician tithe-tariffs (KAI 69, the Marseilles Tariff). The patriarchal-narrative tithe-institution is intelligible in this West-Semitic / ANE economic context.

DOWNSTREAM-RECEPTION (flagged, not preempted): Hebrews 7:1-10 builds an entire Christological priesthood-argument on the pre-Aaronic locus of Gen 14:20 — to him Abraham apportioned a tenth of everything... one might even say that Levi himself, who receives tithes, paid tithes through Abraham, for he was still in the loins of his ancestor (Heb 7:9-10). The reading is post-biblical Christian exegesis; the structural fact it leans on (Abram tithed to a non-Aaronic priest before the Aaronic priesthood existed) is direct.

21
And the king of Sodom said to Abram, "Give me the persons, and take the goods for yourself."
And the king of Sodom said unto Abram, Give me the persons, and take the goods to thyself.
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר מֶֽלֶךְ־סְדֹ֖ם אֶל־אַבְרָ֑ם תֶּן־לִ֣י הַנֶּ֔פֶשׁ וְהָרְכֻ֖שׁ קַֽח־לָֽךְ׃
22
And Abram said to the king of Sodom, "I have lifted up my hand to YHWH, El Elyon, creator of heaven and earth,
And Abram said to the king of Sodom, I have lifted up my hand unto Jehovah, God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth,
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר אַבְרָ֖ם אֶל־מֶ֣לֶךְ סְדֹ֑ם הֲרִימֹ֨תִי יָדִ֤י אֶל־יְהוָה֙ אֵ֣ל עֶלְי֔וֹן קֹנֵ֖ה שָׁמַ֥יִם וָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

This is the chapter's keystone theological move: Abram's oath identifies YHWH with El Elyon by apposition. Harimoti yadi el-YHWH El Elyon koneh shamayim va-aretz — 'I have lifted my hand to YHWH, El Elyon, creator of heaven and earth'. The Hebrew construction places YHWH (the Abrahamic divine name) and El Elyon (the Melchizedek-tradition divine name from vv 18-20) in immediate apposition — no conjunction, no construct relation, no waw — and the natural Hebrew reading is YHWH [who is] El Elyon, creator of heaven and earth.

The appositional reading is uncontested in the versions. LXX renders kyrion ton theon ton hypsiston ('the Lord, the Most High God'), preserving the apposition. Samaritan Pentateuch reads the apposition. Targum Onkelos has Memra de-YHWH El Elyon. Vulgate reads ad Dominum Deum excelsum. The reading is consensus.

The textual variant. A small minority of witnesses (a Samaritan fragment, some Peshitta witnesses) omits YHWH and reads only El Elyon koneh shamayim va-aretz. Westermann (1985, p. 208) reads this as preserving the older pre-redactional form of the verse, with the YHWH element a later Yahwistic harmonizing gloss. The reading is plausible source-critical analysis consistent with the broader patriarchal-El-religion reconstruction (Alt 1929, Cross 1973). The MT reading with YHWH is what the canonical text preserves; the variant is a redactional shadow flagged in the glossary.

The harmonization-pattern across Genesis. The Gen 14:22 move is one instance of a broader Genesis editorial pattern. Exod 6:2-3 is the explicit programmatic harmonization: I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known to them. The Exodus verse states the harmonization-thesis: the patriarchal divine name was El Shaddai, the Mosaic revelation is YHWH, and these are the same deity. The Gen 14:22 appositional construction is the Genesis-narrative-stratum version of the same move, applied to the El Elyon of the Melchizedek tradition. The pattern recurs at the other patriarchal-sanctuary El-compounds: El Olam at Beer-Sheba (Gen 21:33: va-yiqra sham b-shem YHWH El Olam — exactly the same appositional construction); El Bethel (Gen 31:13, 35:7); El Elohei Yisra'el (Gen 33:20). Each patriarchal sanctuary-site preserves a distinctive El-name, and the Genesis editor harmonizes each with YHWH.

Cross (1973), Smith (2001), Albertz (A History of Israelite Religion in the Old Testament Period, 1994), and Mark Smith (The Early History of God, 2nd ed. 2002) treat the harmonization-pattern in detail. The reading is mainstream modern critical scholarship: the Genesis editor inherits a patriarchal El-religion (locally diversified across sanctuary-sites) and works systematically to identify the patriarchs' El-names with the canonical YHWH-name.

The lens-relevance of the v 22 move is direct: the Genesis editor is performing an explicit syncretism, joining the El Elyon of the Canaanite-Melchizedek tradition to the YHWH of the Abrahamic-Yahwistic tradition. What the reader can see, in plain Hebrew syntax, is the editorial seam. The Wheel of Heaven lens is interested in this seam, but the lens does not need to extend the scholarly reading; it can simply read what the modern critical commentaries already read.

The WoH translation renders 'YHWH, El Elyon, creator of heaven and earth' with comma-apposition. The comma preserves the Hebrew construction (no conjunctive between the names) while making the appositional reading unambiguous in English; 'creator' for koneh departs from ASV in favor of the modern critical consensus (see v 19 commentary for the philological rationale).

23
that I will not take a thread nor a sandal-strap nor anything that is yours, lest you should say, 'I have made Abram rich';
that I will not take a thread nor a shoe-latchet nor aught that is thine, lest thou shouldest say, I have made Abram rich:
אִם־מִחוּט֙ וְעַ֣ד שְׂרֽוֹךְ־נַ֔עַל וְאִם־אֶקַּ֖ח מִכָּל־אֲשֶׁר־לָ֑ךְ וְלֹ֣א תֹאמַ֔ר אֲנִ֖י הֶעֱשַׁ֥רְתִּי אֶת־אַבְרָֽם׃
24
save only that which the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men who went with me — Aner, Eshkol, and Mamre — let them take their portion.
save only that which the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men that went with me, Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre. Let them take their portion.
בִּלְעָדַ֗י רַ֚ק אֲשֶׁ֣ר אָֽכְל֣וּ הַנְּעָרִ֔ים וְחֵ֙לֶק֙ הָֽאֲנָשִׁ֔ים אֲשֶׁ֥ר הָלְכ֖וּ אִתִּ֑י עָנֵר֙ אֶשְׁכֹּ֣ל וּמַמְרֵ֔א הֵ֖ם יִקְח֥וּ חֶלְקָֽם׃ (ס)
Chapter 15

Genesis 15 — The covenant between the pieces and the promise of the land

1
After these things the word of YHWH came to Abram in a vision, saying: Fear not, Abram; I am your shield, your reward is exceedingly great.
After these things the word of Jehovah came unto Abram in a vision, saying, Fear not, Abram: I am thy shield, [and] thy exceeding great reward.
אַחַ֣ר ׀ הַדְּבָרִ֣ים הָאֵ֗לֶּה הָיָ֤ה דְבַר־יְהוָה֙ אֶל־אַבְרָ֔ם בַּֽמַּחֲזֶ֖ה לֵאמֹ֑ר אַל־תִּירָ֣א אַבְרָ֗ם אָנֹכִי֙ מָגֵ֣ן לָ֔ךְ שְׂכָרְךָ֖ הַרְבֵּ֥ה מְאֹֽד׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The verse is the inaugural occurrence of the davar-YHWH prophetic-word-formula in the Hebrew Bible. Va-y'hi devar-YHWH el-Avram — and the word of YHWH came to Abram — becomes the standard introductory formula of the prophetic corpus, recurring more than 250 times across Samuel, Kings, Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and the Twelve. The Genesis editor's placement of the formula here marks Abram as the first prophetic recipient — a structural anticipation of the developed prophetic-revelation tradition. Westermann (Genesis 12-36, 1985, p. 220) treats the formula's first occurrence as theologically load-bearing: the patriarchal stratum is here annexed to the prophetic stratum by a single formulaic move.

The verse pairs the davar-YHWH formula with ba-machazeh — in a vision. The lexeme machazeh (from the root ḥ-z-h, to see) is the specific Hebrew Bible term for prophetic vision, distinct from chazon and mar'eh. The recurrence-set is sparse — Num 24:4, 16 (Balaam's oracles); Ezek 13:7 (the false-prophet polemic) — and the term names a state of revelatory seeing rather than a dream or an ordinary vision. The collocation davar-YHWH ... ba-machazeh is unique to this verse; the prophetic word and the prophetic vision converge in a single inaugural revelation.

The divine self-introduction anokhi magen lakh — I am your shield — introduces a recurring divine-epithet. The magen-of-the-fathers register recurs across Deut 33:29, 2 Sam 22:3, 22:31, Ps 3:4, 18:3, 28:7, 33:20, 84:12, 115:9-11, 144:2, and Prov 30:5. The epithet is read in BDB as 'shield, buckler, protector', and at Ps 84:12 the shield-language is explicitly paralleled with shemesh (sun), making the divine epithet cosmographic as well as martial. The translation matches ASV; the commentary surfaces the inaugural status of the formulae rather than altering the English.

2
And Abram said: Lord YHWH, what will you give me, seeing I go childless, and the steward of my house is this Eliezer of Damascus?
And Abram said, O Lord Jehovah, what wilt thou give me, seeing I go childless, and he that shall be possessor of my house is Eliezer of Damascus?
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אַבְרָ֗ם אֲדֹנָ֤י יֱהוִה֙ מַה־תִּתֶּן־לִ֔י וְאָנֹכִ֖י הוֹלֵ֣ךְ עֲרִירִ֑י וּבֶן־מֶ֣שֶׁק בֵּיתִ֔י ה֖וּא דַּמֶּ֥שֶׂק אֱלִיעֶֽזֶר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The verse contains the first patriarchal occurrence of the compound divine-name Adonai YHWH. אֲדֹנָי יֱהוִה — the YHWH tetragrammaton with Elohim-vowels — is the masoretic qere perpetuum substitution Adonai-for-YHWH where Adonai already precedes (otherwise the public reading would produce Adonai Adonai). The compound recurs immediately at v 8 of this chapter and is then sparsely attested in the patriarchal narratives, becoming a fixed Ezekielian formula in the prophetic corpus (more than 200 occurrences in Ezekiel alone). The Genesis editor's deployment of the compound at the chapter's two interrogative-prayer verses (vv 2, 8) marks Abram's address as solemn-petitionary.

The ben-meshek beyti hu Damesek Eliezer is one of the most acknowledged textual cruces in the Abraham cycle. Ben-meshek is a hapax legomenon — the noun meshek occurs nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible — and the etymology is disputed. Speiser (Anchor Bible Genesis, 1964, pp. 111-112) connects the form to Akkadian mašqû (irrigation; one-who-waters) and proposes 'steward / one in charge of provisioning'; HALOT lists alternative derivations from Aramaic and from Akkadian mašš/sšaqu (cup-bearer); Skinner (ICC Genesis, 1910, p. 279) reads the second half of the verse — hu Damesek Eliezer — as a marginal gloss that has entered the text. The translation follows ASV's 'the steward of my house, this Eliezer of Damascus' without preempting the textual question; the difficulty is flagged here in commentary rather than in the English.

3
And Abram said: Behold, to me you have given no seed, and, lo, one born in my house is my heir.
And Abram said, Behold, to me thou hast given no seed: and, lo, one born in my house is mine heir.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אַבְרָ֔ם הֵ֣ן לִ֔י לֹ֥א נָתַ֖תָּה זָ֑רַע וְהִנֵּ֥ה בֶן־בֵּיתִ֖י יוֹרֵ֥שׁ אֹתִֽי׃
4
And, behold, the word of YHWH came to him, saying: This man shall not be your heir; but he that shall come forth out of your own bowels shall be your heir.
And, behold, the word of Jehovah came unto him, saying, This man shall not be thine heir; But he that shall come forth out of thine own bowels shall be thine heir.
וְהִנֵּ֨ה דְבַר־יְהוָ֤ה אֵלָיו֙ לֵאמֹ֔ר לֹ֥א יִֽירָשְׁךָ֖ זֶ֑ה כִּי־אִם֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר יֵצֵ֣א מִמֵּעֶ֔יךָ ה֖וּא יִֽירָשֶֽׁךָ׃
5
And he brought him forth abroad, and said: Look now toward the heavens, and count the stars, if you are able to count them; and he said to him: So shall your seed be.
And he brought him forth abroad, and said, Look now toward heaven, and number the stars, if thou be able to number them: and he said unto him, So shall thy seed be.
וַיּוֹצֵ֨א אֹת֜וֹ הַח֗וּצָה וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ הַבֶּט־נָ֣א הַשָּׁמַ֗יְמָה וּסְפֹר֙ הַכּ֣וֹכָבִ֔ים אִם־תּוּכַ֖ל לִסְפֹּ֣ר אֹתָ֑ם וַיֹּ֣אמֶר ל֔וֹ כֹּ֥ה יִהְיֶ֖ה זַרְעֶֽךָ׃
6
And he believed in YHWH; and he reckoned it to him for righteousness.
And he believed in Jehovah; and he reckoned it to him for righteousness.
וְהֶאֱמִ֖ן בַּֽיהוָ֑ה וַיַּחְשְׁבֶ֥הָ לּ֖וֹ צְדָקָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

The verse is among the three most-quoted Hebrew Bible verses in Pauline theology and a foundational proof-text of the Reformation. Va-he'emin ba-YHWH va-yachshebeha lo tzedakah — and he believed in YHWH; and he reckoned it to him for righteousness. The Hebrew lexical core is direct: he'emin is the hiphil of aman (to support oneself on, to consider firm, to trust); tzedakah is righteousness, justice; yachshebeha is the qal of chashav (to reckon, account, calculate) with a feminine-singular pronominal suffix whose antecedent is ambiguous (the act of believing? Abram's faith? the promise itself?).

The verse must be read on its Hebrew terms, not preempted by its downstream Pauline reception. The downstream Pauline reception — Romans 4:3 (the central proof-text of dikaiosynē ek pisteōs, justification by faith); Rom 4:9, 4:22; Gal 3:6 (the proof-text in the anti-Judaizing argument); James 2:23 (the same verse cited for the opposite — faith without works is dead) — is a Greek-and-Latin reception-history operating on the LXX rendering (kai episteusen Abram tō theō, kai elogisthē autō eis dikaiosynēn). The Reformation justification-by-faith superstructure (Luther, Calvin, the Westminster divines) is downstream of the Pauline reading, not of the Hebrew. The Hebrew itself states that Abram trusted YHWH and that this trusting-act was counted to him as tzedakah — a relational-juridical accounting, not a Pauline forensic-imputation. Moberly (The Old Testament of the Old Testament, 1992) argues at length that the Hebrew verse, on its own terms, is about covenant-loyalty trust rather than about justification-by-faith in the Pauline sense; Sanders (Paul and Palestinian Judaism, 1977) and the New Perspective on Paul (Dunn, Wright, Westerholm) have re-opened the Pauline reading itself.

The translation matches ASV — 'and he believed in YHWH; and he reckoned it to him for righteousness'. The English does not preempt: 'believed in YHWH' carries the aman-trust without theologically loading it; 'reckoned' (ASV, NRSV) matches the chashav accounting register without forensic-imputation language. The translation reads the Hebrew. The Pauline and Reformation reception-histories are flagged in the glossary entry and in this commentary, and belong to downstream wiki and long-form material, not to the translated text.

7
And he said to him: I am YHWH, who brought you out of Ur of the Kasdim, to give you this land to inherit it.
And he said unto him, I am Jehovah that brought thee out of Ur of the Chaldees, to give thee this land to inherit it.
וַיֹּ֖אמֶר אֵלָ֑יו אֲנִ֣י יְהוָ֗ה אֲשֶׁ֤ר הוֹצֵאתִ֙יךָ֙ מֵא֣וּר כַּשְׂדִּ֔ים לָ֧תֶת לְךָ֛ אֶת־הָאָ֥רֶץ הַזֹּ֖את לְרִשְׁתָּֽהּ׃
8
And he said: Lord YHWH, by what shall I know that I shall inherit it?
And he said, O Lord Jehovah, whereby shall I know that I shall inherit it?
וַיֹּאמַ֑ר אֲדֹנָ֣י יֱהוִ֔ה בַּמָּ֥ה אֵדַ֖ע כִּ֥י אִֽירָשֶֽׁנָּה׃
9
And he said to him: Take me a heifer three years old, and a she-goat three years old, and a ram three years old, and a turtle-dove, and a young pigeon.
And he said unto him, Take me a heifer three years old, and a she-goat three years old, and a ram three years old, and a turtle-dove, and a young pigeon.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֵלָ֗יו קְחָ֥ה לִי֙ עֶגְלָ֣ה מְשֻׁלֶּ֔שֶׁת וְעֵ֥ז מְשֻׁלֶּ֖שֶׁת וְאַ֣יִל מְשֻׁלָּ֑שׁ וְתֹ֖ר וְגוֹזָֽל׃
10
And he took to him all these, and divided them in the midst, and laid each half over against the other; but the birds he did not divide.
And he took him all these, and divided them in the midst, and laid each half over against the other: but the birds divided he not.
וַיִּֽקַּֽח־ל֣וֹ אֶת־כָּל־אֵ֗לֶּה וַיְבַתֵּ֤ר אֹתָם֙ בַּתָּ֔וֶךְ וַיִּתֵּ֥ן אִישׁ־בִּתְר֖וֹ לִקְרַ֣את רֵעֵ֑הוּ וְאֶת־הַצִפֹּ֖ר לֹ֥א בָתָֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

Verses 9-11, with v 17, together constitute the brit bein ha-betarim — the covenant between the pieces. Va-y'vater otam ba-tavekh — and he divided them in the midst — followed by va-yiten ish bitro liqrat re'ehu — and he laid each half over against the other. The birds are explicitly not divided (cf. the Levitical sacrificial convention at Lev 1:17). The ritual culminates at v 17 with the smoking-furnace and flaming-torch passing between the halves.

Ancient Near Eastern parallels for the rite are well-documented. Mari treaty-cutting documents (ARM II 37, II 80) describe sacrificial-animal bisection as covenant-ratification; the Alalakh tablets (AT 1, 76) attest the same; the Sefire Aramaic treaties of the eighth century BCE (KAI 222-224, edited by Fitzmyer, The Aramaic Inscriptions of Sefire, 1995) preserve the curse-formula explicitly — as this calf is cut up, so let X be cut up. Weinfeld's 1970 JAOS 90 article The Covenant of Grant in the Old Testament and in the Ancient Near East set the modern frame for reading the Genesis 15 ritual in its ANE legal context; Mendenhall (Law and Covenant in Israel and the Ancient Near East, 1955), Hillers (Covenant, 1969), and McCarthy (Treaty and Covenant, 2nd ed. 1978) develop the comparative case.

The ritual recurs in the Hebrew Bible itself at Jer 34:18-20, where Yahweh condemns the Judahite princes who passed between the parts of the calf as a covenant-ratification and then violated their covenant: the men who transgressed my covenant ... the calf which they cut in two and passed between its parts. The Jeremiah passage explicitly cites the same rite as the Genesis 15 description and confirms that the brit bein ha-betarim was a known Israelite-context legal practice, not only a literary trope.

The lexical pair betarim / gezarim is a Hebrew stylistic variation. Bitro at v 10 (from betar, piece-of-halved-thing) and gezarim at v 17 (from gezer, cut-piece) are different lexemes for the same physical halved-carcasses; the variation is rhetorical rather than referential. The standard 'pieces' translation captures both.

11
And the birds of prey came down upon the carcasses, and Abram drove them away.
And the birds of prey came down upon the carcasses, and Abram drove them away.
וַיֵּ֥רֶד הָעַ֖יִט עַל־הַפְּגָרִ֑ים וַיַּשֵּׁ֥ב אֹתָ֖ם אַבְרָֽם׃
12
And when the sun was going down, a deep sleep fell upon Abram; and, lo, a horror of great darkness fell upon him.
And when the sun was going down, a deep sleep fell upon Abram; and, lo, a horror of great darkness fell upon him.
וַיְהִ֤י הַשֶּׁ֙מֶשׁ֙ לָב֔וֹא וְתַרְדֵּמָ֖ה נָפְלָ֣ה עַל־אַבְרָ֑ם וְהִנֵּ֥ה אֵימָ֛ה חֲשֵׁכָ֥ה גְדֹלָ֖ה נֹפֶ֥לֶת עָלָֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

The lexeme tardemah names a divine-induced deep sleep that recurs across the Hebrew Bible as a vehicle for revelation or for divine action upon the patient. The verb is naflah al-Avram — fell upon Abram — a passive falling-construction matching the Gen 2:21 va-yappel YHWH-Elohim tardemah al-ha-adam (he caused a tardemah to fall upon the human). The lexeme is consistently distinguished from ordinary sleep (shenah) and consistently carries the supernatural-anaesthetic quality.

The recurrence-chain. Gen 2:21 (Adam, during the taking of the tzela); Gen 15:12 (Abram, during the covenant-cutting theophany); 1 Sam 26:12 (Saul and his camp, during David's incursion — ki tardemat YHWH naflah aleihem, for a deep sleep from YHWH had fallen upon them); Job 4:13 (Eliphaz's vision — bi-s'ifim me-chezyonot laylah b-n'fol tardemah al-anashim, amid disquieting thoughts from visions of the night, when deep sleep falls upon men); Job 33:15 (Elihu's dream-revelation, same construction); Isa 29:10 (the tardemah of judgment poured out on the prophets). The LXX renders the lexeme as ekstasis (cf. Gen 2:21 LXX), which the Greek tradition then reads in its own technical sense of trance-state. Lichtenstein (1968, JANES 1, Dream-Theophany and the E Document) collects the dream-revelation contexts.

The Genesis 15 tardemah is paired with eymah chashekhah gedolah — a horror of great darkness — a phrase otherwise unparalleled in the Hebrew Bible. The tardemah + eymah-darkness combination at v 12 frames the v 17 theophany: Abram is unconscious throughout the divine fire's passage between the pieces, which makes the covenant-cutting an Yahweh-only unilateral ratification (Westermann, Genesis 12-36, 1985, p. 226; Wenham, WBC Genesis 1-15, 1987, p. 332). The translation matches ASV ('a deep sleep fell upon Abram; and, lo, a horror of great darkness fell upon him'); the cross-corpus chain lives in the glossary.

13
And he said to Abram: Know of a surety that your seed shall be a sojourner in a land that is not theirs, and shall serve them; and they shall afflict them four hundred years.
And he said unto Abram, Know of a surety that thy seed shall be sojourners in a land that is not theirs, and shall serve them; and they shall afflict them four hundred years;
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר לְאַבְרָ֗ם יָדֹ֨עַ תֵּדַ֜ע כִּי־גֵ֣ר ׀ יִהְיֶ֣ה זַרְעֲךָ֗ בְּאֶ֙רֶץ֙ לֹ֣א לָהֶ֔ם וַעֲבָד֖וּם וְעִנּ֣וּ אֹתָ֑ם אַרְבַּ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

The 400-year affliction-prophecy is in known tension with the canonical Egyptian-sojourn chronology. Gen 15:13 gives arba me'ot shanah — 400 years — for the duration of the seed's sojourning, servitude, and affliction. Exod 12:40-41 gives moshav b'nei Yisra'el asher yashvu b-Mitsrayim shloshim shanah v-arba me'ot shanah — the sojourning of the children of Israel who dwelt in Mitsrayim was four hundred and thirty years. The Samaritan Pentateuch and the LXX harmonize the discrepancy by reading 'in the land of Mitsrayim and in the land of Kena'an' at Exod 12:40 — splitting the 430 years across the patriarchal and Egyptian residences. The 400 of Gen 15:13 is then read as a round figure for the same span (so Sarna, JPS Exodus, 1991, excursus 2; Hoffmeier, Israel in Egypt, 1997).

The New Testament reception follows the LXX harmonization. Acts 7:6 (Stephen's speech) cites 400 years; Gal 3:17 (Paul) cites 430 years — ho de tetrakosia kai triakonta etē gegonōs nomos — from the Abrahamic promise to the Sinai legislation. The two NT figures are not contradictory if Gen 15:13's 400 is read as the affliction-period proper and Exod 12:40's 430 as the full sojourning from Abraham's call.

The verse 16 dor revi'i — the fourth-generation return — compounds the chronology dispute. Four generations against 400 (or 430) years requires either generation-of-100-years (matching the patriarchal lifespans) or one of several textual-reconciliation strategies. Mainstream scholarship (Sarna, Westermann, Wenham, Hamilton) discusses the harmonization at length without committing definitively to a resolution. The translation reads the verse literally — four hundred years — and the chronology-dispute lives in the glossary apparatus.

14
And also that nation, whom they shall serve, will I judge; and afterward shall they come out with great substance.
and also that nation, whom they shall serve, will I judge: and afterward shall they come out with great substance.
וְגַ֧ם אֶת־הַגּ֛וֹי אֲשֶׁ֥ר יַעֲבֹ֖דוּ דָּ֣ן אָנֹ֑כִי וְאַחֲרֵי־כֵ֥ן יֵצְא֖וּ בִּרְכֻ֥שׁ גָּדֽוֹל׃
15
But you shall go to your fathers in peace; you shall be buried in a good old age.
But thou shalt go to thy fathers in peace; thou shalt be buried in a good old age.
וְאַתָּ֛ה תָּב֥וֹא אֶל־אֲבֹתֶ֖יךָ בְּשָׁל֑וֹם תִּקָּבֵ֖ר בְּשֵׂיבָ֥ה טוֹבָֽה׃
16
And in the fourth generation they shall come hither again; for the iniquity of the Emori is not yet complete.
And in the fourth generation they shall come hither again; for the iniquity of the Amorite is not yet full.
וְד֥וֹר רְבִיעִ֖י יָשׁ֣וּבוּ הֵ֑נָּה כִּ֧י לֹא־שָׁלֵ֛ם עֲוֺ֥ן הָאֱמֹרִ֖י עַד־הֵֽנָּה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

The verse states one of the Hebrew Bible's most theologically distinctive moral propositions: divine timing of the conquest is tied to the moral fulfillment of the Amorite's iniquity. Ki lo shalem avon ha-Emori ad henna — for the iniquity of the Emori is not yet complete. The Amorite is not displaced until the cup of iniquity has filled to a divinely-tracked threshold. The construction is the lo shalem participial — not yet complete / not yet full — applied to avon (iniquity, guilt, the weight of moral debt).

The avon ha-Emori formula becomes a recurring Deuteronomic justification for the conquest. Deut 9:4-5 explicitly grounds the displacement in the wickedness of the dispossessed peoples — not for your righteousness... but for the wickedness of these nations; Lev 18:24-28 frames the land itself as vomiting out its previous inhabitants because of their abominations; Lev 20:23 reiterates the formula. The ethical-theological structure is consistent across the Pentateuch: the land is not given to Israel because Israel deserves it, and the previous inhabitants are not displaced for political-military reasons alone; both moves are conditional on a divinely-evaluated moral threshold.

The verse also encodes a divinely-imposed delay on the conquest. The patriarchal generation cannot inherit the land because the prior inhabitants have not yet earned displacement; the four-generation deferral at the verse's opening is conditioned by the moral threshold at its close. Mainstream commentary (Westermann Genesis 12-36, 1985, pp. 227-228; Sarna JPS Genesis, 1989, p. 116; Wenham WBC Genesis 1-15, 1987, p. 333) treats this as one of the most ethically distinctive verses in the Hebrew Bible's conquest-theology. The verse also has a downstream Pauline reception at 1 Thess 2:16 — anaplērōsai autōn tas hamartias pantote — where Paul applies a parallel cup-of-iniquity formula in a polemic against the Judean opponents of the gospel. The Pauline reception is flagged in the glossary; the translation matches ASV without preempting.

17
And it came to pass, when the sun went down, and it was dark, behold, a smoking furnace, and a flaming torch that passed between these pieces.
And it came to pass, that, when the sun went down, and it was dark, behold, a smoking furnace, and a flaming torch that passed between these pieces.
וַיְהִ֤י הַשֶּׁ֙מֶשׁ֙ בָּ֔אָה וַעֲלָטָ֖ה הָיָ֑ה וְהִנֵּ֨ה תַנּ֤וּר עָשָׁן֙ וְלַפִּ֣יד אֵ֔שׁ אֲשֶׁ֣ר עָבַ֔ר בֵּ֖ין הַגְּזָרִ֥ים הָאֵֽלֶּה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

The verse is one of the most concretely described theophanies in the Hebrew Bible. Tanur ashan v-lappid esh asher avar bein ha-gezarim ha-eleh — a smoking furnace, and a flaming torch that passed between these pieces. The two images are described with sensory specificity: a tanur (oven / furnace — the same vessel-lexeme used for cooking at Lev 2:4, 7:9, 11:35) emitting ashan (smoke), and a lappid (torch — cf. Judg 7:16-20, Gideon's torches inside the jars) of esh (fire). The two move together between the halved carcasses laid out at vv 9-10.

The fire-and-smoke theophany pattern recurs across the Hebrew Bible. Exod 19:18 at Sinai: Har Sinai ashan kullo... va-ya'al ashano k-eshen ha-kivshan — Mount Sinai was wholly in smoke... and its smoke went up like the smoke of a furnace. The Sinai theophany explicitly recalls a kivshan (kiln-furnace) — lexically distinct from tanur but the same smoke-from-furnace image. Exod 13:21-22 — the wilderness pillar of cloud by day and pillar of fire by night — pairs the same two elements (smoke / cloud + fire) as the mode of YHWH's presence. Isa 6:4 — the seraph-vision in the Temple — has the house filling with smoke at the divine appearance. Ezek 1 — the chashmal and the wheels-within-wheels — operates in the same fire-cloud-glory register at a higher technical density. The Genesis 15 theophany stands at the head of the canonical Hebrew Bible fire-theophany corpus.

The unilateral passage is theologically load-bearing. In standard ANE covenant-cutting (cf. the v 10 commentary), both parties pass between the halved animals, invoking the curse-formula on whichever side breaks the covenant. At Gen 15:17, Abram is in tardemah (v 12); only the divine fire passes between the pieces. The covenant is therefore unilateral — Yahweh alone takes the self-imprecation. Westermann (Genesis 12-36, 1985, pp. 228-229), Wenham (WBC Genesis 1-15, 1987, pp. 332-333), and von Rad (Genesis, OTL, 1972, p. 187) all treat this as the chapter's keystone theological move. The translation matches ASV without altering the imagery; the lens-relevant cross-references and the broader fire-theophany corpus are carried in the glossary apparatus.

18
On that day YHWH cut a covenant with Abram, saying: To your seed have I given this land, from the river of Mitsrayim unto the great river, the river Perat.
In that day Jehovah made a covenant with Abram, saying, Unto thy seed have I given this land, from the river of Egypt unto the great river, the river Euphrates:
בַּיּ֣וֹם הַה֗וּא כָּרַ֧ת יְהוָ֛ה אֶת־אַבְרָ֖ם בְּרִ֣ית לֵאמֹ֑ר לְזַרְעֲךָ֗ נָתַ֙תִּי֙ אֶת־הָאָ֣רֶץ הַזֹּ֔את מִנְּהַ֣ר מִצְרַ֔יִם עַד־הַנָּהָ֥ר הַגָּדֹ֖ל נְהַר־פְּרָֽת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

The verse contains the first explicit karat brit — cut a covenant — of the Abraham cycle. The Hebrew idiom for covenant-ratification preserves the literal animal-bisection rite of vv 9-11, 17 in its very verb: covenants are cut, not made (so Weinfeld 1970 JAOS 90). The idiom recurs roughly 80 times across the Hebrew Bible (Gen 21:27, 21:32, 26:28, 31:44; Exod 24:8, 34:10, 34:27; Deut 4:23, 5:2-3, etc.); the Genesis 15 occurrence is its load-bearing first appearance.

The land-promise min'har Mitsrayim ad ha-nahar ha-gadol n'har P'rat — from the river of Mitsrayim unto the great river, the river Perat — is the maximal patriarchal land-claim. The southern boundary n'har Mitsrayim is ambiguous: either the Wadi el-Arish (Naḥal Mitsrayim of Num 34:5, 1 Kgs 8:65, Isa 27:12; the standard southern boundary of the land) or an arm of the Nile itself (the maximal reading). The northern boundary is unambiguously the Euphrates (P'rat = the Euphrates, identical to the Gen 2:14 Perat). The reduced 'standard' land-claim — me-Naḥal Mitsrayim ad l'vo Chamat (from the Wadi of Egypt to the entrance of Hamath) — appears at Num 34:5 and 1 Kgs 8:65; the maximal Euphrates-claim is only briefly achieved historically under Solomon (1 Kgs 4:21, 5:1).

Westermann (1985, pp. 230-231) and Sarna (1989, p. 117) read the maximal land-claim here as deliberately paired with the maximal ten-nation enumeration of vv 19-21; the chapter's geography-of-the-promise is consistently at the upper bound of patriarchal land-tradition. The translation preserves both formulae literally.

19
The Qeini, and the Qenizi, and the Qadmoni,
the Kenite, and the Kenizzite, and the Kadmonite,
אֶת־הַקֵּינִי֙ וְאֶת־הַקְּנִזִּ֔י וְאֵ֖ת הַקַּדְמֹנִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 19

The Qeini / Qenizi / Qadmoni triad is unique to this verse. Every other Pentateuchal land-peoples enumeration omits these three (cf. Exod 3:8, 3:17, 13:5, 23:23, 33:2, 34:11 — six peoples; Deut 7:1, Josh 3:10, 24:11 — seven peoples). The Qeini (Kenites) recur as a distinct gentilic group at Num 24:21-22, Judg 1:16, 1 Sam 15:6 (the Saul-Amaleq narrative, in which the Kenites are explicitly spared) and are associated with Moses's Midianite-Kenite affines (Judg 4:11). The Qenizi (Kenizzites) recur at Num 32:12, Josh 14:6, 14:14 as the gentilic of Caleb. The Qadmoni is a hapax in this sense, almost certainly from qedem (east) — the eastern ones — and most likely refers to the Trans-Jordanian / Syrian-desert peoples east of the Jordan. Westermann (Genesis 12-36, 1985, p. 230) and Wenham (WBC, 1987, p. 334) read the ten-fold list as a deliberate editorial maximization, matching the maximal Euphrates-claim of v 18.

20
and the Chitti, and the Perizi, and the Refa'im,
and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, and the Rephaim,
וְאֶת־הַחִתִּ֥י וְאֶת־הַפְּרִזִּ֖י וְאֶת־הָרְפָאִֽים׃
21
and the Emori, and the Kena'ani, and the Girgashi, and the Yevusi.
and the Amorite, and the Canaanite, and the Girgashite, and the Jebusite.
וְאֶת־הָֽאֱמֹרִי֙ וְאֶת־הַֽכְּנַעֲנִ֔י וְאֶת־הַגִּרְגָּשִׁ֖י וְאֶת־הַיְבוּסִֽי׃ (ס)
Chapter 16

Genesis 16 — Hagar, Ishmael, and the messenger of YHWH

1
Now Sarai, Abram's wife, bore him no children; and she had a handmaid, a Mitsrite, whose name was Hagar.
Now Sarai, Abram`s wife, bare him no children: and she had a handmaid, an Egyptian, whose name was Hagar.
וְשָׂרַי֙ אֵ֣שֶׁת אַבְרָ֔ם לֹ֥א יָלְדָ֖ה ל֑וֹ וְלָ֛הּ שִׁפְחָ֥ה מִצְרִ֖ית וּשְׁמָ֥הּ הָגָֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The verse opens with Sarai's barrenness and introduces Hagar as a shifchah Mitsrit — a Mitsrite shifchah. The Hebrew shifchah is the specific designation of a class of female slave whose offspring by the master, at the mistress's initiative, could legally count as the mistress's children. The lexeme is distinct from amah (the standard maidservant class) and is preferred where the substitute-wife arrangement is in view — a pattern verifiable across Hagar (Gen 16:1-8) and Bilhah and Zilpah (Gen 30:3-13). The English handmaid (ASV) is conventional but does not carry the cultural-legal specificity; the modern slave-girl (NRSV/NJPS) carries the slave-status but loses the substitute-wife institutional reading. The WoH translation preserves ASV's handmaid in the running English and carries the institutional reading in this commentary and in the glossary apparatus.

The Egyptian provenance. Hagar is introduced as Mitsrit — Mitsrite, of Mitsrayim — anchoring her as a foreign woman of Egyptian origin. The narrative-chronological link back to Gen 12:16 is editorially direct: Pharaoh's gifts to Abram during the Mitsrayim-sojourn included shefachot (the plural of shifchah), and Hagar is naturally read as one of these. The Genesis editor preserves the Mitsrite-ness of Hagar as a load-bearing narrative feature throughout (vv 1, 3) and through her son's later Egyptian marriage (Gen 21:21).

2
And Sarai said to Abram: Behold now, YHWH has restrained me from bearing; go in, I pray you, unto my handmaid; perhaps I shall be built up through her. And Abram hearkened to the voice of Sarai.
And Sarai said unto Abram, Behold now, Jehovah hath restrained me from bearing; go in, I pray thee, unto my handmaid; it may be that I shall obtain children by her. And Abram hearkened to the voice of Sarai.
וַתֹּ֨אמֶר שָׂרַ֜י אֶל־אַבְרָ֗ם הִנֵּה־נָ֞א עֲצָרַ֤נִי יְהוָה֙ מִלֶּ֔דֶת בֹּא־נָא֙ אֶל־שִׁפְחָתִ֔י אוּלַ֥י אִבָּנֶ֖ה מִמֶּ֑נָּה וַיִּשְׁמַ֥ע אַבְרָ֖ם לְק֥וֹל שָׂרָֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

Sarai's ulai ibaneh mimenah — perhaps I shall be built up through her — is the canonical Hebrew Bible expression of the substitute-wife institutional logic. The niphal ibaneh (be built, be built up) from b-n-h (build) is a play on ben (son): to be built up is to acquire sons. The same idiom recurs at Gen 30:3 (Rachel offering Bilhah to Jacob: v-ibaneh gam-anokhi mimenah — that I also may be built up through her). The institutional logic — that the shifchah's children belong to the mistress, not to the slave-girl — is preserved verbatim across the two narratives. The ANE legal parallels are direct: Code of Hammurabi §146 (the šugītum substitute slave-girl, with explicit provision for the case where the slave-girl makes herself equal with her mistress) and Nuzi HSS V 67 (the Hurrian marriage-contract provisions for substitute-wife arrangements; Speiser reads the Nuzi parallels as closer in detail to the patriarchal narratives than the Hammurabi).

3
And Sarai, Abram's wife, took Hagar the Mitsrite, her handmaid, after Abram had dwelt ten years in the land of Kena'an, and gave her to Abram her husband to be his wife.
And Sarai, Abram`s wife, took Hagar the Egyptian, her handmaid, after Abram had dwelt ten years in the land of Canaan, and gave her to Abram her husband to be his wife.
וַתִּקַּ֞ח שָׂרַ֣י אֵֽשֶׁת־אַבְרָ֗ם אֶת־הָגָ֤ר הַמִּצְרִית֙ שִׁפְחָתָ֔הּ מִקֵּץ֙ עֶ֣שֶׂר שָׁנִ֔ים לְשֶׁ֥בֶת אַבְרָ֖ם בְּאֶ֣רֶץ כְּנָ֑עַן וַתִּתֵּ֥ן אֹתָ֛הּ לְאַבְרָ֥ם אִישָׁ֖הּ ל֥וֹ לְאִשָּֽׁה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

The verse formalizes the substitute-wife arrangement: va-titen otah l-Avram ishah lo l-ishah — and she gave her to Abram her husband to be his wife. The Hebrew is grammatically explicit: Hagar is given as ishah (wife), not as concubine (pilegesh); the legal status under the substitute-wife institution is full wifehood, with the proviso that the offspring count as the mistress's. The ten-year time-marker (mi-ketz eser shanim l-shevet Avram b-eretz Kena'an — after ten years of Abram's dwelling in Kena'an) is editorially significant: it anchors the narrative to the post-Gen 12 chronology and frames the substitute-wife move as Sarai's response to the unfulfilled seed-promise of Gen 12, 13, 15.
