The Genesis editor presents the move not as an act of impatience but as a culturally available institutional resolution — within the ANE substitute-wife legal framework — to the chapter's narrative problem.

4
And he went in unto Hagar, and she conceived; and when she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was lightly esteemed in her eyes.
And he went in unto Hagar, and she conceived: and when she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was despised in her eyes.
וַיָּבֹ֥א אֶל־הָגָ֖ר וַתַּ֑הַר וַתֵּ֙רֶא֙ כִּ֣י הָרָ֔תָה וַתֵּקַ֥ל גְּבִרְתָּ֖הּ בְּעֵינֶֽיהָ׃
5
And Sarai said to Abram: My wrong be upon you; I gave my handmaid into your bosom, and when she saw that she had conceived, I was lightly esteemed in her eyes; YHWH judge between me and you.
And Sarai said unto Abram, My wrong be upon thee: I gave my handmaid into they bosom; and when she saw that she had conceived, I was despised in her eyes: Jehovah judge between me and thee.
וַתֹּ֨אמֶר שָׂרַ֣י אֶל־אַבְרָם֮ חֲמָסִ֣י עָלֶיךָ֒ אָנֹכִ֗י נָתַ֤תִּי שִׁפְחָתִי֙ בְּחֵיקֶ֔ךָ וַתֵּ֙רֶא֙ כִּ֣י הָרָ֔תָה וָאֵקַ֖ל בְּעֵינֶ֑יהָ יִשְׁפֹּ֥ט יְהוָ֖ה בֵּינִ֥י וּבֵינֶֽיׄךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

Sarai's declaration deploys two paired legal-religious formulae. Chamasi alekha — my violence (be) upon you — names the wrong as chamas, the strong Hebrew term for criminal violence or wrongful injury, and rhetorically transfers it onto the addressee. The second clause yishpot YHWH beyni u-veynekha — YHWH judge between me and you — invokes YHWH as covenant-litigation arbiter between the two parties. The pairing is a formal legal speech-act in the strict sense: Sarai is not merely complaining, she is initiating a religious-juridical proceeding. Westermann (Genesis 12-36, 1985, p. 240), Sarna (JPS Genesis, 1989, p. 120), and Wenham (WBC Genesis 16-50, 1994, p. 10) read the v 5 declaration in this register.

The chamas register. The noun chamas is the Hebrew Bible's strongest term for criminal violence — the same word that fills the earth in Gen 6:11, 13 and triggers the flood (the pre-existing shachat-chamas glossary entry covers the antediluvian usage). Cross-corpus distribution is dense in the prophetic-indictment corpus (Hab 1:2-3, Hos 12:1, Mic 6:12, Jer 6:7, Ezek 7:11-23, Joel 4:19, etc.), where chamas names the social-criminal injustice that triggers YHWH's judgment. The conventional English my wrong be upon you (ASV) preserves the formula's register without preempting; the strong chamas-as-violence reading is carried at the glossary level.

The yishpot YHWH beyni u-veynekha idiom. The litigation-invocation recurs at Gen 31:53 (Laban and Jacob at Mizpah), 1 Sam 24:13, 24:16 (David before Saul), and Judg 11:27 (Jephthah's messenger to the Ammonites). The idiom names YHWH as the third party in a two-party dispute, invoking divine arbitration. The translation preserves ASV's YHWH judge between me and you.

6
But Abram said to Sarai: Behold, your handmaid is in your hand; do to her that which is good in your eyes. And Sarai dealt hardly with her, and she fled from her face.
But Abram said unto Sarai, Behold, thy maid is in thy hand; do to her that which is good in thine eyes. And Sarai dealt hardly with her, and she fled from her face.
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר אַבְרָ֜ם אֶל־שָׂרַ֗י הִנֵּ֤ה שִׁפְחָתֵךְ֙ בְּיָדֵ֔ךְ עֲשִׂי־לָ֖הּ הַטּ֣וֹב בְּעֵינָ֑יִךְ וַתְּעַנֶּ֣הָ שָׂרַ֔י וַתִּבְרַ֖ח מִפָּנֶֽיהָ׃
7
And the messenger of YHWH found her by a spring of water in the wilderness, by the spring on the way to Shur.
And the angel of Jehovah found her by a fountain of water in the wilderness, by the fountain in the way to Shur.
וַֽיִּמְצָאָ֞הּ מַלְאַ֧ךְ יְהוָ֛ה עַל־עֵ֥ין הַמַּ֖יִם בַּמִּדְבָּ֑ר עַל־הָעַ֖יִן בְּדֶ֥רֶךְ שֽׁוּר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

This verse is the first occurrence of mal'akh YHWH — the messenger of YHWH — in the Hebrew Bible. מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה. The figure recurs four times in this single scene (vv 7, 9, 10, 11) and across the patriarchal cycle and the wider Hebrew Bible — Gen 21:17, 22:11, 22:15, 31:11, 48:16; Exod 3:2, 14:19, 23:20-23, 32:34, 33:2; Num 22:22-35 (the Balaam mal'akh with drawn sword); Josh 5:13-15; Judg 2:1, 5:23, 6:11-22 (Gideon), 13:3-21 (Manoah and Samson); 2 Sam 24:16; 1 Kgs 19:7; 2 Kgs 1:3-15, 19:35; Isa 37:36, 63:9; Zech 1:11-12, 3:1-6; Ps 34:8, 35:5-6 — for a total of 60+ occurrences.

The translation choice messenger of YHWH preserves the Hebrew lexical etymology and does not preempt the messenger-identity scholarly question. The lexeme mal'akh derives from a root l-ʾ-k (cognate Ugaritic lʾk, to send) and denotes one who is sent. The same word is the standard Hebrew designation of a human messenger (Gen 32:4 Jacob sends mal'akhim to Esau; 1 Sam 6:21 the people of Beth-Shemesh send mal'akhim; 1 Kgs 19:2 Jezebel sends a mal'akh to Elijah). The conventional English angel of the LORD imports later Greek-and-Latin theology (angelos / angelus as a class of created spiritual being) and tilts the reader toward a particular reading of the messenger-identity question. Messenger is etymologically literal and editorially neutral.

The messenger-identity question is the load-bearing scholarly issue of these scenes. Modern critical scholarship recognizes three main positions — none uniquely consensus, all defended in print by serious philological scholars.

Position (a) — the messenger is a distinct created envoy. The classic Christian-tradition reading: the mal'akh YHWH is a distinct spiritual being who speaks on YHWH's behalf and is sometimes mistaken for YHWH by the human recipient. The reading is supported by passages where the mal'akh is clearly distinguished from YHWH (Zech 1:11-12 the mal'akh YHWH intercedes to YHWH; 1 Kgs 19:7). Heidt's Angelology of the Old Testament (1949) is the standard mid-twentieth-century defense.

Position (b) — the messenger IS YHWH (the identity reading). Modern critical scholarship has increasingly read the mal'akh YHWH as a mode of YHWH's own appearance rather than as a distinct envoy. The reading is supported by passages where the mal'akh speaks in the first-person AS YHWH (Gen 16:10 I will multiply your seed; Gen 22:12 now I know that you fear God; Exod 3:6 I am the God of your father), where the human recipient identifies the mal'akh AS YHWH (Gen 16:13 — this very chapter; Judg 6:22-23 Gideon's anxiety; Judg 13:22 Manoah's anxiety), and where YHWH and the mal'akh YHWH are interchanged in narration (Gen 22:11/15, Exod 3:2/4). Westermann (Genesis 12-36, 1985, pp. 242-244) and von Rad (Genesis, OTL, 1972, pp. 192-193) defend the identity reading; it is the modern critical default in continental scholarship.

Position (c) — the messenger is a literary-theological device. A third position reads the mal'akh YHWH as a literary device that allows YHWH to appear without theophanic risk — preserving divine transcendence while permitting personal encounter. The alternation between mal'akh YHWH and YHWH is not a doctrinal claim about a distinct figure but a narrative-rhetorical strategy. Hamori (When Gods Were Men: Biblical Theophany and Anthropomorphic Realism, 2008) develops the position; Sommer (The Bodies of God and the World of Ancient Israel, 2009) develops a related fluidity model in which the divine self can have multiple bodies or manifestations; MacDonald 2009 addresses the diversity of divine manifestations.

The WoH translation commits to none of the three positions. The Genesis 16 narrative itself foregrounds the question — Hagar identifies the speaker AS YHWH at v 13 — but does not adjudicate it. The translation messenger of YHWH is editorially neutral; the apparatus (this commentary and the malakh-yhwh-messenger glossary entry) presents the three positions in parallel; project-specific synthesis is reserved for the wiki and long-form material downstream. The note on the post-exilic developed angelology — the class-of-angels framework that emerges in Daniel, 1 Enoch, Tobit, and the Qumran corpus — is also flagged: the patriarchal mal'akh YHWH belongs to the pre-developed-angelology stratum and should not be read forward through it.

The geography. The wilderness spring of water on the way to Shur (derekh Shur) places Hagar on the route from Kena'an toward Egypt — she is fleeing back toward Mitsrayim, her homeland. Shur is the wilderness region east of the Egyptian frontier (cf. Gen 25:18 the Ishmaelite tribes from Havilah unto Shur; Exod 15:22 the wilderness of Shur after the Sea crossing). The spring is later identified as Be'er Lachai Ro'i (v 14).

8
And he said: Hagar, Sarai's handmaid, from where have you come? and where will you go? And she said: I am fleeing from the face of Sarai my mistress.
And he said, Hagar, Sarai`s handmaid, whence camest thou? and whither goest thou? And she said, I am fleeing from the face of my mistress Sarai.
וַיֹּאמַ֗ר הָגָ֞ר שִׁפְחַ֥ת שָׂרַ֛י אֵֽי־מִזֶּ֥ה בָ֖את וְאָ֣נָה תֵלֵ֑כִי וַתֹּ֕אמֶר מִפְּנֵי֙ שָׂרַ֣י גְּבִרְתִּ֔י אָנֹכִ֖י בֹּרַֽחַת׃
9
And the messenger of YHWH said to her: Return to your mistress, and submit yourself under her hands.
And the angel of Jehovah said unto her, Return to thy mistress, and submit thyself under her hands.
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר לָהּ֙ מַלְאַ֣ךְ יְהוָ֔ה שׁ֖וּבִי אֶל־גְּבִרְתֵּ֑ךְ וְהִתְעַנִּ֖י תַּ֥חַת יָדֶֽיהָ׃
10
And the messenger of YHWH said to her: I will greatly multiply your seed, that it shall not be numbered for multitude.
And the angel of Jehovah said unto her, I will greatly multiply thy seed, that it shall not be numbered for multitude.
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר לָהּ֙ מַלְאַ֣ךְ יְהוָ֔ה הַרְבָּ֥ה אַרְבֶּ֖ה אֶת־זַרְעֵ֑ךְ וְלֹ֥א יִסָּפֵ֖ר מֵרֹֽב׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

The Hebrew harbah arbeh et-zarekh — multiplying I will multiply your seed — deploys the doubled-infinitive-absolute intensifying construction. The infinitive absolute harbah (from rbh, to be many, to multiply) prefixed before the finite hiphil yiqtol arbeh (I will multiply) conveys emphasis, certainty, or intensification of the verbal action; the construction is treated systematically in Gesenius-Kautzsch-Cowley §113l-r and Joüon-Muraoka §123. The conventional English I will greatly multiply (ASV) captures the intensification adequately and is preserved.

The rbh doubled-infinitive subset is concentrated in the patriarchal seed-multiplication promises. Cross-corpus harbah arbeh: Gen 3:16 (the curse on the woman, multiplying I will multiply your sorrow and your conception); Gen 16:10 (here, to Hagar); Gen 17:2 (the Abrahamic covenant); Gen 17:20 (specifically of Ishmael at the same covenant); Gen 22:17 (paired with the comparison-triad to Abraham post-Aqedah). The form is theologically loaded in these passages.

Hagar's structurally distinctive position. This is the only seed-multiplication formula in Genesis addressed to a woman, and addressed to a non-Israelite woman (an Egyptian shifchah). The formula's address-recipient is structurally significant: Hagar receives in her own person a promise that elsewhere in Genesis is given to the patriarchs (Abram at 13:16, 15:5, 17:2; Isaac at 26:24; Jacob at 28:14, 32:13). The closing clause v-lo yisafer me-rov (it shall not be numbered for multitude) echoes the cannot be numbered phrasing of Gen 13:16 (Abraham) and 32:13 (Jacob), formally aligning the Hagar promise with the patriarchal-promise corpus.

Distinction from the patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad. The pre-existing patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad glossary entry (v2.10.0) covers the dust/stars/sand comparison formulae (Gen 13:16, 15:5, 22:17). The harbah arbeh construction is grammatically distinct: it is an intensifying syntactic construction, not a comparison. At Gen 22:17 the two are paired; at Gen 16:10 the doubled-infinitive intensive appears without the comparison-triad. The two entries cover distinct features.

11
And the messenger of YHWH said to her: Behold, you are with child, and shall bear a son; and you shall call his name Yishma'el, because YHWH has heard your affliction.
And the angel of Jehovah said unto her, Behold, thou art with child, and shalt bear a son; and thou shalt call his name Ishmael, because Jehovah hath heard thy affliction.
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר לָהּ֙ מַלְאַ֣ךְ יְהוָ֔ה הִנָּ֥ךְ הָרָ֖ה וְיֹלַ֣דְתְּ בֵּ֑ן וְקָרָ֤את שְׁמוֹ֙ יִשְׁמָעֵ֔ל כִּֽי־שָׁמַ֥ע יְהוָ֖ה אֶל־עָנְיֵֽךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

Verse 11 contains the first in-text patriarchal name-etymology of the Hebrew Bible. V-qarat sh'mo Yishma'el ki-shama YHWH el-onyekh — and you shall call his name Yishma-El, because YHWH has heard your affliction. The name Yishma'el is a theophoric construction: the finite-verb element yishma (he hears, from shama) + the divine name element El. The narrator explains the name by direct reference to the verb (shama) whose subject is YHWH (shama YHWH); the etymological formula qara sh'mo X ki Y — (named) X because Y — is the standard Hebrew Bible name-etymology pattern.

The cross-corpus patriarchal name-etymology pattern. The convention recurs across the patriarchal corpus: Yitzchaq (Isaac) at Gen 17:17, 19, 18:12-15, 21:6 — from tzachaq (laugh); Esav at Gen 25:25; Ya'aqov at Gen 25:26 — from aqev (heel); Yosef at Gen 30:24 — from asaf (gather); Re'uven at Gen 29:32; Shim'on at Gen 29:33 — from shama (directly parallel to Yishma'el's etymology); Yehuda at Gen 29:35. The pattern's load-bearing function is to fix the name in the narrative; the names are story-compressions, not arbitrary labels.

The Yishma'el / Shim'on parallel. Among the patriarchal name-etymologies, Yishma'el and Shim'on share the same root verb shama (hear): Yishma'el — El will hear; Shim'on — YHWH has heard. The pairing is structurally significant: the shama root names the relation between a divine subject and a human affliction, and Gen 16 is the inaugural instance of the pattern.

The El / YHWH co-occurrence. A small but theologically interesting feature of v 11 is the Hebrew juxtaposition: the name is Yishma-El (theophoric in El); the subject of the verb explaining the name is YHWH (ki shama YHWH). The Genesis editor permits the El-theophoric child-naming alongside YHWH-narrative-action without harmonization — direct documentary evidence for a pre-monotheistic-harmonization stratum of the patriarchal corpus. The pre-existing el-elyon and yhwh-el-elyon-apposition glossary entries (Gen 14) cover the broader pattern.

The historical Ishmaelite background. Knauf (Ismael, 1989) reconstructs the Ishmaelite confederation as a North-Arabian / Trans-Jordanian camel-pastoralist league active in the late Iron Age (8th-6th centuries BCE); the b'nei Yishma'el of Gen 25:13-16 overlaps with attested North-Arabian tribal names from the Neo-Assyrian and Neo-Babylonian record (Tema, Dumah, Adbeel, Kedar, Nebaioth). The Knauf reconstruction is documentary-evidence-based and mainstream in modern ANE-historical scholarship (Eph'al 1982; Retsö 2003).

12
And he shall be a wild-ass of a man; his hand shall be against every man, and every man's hand against him; and he shall dwell over against all his brothers.
And he shall be [as] a wild ass among men; his hand [shall be] against every man, and every man`s hand against him; and he shall dwell over against all his brethren.
וְה֤וּא יִהְיֶה֙ פֶּ֣רֶא אָדָ֔ם יָד֣וֹ בַכֹּ֔ל וְיַ֥ד כֹּ֖ל בּ֑וֹ וְעַל־פְּנֵ֥י כָל־אֶחָ֖יו יִשְׁכֹּֽן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

The phrase pere adam — wild-ass of a man — is a hapax compound in the Hebrew Bible. The compound combines pere (wild-ass, Equus hemionus, the Asiatic onager) with adam (man); the precise grammatical relation is appositional or construct, and the conventional ASV wild-ass of a man preserves the ambiguity. The phrase introduces the Ishmael oracle and is one of the chapter's most editorially delicate items because of its downstream reception history.

The pere lexeme. Pere is the standard biblical designation of the wild ass — the standard symbol of untameable wilderness-freedom outside human domestication. The longest pere passage is Job 39:5-8 (the pere who laughs at the multitude of the city / hears not the shouting of the driver), where the wild-ass is the YHWH-speech's exemplar of created creature beyond human management. Cross-corpus distribution: Jer 2:24, 14:6; Hos 8:9; Job 6:5, 11:12, 24:5, 39:5; Ps 104:11; Isa 32:14. The lexeme's semantic core is wild, free, untamed, of the wilderness, outside human ownership — not in itself negative.

Modern critical reading: the wilderness-pastoralist mode-of-life. Westermann (Genesis 12-36, 1985, pp. 245-246), Sarna (JPS Genesis, 1989, p. 121), Wenham (WBC Genesis 16-50, 1994, p. 12), and Hamilton (NICOT Genesis 1-17, 1990, pp. 454-455) all read the verse as the ancestor-oracle of a wilderness-pastoralist mode-of-life: Ishmael will live as a wild, free, untamed pastoralist of the wilderness, not as a settled agriculturalist. The wild-ass is the vehicle of the metaphor, not its negative valence. The second clause (his hand against every man and every man's hand against him) is read as the elaboration of nomadic-pastoralist relations with settled neighbors, not as a moral condemnation. The closing clause's al-pnei kol echav yishkon preserves the geographical-or-relational ambiguity of al-pnei — before / east of (geographical) or in defiance of / in the face of (relational); the ASV over against preserves both readings. The geographical east of reading is supported by the parallel use at Gen 25:18 (the Ishmaelite tribes dwell from Havilah unto Shur ... al-pnei kol echav yipol).

Downstream Islamic reception — flagged, not preempted. The verse has a major reception in Islamic tradition. The Quran identifies Ishmael (Isma'il) as the ancestor of the Arabs and as the firstborn-prophet-son of Ibrahim; the pere adam phrasing is received in Islamic tradition as a positive image of the desert-freedom of the Arab pastoralist heritage. Sura 14:39 (Abraham's prayer of thanks for Ishmael and Isaac); Sura 19:54-55 (Ishmael as prophet); Sura 2:125-129 (Abraham and Ishmael at the Ka'ba). The early-Islamic tafsir tradition reads Ishmael's wilderness-mode-of-life as dignified Arab tribal ancestry. The reception is downstream of the Hebrew text and belongs to Islamic interpretive tradition, not to the philological reading of Gen 16:12. Genesis Rabbah on Ishmael (Gen Rab 45:9-11, 53:11, 71:9) collects a range of Jewish-midrashic readings, some valorizing and some critical; the rabbinic tradition is not monolithic.

Downstream Orientalist reception — flagged for editorial caution. The 19th- and 20th-century Orientalist scholarly tradition read pere adam as a divine prophecy of Arab belligerence and used the reading to underwrite racializing characterizations of Arab peoples. The reading is not in the Hebrew text — the Hebrew text presents the wilderness-pastoralist mode-of-life of an ancestral figure, not a racial prophecy — and modern critical scholarship explicitly rejects the racializing receptions (Said Orientalism, 1978; Knauf 1989; Hamori 2008). The Ishmael oracle is the ancestor-oracle of a historical pastoralist tribal confederation, not a divine sanction of any modern racial-political claim. The WoH translation and apparatus explicitly distance the project from any such reception.

13
And she called the name of YHWH who spoke to her: You are El Ro'i; for she said: Have I even here looked after him that sees me?
And she called the name of Jehovah that spake unto her, Thou art a God that seeth: for she said, Have I even here looked after him that seeth me?
וַתִּקְרָ֤א שֵׁם־יְהוָה֙ הַדֹּבֵ֣ר אֵלֶ֔יהָ אַתָּ֖ה אֵ֣ל רֳאִ֑י כִּ֣י אָֽמְרָ֗ה הֲגַ֥ם הֲלֹ֛ם רָאִ֖יתִי אַחֲרֵ֥י רֹאִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

Verse 13 is the chapter's climactic theological moment and contains two entangled philological cruxes.

First: El Ro'i, the first divine-name-naming by a woman in the Hebrew Bible. Va-tiqra shem-YHWH ha-dover eleha atah El Ro'i — and she called the name of YHWH who spoke to her: You are El Ro'i. The verse is the first occurrence in the Hebrew Bible of a divine-name-naming by a woman, and by a non-Israelite (Egyptian) woman at that. The convention of divine-name-bestowal by patriarchs is well-developed in Genesis (Abram names YHWH El Olam at Gen 21:33), and Hagar's act at 16:13 anticipates this pattern and precedes it in the narrative-chronological order. Trible (Texts of Terror, 1984, pp. 9-35) and Reis (Reading the Lines, 2002) read the Hagar narrative as theologically load-bearing exactly because of this first-divine-name-naming feature. The name combines El (אֵל) with the participial-or-nominal form Ro'i (רֳאִי) from r-ʾ-h (to see); the two main grammatical readings are El who sees / God of seeing (participial) and El of my seeing / El of the vision (nominal). Westermann and Sarna read primarily as El who sees; Speiser reads as El of vision.

Second: the halom / Elohim textual variant. The second clause — ha-gam halom ra'iti acharei ro'i — is one of the most opaque clauses in the Pentateuch. The MT reads halom (here, hither, in this place); on a literal reading, have I even here (or: even still) seen after the one who sees me? (ASV: Have I even here looked after him that seeth me?). The emended reading replaces halom with Elohim (God), giving have I really seen God and remained alive after seeing him? — a reading that parallels the seeing-God-and-living anxiety formula at Exod 33:20 (lo yir'ani ha-adam va-chai), Judg 6:22-23 (Gideon), and Judg 13:22 (Manoah: mot namut ki Elohim ra'inu). The emendation is supported by some LXX witnesses, the Vulgate, Speiser AB, NRSV, and Westermann. The MT reading is preserved by ASV, NJPS, Alter 2004, JPS 1985, NIV, and the MT-priority defenders. Modern critical scholarship is genuinely divided.

The WoH choice: MT preserved. Consistent with established WoH practice (cf. the chapter-15 Tzeboim ketiv/qere resolution), the WoH translation preserves the Masoretic Text reading. The halom is rendered with ASV's Have I even here looked after him that sees me? — preserving the opacity in the English. The emendation is flagged in this commentary and in the el-roi-and-halom-elohim-variant glossary entry but not adopted. The choice is editorially conservative: the MT is the canonical text, and the Elohim-emendation, while defensible, is itself a scholarly reconstruction.

Implications for the messenger-identity question. Hagar identifies the speaker — narrated as mal'akh YHWH throughout vv 7-12 — as YHWH himself (va-tiqra shem-YHWH ha-dover eleha). The identification supports position (b) of the messenger-identity question (the mal'akh IS YHWH), but does not preempt positions (a) or (c) — see the malakh-yhwh-messenger entry. The chapter does not adjudicate; the apparatus presents the three positions in parallel.

14
Therefore the well was called Be'er Lachai Ro'i; behold, it is between Qadesh and Bered.
Wherefore the well was called Beer-lahai-roi; behold, it is between Kadesh and Bered.
עַל־כֵּן֙ קָרָ֣א לַבְּאֵ֔ר בְּאֵ֥ר לַחַ֖י רֹאִ֑י הִנֵּ֥ה בֵין־קָדֵ֖שׁ וּבֵ֥ין בָּֽרֶד׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

The well-name Be'er Lachai Ro'i — well of the Living One who sees me — commemorates the v 13 divine-encounter and the v 13 divine-name El Ro'i. The tripartite construct chain Be'er (well) + Lachai (of the Living One / of the Living) + Ro'i (of seeing me / who sees me) is a compact memorialization of the Hagar encounter — the YHWH who saw Hagar; Hagar who saw YHWH; the well at the site of mutual seeing. The middle element Lachai preserves a Chai (Living) epithet linked to the wider Hebrew Bible El Chai (Living God) tradition (Josh 3:10, 1 Sam 17:26, 2 Kgs 19:4, Ps 42:3, 84:3, Hos 2:1).

Cross-corpus recurrence. The well recurs at Gen 24:62 (v-Yitzchaq ba mi-bo Be'er Lachai Ro'i — Isaac came from going to Beer-Lachai-Roi) and Gen 25:11 (v-yeshev Yitzchaq im Be'er Lachai Ro'i — Isaac dwelt by Beer-Lachai-Roi after Abraham's death). The well is a fixed point in the patriarchal geography and a recurring residence-site for Isaac — an editorially significant inter-narrative connection that the Genesis editor leaves implicit. Sarna and Westermann both note the Hagar-Isaac structural pairing.

The patriarchal well-naming pattern. Be'er Lachai Ro'i is the first well-naming of the patriarchal cycle (preceding Be'er-Sheva at Gen 21:31) and is the only patriarchal well named after a divine-encounter (the others are named after human events: Be'er-Sheva after Abraham-Abimelech covenant, Eseq/Sitnah/Rechovot after Isaac's disputes with the Philistines at Gen 26:20-22).

The geography. The well is located between Qadesh and Bered. Qadesh is identifiable with Kadesh-Barnea (modern Ain Qudeirat in the north-eastern Sinai), the well-attested wilderness oasis on the southern boundary of the patriarchal land. Bered is unidentified.

A foreign-woman-cultic-site register. A small but theologically interesting feature: Be'er Lachai Ro'i is the first patriarchal cultic-site named for a divine-encounter, and it is named by an Egyptian foreign woman. The pattern of foreign-woman-cultic-site-naming is rare in the Hebrew Bible; the Hagar/Be'er-Lachai-Roi case is nearly unique.

15
And Hagar bore Abram a son; and Abram called the name of his son, whom Hagar bore, Yishma'el.
And Hagar bare Abram a son: and Abram called the name of his son, whom Hagar bare, Ishmael.
וַתֵּ֧לֶד הָגָ֛ר לְאַבְרָ֖ם בֵּ֑ן וַיִּקְרָ֨א אַבְרָ֧ם שֶׁם־בְּנ֛וֹ אֲשֶׁר־יָלְדָ֥ה הָגָ֖ר יִשְׁמָעֵֽאל׃
16
And Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Yishma'el to Abram.
And Abram was fourscore and six years old, when Hagar bare Ishmael to Abram.
וְאַבְרָ֕ם בֶּן־שְׁמֹנִ֥ים שָׁנָ֖ה וְשֵׁ֣שׁ שָׁנִ֑ים בְּלֶֽדֶת־הָגָ֥ר אֶת־יִשְׁמָעֵ֖אל לְאַבְרָֽם׃ (ס)
Chapter 17

Genesis 17 — El Shaddai, the covenant of circumcision, and the renaming of Avram and Sarai

1
And when Avram was ninety years old and nine, YHWH appeared to Avram and said to him: I am El Shaddai; walk before me, and be you blameless.
And when Abram was ninety years old and nine, Jehovah appeared to Abram, and said unto him, I am God Almighty; walk before me, and be thou perfect.
וַיְהִ֣י אַבְרָ֔ם בֶּן־תִּשְׁעִ֥ים שָׁנָ֖ה וְתֵ֣שַׁע שָׁנִ֑ים וַיֵּרָ֨א יְהוָ֜ה אֶל־אַבְרָ֗ם וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֵלָיו֙ אֲנִי־אֵ֣ל שַׁדַּ֔י הִתְהַלֵּ֥ךְ לְפָנַ֖י וֶהְיֵ֥ה תָמִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

El Shaddai (אֵל שַׁדַּי) is the chapter's load-bearing divine-name disclosure and the first occurrence of the name in the Hebrew Bible. The verse stages a formal divine self-introduction (ani El Shaddai — I am El Shaddai), the first such ani-formula in the patriarchal narratives. The compound combines the West-Semitic high-god name El with the epithet Shaddai of disputed etymology.

The etymology is genuinely unresolved. Three positions stand in modern critical scholarship: (i) from the verbal root shadad — destroy, be powerful, overpower — yielding El the Mighty / the Destroyer, the etymology behind LXX pantokrator and Vulgate omnipotens and ASV's God Almighty; this is the traditional reading and is grammatically defensible from the root but is not the modern critical preference. (ii) From Akkadian šadû — mountain — yielding El of the mountain, the etymology favoured by Cross (Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic, 1973, pp. 52-60) and Albright (JBL 54, 1935, pp. 173-204); this reading places the patriarchal-El cult in the broader West-Semitic mountain-god type and is supported by the geographic-cultic distribution of the name. (iii) From a West-Semitic divine epithet Šaddai, attested independently in the Deir ʿAlla inscription's shdyn (the shaddayin as a class of divine beings), and reflected in personal-name elements at Mari and elsewhere; Ouellette (RB 76, 1969) and Hess (Israelite Religions, 2007, pp. 142-145) develop this reading. The Deir ʿAlla parallel is the strongest documentary evidence for an independent West-Semitic Šaddai tradition.

The name is the programmatic patriarchal-name register of the P-document. Exod 6:3 states the harmonization explicitly: va-era el-Avraham el-Yitzchaq v-el-Ya'aqov b-El Shaddai u-shemi YHWH lo nodati lahem — I appeared to Avraham, to Yitzchaq, and to Ya'aqov as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known to them. P claims that the patriarchs knew the deity only as El Shaddai; the disclosure of the YHWH name belongs to the Moses-revelation at Exod 3 and 6. The Genesis 17 narrative is the canonical occurrence of P's programmatic claim. The name recurs at Gen 28:3 (Isaac's blessing of Jacob), 35:11 (the Bethel renaming), 43:14 (Jacob's send-off of Benjamin to Egypt), 48:3 (Jacob's blessing of Joseph's sons), 49:25 (Jacob's blessing of Joseph) — six patriarchal occurrences, all in P or P-adjacent material.

The translation preserves the transliterated El Shaddai over ASV's God Almighty, matching the WoH practice with El Elyon at Gen 14:22 and El Ro'i at Gen 16:13. The transliteration preserves the West-Semitic El-compound that the conventional English collapses into a generic divine title.

The imperative pair hithallekh lefanai veheyeh tamim — walk before me and be blameless — combines the patriarchal walking-before-God covenant register (cf. Gen 5:22, 5:24 of Enoch; Gen 6:9 of Noah; Gen 24:40 of Avraham himself in retrospect) with the tamim lexeme. The hithpael hithallekh (iterative-reflexive walk about) carries the sense of habitual conduct of life in the divine presence, not a single act of walking. Tamim (תָּמִים — whole / complete / unblemished / blameless) is the same lexeme that describes Noah at Gen 6:9 and that names the unblemished sacrificial animal at Exod 12:5 and across Leviticus. The covenant-ethical sense is integrity, wholeness, completeness in covenant conduct — not moral perfectionism in the later Christian-theological sense. NJPS reads be wholehearted; the translation preserves ASV's blameless as the closest single-word English match while flagging the moral-perfectionism connotation as a misreading.

2
And I will make my covenant between me and you, and will multiply you exceedingly.
And I will make my covenant between me and thee, and will multiply thee exceedingly.
וְאֶתְּנָ֥ה בְרִיתִ֖י בֵּינִ֣י וּבֵינֶ֑ךָ וְאַרְבֶּ֥ה אוֹתְךָ֖ בִּמְאֹ֥ד מְאֹֽד׃
3
And Avram fell on his face; and Elohim talked with him, saying:
And Abram fell on his face: and God talked with him, saying,
וַיִּפֹּ֥ל אַבְרָ֖ם עַל־פָּנָ֑יו וַיְדַבֵּ֥ר אִתּ֛וֹ אֱלֹהִ֖ים לֵאמֹֽר׃
4
As for me, behold, my covenant is with you, and you shall be the father of a multitude of nations.
As for me, behold, my covenant is with thee, and thou shalt be the father of a multitude of nations.
אֲנִ֕י הִנֵּ֥ה בְרִיתִ֖י אִתָּ֑ךְ וְהָיִ֕יתָ לְאַ֖ב הֲמ֥וֹן גּוֹיִֽם׃
5
Neither shall your name any more be called Avram, but your name shall be Avraham; for the father of a multitude of nations have I made you.
Neither shall thy name any more be called Abram, but thy name shall be Abraham; for the father of a multitude of nations have I made thee.
וְלֹא־יִקָּרֵ֥א ע֛וֹד אֶת־שִׁמְךָ֖ אַבְרָ֑ם וְהָיָ֤ה שִׁמְךָ֙ אַבְרָהָ֔ם כִּ֛י אַב־הֲמ֥וֹן גּוֹיִ֖ם נְתַתִּֽיךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

Avram → Avraham is the first patriarchal name-change in the Hebrew Bible (Ya'aqov → Israel at Gen 32:29 / 35:10 follows; the structurally parallel Sarai → Sarah follows in this same chapter at v 15). The text supplies its own folk-etymology: ki av-hamon goyim n'tatikha — for the father of a multitude of nations have I made you — glossing the new name as av (אַב, father) + hamon (הֲמוֹן, multitude). The folk-etymology is in-text and is the canonical narrative justification.

The philological status of the change is, however, disputed. Most modern Semitic-name scholarship treats Avram and Avraham as dialectal variants of the same West-Semitic personal name, both meaning the (divine) father is exalted. Avram is straightforward — av + ram (exalted), an attested West-Semitic personal-name type (cf. Abi-ram in the Mari onomasticon; the Hebrew Aviram at Num 16:1, 1 Kgs 16:34). Avraham is read by Speiser (Anchor Bible Genesis, 1964, pp. 124-125) and Albright (BASOR 163, 1961, pp. 47-48) as preserving an older or dialectal form Abi-raham, with raham as a by-form of ram (cognate with Arabic raḥuma / rahima on a different reading, or simply a phonological variant). Sarna (JPS Genesis, 1989, pp. 124-125) is more sympathetic to the in-text folk-etymology, reading raham as cognate with Arabic ruhām (multitude) and treating the verse's gloss as philologically defensible. The modern critical consensus tends toward Speiser's dialectal-variant reading: the name-change is not a semantic shift but a status-marker, and the folk-etymology supplies the narrative meaning post hoc.

The translation preserves Avraham in transliteration consistent with WoH practice across the patriarchal corpus (Mitsrayim, Kena'an, Yishma'el, Yitzchaq); the conventional English Abraham would silently re-import the Christian-tradition phonology. The in-text folk-etymology is preserved literally in the English (the father of a multitude of nations).

6
And I will make you exceedingly fruitful, and I will make of you nations, and kings shall come out of you.
And I will make thee exceeding fruitful, and I will make nations of thee, and kings shall come out of thee.
וְהִפְרֵתִ֤י אֹֽתְךָ֙ בִּמְאֹ֣ד מְאֹ֔ד וּנְתַתִּ֖יךָ לְגוֹיִ֑ם וּמְלָכִ֖ים מִמְּךָ֥ יֵצֵֽאוּ׃
7
And I will establish my covenant between me and you and your seed after you throughout their generations for an everlasting covenant, to be a God to you and to your seed after you.
And I will establish my covenant between me and thee and thy seed after thee throughout their generations for an everlasting covenant, to be a God unto thee and to thy seed after thee.
וַהֲקִמֹתִ֨י אֶת־בְּרִיתִ֜י בֵּינִ֣י וּבֵינֶ֗ךָ וּבֵ֨ין זַרְעֲךָ֧ אַחֲרֶ֛יךָ לְדֹרֹתָ֖ם לִבְרִ֣ית עוֹלָ֑ם לִהְי֤וֹת לְךָ֙ לֵֽאלֹהִ֔ים וּֽלְזַרְעֲךָ֖ אַחֲרֶֽיךָ׃
8
And I will give to you, and to your seed after you, the land of your sojournings, all the land of Kena'an, for an everlasting possession; and I will be their God.
And I will give unto thee, and to thy seed after thee, the land of thy sojournings, all the land of Canaan, for an everlasting possession; and I will be their God.
וְנָתַתִּ֣י לְ֠ךָ וּלְזַרְעֲךָ֨ אַחֲרֶ֜יךָ אֵ֣ת ׀ אֶ֣רֶץ מְגֻרֶ֗יךָ אֵ֚ת כָּל־אֶ֣רֶץ כְּנַ֔עַן לַאֲחֻזַּ֖ת עוֹלָ֑ם וְהָיִ֥יתִי לָהֶ֖ם לֵאלֹהִֽים׃
9
And Elohim said to Avraham: And as for you, you shall keep my covenant, you, and your seed after you throughout their generations.
And God said unto Abraham, And as for thee, thou shalt keep my covenant, thou, and thy seed after thee throughout their generations.
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֱלֹהִים֙ אֶל־אַבְרָהָ֔ם וְאַתָּ֖ה אֶת־בְּרִיתִ֣י תִשְׁמֹ֑ר אַתָּ֛ה וְזַרְעֲךָ֥ אַֽחֲרֶ֖יךָ לְדֹרֹתָֽם׃
10
This is my covenant, which you shall keep, between me and you and your seed after you: every male among you shall be circumcised.
This is my covenant, which ye shall keep, between me and you and thy seed after thee: every male among you shall be circumcised.
זֹ֣את בְּרִיתִ֞י אֲשֶׁ֣ר תִּשְׁמְר֗וּ בֵּינִי֙ וּבֵ֣ינֵיכֶ֔ם וּבֵ֥ין זַרְעֲךָ֖ אַחֲרֶ֑יךָ הִמּ֥וֹל לָכֶ֖ם כָּל־זָכָֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

Brit milah — the covenant of circumcision. The institution introduced at vv 10-14 is the first explicit covenant-sign-on-the-body in the Hebrew Bible and the load-bearing identity-marker of the Abrahamic covenant in its P-document form. The structural opening zot briti asher tishmru — this is my covenant which you shall keep — is a P-document formal-prescription opening (cf. Exod 12:43 of the Passover; Lev 11:2 of the dietary laws); it stages the institution as canonical legislation, not as a one-off command.

The legal apparatus across vv 10-14 contains four canonical elements that the later rabbinic halakhah treats as load-bearing: (i) the eighth-day timing of v 12 (u-ven shmonat yamim yimol — formalized at Lev 12:3); (ii) the household-slave inclusion of vv 12-13 (the yelid bayit and miknat kesef categories, on which see the v 12 ANE-comparative note in the broader glossary); (iii) the karet penalty of v 14 (cross-corpus standard P/H sanction; see commentary at v 14); (iv) the covenant-in-flesh formula of v 13 (v-hayeta briti b-vsarkhem li-vrit olam — the inscription is permanent and the covenant unbounded in time).

The ANE comparative material is substantial. Egyptian circumcision is attested from the Sixth Dynasty (Herodotus II.36-37 reports it as a Levantine custom learned from Egypt; the Karnak temple reliefs depict the procedure; Sasson, JBL 85 [1966] pp. 473-476, surveys the evidence). Bergmann's Circumcision in Antiquity (1968) collects the broader Bronze Age evidence across the Levant. West-Semitic circumcision is attested independently of Israel; the contrast with the uncircumcised Philistines (arelim, recurring across Judg 14:3, 15:18, 1 Sam 14:6, 17:26, 17:36, 31:4, 2 Sam 1:20) is editorial within the Hebrew Bible, not absolute across the ANE — Egypt, Phoenicia, and Edom all practiced circumcision in some form. What distinguishes the Israelite institution is the eighth-day infant timing (other ANE cultures circumcised at puberty or initiation, not in infancy) and the covenant-sign theological framing (other ANE cultures had circumcision as a rite of passage or purity, not as a covenant-marker).

The documentary-hypothesis stratification places Gen 17 in P (Priestly), and the covenant-of-circumcision is P's distinctive Abrahamic-covenant marker — structurally distinct from J/E's covenant-between-the-pieces in Gen 15. Friedman (Who Wrote the Bible?, 1987, pp. 169-178) treats the chapter as a paradigmatic P-document composition; Knohl (The Sanctuary of Silence, 1995) refines the P/H stratification within the Priestly material.

The downstream reception is high-volume and editorially sensitive. The Maccabean revolt was triggered in part by Antiochus IV's circumcision-decree (1 Macc 1:48, 60-61). Paul's anti-circumcision argument in Galatians 2-5 and Romans 2:25-29 / 4:9-12 inaugurates a long Christian theological position on circumcision-as-superseded; this reception is downstream of the Hebrew text and belongs to first-century Pauline interpretation, not to the Gen 17 institution itself. Cohen (The Beginnings of Jewishness, 1996) traces the long second-temple-to-rabbinic development of the institution as Jewish identity-marker. The glossary entry presents the Hebrew Bible material in its own register and flags the downstream Christian and rabbinic receptions as downstream — neither preempted nor adjudicated.

11
And you shall be circumcised in the flesh of your foreskin; and it shall be a sign of a covenant between me and you.
And ye shall be circumcised in the flesh of your foreskin; and it shall be a token of a covenant betwixt me and you.
וּנְמַלְתֶּ֕ם אֵ֖ת בְּשַׂ֣ר עָרְלַתְכֶ֑ם וְהָיָה֙ לְא֣וֹת בְּרִ֔ית בֵּינִ֖י וּבֵינֵיכֶֽם׃
12
And he that is eight days old shall be circumcised among you, every male throughout your generations, he that is born in the house, or bought with money of any foreigner that is not of your seed.
And he that is eight days old shall be circumcised among you, every male throughout your generations, he that is born in the house, or bought with money of any foreigner that is not of thy seed.
וּבֶן־שְׁמֹנַ֣ת יָמִ֗ים יִמּ֥וֹל לָכֶ֛ם כָּל־זָכָ֖ר לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶ֑ם יְלִ֣יד בָּ֔יִת וּמִקְנַת־כֶּ֙סֶף֙ מִכֹּ֣ל בֶּן־נֵכָ֔ר אֲשֶׁ֛ר לֹ֥א מִֽזַּרְעֲךָ֖ הֽוּא׃
13
He that is born in your house, and he that is bought with your money, must surely be circumcised; and my covenant shall be in your flesh for an everlasting covenant.
He that is born in thy house, and he that is bought with thy money, must needs be circumcised: and my covenant shall be in your flesh for an everlasting covenant.
הִמּ֧וֹל ׀ יִמּ֛וֹל יְלִ֥יד בֵּֽיתְךָ֖ וּמִקְנַ֣ת כַּסְפֶּ֑ךָ וְהָיְתָ֧ה בְרִיתִ֛י בִּבְשַׂרְכֶ֖ם לִבְרִ֥ית עוֹלָֽם׃
14
And the uncircumcised male who is not circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin, that nefesh shall be cut off from his people; he has broken my covenant.
And the uncircumcised male who is not circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin, that soul shall be cut off from his people; he hath broken my covenant.
וְעָרֵ֣ל ׀ זָכָ֗ר אֲשֶׁ֤ר לֹֽא־יִמּוֹל֙ אֶת־בְּשַׂ֣ר עָרְלָת֔וֹ וְנִכְרְתָ֛ה הַנֶּ֥פֶשׁ הַהִ֖וא מֵעַמֶּ֑יהָ אֶת־בְּרִיתִ֖י הֵפַֽר׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 14

The karet penalty. V-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi me-ameha — that nefesh shall be cut off from his people — is the chapter's only direct legal sanction and the first karet formula in the Hebrew Bible. The construction is niphal of karat (cut, sever) with ha-nefesh (the nefesh, the life / person) as subject and me-ameha (from her peoples, plural) as the standard adverbial complement; the closing clause et briti hefar (my covenant he has broken) is the formal violation-statement.

The karet formula is the standard P/H covenant-violation sanction and recurs across the Pentateuch at roughly 30 verses: at Exod 12:15, 12:19 (leavened bread at Passover), 30:33, 30:38 (incense-oil violations), 31:14 (Sabbath violation); across Lev 7:20-21, 7:25, 7:27 (sacrificial blood violations), 17:4, 17:9, 17:10, 17:14 (blood prohibition), 18:29 (forbidden sexual relations summary), 19:8 (leftover sacrifice), 20:3, 20:5, 20:6, 20:17, 20:18 (various capital and quasi-capital violations), 22:3 (priestly impurity), 23:29 (Day of Atonement non-affliction); at Num 9:13 (Passover non-observance), 15:30-31 (high-handed sin), 19:13, 19:20 (corpse impurity). The distribution across the H (Holiness Code) and P strata is the standard documentary marker of the karet sanction as a P/H legal-theological category.

The nature of the penalty is debated. (i) Divine-direct execution: Milgrom (Anchor Bible Leviticus 1-16, 1991, pp. 457-460, Excursus on karet) reads karet as direct divine punishment, typically premature death and extinction of line; the agent is YHWH, not the human court. (ii) Excommunication from community: Phillips 1970 reads karet as juridical excommunication enforced by the community. (iii) Premature death: Tigay (JPS Deuteronomy, 1996) reads karet as shortened life-span without descendants. (iv) Extinction of line: Levine (JPS Leviticus, 1989) reads karet as the cutting-off of progeny — the violator's line ends. Wold (The Meaning of the Biblical Penalty Kareth, 1979 dissertation) is the standard monograph and surveys the positions. The modern critical consensus is closer to Milgrom's reading (divine-direct execution, premature death and extinction of line) than to the excommunication-reading; the formula's distribution across cultic violations (where no human court would act) supports the divine-agent reading.

The translation preserves nefesh untranslated per the WoH chapter-2 convention (preserving the Hebrew lexeme over ASV's soul and NRSV's person), and renders v-nikhretah ... me-ameha with the literal shall be cut off from his people.

15
And Elohim said to Avraham: As for Sarai your wife, you shall not call her name Sarai, but Sarah shall her name be.
And God said unto Abraham, As for Sarai thy wife, thou shalt not call her name Sarai, but Sarah shall her name be.
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֱלֹהִים֙ אֶל־אַבְרָהָ֔ם שָׂרַ֣י אִשְׁתְּךָ֔ לֹא־תִקְרָ֥א אֶת־שְׁמָ֖הּ שָׂרָ֑י כִּ֥י שָׂרָ֖ה שְׁמָֽהּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 15

Sarai → Sarah. Unlike the Avram → Avraham change at v 5, the text supplies no in-text rationale; the change is presented bare. Philologically, both Sarai (שָׂרַי) and Sarah (שָׂרָה) derive from the same root s-r-r (rule, exercise authority, be princess); both names mean princess / noblewoman. The morphological difference is in the feminine ending: Sarai ends in a final yod (possibly an archaic feminine ending, possibly a possessive my princess); Sarah ends in a final he (the standard West-Semitic feminine ending). Sarna (JPS Genesis, 1989, p. 126) puts the consensus cleanly: both names mean princess; the change is purely a formal sign of new covenantal status. The change is a status-marker, not a semantic redefinition.

The paired-with-Avraham structure is editorially deliberate. The chapter performs both name-changes within the same speech-act sequence (Avraham at v 5, Sarah at v 15), framing them as joint covenantal renamings. The narrative pattern foreshadows the Ya'aqov → Israel renaming at Gen 32:29 / 35:10, the only other patriarchal name-change in Genesis.

16
And I will bless her, and moreover I will give you a son by her; yes, I will bless her, and she shall be a mother of nations; kings of peoples shall be of her.
And I will bless her, and moreover I will give thee a son of her: yea, I will bless her, and she shall be [a mother of] nations; kings of peoples shall be of her.
וּבֵרַכְתִּ֣י אֹתָ֔הּ וְגַ֨ם נָתַ֧תִּי מִמֶּ֛נָּה לְךָ֖ בֵּ֑ן וּבֵֽרַכְתִּ֙יהָ֙ וְהָֽיְתָ֣ה לְגוֹיִ֔ם מַלְכֵ֥י עַמִּ֖ים מִמֶּ֥נָּה יִהְיֽוּ׃
17
Then Avraham fell on his face, and laughed, and said in his heart: Shall a child be born to him that is a hundred years old? And shall Sarah, that is ninety years old, bear?
Then Abraham fell upon his face, and laughed, and said in his heart, Shall a child be born unto him that is a hundred years old? and shall Sarah, that is ninety years old, bear?
וַיִּפֹּ֧ל אַבְרָהָ֛ם עַל־פָּנָ֖יו וַיִּצְחָ֑ק וַיֹּ֣אמֶר בְּלִבּ֗וֹ הַלְּבֶ֤ן מֵאָֽה־שָׁנָה֙ יִוָּלֵ֔ד וְאִ֨ם־שָׂרָ֔ה הֲבַת־תִּשְׁעִ֥ים שָׁנָ֖ה תֵּלֵֽד׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

Yitzchaq — the laughter pattern. The verb va-yitzchaq (qal of tzachaq — to laugh) is the same root as the name Yitzchaq (יִצְחָק — he will laugh / let him laugh), the future-name of the son disclosed at v 19. The text is staging a deliberate name-etymology word-play: Avraham laughs (va-yitzchaq) at the very moment the name Yitzchaq is about to be announced. The play is the first of three laughter-Isaac plays across the Avraham cycle.

The three instances form a structural triad: (i) 17:17 — Avraham's laugh, internal (va-yomer b-libo), at the announcement of Isaac's conception; framed as disbelief-laugh, followed by the halben me'ah shanah yivaled objection. (ii) 18:12-15 — Sarah's laugh, equally internal (va-titzchaq Sarah b-qirbah), equally at the announcement, equally followed by an objection-and-denial sequence. (iii) 21:6 — Sarah's tzachoq word-play at Isaac's birth: tzachoq asah li Elohim kol ha-shomea yitzchaq li — Elohim has made me laughter; everyone who hears will laugh with me; here the laughter is finally joy-laughter, not disbelief.

The pattern's editorial function is the inversion-of-disbelief: the first two laughs are disbelief at the impossibility of conception in old age; the third laugh is the post-conception joy that reverses the disbelief. The name Yitzchaq compresses the whole trajectory. The translation preserves the implicit play (ASV-baseline and laughed) rather than flagging it parenthetically; the cross-corpus pattern is carried in the glossary entry.

The falling-on-the-face posture (va-yipol Avraham al-panav) repeats v 3 but with a new register: the v 3 falling is reverence at the divine self-introduction; the v 17 falling is partly reverence and partly the disbelief-response to the conception announcement. The same physical posture frames two different inner states.

18
And Avraham said to the Elohim: Oh that Yishma'el might live before you!
And Abraham said unto God, Oh that Ishmael might live before thee!
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר אַבְרָהָ֖ם אֶל־הָֽאֱלֹהִ֑ים ל֥וּ יִשְׁמָעֵ֖אל יִחְיֶ֥ה לְפָנֶֽיךָ׃
19
And Elohim said: No, but Sarah your wife shall bear you a son; and you shall call his name Yitzchaq; and I will establish my covenant with him for an everlasting covenant for his seed after him.
And God said, Nay, but Sarah thy wife shall bear thee a son; and thou shalt call his name Isaac: and I will establish my covenant with him for an everlasting covenant for his seed after him.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֗ים אֲבָל֙ שָׂרָ֣ה אִשְׁתְּךָ֗ יֹלֶ֤דֶת לְךָ֙ בֵּ֔ן וְקָרָ֥אתָ אֶת־שְׁמ֖וֹ יִצְחָ֑ק וַהֲקִמֹתִ֨י אֶת־בְּרִיתִ֥י אִתּ֛וֹ לִבְרִ֥ית עוֹלָ֖ם לְזַרְע֥וֹ אַחֲרָֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 19

The covenant distinction: Yitzchaq, not Yishma'el. The verse contains the chapter's most consequential theological move: the covenant-bearing line is named — Yitzchaq — and the covenant is explicitly directed to him and his seed (va-haqimoti et briti ito li-vrit olam l-zaro acharav). Avraham's plea at v 18 (lu Yishma'el yichye lefanekha — oh that Yishma'el might live before you) is met not with refusal but with a deliberate redirection: the brit olam line passes through Yitzchaq, the son not yet conceived; Yishma'el is blessed (v 20) but not covenant-bearing.

The editorial logic is structurally important across the Genesis-to-Exodus arc: the covenant is not transmitted by primogeniture but by election. The same pattern recurs at Yitzchaq → Ya'aqov (over Esav, Gen 25-28) and at Ya'aqov → Yosef / Yehuda (over the older brothers). The Yishma'el / Yitzchaq distinction at 17:19-21 inaugurates the pattern.

The verb haqimoti (hiphil of qum, establish) — see the cross-corpus meqim-brit entry — is the technical P-document covenant-establishment verb, here applied specifically to the Yitzchaq line. The covenant-verb selection is precise: with Yitzchaq the covenant will be established (heqim); with Yishma'el (v 20) blessings are given but no brit is established.

20
And as for Yishma'el, I have heard you: behold, I have blessed him, and will make him fruitful, and will multiply him exceedingly; twelve princes shall he beget, and I will make him a great nation.
And as for Ishmael, I have heard thee: behold, I have blessed him, and will make him fruitful, and will multiply him exceedingly; twelve princes shall he beget, and I will make him a great nation.
וּֽלְיִשְׁמָעֵ֘אל שְׁמַעְתִּיךָ֒ הִנֵּ֣ה ׀ בֵּרַ֣כְתִּי אֹת֗וֹ וְהִפְרֵיתִ֥י אֹת֛וֹ וְהִרְבֵּיתִ֥י אֹת֖וֹ בִּמְאֹ֣ד מְאֹ֑ד שְׁנֵים־עָשָׂ֤ר נְשִׂיאִם֙ יוֹלִ֔יד וּנְתַתִּ֖יו לְג֥וֹי גָּדֽוֹל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

The twelve princes of Yishma'el. Shneim asar nesi'im yolid — twelve princes shall he beget — is the source-text for the Gen 25:13-16 Ishmaelite genealogy: Nebayot, Qedar, Adbeel, Mibsam, Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema, Yetur, Naphish, Qedmah. The nasi lexeme (נָשִׂיא — lifted-up-one, chieftain, prince) is the standard P/Numbers term for tribal chieftain (cf. the twelve nesi'im of Israel at Num 1:5-15, 2:3-31, 7:11-83). The structural parallelism between twelve-tribes-of-Israel and twelve-princes-of-Ishmael is editorially deliberate: Yishma'el is a peer tribal-confederation, not a sub-Israelite category.

Knauf (Ismael, 1989) reconstructs the historical Ishmaelite confederation as a North-Arabian camel-pastoralist league active in the 8th-6th centuries BCE. The Gen 25:13-16 list overlaps with attested North-Arabian tribal names from the Neo-Assyrian and Neo-Babylonian record: Tema (Tayma), Dumah (Adummatu), Adbeel (Idiba'ilu), Qedar (Qidru), Nebayot (Nabaiati). The historical reconstruction supports reading the Yishma'el genealogy as preserving real Late-Iron-Age tribal data, not as ad hoc fiction.

The verse's shema'ti-kha (I have heard you) on Yishma'el's behalf is a deliberate verbal play on Yishma'el's name (see the yishmael-name-etymology glossary entry): YHWH hears Avraham's plea on behalf of the son whose very name memorializes a previous instance of divine hearing (Gen 16:11 ki shama YHWH el-onyekh). The two shama moments bracket the Yishma'el-narrative: at his naming, YHWH heard Hagar's affliction; at his father's plea, YHWH hears Avraham's intercession. The blessing-but-not-covenant distinction is then layered onto the shama repetition: hearing is honored; covenant is reserved.

21
But my covenant will I establish with Yitzchaq, whom Sarah shall bear to you at this set time in the next year.
But my covenant will I establish with Isaac, whom Sarah shall bear unto thee at this set time in the next year.
וְאֶת־בְּרִיתִ֖י אָקִ֣ים אֶת־יִצְחָ֑ק אֲשֶׁר֩ תֵּלֵ֨ד לְךָ֤ שָׂרָה֙ לַמּוֹעֵ֣ד הַזֶּ֔ה בַּשָּׁנָ֖ה הָאַחֶֽרֶת׃
22
And he finished speaking with him, and Elohim went up from Avraham.
And he left off talking with him, and God went up from Abraham.
וַיְכַ֖ל לְדַבֵּ֣ר אִתּ֑וֹ וַיַּ֣עַל אֱלֹהִ֔ים מֵעַ֖ל אַבְרָהָֽם׃
23
And Avraham took Yishma'el his son, and all that were born in his house, and all that were bought with his money, every male among the men of Avraham's house, and circumcised the flesh of their foreskin in the selfsame day, as Elohim had spoken to him.
And Abraham took Ishmael his son, and all that were born in his house, and all that were bought with his money, every male among the men of Abraham`s house, and circumcised the flesh of their foreskin in the selfsame day, as God had said unto him.
וַיִּקַּ֨ח אַבְרָהָ֜ם אֶת־יִשְׁמָעֵ֣אל בְּנ֗וֹ וְאֵ֨ת כָּל־יְלִידֵ֤י בֵיתוֹ֙ וְאֵת֙ כָּל־מִקְנַ֣ת כַּסְפּ֔וֹ כָּל־זָכָ֕ר בְּאַנְשֵׁ֖י בֵּ֣ית אַבְרָהָ֑ם וַיָּ֜מָל אֶת־בְּשַׂ֣ר עָרְלָתָ֗ם בְּעֶ֙צֶם֙ הַיּ֣וֹם הַזֶּ֔ה כַּאֲשֶׁ֛ר דִּבֶּ֥ר אִתּ֖וֹ אֱלֹהִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

The same-day-compliance formula. B-etzem ha-yom ha-ze (בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה — literally in the very bone of this day, idiomatically on this very day, on this selfsame day) is the P-document's formal same-day-event marker. The formula recurs at Gen 7:13 (Noah enters the ark on the selfsame day the flood begins), Exod 12:17 / 12:41 / 12:51 (Exodus-day markers), Lev 23:14 / 23:21 / 23:28 / 23:29 / 23:30 (festival-day same-day markers), Deut 32:48 (the day of Moses' death-announcement), Josh 5:11 (first Passover in the land), Ezek 2:3 / 24:2 / 40:1. The distribution is characteristically P/H — the formula is one of the documentary markers of Priestly-stratum composition.

The formula's editorial function is to mark moments of formal covenantal or institutional compliance: the institution is given, and on the very day the addressee acts to comply. The double occurrence here (v 23 and v 26) emphasizes the same-day timing: Avraham circumcises Yishma'el, the household slaves, and himself on the day the command is given, without delay. The compliance pattern is canonical patriarchal obedience — paired with Avraham's immediate compliance at Gen 12:4 (va-yelekh Avram) and Gen 22:3 (va-yashkem Avraham ba-boqer, the binding-of-Isaac). The ASV's in the selfsame day preserves the formula; on this very day (NRSV/NIV) modernizes the idiom; the most literal on the bone of this day exposes the etzem metaphor but reads opaquely in English.

24
And Avraham was ninety years old and nine, when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin.
And Abraham was ninety years old and nine, when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin.
וְאַ֨בְרָהָ֔ם בֶּן־תִּשְׁעִ֥ים וָתֵ֖שַׁע שָׁנָ֑ה בְּהִמֹּל֖וֹ בְּשַׂ֥ר עָרְלָתֽוֹ׃
25
And Yishma'el his son was thirteen years old, when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin.
And Ishmael his son was thirteen years old, when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin.
וְיִשְׁמָעֵ֣אל בְּנ֔וֹ בֶּן־שְׁלֹ֥שׁ עֶשְׂרֵ֖ה שָׁנָ֑ה בְּהִ֨מֹּל֔וֹ אֵ֖ת בְּשַׂ֥ר עָרְלָתֽוֹ׃
26
In the selfsame day was Avraham circumcised, and Yishma'el his son.
In the selfsame day was Abraham circumcised, and Ishmael his son.
בְּעֶ֙צֶם֙ הַיּ֣וֹם הַזֶּ֔ה נִמּ֖וֹל אַבְרָהָ֑ם וְיִשְׁמָעֵ֖אל בְּנֽוֹ׃
27
And all the men of his house, those born in the house, and those bought with money of a foreigner, were circumcised with him.
And all the men of his house, those born in the house, and those bought with money of a foreigner, were circumcised with him.
וְכָל־אַנְשֵׁ֤י בֵיתוֹ֙ יְלִ֣יד בָּ֔יִת וּמִקְנַת־כֶּ֖סֶף מֵאֵ֣ת בֶּן־נֵכָ֑ר נִמֹּ֖לוּ אִתּֽוֹ׃ (פ)
Chapter 18

Genesis 18 — The three visitors at Mamre and Avraham's intercession for Sodom

1
And YHWH appeared to him by the oaks of Mamre, as he sat at the entrance of the tent in the heat of the day.
And Jehovah appeared unto him by the oaks of Mamre, as he sat in the tent door in the heat of the day;
וַיֵּרָ֤א אֵלָיו֙ יְהוָ֔ה בְּאֵלֹנֵ֖י מַמְרֵ֑א וְה֛וּא יֹשֵׁ֥ב פֶּֽתַח־הָאֹ֖הֶל כְּחֹ֥ם הַיּֽוֹם׃
2
And he lifted up his eyes and looked, and, behold, three men stood over against him; and when he saw them, he ran to meet them from the entrance of the tent, and bowed himself to the earth,
and he lifted up his eyes and looked, and, lo, three men stood over against him: and when he saw them, he ran to meet them from the tent door, and bowed himself to the earth,
וַיִּשָּׂ֤א עֵינָיו֙ וַיַּ֔רְא וְהִנֵּה֙ שְׁלֹשָׁ֣ה אֲנָשִׁ֔ים נִצָּבִ֖ים עָלָ֑יו וַיַּ֗רְא וַיָּ֤רָץ לִקְרָאתָם֙ מִפֶּ֣תַח הָאֹ֔הֶל וַיִּשְׁתַּ֖חוּ אָֽרְצָה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

Shloshah anashim nitzavim alav — three men stood over against him. The v 1 frame announces a YHWH-theophany (singular); v 2 immediately reframes the appearance as three men. The narrative then sustains the singular-plural oscillation across the chapter without editorial smoothing: Avraham addresses one of the three as singular adonai (v 3), then pivots to plural imperatives directed at all three (v 4 wash your feet, v 5 and they said); a singular voice speaks at v 10; YHWH is named again at v 13; the men (plural) rise at v 16; v 22 splits the figures explicitly — the men turn toward Sodom while Avraham still stood before YHWH. The two of the three then arrive at Sodom as shnei ha-malakhim (Gen 19:1; cross-reference to the malakh-yhwh-messenger entry).

The chapter's singular-plural oscillation is one of the most-discussed theophanies in the entire patriarchal corpus. Westermann (Genesis 12-36, Hermeneia 1985, pp. 271-281) reads the scene as a J-document theophany in which YHWH and the three men are not separable into distinct beings but are alternative narrative perspectives on a single visitation; Sarna (JPS Genesis 1989, pp. 128-131) treats the oscillation as a deliberate literary effect that lets the visit be experienced first as human hospitality and then as theophany; Letellier (Day in Mamre, Night in Sodom, 1995) reads the chapter as the day-half of a chiastic Mamre/Sodom diptych; Hamori (When Gods Were Men, BZAW 384, 2008, chs 3-4) develops the anthropomorphic realism reading on which the J-source presents YHWH and his attendants in concretely human form; Sommer (The Bodies of God and the World of Ancient Israel, 2009) develops the fluidity model on which the divine self can have multiple bodies or manifestations simultaneously. The translation preserves the oscillation literally.

Downstream reception is flagged but not preempted. The Christian hospitality of strangers reading derives from Heb 13:2 (do not forget to entertain strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels unawares) — explicitly the Gen 18 scene. The patristic and Eastern-Orthodox Trinity of Mamre reading (cf. Augustine De Trinitate II.10-11; the iconographic tradition culminating in Andrei Rublev's icon of 1411) reads the three men as a Trinitarian prefiguration. The Hebrew text supports none of these readings and refuses none of them; the WoH translation preserves the Hebrew oscillation and lets the receptions be carried at the apparatus level.

3
and said: My lord, if now I have found favor in your sight, do not pass on, I pray you, from your servant.
and said, My lord, if now I have found favor in thy sight, pass not away, I pray thee, from thy servant:
וַיֹּאמַ֑ר אֲדֹנָ֗י אִם־נָ֨א מָצָ֤אתִי חֵן֙ בְּעֵינֶ֔יךָ אַל־נָ֥א תַעֲבֹ֖ר מֵעַ֥ל עַבְדֶּֽךָ׃
4
Let now a little water be fetched, and wash your feet, and rest yourselves under the tree.
let now a little water be fetched, and wash your feet, and rest yourselves under the tree:
יֻקַּֽח־נָ֣א מְעַט־מַ֔יִם וְרַחֲצ֖וּ רַגְלֵיכֶ֑ם וְהִֽשָּׁעֲנ֖וּ תַּ֥חַת הָעֵֽץ׃
5
And I will fetch a morsel of bread, and you shall sustain your heart; afterward you shall pass on, since you have come over to your servant. And they said: So do, as you have spoken.
and I will fetch a morsel of bread, and strengthen ye your heart; after that ye shall pass on: forasmuch as ye are come to your servant. And they said, So do, as thou hast said.
וְאֶקְחָ֨ה פַת־לֶ֜חֶם וְסַעֲד֤וּ לִבְּכֶם֙ אַחַ֣ר תַּעֲבֹ֔רוּ כִּֽי־עַל־כֵּ֥ן עֲבַרְתֶּ֖ם עַֽל־עַבְדְּכֶ֑ם וַיֹּ֣אמְר֔וּ כֵּ֥ן תַּעֲשֶׂ֖ה כַּאֲשֶׁ֥ר דִּבַּֽרְתָּ׃
6
And Avraham hastened into the tent to Sarah, and said: Make ready quickly three measures of fine meal, knead it, and make cakes.
And Abraham hastened into the tent unto Sarah, and said, Make ready quickly three measures of fine meal, knead it, and make cakes.
וַיְמַהֵ֧ר אַבְרָהָ֛ם הָאֹ֖הֱלָה אֶל־שָׂרָ֑ה וַיֹּ֗אמֶר מַהֲרִ֞י שְׁלֹ֤שׁ סְאִים֙ קֶ֣מַח סֹ֔לֶת ל֖וּשִׁי וַעֲשִׂ֥י עֻגֽוֹת׃
7
And Avraham ran to the herd, and took a calf tender and good, and gave it to the servant; and he hastened to dress it.
And Abraham ran unto the herd, and fetched a calf tender and good, and gave it unto the servant; and he hasted to dress it.
וְאֶל־הַבָּקָ֖ר רָ֣ץ אַבְרָהָ֑ם וַיִּקַּ֨ח בֶּן־בָּקָ֜ר רַ֤ךְ וָטוֹב֙ וַיִּתֵּ֣ן אֶל־הַנַּ֔עַר וַיְמַהֵ֖ר לַעֲשׂ֥וֹת אֹתֽוֹ׃
8
And he took curds, and milk, and the calf which he had dressed, and set it before them; and he stood by them under the tree, and they ate.
And he took butter, and milk, and the calf which he had dressed, and set it before them; and he stood by them under the tree, and they did eat.
וַיִּקַּ֨ח חֶמְאָ֜ה וְחָלָ֗ב וּבֶן־הַבָּקָר֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר עָשָׂ֔ה וַיִּתֵּ֖ן לִפְנֵיהֶ֑ם וְהֽוּא־עֹמֵ֧ד עֲלֵיהֶ֛ם תַּ֥חַת הָעֵ֖ץ וַיֹּאכֵֽלוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

Va-yokhelu — and they ate. The chapter's most concretely anthropomorphic moment. Avraham serves curds (chem'ah), milk, and the prepared calf; the visitors eat. The Hebrew is direct and unhedged: va-yokhelu, qal imperfect waw-consecutive 3mp of akhal — the ordinary verb for human eating. The narrative does not editorialize, qualify, or interpose any hedge.

The eating-of-visitors-by-Lot is repeated unhedged at Gen 19:3 (va-yokhelu again, the same verb form). The contrast within the corpus is sharp at Judg 13:15-20, where Manoah offers a meal to the mal'akh YHWH and is refused: if you detain me I will not eat of your bread; the messenger ascends in the flame of the altar instead. The second-Temple Tobit reception (Tob 12:19 LXX) offers an explicit harmonizing gloss: all those days I appeared to eat and drink with you, but I did not eat or drink — it was a vision. The Gen 18 / Gen 19 text knows nothing of this harmonization and preserves the unhedged eating.

Mainstream scholarly treatments: Hamori (When Gods Were Men, 2008, chs 3-4) develops the anthropomorphic realism of the J-source on which divine visitors in Genesis are presented in concretely human form; Sommer (The Bodies of God, 2009) develops the fluidity model on which divine bodies are real bodies; Westermann (Genesis 12-36, 1985, pp. 278-279) reads the meal as the cultural-anthropological hospitality scene's normal completion. ASV-baseline preserved (and they did eat); the WoH translation preserves the unhedged form.

9
And they said to him: Where is Sarah your wife? And he said: Behold, in the tent.
And they said unto him, Where is Sarah thy wife? And he said, Behold, in the tent.
וַיֹּאמְר֣וּ אֵׄלָׄ֔יׄוׄ אַיֵּ֖ה שָׂרָ֣ה אִשְׁתֶּ֑ךָ וַיֹּ֖אמֶר הִנֵּ֥ה בָאֹֽהֶל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The Masoretic text preserves four supralinear dots over the letters of elav (אֵׄלָׄיׄוׄ — to him) at v 9. The marks are one of the canonical fifteen puncta extraordinaria (nequdot) of the Hebrew Bible (the standard list: Gen 16:5, 18:9, 19:33, 33:4, 37:12; Num 3:39, 9:10, 21:30, 29:15; Deut 29:28; 2 Sam 19:20; Isa 44:9; Ezek 41:20, 46:22; Ps 27:13). The dots are preserved without editorial alteration; their function is contested.

Two readings circulate. The traditional rabbinic reading (Soferim 6:3; Bereshit Rabbah 48:15; b. Bava Metzia 87a) treats the dots as marking a textual reservation: the visitors knew Sarah was inside but asked the question for Avraham's sake (or, in an alternate midrash, they asked one another rather than only Avraham — to him is dotted because it is not strictly accurate). The modern text-critical reading (Tov Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible 3rd ed. 2012, §6.B.5; McCarthy The Tiqqune Sopherim OBO 36, 1981; Yeivin Introduction to the Tiberian Masorah 1980) treats the puncta as the Masoretes' preservation of an ancient editorial flag — most likely marking words whose presence in the text was uncertain to early scribes. The dots are thus the Masoretic apparatus's notation of editorial doubt rather than a doctrinal claim. The ASV-baseline English (and they said to him) is preserved; the dots are recorded in the apparatus.

10
And he said: I will certainly return to you when the season comes around; and, behold, Sarah your wife shall have a son. And Sarah was listening at the entrance of the tent, which was behind him.
And he said, I will certainly return unto thee when the season cometh round; and, lo, Sarah thy wife shall have a son. And Sarah heard in the tent door, which was behind him.
וַיֹּ֗אמֶר שׁ֣וֹב אָשׁ֤וּב אֵלֶ֙יךָ֙ כָּעֵ֣ת חַיָּ֔ה וְהִנֵּה־בֵ֖ן לְשָׂרָ֣ה אִשְׁתֶּ֑ךָ וְשָׂרָ֥ה שֹׁמַ֛עַת פֶּ֥תַח הָאֹ֖הֶל וְה֥וּא אַחֲרָֽיו׃
11
Now Avraham and Sarah were old, well stricken in years; it had ceased to be with Sarah after the manner of women.
Now Abraham and Sarah were old, [and] well stricken in age; it had ceased to be with Sarah after the manner of women.
וְאַבְרָהָ֤ם וְשָׂרָה֙ זְקֵנִ֔ים בָּאִ֖ים בַּיָּמִ֑ים חָדַל֙ לִהְי֣וֹת לְשָׂרָ֔ה אֹ֖רַח כַּנָּשִֽׁים׃
12
And Sarah laughed within herself, saying: After I am worn out, shall I have pleasure, my lord being old also?
And Sarah laughed within herself, saying, After I am waxed old shall I have pleasure, my lord being old also?
וַתִּצְחַ֥ק שָׂרָ֖ה בְּקִרְבָּ֣הּ לֵאמֹ֑ר אַחֲרֵ֤י בְלֹתִי֙ הָֽיְתָה־לִּ֣י עֶדְנָ֔ה וַֽאדֹנִ֖י זָקֵֽן׃
13
And YHWH said to Avraham: Why did Sarah laugh, saying, Shall I indeed bear a child, who am old?
And Jehovah said unto Abraham, Wherefore did Sarah laugh, saying, Shall I of a surety bear a child, who am old?
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר יְהוָ֖ה אֶל־אַבְרָהָ֑ם לָ֣מָּה זֶּה֩ צָחֲקָ֨ה שָׂרָ֜ה לֵאמֹ֗ר הַאַ֥ף אֻמְנָ֛ם אֵלֵ֖ד וַאֲנִ֥י זָקַֽנְתִּי׃
14
Is anything too wonderful for YHWH? At the set time I will return to you, when the season comes around, and Sarah shall have a son.
Is anything too hard for Jehovah? At the set time I will return unto thee, when the season cometh round, and Sarah shall have a son.
הֲיִפָּלֵ֥א מֵיְהוָ֖ה דָּבָ֑ר לַמּוֹעֵ֞ד אָשׁ֥וּב אֵלֶ֛יךָ כָּעֵ֥ת חַיָּ֖ה וּלְשָׂרָ֥ה בֵֽן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

Ha-yipale me-YHWH davar — is anything too wonderful for YHWH? The verb yipale is niphal imperfect 3ms of pala — be wonderful, surpassing, extraordinary, beyond ordinary capacity. The question is the chapter's load-bearing theological response to Sarah's disbelief-laughter. The Hebrew pala does not carry the sense of hardness that ASV's too hard and NIV's too hard import; it carries beyond ordinary capacity, marvellous, miraculous. The translation preserves the lexeme (NRSV's too wonderful).

The formula recurs at structurally similar moments across the Hebrew Bible: Deut 17:8 (a juridical case that is yipale — too hard for ordinary judges — goes up to the chosen sanctuary); Deut 30:11 (the commandment is not yipale from you, not beyond reach); Jer 32:17 (Jeremiah's confession at the covenant-renewal: for you nothing is too wonderful); Jer 32:27 (YHWH's direct echo: is anything too wonderful for me? — verbal mirror of Gen 18:14); Zech 8:6 (the survival of the remnant: if it is too wonderful in the eyes of this people's remnant, will it also be too wonderful in my eyes?). The recurrence-set is editorially deliberate. The Lukan reception at Luke 1:37 — ouk adynatesei para tou theou pan rhema (for nothing shall be impossible with God) — is the angel Gabriel's response to Mary's question about the conception of Yochanan/John; the LXX of Gen 18:14 renders me adynatesei para to theo rhema, with which Luke's phrasing aligns word-for-word. The reception is flagged as downstream Greek and is not preempted in the translation.

15
Then Sarah denied, saying: I did not laugh; for she was afraid. And he said: No, but you did laugh.
Then Sarah denied, saying, I laughed not; for she was afraid. And he said, Nay; but thou didst laugh.
וַתְּכַחֵ֨שׁ שָׂרָ֧ה ׀ לֵאמֹ֛ר לֹ֥א צָחַ֖קְתִּי כִּ֣י ׀ יָרֵ֑אָה וַיֹּ֥אמֶר ׀ לֹ֖א כִּ֥י צָחָֽקְתְּ׃
16
And the men rose up from there, and looked toward Sodom; and Avraham went with them to send them on the way.
And the men rose up from thence, and looked toward Sodom: and Abraham went with them to bring them on the way.
וַיָּקֻ֤מוּ מִשָּׁם֙ הָֽאֲנָשִׁ֔ים וַיַּשְׁקִ֖פוּ עַל־פְּנֵ֣י סְדֹ֑ם וְאַ֨בְרָהָ֔ם הֹלֵ֥ךְ עִמָּ֖ם לְשַׁלְּחָֽם׃
17
And YHWH said: Shall I hide from Avraham what I am doing,
And Jehovah said, Shall I hide from Abraham that which I do;
וַֽיהֹוָ֖ה אָמָ֑ר הַֽמְכַסֶּ֤ה אֲנִי֙ מֵֽאַבְרָהָ֔ם אֲשֶׁ֖ר אֲנִ֥י עֹשֶֽׂה׃
18
seeing that Avraham shall surely become a great and mighty nation, and all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in him?
seeing that Abraham had surely become a great and mighty nation, and all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in him?
וְאַ֨בְרָהָ֔ם הָי֧וֹ יִֽהְיֶ֛ה לְג֥וֹי גָּד֖וֹל וְעָצ֑וּם וְנִ֨בְרְכוּ ב֔וֹ כֹּ֖ל גּוֹיֵ֥י הָאָֽרֶץ׃
19
For I have known him, to the end that he may command his sons and his household after him, that they may keep the way of YHWH, to do righteousness and justice; that YHWH may bring upon Avraham that which he has spoken of him.
For I have known him, to the end that he may command his children and his household after him, that they may keep the way of Jehovah, to do righteousness and justice; to the end that Jehovah may bring upon Abraham that which he hath spoken of him.
כִּ֣י יְדַעְתִּ֗יו לְמַעַן֩ אֲשֶׁ֨ר יְצַוֶּ֜ה אֶת־בָּנָ֤יו וְאֶת־בֵּיתוֹ֙ אַחֲרָ֔יו וְשָֽׁמְרוּ֙ דֶּ֣רֶךְ יְהוָ֔ה לַעֲשׂ֥וֹת צְדָקָ֖ה וּמִשְׁפָּ֑ט לְמַ֗עַן הָבִ֤יא יְהוָה֙ עַל־אַבְרָהָ֔ם אֵ֥ת אֲשֶׁר־דִּבֶּ֖ר עָלָֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 19

La'asot tzedakah u-mishpat — to do righteousness and justice. The first occurrence in the Hebrew Bible of the tzedakah / mishpat pair, which becomes the foundational HB justice-hendiadys across the prophets and Psalter. The lexemes: tzedakah (צְדָקָה) covers a semantic range from righteousness (relational covenant-fidelity) to vindication (forensic outcome); mishpat (מִשְׁפָּט) covers judgment, justice, legal-decision, due-process. Paired, they form the canonical Hebrew justice-formula.

The pair recurs at the load-bearing prophetic-justice verses: Amos 5:24 (yigal ka-mayim mishpat u-tzedakah ke-nachal eitan — let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like a perennial stream); Isa 1:21 (mishpat yalin bah tzedek — justice once lodged in her); Isa 5:7 (va-yqav l-mishpat v-hineh mispach li-tzedakah v-hineh tze'akah — he looked for justice and behold bloodshed, for righteousness and behold a cry); Isa 28:17, 32:16-17, 33:5; Jer 22:3 (asu mishpat u-tzedakah — do justice and righteousness); Jer 22:15, 23:5, 33:15; Ezek 18:5, 18:19, 18:21, 18:27, 33:14, 33:16, 33:19, 45:9; Hos 2:21; Mic 6:8 (with chesed); Ps 33:5, 72:1-2, 89:14, 97:2, 99:4, 103:6; Prov 1:3, 2:9, 8:20, 21:3. The pair-as-hendiadys reading is mainstream (Weinfeld Social Justice in Ancient Israel and in the Ancient Near East 1995).

The verse stages the chapter's distinctive YHWH soliloquy (vv 17-19): YHWH disclosing the divine plan to a human counterpart before judgment. The Amos 3:7 formulation — for the Lord YHWH does nothing without revealing his secret to his servants the prophets — is the Northern-prophetic intertext. The disclosure-before-judgment pattern grounds the intercession that follows in vv 22-33.

20
And YHWH said: Because the outcry of Sodom and Amorah is great, and because their sin is exceedingly heavy,
And Jehovah said, Because the cry of Sodom and Gomorrah is great, and because their sin is very grievous;
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר יְהוָ֔ה זַעֲקַ֛ת סְדֹ֥ם וַעֲמֹרָ֖ה כִּי־רָ֑בָּה וְחַ֨טָּאתָ֔ם כִּ֥י כָבְדָ֖ה מְאֹֽד׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

Za'aqat Sodom va-Amorah — the outcry of Sodom and Amorah. The Hebrew lexeme za'aqah (זְעָקָה — outcry, cry-of-distress, forensic-plea; the parallel tza'aqah at v 21 ha-kee-tza'aqatah) is the Hebrew Bible's standard term for the cry of the oppressed reaching the divine. It carries built-in forensic-judicial weight: the cry is the cry of victims, and the divine response is judicial.

The lexeme's load-bearing distribution: Gen 4:10 (Abel's blood — qol demei achikha tzo'aqim elai min ha-adamah — the voice of your brother's blood cries to me from the ground; the first occurrence, framing all subsequent uses); Exod 2:23-25, 3:7-9 (Israel's cry in Egypt — va-ta'al shav'atam el-ha-Elohim — and YHWH's response: I have surely seen the affliction of my people, I have heard their cry); Exod 22:22-24 (the widow-and-orphan formula: if you afflict them in any way, and they cry at all to me, I will surely hear their cry); 1 Sam 9:16 (Israel's cry reaches God); Job 34:28 (the cry of the afflicted that God hears); Neh 9:9; numerous psalms (9:13, 22:6, 34:18, 107); James 5:4 (the laborers' wages crying out — explicit Septuagintal continuation). The reading of za'aqat Sodom as the outcry against Sodom — the cry of those Sodom has oppressed reaches the divine — is the mainstream scholarly consensus (Sarna JPS Genesis 1989, pp. 131-132; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985, pp. 287-288), and is corroborated by Ezek 16:49's explicit characterization of Sodom's sin as pride, fullness of bread, and prosperous ease — and the poor and needy she did not strengthen.

The WoH translation reads outcry over ASV's colorless cry to preserve the forensic edge of the lexeme.

21
I will go down now, and see whether they have done altogether according to the outcry of it that has come to me; and if not, I will know.
I will go down now, and see whether they have done altogether according to the cry of it, which is come unto me; and if not, I will know.
אֵֽרֲדָה־נָּ֣א וְאֶרְאֶ֔ה הַכְּצַעֲקָתָ֛הּ הַבָּ֥אָה אֵלַ֖י עָשׂ֣וּ ׀ כָּלָ֑ה וְאִם־לֹ֖א אֵדָֽעָה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

Erdah-na v-er'eh — I will go down now and see. The divine-descent-to-investigate formula. The verb yarad (qal — go down, descend) in cohortative or imperfect, paired with a verb of seeing or acting, recurs at a defined set of moments in the primeval and patriarchal narratives. The pattern: the divine subject is in a place above; the human or earthly object of investigation is below; the descent is deliberate, spatial, and motivated by the need to see for oneself.

The canonical instances: Gen 11:5 at Babel — va-yered YHWH lir'ot et-ha-ir v-et-ha-migdal asher banu bnei ha-adam (and YHWH went down to see the city and the tower the sons of man had built); Gen 11:7 at Babel — havah neredah v-navelah sham sefatam (come, let us go down and confound their language there); here at Gen 18:21; Exod 3:8 at the burning bush — va-ered l-hatzilo m-yad Mitsrayim (and I have come down to deliver them from the hand of Egypt); Exod 19:11, 19:18, 19:20 of the Sinai-descent. The prophetic continuation: Mic 1:3 (for behold, YHWH comes out of his place, and will come down and tread upon the high places of the earth); Isa 64:1 (lu qarata shamayim yaradta — if only you would rend the heavens and come down).

The pattern is treated as a recurring divine-descent formula by Sarna (JPS Genesis 1989, pp. 132, 81) and Westermann (Genesis 1-11 1984, pp. 547-548 on the Babel comparison; Genesis 12-36 1985, pp. 287-288); Niditch (Chaos to Cosmos 1985) treats it as part of the corpus's cosmographic vertical-axis architecture. The verbal parallels between Gen 11:5, 18:21, and Exod 3:8 are editorially deliberate.

22
And the men turned from there, and went toward Sodom; but Avraham still stood before YHWH.
And the men turned from thence, and went toward Sodom: but Abraham stood yet before Jehovah.
וַיִּפְנ֤וּ מִשָּׁם֙ הָֽאֲנָשִׁ֔ים וַיֵּלְכ֖וּ סְדֹ֑מָה וְאַ֨בְרָהָ֔ם עוֹדֶ֥נּוּ עֹמֵ֖ד לִפְנֵ֥י יְהוָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

V-Avraham odenu omed lifnei YHWH — and Avraham still stood before YHWH. The verse is one of the canonical tiqune soferim (scribal emendations) of the Hebrew Bible. The Masoretic tradition (recorded in the Massorah Magna's marginal lists; b. Megillah 25b; Tanchuma Yashan Beshallach 16; Mekhilta Shirta 6; Bereshit Rabbah 49:7) holds that the original reading was v-YHWH odenu omed lifnei Avraham — and YHWH still stood before Avraham. The original was deliberately emended (kinah ha-katuv — Scripture used a euphemism) for reverence, because the original is theologically uncomfortable: it depicts YHWH standing in attendance before a human being. The MT preserves the emended form; the tradition explicitly records the original.

There are eighteen verses on the traditional list of tiqune soferim (the exact count and contents vary slightly across rabbinic sources — some lists have eight, some eleven, some thirteen, some seventeen, some eighteen — but Gen 18:22 appears in every standard list and is one of the three or four most frequently cited examples). The cross-corpus distribution of the standard eighteen: Gen 18:22; Num 11:15; Num 12:12; 1 Sam 3:13; 2 Sam 16:12; 2 Sam 20:1; 1 Kgs 12:16; 2 Chr 10:16; Jer 2:11; Ezek 8:17; Hos 4:7; Hab 1:12; Zech 2:12; Mal 1:13; Ps 106:20; Job 7:20; Job 32:3; Lam 3:20.

The modern textual-critical position on the tiqun-tradition is divided. The conservative-traditional reading (McCarthy The Tiqqune Sopherim, OBO 36, 1981 — the canonical monograph) treats the tradition as recording actual scribal emendations preserved in the Masoretic apparatus, on the model of the qere/ketiv and itture soferim. The skeptical reading (Tov Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible, 3rd ed. 2012, §6.B.3, pp. 64-65; Würthwein The Text of the Old Testament 2nd ed. 1995, pp. 18-19) treats the tradition as a later rabbinic interpretive flag retrospectively justifying the MT reading — the tradition records what the rabbis believed the original should have been, not necessarily what an earlier text did read. The textual evidence for an actual variant is weak (no LXX, Samaritan, Qumran, or Targumic witness preserves the YHWH still stood before Avraham reading); but the tradition's witness to a theologically-motivated emendation is itself preserved by the Masoretes and constitutes a Masoretic-tradition fact independent of whether the variant was ever physically in a manuscript.

The theological force of the tiqun-attested original is substantial: it depicts YHWH as standing-in-attendance before a human intercessor, with Avraham as the figure-being-petitioned. The MT inversion reverses the relation (Avraham is the petitioner, YHWH stands as the petitioned-judge), conforming the scene to standard Hebrew Bible decorum on divine-human spatial relation. Both readings have substantial textual presence in the tradition: the MT in the manuscript; the tiqun-attested original in the Masoretic-marginal apparatus. The WoH translation preserves the MT and records the tiqun tradition in the apparatus.

23
And Avraham drew near, and said: Will you indeed sweep away the righteous with the wicked?
And Abraham drew near, and said, Wilt thou consume the righteous with the wicked?
וַיִּגַּ֥שׁ אַבְרָהָ֖ם וַיֹּאמַ֑ר הַאַ֣ף תִּסְפֶּ֔ה צַדִּ֖יק עִם־רָשָֽׁע׃
24
Perhaps there are fifty righteous within the city: will you indeed sweep away and not spare the place for the sake of the fifty righteous that are in it?
Peradventure there are fifty righteous within the city: wilt thou consume and not spare the place for the fifty righteous that are therein?
אוּלַ֥י יֵ֛שׁ חֲמִשִּׁ֥ים צַדִּיקִ֖ם בְּת֣וֹךְ הָעִ֑יר הַאַ֤ף תִּסְפֶּה֙ וְלֹא־תִשָּׂ֣א לַמָּק֔וֹם לְמַ֛עַן חֲמִשִּׁ֥ים הַצַּדִּיקִ֖ם אֲשֶׁ֥ר בְּקִרְבָּֽהּ׃
25
Far be it from you to do after this manner, to slay the righteous with the wicked, so that the righteous should be as the wicked; far be it from you: shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice?
That be far from thee to do after this manner, to slay the righteous with the wicked, that so the righteous should be as the wicked; that be far from thee: shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?
