חָלִ֨לָה לְּךָ֜ מֵעֲשֹׂ֣ת ׀ כַּדָּבָ֣ר הַזֶּ֗ה לְהָמִ֤ית צַדִּיק֙ עִם־רָשָׁ֔ע וְהָיָ֥ה כַצַּדִּ֖יק כָּרָשָׁ֑ע חָלִ֣לָה לָּ֔ךְ הֲשֹׁפֵט֙ כָּל־הָאָ֔רֶץ לֹ֥א יַעֲשֶׂ֖ה מִשְׁפָּֽט׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

Ha-shofet kol ha-aretz lo ya'aseh mishpat — shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice? The chapter's foundational theological-rhetorical question, posed by Avraham as the load-bearing premise of the intercession. The verse establishes three things in one sentence: (a) YHWH's universal-judicial-competence (shofet kol ha-aretz — judge of the whole earth, not merely of Avraham's lineage or of the covenant-people); (b) the moral-coherence requirement (a true judge must do mishpat — must enact justice consistent with the moral order); (c) the inadmissibility of indiscriminate destruction — destroying the righteous along with the wicked would violate the moral-coherence requirement.

The argument is the canonical patriarchal-prophetic precedent for later prophetic justice-arguments against indiscriminate divine wrath: Amos 7:1-9 and 8:1-3 (the visions of judgment, where Amos intercedes against destruction); Jer 4:23-26 (the un-creation imagery counterposed to the moral order); Ezek 9:8, 11:13 (Ezekiel's intercession: will you destroy all the remnant of Israel?); Hab 1:13 (your eyes are too pure to behold evil, and you cannot look on iniquity — the Habakkuk lawsuit against divine acquiescence in wickedness). The shofet kol ha-aretz formula is the inaugural articulation of the Hebrew-Bible anti-divine-injustice prophetic register (Brueggemann Theology of the Old Testament 1997, pp. 384-389).

The v 25 question is rhetorical: Avraham is not entertaining the possibility that the judge of all the earth might fail to do justice; he is invoking the principle to bind YHWH to act in accordance with it. The bargaining-pattern of vv 26-32 that follows is the working-out of the principle in casuistic detail. See the abraham-intercession-bargaining glossary entry.

26
And YHWH said: If I find in Sodom fifty righteous within the city, then I will spare all the place for their sake.
And Jehovah said, If I find in Sodom fifty righteous within the city, then I will spare all the place for their sake.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר יְהוָ֔ה אִם־אֶמְצָ֥א בִסְדֹ֛ם חֲמִשִּׁ֥ים צַדִּיקִ֖ם בְּת֣וֹךְ הָעִ֑יר וְנָשָׂ֥אתִי לְכָל־הַמָּק֖וֹם בַּעֲבוּרָֽם׃
27
And Avraham answered and said: Behold now, I have taken upon myself to speak to my Lord, who am but dust and ashes:
And Abraham answered and said, Behold now, I have taken upon me to speak unto the Lord, who am but dust and ashes:
וַיַּ֥עַן אַבְרָהָ֖ם וַיֹּאמַ֑ר הִנֵּה־נָ֤א הוֹאַ֙לְתִּי֙ לְדַבֵּ֣ר אֶל־אֲדֹנָ֔י וְאָנֹכִ֖י עָפָ֥ר וָאֵֽפֶר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 27

V-anokhi afar va-efer — who am but dust and ashes. The self-deprecation formula. The pairing afar va-efer (עָפָר וָאֵפֶר — dust and ashes) combines the creation-from-dust motif (Gen 2:7 afar min-ha-adamah; Gen 3:19 ki afar atah v-el-afar tashuv) with the mourning-and-repentance ashes motif (the sackcloth-and-ashes register at 2 Sam 13:19, Esth 4:1-3, Isa 58:5, Jer 6:26, Lam 2:10). The compound afar va-efer is rare: it occurs three times in the Hebrew Bible — here at Gen 18:27, and twice in Job (30:19, 42:6). The Job occurrences are the keystone framing of the book's lament-and-repentance register (see the existing glossary entry em-as-v-nikhamti-al-afar-va-efer on Job 42:6).

The phrase's placement in Avraham's intercession is editorially significant. Avraham, the patriarch in covenant with YHWH, frames his right-to-intercede not on covenant-status (which he holds) but on creatural humility — on the ontological fact of being made of dust and destined for ashes. The argument structure is: because I am dust and ashes, and yet I take it upon myself to speak — therefore, hear me. The pattern recurs in Job 42:6 with the inverted polarity (Job repenting on dust and ashes after his theophanic encounter) and is invoked in later Jewish liturgy and Christian penitential tradition (cf. the Ash Wednesday formula memento homo quia pulvis es).

Second Temple parallels: Sir 10:9; Sir 17:32; 40:3 (in the LXX/deuterocanon). The phrase becomes a stock-formula for creatural humility in the wisdom and apocalyptic literature.

28
Perhaps there shall lack five of the fifty righteous: will you destroy all the city for lack of five? And he said: I will not destroy it, if I find there forty and five.
peradventure there shall lack five of the fifty righteous: wilt thou destroy all the city for lack of five? And he said, I will not destroy it, if I find there forty and five.
א֠וּלַי יַחְסְר֞וּן חֲמִשִּׁ֤ים הַצַּדִּיקִם֙ חֲמִשָּׁ֔ה הֲתַשְׁחִ֥ית בַּחֲמִשָּׁ֖ה אֶת־כָּל־הָעִ֑יר וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ לֹ֣א אַשְׁחִ֔ית אִם־אֶמְצָ֣א שָׁ֔ם אַרְבָּעִ֖ים וַחֲמִשָּֽׁה׃
29
And he spoke to him yet again, and said: Perhaps there shall be forty found there. And he said: I will not do it for the forty's sake.
And he spake unto him yet again, and said, Peradventure there shall be forty found there. And he said, I will not do it for the forty`s sake.
וַיֹּ֨סֶף ע֜וֹד לְדַבֵּ֤ר אֵלָיו֙ וַיֹּאמַ֔ר אוּלַ֛י יִמָּצְא֥וּן שָׁ֖ם אַרְבָּעִ֑ים וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ לֹ֣א אֶֽעֱשֶׂ֔ה בַּעֲב֖וּר הָאַרְבָּעִֽים׃
30
And he said: Oh let not my Lord be angry, and I will speak: Perhaps there shall be thirty found there. And he said: I will not do it, if I find there thirty.
And he said, Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak: peradventure there shall thirty be found there. And he said, I will not do it, if I find thirty there.
וַ֠יֹּאמֶר אַל־נָ֞א יִ֤חַר לַֽאדֹנָי֙ וַאֲדַבֵּ֔רָה אוּלַ֛י יִמָּצְא֥וּן שָׁ֖ם שְׁלֹשִׁ֑ים וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ לֹ֣א אֶֽעֱשֶׂ֔ה אִם־אֶמְצָ֥א שָׁ֖ם שְׁלֹשִֽׁים׃
31
And he said: Behold now, I have taken upon myself to speak to my Lord: Perhaps there shall be twenty found there. And he said: I will not destroy it for the twenty's sake.
And he said, Behold now, I have taken upon me to speak unto the Lord: peradventure there shall be twenty found there. And he said, I will not destroy it for the twenty`s sake.
וַיֹּ֗אמֶר הִנֵּֽה־נָ֤א הוֹאַ֙לְתִּי֙ לְדַבֵּ֣ר אֶל־אֲדֹנָ֔י אוּלַ֛י יִמָּצְא֥וּן שָׁ֖ם עֶשְׂרִ֑ים וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ לֹ֣א אַשְׁחִ֔ית בַּעֲב֖וּר הָֽעֶשְׂרִֽים׃
32
And he said: Oh let not my Lord be angry, and I will speak just this once: Perhaps there shall be ten found there. And he said: I will not destroy it for the ten's sake.
And he said, Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak yet but this once: peradventure ten shall be found there. And he said, I will not destroy it for the ten`s sake.
וַ֠יֹּאמֶר אַל־נָ֞א יִ֤חַר לַֽאדֹנָי֙ וַאֲדַבְּרָ֣ה אַךְ־הַפַּ֔עַם אוּלַ֛י יִמָּצְא֥וּן שָׁ֖ם עֲשָׂרָ֑ה וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ לֹ֣א אַשְׁחִ֔ית בַּעֲב֖וּר הָעֲשָׂרָֽה׃
33
And YHWH went his way, as soon as he had finished speaking to Avraham; and Avraham returned to his place.
And Jehovah went his way, as soon as he had left off communing with Abraham: and Abraham returned unto his place.
וַיֵּ֣לֶךְ יְהוָ֔ה כַּאֲשֶׁ֣ר כִּלָּ֔ה לְדַבֵּ֖ר אֶל־אַבְרָהָ֑ם וְאַבְרָהָ֖ם שָׁ֥ב לִמְקֹמֽוֹ׃
Chapter 19

Genesis 19 — The destruction of Sodom and Amorah and the deliverance of Lot

1
And the two messengers came to Sodom at evening; and Lot sat at the gate of Sodom; and Lot saw them, and rose up to meet them; and he bowed himself with his face to the earth,
And the two angels came to Sodom at even; and Lot sat in the gate of Sodom: and Lot saw them, and rose up to meet them; and he bowed himself with his face to the earth;
וַ֠יָּבֹאוּ שְׁנֵ֨י הַמַּלְאָכִ֤ים סְדֹ֙מָה֙ בָּעֶ֔רֶב וְל֖וֹט יֹשֵׁ֣ב בְּשַֽׁעַר־סְדֹ֑ם וַיַּרְא־לוֹט֙ וַיָּ֣קָם לִקְרָאתָ֔ם וַיִּשְׁתַּ֥חוּ אַפַּ֖יִם אָֽרְצָה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

Shnei ha-malakhim — the two messengers. The arithmetic of Gen 18's three men (shloshah anashim, v 2) here resolves: at Gen 18:22 the men turned toward Sodom while Avraham stood before YHWH; the two who continued to Sodom arrive here at evening. The Mamre / Sodom diptych (Letellier 1995) pairs the day-half at the oaks with the night-half at the city gate. The translation preserves messengers per the malakh-yhwh-messenger convention (Gen 16 onward), declining the conventional angels which imports later angelology. The appliesTo of both the malakh-yhwh-messenger and shloshah-anashim-three-visitors-theophany entries are extended in glossary v2.16.0 to include this verse.

2
and he said: Behold now, my lords, turn aside, I pray you, into your servant's house, and tarry all night, and wash your feet, and you shall rise up early, and go on your way. And they said: No; but we will abide in the street all night.
and he said, Behold now, my lords, turn aside, I pray you, into your servant`s house, and tarry all night, and wash your feet, and ye shall rise up early, and go on your way. And they said, Nay; but we will abide in the street all night.
וַיֹּ֜אמֶר הִנֶּ֣ה נָּא־אֲדֹנַ֗י ס֣וּרוּ נָ֠א אֶל־בֵּ֨ית עַבְדְּכֶ֤ם וְלִ֙ינוּ֙ וְרַחֲצ֣וּ רַגְלֵיכֶ֔ם וְהִשְׁכַּמְתֶּ֖ם וַהֲלַכְתֶּ֣ם לְדַרְכְּכֶ֑ם וַיֹּאמְר֣וּ לֹּ֔א כִּ֥י בָרְח֖וֹב נָלִֽין׃
3
And he urged them greatly; and they turned in to him, and entered into his house; and he made them a feast, and baked unleavened bread, and they ate.
And he urged them greatly; and they turned in unto him, and entered into his house; and he made them a feast, and did bake unleavened bread, and they did eat.
וַיִּפְצַר־בָּ֣ם מְאֹ֔ד וַיָּסֻ֣רוּ אֵלָ֔יו וַיָּבֹ֖אוּ אֶל־בֵּית֑וֹ וַיַּ֤עַשׂ לָהֶם֙ מִשְׁתֶּ֔ה וּמַצּ֥וֹת אָפָ֖ה וַיֹּאכֵֽלוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

Va-yokhelu — and they ate. The second of the chapter-18 / chapter-19 pair of unhedged divine-figure eating scenes; the same verb form as Gen 18:8, preserved without harmonization. The Mamre-Sodom diptych (Letellier 1995) is structurally explicit here: hospitality-at-Mamre and hospitality-at-Sodom both stage the messengers eating human food. The divine-eating-anthropomorphism entry already governs this verse (appliesTo already includes GEN-19:3); see the Gen 18:8 commentary and entry rationale for the Hamori 2008 anthropomorphic-realism reading and the Tob 12:19 / Judg 13:15-20 corpus-internal harmonization-vs-direct-eating contrast.

4
But before they lay down, the men of the city, even the men of Sodom, surrounded the house, both young and old, all the people from every quarter;
But before they lay down, the men of the city, [even] the men of Sodom, compassed the house round, both young and old, all the people from every quarter;
טֶרֶם֮ יִשְׁכָּבוּ֒ וְאַנְשֵׁ֨י הָעִ֜יר אַנְשֵׁ֤י סְדֹם֙ נָסַ֣בּוּ עַל־הַבַּ֔יִת מִנַּ֖עַר וְעַד־זָקֵ֑ן כָּל־הָעָ֖ם מִקָּצֶֽה׃
5
and they called unto Lot, and said unto him: Where are the men that came in to you this night? Bring them out unto us, that we may know them.
and they called unto Lot, and said unto him, Where are the men that came in to thee this night? bring them out unto us, that we may know them.
וַיִּקְרְא֤וּ אֶל־לוֹט֙ וַיֹּ֣אמְרוּ ל֔וֹ אַיֵּ֧ה הָאֲנָשִׁ֛ים אֲשֶׁר־בָּ֥אוּ אֵלֶ֖יךָ הַלָּ֑יְלָה הוֹצִיאֵ֣ם אֵלֵ֔ינוּ וְנֵדְעָ֖ה אֹתָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

Hotzi'em eleinu v-neda otam — bring them out unto us, that we may know them. The chapter's hinge-verse and the reception-history flashpoint of the entire Sodom narrative. The verb yada (to know) carries a documented sexual sense in the HB (Gen 4:1, v-ha-adam yada et-Chavvah — and the man knew Eve; Judg 19:22-25, the verbatim parallel-scene at Gibeah), and the demand is read by the great majority of modern scholars as a sexual demand. The chapter's reception then bifurcates sharply.

The Christian patristic and medieval tradition (Augustine, Aquinas; the medieval penitentials) read the demand as the textual ground of the sodomy category — the place-name Sodom becoming, through Latin sodomia, the English sodomy. The reading was the standard Western Christian reception from late antiquity through the early modern period and remains the popular-cultural association of the chapter today.

The modern critical reading (Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 134-136; Brueggemann Genesis Interpretation 1982; Letellier Day in Mamre, Night in Sodom 1995 chs 4-5; Tornkvist Eros Defiled? 1998; Carden Sodomy: A History of a Christian Biblical Myth 2004 — full-length monograph; Loader A Tale of Two Cities: Sodom and Gomorrah 1990) reads the scene as a gang-rape-violence directed at outsiders / hospitality-violation episode, distinct from any claim about orientation. The HB's own characterization of Sodom's sin elsewhere is decisive evidence for this reading: Ezek 16:48-50 enumerates Sodom's iniquity as pride, fullness of bread, prosperous ease, and failure to strengthen the hand of the poor and needy — not as same-sex contact. The Gen 18:20-21 outcry (see zaaqah-outcry-of-the-oppressed) is the cry of victims, paralleled at Gen 4:10 (Abel's blood) and Exod 2:23-25 (Israel in Egypt); the structure presupposes ongoing oppression-of-the-vulnerable, not a single sexual episode. The Judg 19 Gibeah parallel — where a Levite's concubine is in fact handed over and gang-raped to death — makes the violence-and-outsider-abuse reading editorially explicit in the wider canon.

The translation preserves the literal that we may know them, preempting neither reading. The full bifurcation is documented in the sodom-as-sodomy-reception-vs-inhospitality glossary entry.

6
And Lot went out unto them to the entrance, and shut the door after him.
And Lot went out unto them to the door, and shut the door after him.
וַיֵּצֵ֧א אֲלֵהֶ֛ם ל֖וֹט הַפֶּ֑תְחָה וְהַדֶּ֖לֶת סָגַ֥ר אַחֲרָֽיו׃
7
And he said: I pray you, my brothers, do not so wickedly.
And he said, I pray you, my brethren, do not so wickedly.
וַיֹּאמַ֑ר אַל־נָ֥א אַחַ֖י תָּרֵֽעוּ׃
8
Behold now, I have two daughters who have not known a man; let me, I pray you, bring them out unto you, and do you to them as is good in your eyes; only unto these men do nothing, since they have come under the shadow of my roof.
Behold now, I have two daughters that have not known man; let me, I pray you, bring them out unto you, and do ye to them as is good in your eyes: only unto these men do nothing, forasmuch as they are come under the shadow of my roof.
הִנֵּה־נָ֨א לִ֜י שְׁתֵּ֣י בָנ֗וֹת אֲשֶׁ֤ר לֹֽא־יָדְעוּ֙ אִ֔ישׁ אוֹצִֽיאָה־נָּ֤א אֶתְהֶן֙ אֲלֵיכֶ֔ם וַעֲשׂ֣וּ לָהֶ֔ן כַּטּ֖וֹב בְּעֵינֵיכֶ֑ם רַ֠ק לָֽאֲנָשִׁ֤ים הָאֵל֙ אַל־תַּעֲשׂ֣וּ דָבָ֔ר כִּֽי־עַל־כֵּ֥ן בָּ֖אוּ בְּצֵ֥ל קֹרָתִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

Hineh-na li shtei vanot ... v-asu lahen ka-tov b-eyneykhem — Behold, I have two daughters ... do to them as is good in your eyes. Lot offers his two virgin daughters to the mob in place of the messengers. The narrator records the offer without editorial comment; the mob does not accept it (vv 9-11) and the daughters are not in fact handed over, but the offer is made.

The verse has acquired heavy modern feminist-critical reception. Trible (Texts of Terror, 1984) reads the parallel offer at Judg 19:22-25 — where the Levite's concubine is in fact handed over and gang-raped to death by the men of Gibeah — as the structurally explicit working-out of the Lot-offer logic: when the daughter-or-concubine-trade is in fact accepted, the consequence is the woman's death. Fewell and Gunn (Gender, Power, and Promise, 1993) read the offer as evidence of the patriarchal narrative's acceptable trade of female bodies for male hospitality-honor, with the narrator's silence participating in the structure it records. Bach (Women, Seduction, and Betrayal in Biblical Narrative, 1997/1999) and Exum (Plotted, Shot, Painted, 1996) extend the reading across the wider corpus.

The ancient hospitality-and-honor reading (Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 301-303; Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 136) reads the offer as an extreme expression of the ANE host-protects-his-guests obligation: the host's honor is so bound to the protection of those under the shadow of his roof that he will offer his own daughters in their place. Both readings can be held in apparatus: the offer's ANE-hospitality logic is real; the modern ethical critique of that logic is also serious work. The translation preserves the ASV-baseline do to them as is good in your eyes literally; the apparatus carries the bifurcation.

9
And they said: Stand back. And they said: This one fellow came in to sojourn, and he will play the judge; now will we deal worse with you than with them. And they pressed sorely upon the man, even Lot, and drew near to break the door.
And they said, Stand back. And they said, This one fellow came in to sojourn, and he will needs be a judge: now will we deal worse with thee, than with them. And they pressed sore upon the man, even Lot, and drew near to break the door.
וַיֹּאמְר֣וּ ׀ גֶּשׁ־הָ֗לְאָה וַיֹּֽאמְרוּ֙ הָאֶחָ֤ד בָּֽא־לָגוּר֙ וַיִּשְׁפֹּ֣ט שָׁפ֔וֹט עַתָּ֕ה נָרַ֥ע לְךָ֖ מֵהֶ֑ם וַיִּפְצְר֨וּ בָאִ֤ישׁ בְּלוֹט֙ מְאֹ֔ד וַֽיִּגְּשׁ֖וּ לִשְׁבֹּ֥ר הַדָּֽלֶת׃
10
But the men put forth their hand, and brought Lot into the house to them, and shut the door.
But the men put forth their hand, and brought Lot into the house to them, and shut to the door.
וַיִּשְׁלְח֤וּ הָֽאֲנָשִׁים֙ אֶת־יָדָ֔ם וַיָּבִ֧יאוּ אֶת־ל֛וֹט אֲלֵיהֶ֖ם הַבָּ֑יְתָה וְאֶת־הַדֶּ֖לֶת סָגָֽרוּ׃
11
And they struck the men that were at the entrance of the house with blindness, both small and great; so that they wearied themselves to find the entrance.
And they smote the men that were at the door of the house with blindness, both small and great, so that they wearied themselves to find the door.
וְֽאֶת־הָאֲנָשִׁ֞ים אֲשֶׁר־פֶּ֣תַח הַבַּ֗יִת הִכּוּ֙ בַּסַּנְוֵרִ֔ים מִקָּטֹ֖ן וְעַד־גָּד֑וֹל וַיִּלְא֖וּ לִמְצֹ֥א הַפָּֽתַח׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

Va-yaku ba-sanverim — they struck them with blindness. The lexeme sanverim (סַנְוֵרִים) is one of only two HB occurrences of the term — here at Gen 19:11 and at 2 Kgs 6:18 (Elisha's prayer striking the Aramean army at Dotan). The two attestations are structurally parallel: a divine-or-prophetic blinding of a hostile group surrounding a protected house or city. The plural form is itself unusual — the ordinary HB term for blindness is ivvaron (singular). HALOT and Tigay (JPS Excursus) note the possible Akkadian loanword from šunwurum (dazzling, blinding-by-light), suggesting a flash-of-light sense rather than ordinary loss of sight; the alternative West-Semitic and Aramaic derivations are documented but less clearly motivated. The translation preserves the ASV-baseline blindness; the dazzling-light nuance is carried in the sanverim-blindness-hapax glossary entry.

12
And the men said unto Lot: Have you here any besides? Son-in-law, and your sons, and your daughters, and whomever you have in the city, bring them out of the place;
And the men said unto Lot, Hast thou here any besides? son-in-law, and thy sons, and thy daughters, and whomsoever thou hast in the city, bring them out of the place:
וַיֹּאמְר֨וּ הָאֲנָשִׁ֜ים אֶל־ל֗וֹט עֹ֚ד מִֽי־לְךָ֣ פֹ֔ה חָתָן֙ וּבָנֶ֣יךָ וּבְנֹתֶ֔יךָ וְכֹ֥ל אֲשֶׁר־לְךָ֖ בָּעִ֑יר הוֹצֵ֖א מִן־הַמָּקֽוֹם׃
13
for we will destroy this place, because the outcry of them has grown great before YHWH; and YHWH has sent us to destroy it.
for we will destroy this place, because the cry of them is waxed great before Jehovah: and Jehovah hath sent us to destroy it.
כִּֽי־מַשְׁחִתִ֣ים אֲנַ֔חְנוּ אֶת־הַמָּק֖וֹם הַזֶּ֑ה כִּֽי־גָֽדְלָ֤ה צַעֲקָתָם֙ אֶת־פְּנֵ֣י יְהוָ֔ה וַיְשַׁלְּחֵ֥נוּ יְהוָ֖ה לְשַׁחֲתָֽהּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

Ki gadlah tza'aqatam et-pnei YHWH — for their outcry has grown great before YHWH. The verse cycles the za'aqah / tza'aqah lexeme of Gen 18:20-21 back to its conclusion: the outcry against Sodom that triggered the divine investigation at Mamre now becomes the explicit rationale for the destruction. The zaaqah-outcry-of-the-oppressed entry (already governing this verse) documents the cross-corpus distribution at Gen 4:10 (Abel), Exod 2:23-25 (Israel in Egypt), Exod 22:22-24 (the widow-and-orphan formula), Job 34:28 and the Psalter; the consensus reading is outcry of the oppressed, not of the perpetrators — corroborated by Ezek 16:48-50's enumeration of Sodom's sin as oppression of the poor.

14
And Lot went out, and spoke unto his sons-in-law, who were to marry his daughters, and said: Arise, get out of this place; for YHWH will destroy the city. But he seemed unto his sons-in-law as one that jested.
And Lot went out, and spake unto his sons-in-law, who married his daughters, and said, Up, get you out of this place; for Jehovah will destroy the city. But he seemed unto his sons-in-law as one that mocked.
וַיֵּצֵ֨א ל֜וֹט וַיְדַבֵּ֣ר ׀ אֶל־חֲתָנָ֣יו ׀ לֹקְחֵ֣י בְנֹתָ֗יו וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ ק֤וּמוּ צְּאוּ֙ מִן־הַמָּק֣וֹם הַזֶּ֔ה כִּֽי־מַשְׁחִ֥ית יְהוָ֖ה אֶת־הָעִ֑יר וַיְהִ֥י כִמְצַחֵ֖ק בְּעֵינֵ֥י חֲתָנָֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

Va-yhi ki-m'tzacheq b-eynei chatanav — and he was as one who jested in the eyes of his sons-in-law. The verb is the piel participle m'tzacheq of the same root (tzachaq) as Avraham's and Sarah's disbelief-laughter at Gen 17:17 and 18:12-15 and the name Yitzchaq of Gen 17:19 (see yitzchaq-name-etymology-laughter-pattern). The Genesis editor's placement is editorially deliberate: the same root that names the covenant-son's laughter immediately re-occurs, in the piel-mocking sense, at the moment Lot's sons-in-law disregard the warning of destruction. The appliesTo of the laughter-pattern entry is extended in glossary v2.16.0 to include this verse, marking it as the piel-mockery instance in the wider patriarchal tzachaq cluster (qal disbelief and joy at 17:17 / 18:12-15 / 21:6; piel mockery and play at 19:14 / 21:9 / 26:8 / Exod 32:6).

15
And when the dawn arose, then the messengers hastened Lot, saying: Arise, take your wife, and your two daughters that are here, lest you be consumed in the iniquity of the city.
And when the morning arose, then the angels hastened Lot, saying, Arise, take thy wife, and thy two daughters that are here, lest thou be consumed in the iniquity of the city.
וּכְמוֹ֙ הַשַּׁ֣חַר עָלָ֔ה וַיָּאִ֥יצוּ הַמַּלְאָכִ֖ים בְּל֣וֹט לֵאמֹ֑ר קוּם֩ קַ֨ח אֶֽת־אִשְׁתְּךָ֜ וְאֶת־שְׁתֵּ֤י בְנֹתֶ֙יךָ֙ הַנִּמְצָאֹ֔ת פֶּן־תִּסָּפֶ֖ה בַּעֲוֺ֥ן הָעִֽיר׃
16
But he lingered; and the men laid hold upon his hand, and upon the hand of his wife, and upon the hand of his two daughters, YHWH being merciful unto him; and they brought him forth, and set him without the city.
But he lingered; and the men laid hold upon his hand, and upon the hand of his wife, and upon the hand of his two daughters, Jehovah being merciful unto him; and they brought him forth, and set him without the city.
וַֽיִּתְמַהְמָ֓הּ ׀ וַיַּחֲזִ֨קוּ הָאֲנָשִׁ֜ים בְּיָד֣וֹ וּבְיַד־אִשְׁתּ֗וֹ וּבְיַד֙ שְׁתֵּ֣י בְנֹתָ֔יו בְּחֶמְלַ֥ת יְהוָ֖ה עָלָ֑יו וַיֹּצִאֻ֥הוּ וַיַּנִּחֻ֖הוּ מִח֥וּץ לָעִֽיר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

B-chemlat YHWH alav — in YHWH's pity upon him. The lexeme chemlah (חֶמְלָה — pity, sparing, compassion-to-spare-from-destruction) is a marked HB term distinct from the more common chesed (covenant loving-kindness) and rachamim (womb-compassion). The chemlah sense is specifically sparing-from-deserved-or-imminent-destruction: cf. 1 Sam 15:3-15 (Saul's chemlah on Agag, explicitly contrasted with the cherem command); 2 Sam 12:4 (the chemlah in Nathan's parable, the rich man's failure to spare from his own flock); Mal 3:17 (YHWH's chemlah on those who fear him as a man spares his son who serves him); Joel 2:18; Jon 4:10-11 (YHWH's chemlah on Nineveh); Ezek 9:5 (the negative formula — al-tachos eynkhem v-al-tachmolu — let not your eye spare, do not have pity). The Gen 19:16 occurrence is the lexeme's first HB attestation and the only patriarchal-narrative instance, structurally pairing with the v 29 va-yizkor Elohim et-Avraham — the divine-remembrance and divine-sparing together constitute the mechanism of Lot's deliverance.

17
And it came to pass, when they had brought them forth abroad, that he said: Escape for your life; do not look behind you, nor stay in all the Plain; escape to the mountain, lest you be consumed.
And it came to pass, when they had brought them forth abroad, that he said, Escape for thy life; look not behind thee, neither stay thou in all the Plain; escape to the mountain, lest thou be consumed.
וַיְהִי֩ כְהוֹצִיאָ֨ם אֹתָ֜ם הַח֗וּצָה וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ הִמָּלֵ֣ט עַל־נַפְשֶׁ֔ךָ אַל־תַּבִּ֣יט אַחֲרֶ֔יךָ וְאַֽל־תַּעֲמֹ֖ד בְּכָל־הַכִּכָּ֑ר הָהָ֥רָה הִמָּלֵ֖ט פֶּן־תִּסָּפֶֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

Al-tabit acharekha — do not look behind you. The do-not-look-back prohibition. The Hebrew uses the verb navat (hiphil — to look intently, gaze) rather than the more general ra'ah (to see); the v 26 fulfillment uses the same root (va-tabet ishto me-acharav). The formula has heavy downstream reception. The NT recall at Luke 17:32 — mnemoneuete tes gynaikos Lot — remember Lot's wife — uses Lot's wife's looking-back as a typological warning in Jesus's apocalyptic discourse on the day of the Son of Man.

The do-not-look-back / do-not-touch / do-not-eat prohibition is also a widespread ANE and cross-cultural narrative pattern (the Orpheus-and-Eurydice myth in Greek; the Sumerian Descent of Inanna; the Akkadian Adapa myth's do-not-eat warning). The pattern is documented in comparative mythology as the taboo-on-the-threshold type-scene. The cross-cultural parallel is noted here as comparative-mythological observation, not as direct evidence of literary dependence; the WoH lens does not extend further into project-distinctive synthesis from this verse.

18
And Lot said unto them: Oh, not so, my lord.
And Lot said unto them, Oh, not so, my lord:
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר ל֖וֹט אֲלֵהֶ֑ם אַל־נָ֖א אֲדֹנָֽי׃
19
Behold now, your servant has found favor in your sight, and you have magnified your loving-kindness, which you have shown unto me in saving my life; and I cannot escape to the mountain, lest evil overtake me, and I die.
behold now, thy servant hath found favor in thy sight, and thou hast magnified thy lovingkindness, which thou hast showed unto me in saving my life; and I cannot escape to the mountain, lest evil overtake me, and I die:
הִנֵּה־נָ֠א מָצָ֨א עַבְדְּךָ֣ חֵן֮ בְּעֵינֶיךָ֒ וַתַּגְדֵּ֣ל חַסְדְּךָ֗ אֲשֶׁ֤ר עָשִׂ֙יתָ֙ עִמָּדִ֔י לְהַחֲי֖וֹת אֶת־נַפְשִׁ֑י וְאָנֹכִ֗י לֹ֤א אוּכַל֙ לְהִמָּלֵ֣ט הָהָ֔רָה פֶּן־תִּדְבָּקַ֥נִי הָרָעָ֖ה וָמַֽתִּי׃
20
Behold now, this city is near to flee unto, and it is a little one. Oh let me escape there — is it not a little one? — and my life shall live.
behold now, this city is near to flee unto, and it is a little one. Oh let me escape thither (is it not a little one?), and my soul shall live.
הִנֵּה־נָ֠א הָעִ֨יר הַזֹּ֧את קְרֹבָ֛ה לָנ֥וּס שָׁ֖מָּה וְהִ֣יא מִצְעָ֑ר אִמָּלְטָ֨ה נָּ֜א שָׁ֗מָּה הֲלֹ֥א מִצְעָ֛ר הִ֖וא וּתְחִ֥י נַפְשִֽׁי׃
21
And he said unto him: See, I have accepted you concerning this thing also, that I will not overthrow the city of which you have spoken.
And he said unto him, See, I have accepted thee concerning this thing also, that I will not overthrow the city of which thou hast spoken.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֵלָ֔יו הִנֵּה֙ נָשָׂ֣אתִי פָנֶ֔יךָ גַּ֖ם לַדָּבָ֣ר הַזֶּ֑ה לְבִלְתִּ֛י הָפְכִּ֥י אֶת־הָעִ֖יר אֲשֶׁ֥ר דִּבַּֽרְתָּ׃
22
Hasten, escape there; for I cannot do anything until you come there. Therefore the name of the city was called Tzo'ar.
Haste thee, escape thither; for I cannot do anything till thou be come thither. Therefore the name of the city was called Zoar.
מַהֵר֙ הִמָּלֵ֣ט שָׁ֔מָּה כִּ֣י לֹ֤א אוּכַל֙ לַעֲשׂ֣וֹת דָּבָ֔ר עַד־בֹּאֲךָ֖ שָׁ֑מָּה עַל־כֵּ֛ן קָרָ֥א שֵׁם־הָעִ֖יר צֽוֹעַר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

Al-ken qara shem-ha-ir Tzo'ar — therefore the name of the city was called Tzo'ar. The folk-etymology of Tzo'ar (צוֹעַר) from the tza'ar / mitzar (small / little one) root deployed at v 20 (ha-ir ha-zot ... v-hi mitzar — this city ... and it is a little one). The translation transliterates Tzo'ar per the WoH patriarchal-place-name convention; the etymological pun is preserved at the lexical level via the little one rendering at v 20. The town is identified by tradition with the southeastern Dead Sea zone (Deut 34:3 kikkar Yericho ... ad-Tzoar; Isa 15:5; Jer 48:34, in the Moab oracles). No separate glossary entry; the etymology is documented in commentary only.

23
The sun was risen upon the land when Lot came unto Tzo'ar.
The sun was risen upon the earth when Lot came unto Zoar.
הַשֶּׁ֖מֶשׁ יָצָ֣א עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וְל֖וֹט בָּ֥א צֹֽעֲרָה׃
24
Then YHWH rained upon Sodom and upon Amorah sulfur and fire from YHWH out of the heavens;
Then Jehovah rained upon Sodom and upon Gomorrah brimstone and fire from Jehovah out of heaven;
וַֽיהוָ֗ה הִמְטִ֧יר עַל־סְדֹ֛ם וְעַל־עֲמֹרָ֖ה גָּפְרִ֣ית וָאֵ֑שׁ מֵאֵ֥ת יְהוָ֖ה מִן־הַשָּׁמָֽיִם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 24

Va-YHWH himtir al-Sodom v-al-Amorah gophrit va-esh me-et YHWH min ha-shamayim — Then YHWH rained upon Sodom and upon Amorah sulfur and fire from YHWH out of the heavens. The chapter's destruction-verse contains two of the most-cited formulae in the entire Hebrew Bible.

Gophrit va-esh — sulfur and fire. The phrase is the HB's canonical destruction-formula and recurs across the corpus with editorial deliberation. Deut 29:22 (gophrit va-melach srefah kol-artzah — sulfur and salt, a burning, all its land, in the curse-list); Job 18:15 (y-zoreh al-navehu gophrit — sulfur is scattered on his habitation); Isa 30:33 (nishmat YHWH k-nachal gophrit bo'erah-bah — the breath of YHWH like a torrent of sulfur kindles it); Isa 34:9 (Edom-oracle, sulfur as the destruction-element); Ezek 38:22 (Gog-Magog oracle, fire and sulfur rained on the invader); Ps 11:6 (yamter al-rasha'im pachim esh v-gophrit — snares, fire and sulfur on the wicked). The NT recall is explicit: Luke 17:29 (the Lot-typology ebrexen pyr kai theion ap' ouranou — it rained fire and sulfur from heaven); Rev 14:10, 19:20, 20:10, 21:8 (the eschatological lake of fire and sulfur). The cross-corpus reach is among the most distributed in the HB.

Me-et YHWH min ha-shamayim — from YHWH out of the heavens. The verse uses the divine name YHWH twice in a single clause: va-YHWH himtir ... me-et YHWH min ha-shamayim. The construction can be read as intensifying repetition (the second YHWH simply restating the first) or as referring to two distinct loci of agency — a reading that became the foundational text of the rabbinic two-powers in heaven debate. The classical rabbinic engagement is b. Sanhedrin 38b, where a heretic uses this very verse to argue for two divine powers; Rav Chisda's response is that Moses simply wrote the divine name twice. The same verse is load-bearing for early Christian binitarian / Logos exegesis: Justin Martyr's Dialogue with Trypho 56-59 uses Gen 19:24 to argue for the Logos as a second divine figure distinct from but coordinate with the Father.

Modern scholarly treatments of the wider two-powers question — Segal Two Powers in Heaven (1977), Boyarin Border Lines: The Partition of Judaeo-Christianity (2004), Boyarin The Jewish Gospels (2012) — read the verse as one of the principal Hebrew Bible texts that second-temple and rabbinic exegetes worked from when articulating positions on divine multiplicity. The two-powers reading is a recognized minority position in both Jewish and Christian reception history. The translation preserves the doubled divine name literally; the apparatus carries the debate. The WoH translation does not preempt the question and does not synthesize a project-distinctive reading from the verse — the lens stays in the wiki and long-form material, not in the glossary or commentary.

25
and he overthrew those cities, and all the Plain, and all the inhabitants of the cities, and that which grew upon the ground.
and he overthrew those cities, and all the Plain, and all the inhabitants of the cities, and that which grew upon the ground.
וַֽיַּהֲפֹךְ֙ אֶת־הֶעָרִ֣ים הָאֵ֔ל וְאֵ֖ת כָּל־הַכִּכָּ֑ר וְאֵת֙ כָּל־יֹשְׁבֵ֣י הֶעָרִ֔ים וְצֶ֖מַח הָאֲדָמָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

Va-yahafokh et-he-arim ha-el — and he overthrew those cities. The verb hafakh (to overturn, overthrow) gives the noun mahapekhah (the overthrowing) at v 29, and the verb-noun pair becomes the standard HB designation for the Sodom-Amorah destruction. The cross-corpus chain is editorially deliberate: Deut 29:22, Isa 1:7 and 13:19 (Babylon-overthrow explicitly typed to Sodom), Jer 49:18 (Edom-oracle), Jer 50:40 (Babylon-oracle), Amos 4:11 (k-mahapekhat Elohim et-Sodom v-et-Amorah — as Elohim overthrew Sodom and Amorah, the covenant-lawsuit formula), Lam 4:6 (Jerusalem's punishment compared to Sodom's). Like the overthrow of Sodom and Amorah becomes the HB's fixed destruction-comparison.

26
But his wife looked back from behind him, and she became a pillar of salt.
But his wife looked back from behind him, and she became a pillar of salt.
וַתַּבֵּ֥ט אִשְׁתּ֖וֹ מֵאַחֲרָ֑יו וַתְּהִ֖י נְצִ֥יב מֶֽלַח׃
Translation Commentary Verse 26

Va-t'hi netziv melach — and she became a pillar of salt. The verb navat (look intently) is the same as in the v 17 prohibition. The noun netziv is from natzav (to stand, the root of the nitzavim of Gen 18:2 shloshah anashim nitzavim alav); the netziv has the sense of one-set-up, fixed-installation. The verse is the etiology of the salt-formations on the western shore of the Dead Sea — Mt. Sodom / Jabal Usdum, a 235-meter halite-and-marl ridge with multiple anthropomorphic salt-pillars produced by differential erosion, identified across two millennia of pilgrimage and traveler accounts as Lot's wife. Josephus Antiquities I.11.4 already references the pillar as a continuing physical site. The Bronze-Age destruction-archaeology of the southeastern Dead Sea (Bab edh-Dhra, Numeira; Rast and Schaub BASOR 1974 and Bab edh-Dhra: Excavations at the Town Site 2003) provides the modern archaeological context. The translation preserves the ASV-baseline pillar of salt.

27
And Avraham got up early in the morning to the place where he had stood before YHWH;
And Abraham gat up early in the morning to the place where he had stood before Jehovah:
וַיַּשְׁכֵּ֥ם אַבְרָהָ֖ם בַּבֹּ֑קֶר אֶל־הַ֨מָּק֔וֹם אֲשֶׁר־עָ֥מַד שָׁ֖ם אֶת־פְּנֵ֥י יְהוָֽה׃
28
and he looked toward Sodom and Amorah, and toward all the land of the Plain, and beheld, and, lo, the smoke of the land went up as the smoke of a furnace.
and he looked toward Sodom and Gomorrah, and toward all the land of the Plain, and beheld, and, lo, the smoke of the land went up as the smoke of a furnace.
וַיַּשְׁקֵ֗ף עַל־פְּנֵ֤י סְדֹם֙ וַעֲמֹרָ֔ה וְעַֽל־כָּל־פְּנֵ֖י אֶ֣רֶץ הַכִּכָּ֑ר וַיַּ֗רְא וְהִנֵּ֤ה עָלָה֙ קִיטֹ֣ר הָאָ֔רֶץ כְּקִיטֹ֖ר הַכִּבְשָֽׁן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 28

Alah qitor ha-aretz k-qitor ha-kivshan — the smoke of the land went up as the smoke of a furnace. The verse is one of the most concretely physical destruction-images in the Hebrew Bible. The lexeme qitor (thick condensed smoke or vapor) is rare — only here (twice), at Ps 119:83 (k-nod b-qitor — like a wineskin in smoke), and Ps 148:8 (esh u-varad sheleg v-qitor). The lexeme is distinct from the more common ashan.

The comparison-noun kivshan (כִּבְשָׁן — furnace, kiln) is the load-bearing element. The lexeme recurs at two structurally significant verses. Exod 9:8 — Moses casts piach kivshan (soot of the furnace) into the air at the sixth plague of boils. Exod 19:18 — at the Sinai theophany, va-ya'al ashano k-eshen ha-kivshan — its smoke went up as the smoke of the furnace, the verbatim echo of Gen 19:28. The cross-corpus chain between the Sodom-overthrow's smoke and the Sinai-theophany's smoke is editorially deliberate; the same furnace-simile frames the two paradigmatic divine-fire events of the Pentateuch.

The kivshan is the standard ANE pottery-kiln or metallurgical-smelter — a high-temperature confined-combustion device producing thick black smoke. It is distinct from kur (smelting-furnace, the figurative iron furnace of Egypt at Deut 4:20 / 1 Kgs 8:51 / Jer 11:4). The recent scientific literature on a possible Late-Bronze-Age cosmic-airburst destruction event at Tall el-Hammam in the southern Jordan Valley (Bunch et al. Scientific Reports 11, 2021) is one current scientific hypothesis for the geological context of the Sodom-tradition; the hypothesis is contested in the geoarchaeological literature and is cited here as a scientific reading, not as a project-distinctive synthesis. The translation preserves the ASV-baseline the smoke of the land went up as the smoke of a furnace.

29
And it came to pass, when Elohim destroyed the cities of the Plain, that Elohim remembered Avraham, and sent Lot out of the midst of the overthrow, when he overthrew the cities in which Lot dwelt.
And it came to pass, when God destroyed the cities of the Plain, that God remembered Abraham, and sent Lot out of the midst of the overthrow, when he overthrew the cities in which Lot dwelt.
וַיְהִ֗י בְּשַׁחֵ֤ת אֱלֹהִים֙ אֶת־עָרֵ֣י הַכִּכָּ֔ר וַיִּזְכֹּ֥ר אֱלֹהִ֖ים אֶת־אַבְרָהָ֑ם וַיְשַׁלַּ֤ח אֶת־לוֹט֙ מִתּ֣וֹךְ הַהֲפֵכָ֔ה בַּהֲפֹךְ֙ אֶת־הֶ֣עָרִ֔ים אֲשֶׁר־יָשַׁ֥ב בָּהֵ֖ן לֽוֹט׃
Translation Commentary Verse 29

Va-yizkor Elohim et-Avraham — Elohim remembered Avraham. The intercession-arc that opened at Gen 18:23-33 closes here with the zakhar (remember) verb. The cross-corpus distribution of divine-remembrance is editorially patterned: Gen 8:1 (va-yizkor Elohim et-Noach — at the flood-cessation), Gen 30:22 (va-yizkor Elohim et-Rachel — at Yosef's conception), Exod 2:24 (va-yizkor Elohim et-brito et-Avraham — at the Exodus prelude), 1 Sam 1:19 (Hannah's conception). The Gen 19:29 occurrence pairs with the v 16 b-chemlat YHWH (chemlat-yhwh-compassion-formula) — divine-sparing and divine-remembrance together constituting the mechanism of Lot's deliverance.

The divine-name shift is also editorially marked: the chapter has used YHWH throughout (vv 13, 14, 16, 17, 21, 24, 27), but here twice uses Elohim. The pattern matches the other zakhar-mechanism verses (Gen 8:1, Gen 30:22, Exod 2:24, all Elohim) and is a P-document compositional marker. The existing vayyizkor-elohim entry (originally governing Gen 8:1 only) is extended in glossary v2.16.0 to include this verse and the wider zakhar-with-divine-subject cluster.

30
And Lot went up out of Tzo'ar, and dwelt in the mountain, and his two daughters with him; for he feared to dwell in Tzo'ar; and he dwelt in the cave, he and his two daughters.
And Lot went up out of Zoar, and dwelt in the mountain, and his two daughters with him; for he feared to dwell in Zoar: and he dwelt in a cave, he and his two daughters.
וַיַּעַל֩ ל֨וֹט מִצּ֜וֹעַר וַיֵּ֣שֶׁב בָּהָ֗ר וּשְׁתֵּ֤י בְנֹתָיו֙ עִמּ֔וֹ כִּ֥י יָרֵ֖א לָשֶׁ֣בֶת בְּצ֑וֹעַר וַיֵּ֙שֶׁב֙ בַּמְּעָרָ֔ה ה֖וּא וּשְׁתֵּ֥י בְנֹתָֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 30

The chapter's coda (vv 30-38) is the etiology of Moab and Ammon — Israel's two transjordan-neighbor peoples — through Lot's daughters' incest with their drunken father. The narrative is one of the canonical HB polemics-against-neighbor-peoples and is the source-text for the Deut 23:4-5 exclusion of Ammonites and Moabites from the assembly of YHWH; see the moab-ammon-etiology-incest entry and the commentary at vv 37-38 for full treatment.

31
And the firstborn said unto the younger: Our father is old, and there is not a man in the land to come in unto us after the manner of all the land;
And the first-born said unto the younger, Our father is old, and there is not a man in the earth to come in unto us after the manner of all the earth:
וַתֹּ֧אמֶר הַבְּכִירָ֛ה אֶל־הַצְּעִירָ֖ה אָבִ֣ינוּ זָקֵ֑ן וְאִ֨ישׁ אֵ֤ין בָּאָ֙רֶץ֙ לָב֣וֹא עָלֵ֔ינוּ כְּדֶ֖רֶךְ כָּל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
32
come, let us make our father drink wine, and we will lie with him, that we may preserve seed from our father.
come, let us make our father drink wine, and we will lie with him, that we may preserve seed of our father.
לְכָ֨ה נַשְׁקֶ֧ה אֶת־אָבִ֛ינוּ יַ֖יִן וְנִשְׁכְּבָ֣ה עִמּ֑וֹ וּנְחַיֶּ֥ה מֵאָבִ֖ינוּ זָֽרַע׃
33
And they made their father drink wine that night; and the firstborn went in, and lay with her father; and he knew not when she lay down, nor when she arose.
And they made their father drink wine that night: and the first-born went in, and lay with her father; and he knew not when she lay down, nor when she arose.
וַתַּשְׁקֶ֧יןָ אֶת־אֲבִיהֶ֛ן יַ֖יִן בַּלַּ֣יְלָה ה֑וּא וַתָּבֹ֤א הַבְּכִירָה֙ וַתִּשְׁכַּ֣ב אֶת־אָבִ֔יהָ וְלֹֽא־יָדַ֥ע בְּשִׁכְבָ֖הּ וּבְקׄוּמָֽהּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 33

U-v-qumah — and when she arose. The Masoretic text marks the vav of uvqumah with a supralinear dot — one of the canonical fifteen puncta extraordinaria documented in the existing puncta-extraordinaria-v9-elav entry (Gen 18:9 list). The rabbinic reading (b. Bava Metzia 87a; Bereshit Rabbah 51:8) reads the dot as marking that Lot in fact did know when she arose (even if not when she lay down); the modern text-critical reading (Tov 2012) treats it as an editorial-flag of textual uncertainty. The translation does not represent the dot; it is noted here for consistency with the chapter-18 entry.

34
And it came to pass on the next day, that the firstborn said unto the younger: Behold, I lay last night with my father; let us make him drink wine this night also, and go in, and lie with him, that we may preserve seed from our father.
And it came to pass on the morrow, that the first-born said unto the younger, Behold, I lay yesternight with my father: let us make him drink wine this night also; and go thou in, and lie with him, that we may preserve seed of our father.
וַֽיְהִי֙ מִֽמָּחֳרָ֔ת וַתֹּ֤אמֶר הַבְּכִירָה֙ אֶל־הַצְּעִירָ֔ה הֵן־שָׁכַ֥בְתִּי אֶ֖מֶשׁ אֶת־אָבִ֑י נַשְׁקֶ֨נּוּ יַ֜יִן גַּם־הַלַּ֗יְלָה וּבֹ֙אִי֙ שִׁכְבִ֣י עִמּ֔וֹ וּנְחַיֶּ֥ה מֵאָבִ֖ינוּ זָֽרַע׃
35
And they made their father drink wine that night also; and the younger arose, and lay with him; and he knew not when she lay down, nor when she arose.
And they made their father drink wine that night also: and the younger arose, and lay with him; and he knew not when she lay down, nor when she arose.
וַתַּשְׁקֶ֜יןָ גַּ֣ם בַּלַּ֧יְלָה הַה֛וּא אֶת־אֲבִיהֶ֖ן יָ֑יִן וַתָּ֤קָם הַצְּעִירָה֙ וַתִּשְׁכַּ֣ב עִמּ֔וֹ וְלֹֽא־יָדַ֥ע בְּשִׁכְבָ֖הּ וּבְקֻמָֽהּ׃
36
Thus were both the daughters of Lot with child by their father.
Thus were both the daughters of Lot with child by their father.
וַֽתַּהֲרֶ֛יןָ שְׁתֵּ֥י בְנֽוֹת־ל֖וֹט מֵאֲבִיהֶֽן׃
37
And the firstborn bore a son, and called his name Moab; he is the father of the Moabites unto this day.
And the first-born bare a son, and called his name Moab: the same is the father of the Moabites unto this day.
וַתֵּ֤לֶד הַבְּכִירָה֙ בֵּ֔ן וַתִּקְרָ֥א שְׁמ֖וֹ מוֹאָ֑ב ה֥וּא אֲבִֽי־מוֹאָ֖ב עַד־הַיּֽוֹם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 37

Va-tiqra shemo Moab — and she called his name Moab. The MT does not preserve an explicit etymological gloss for Moab (מוֹאָב), but the LXX adds legousa ek tou patros mou (saying: from my father) — preserving an interpretive etymology me-av (from-father) that is presupposed by the Hebrew narrative but not stated. The folk-etymology of the place-name from me-av (from-father, i.e. by incest) is one of the most editorially marked etymological cases in Genesis.

38
And the younger, she also bore a son, and called his name Ben-Ammi; he is the father of the children of Ammon unto this day.
And the younger, she also bare a son, and called his name Ben-ammi: the same is the father of the children of Ammon unto this day.
וְהַצְּעִירָ֤ה גַם־הִוא֙ יָ֣לְדָה בֵּ֔ן וַתִּקְרָ֥א שְׁמ֖וֹ בֶּן־עַמִּ֑י ה֛וּא אֲבִ֥י בְנֵֽי־עַמּ֖וֹן עַד־הַיּֽוֹם׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 38

Va-tiqra shemo Ben-Ammi — and she called his name Ben-Ammi. The folk-etymology of Ben-Ammi (literally son of my people / son of my kin) is explicit in the verse itself: ben ammi — son of my kin — i.e. from-within-the-family, the incestuous origin marked at the name-level. The pair-etymology with Moab (v 37) frames the transjordan-neighbor-peoples (Moab and Ammon) as descended-from-incest.

The Deut 23:4-5 exclusion of Ammonites and Moabites from the assembly of YHWH (lo yavo Ammoni u-Moavi b-qhal YHWH gam dor asiri lo yavo lahem b-qhal YHWH ad olam) is grounded in this etiology. Modern scholarly treatments (Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 139-140; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 313-314; Wenham WBC Genesis 16-50 1994; Hamilton NICOT Genesis 18-50 1995; Bauer Reading Lot's Daughters 1999) read the etymology as polemical-etiological rather than philological-historical: the Mesha Stele (ca. 840 BCE) preserves Moab without comment on the etymology, and the Akkadian and Egyptian textual references to Moab and Ammon are independent of the Hebrew etiology. The pair-etymology also produces the foundational tension of the book of Ruth: Ruth the Moabitess descends from the incest-line but enters the Davidic genealogy (Ruth 4:18-22), and the Ruth narrative is in part the canonical Hebrew Bible's working-through of the Deut 23:4-5 exclusion. Solomon's Moabite wives (1 Kgs 11), 2 Kgs 3 (the Moab campaign), and Neh 13:1-3 (the post-exilic re-application of the exclusion) extend the canonical reception of the etiology. The moab-ammon-etiology-incest entry documents the full cross-corpus context.

Chapter 20

Genesis 20 — Avraham at Gerar and the wife-sister episode with Avimelekh

1
And Abraham journeyed from there toward the land of the Negev, and dwelt between Kadesh and Shur; and he sojourned in Gerar.
And Abraham journeyed from thence toward the land of the South, and dwelt between Kadesh and Shur. And he sojourned in Gerar.
וַיִּסַּ֨ע מִשָּׁ֤ם אַבְרָהָם֙ אַ֣רְצָה הַנֶּ֔גֶב וַיֵּ֥שֶׁב בֵּין־קָדֵ֖שׁ וּבֵ֣ין שׁ֑וּר וַיָּ֖גָר בִּגְרָֽר׃
2
And Abraham said of Sarah his wife, She is my sister: and Avimelekh king of Gerar sent, and took Sarah.
And Abraham said of Sarah his wife, She is my sister. And Abimelech king of Gerar sent, and took Sarah.
וַיֹּ֧אמֶר אַבְרָהָ֛ם אֶל־שָׂרָ֥ה אִשְׁתּ֖וֹ אֲחֹ֣תִי הִ֑וא וַיִּשְׁלַ֗ח אֲבִימֶ֙לֶךְ֙ מֶ֣לֶךְ גְּרָ֔ר וַיִּקַּ֖ח אֶת־שָׂרָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The second instance of the wife-sister type-scene (Gen 12, 20, 26; see wife-sister-type-scene). The sister-claim here is unattributed-to-anyone — Abraham said of Sarah his wife, She is my sister — without the earlier imri-na achoti at coaching-speech form of Gen 12:13. The chapter's narrator presents the deception at a remove; the rationale will be retrospectively supplied by Abraham at vv 11-13. The verb va-yiqach (and he took) is the same taking-verb used at Gen 12:15 (va-tuqach ha-ishah); the type-scene vocabulary is being deliberately reused.

3
But the Elohim came to Avimelekh in a dream of the night, and said to him, Behold, you are a dead man, because of the woman whom you have taken; for she is a man's wife.
But God came to Abimelech in a dream of the night, and said to him, Behold, thou art but a dead man, because of the woman whom thou hast taken. For she is a man`s wife.
וַיָּבֹ֧א אֱלֹהִ֛ים אֶל־אֲבִימֶ֖לֶךְ בַּחֲל֣וֹם הַלָּ֑יְלָה וַיֹּ֣אמֶר ל֗וֹ הִנְּךָ֥ מֵת֙ עַל־הָאִשָּׁ֣ה אֲשֶׁר־לָקַ֔חְתָּ וְהִ֖וא בְּעֻ֥לַת בָּֽעַל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

Va-yavo Elohim el-Avimelekh ba-chalom ha-lailah — and the Elohim came to Avimelekh in the dream of the night. The verse is the first occurrence in the Hebrew Bible of the night-dream-revelation formula (see divine-dream-communication-formula). The formula recurs as a structural type across the patriarchal and Joseph cycles (Gen 28:12 Jacob's ladder; Gen 31:24 Laban warned not to speak good-or-evil to Jacob; Gen 37:5-11 Joseph's dreams; Gen 40-41 Pharaoh's cupbearer, baker, and Pharaoh himself; cf. 1 Kgs 3:5 Solomon at Gibeon, the direct formula-parallel) and is one of the Hebrew Bible's principal modes of divine address to non-Israelite figures.

The parallel-structure with Num 12:6 (im yihyeh nevi'akhem YHWH ba-mar'ah elav etvada ba-chalom adaber bo — if there is a prophet of YHWH among you, in a vision I make myself known to him, in a dream I speak with him) makes explicit what this verse instances: the dream is the principal medium of prophetic-revelatory communication outside the Mosaic peh-el-peh face-to-face mode (Num 12:8). Husser 1999 Dreams and Dream Narratives in the Biblical World and Lichtenstein 1968 JANES 1 are the standard scholarly treatments; the ANE comparanda include the Mesopotamian šuttu dream-revelation literature and the Egyptian dream-books of the late New Kingdom (Papyrus Chester Beatty III).

The ASV's in a dream of the night (preserved here over modern smoothings in a dream by night / in a night-time dream) preserves the construct chain chalom ha-lailah visibly, treating the dream-of-the-night as a particular communicative medium rather than as a time-of-day adverbial.

4
Now Avimelekh had not come near her: and he said, Lord, will you slay even a righteous nation?
Now Abimelech had not come near her. And he said, Lord, wilt thou slay even a righteous nation?
וַאֲבִימֶ֕לֶךְ לֹ֥א קָרַ֖ב אֵלֶ֑יהָ וַיֹּאמַ֕ר אֲדֹנָ֕י הֲג֥וֹי גַּם־צַדִּ֖יק תַּהֲרֹֽג׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

Adonai ha-goy gam-tzaddiq taharog — Lord, will you slay even a righteous nation? The verse is a deliberate verbal echo of Abraham's intercession at Gen 18:23-25 (ha-af tispeh tzaddiq im-rasha; chalilah lekha la-asot ka-davar ha-ze — will you indeed sweep away the righteous with the wicked; far be it from you to do such a thing; see abraham-intercession-bargaining). The structural reversal is worth flagging: it is now the foreign king who takes up Abraham's stance against indiscriminate divine slaying — same tzaddiq lexeme, same indignant-vocative Adonai, same accusatory-interrogative form challenging divine justice. Avimelekh-the-non-Israelite-king is the chapter's moral voice; Abraham-the-patriarch will be the morally compromised figure (vv 9-10). Sarna (JPS Genesis 1989, p. 142) reads this as a deliberate compositional inversion of the previous chapter's intercession-scene.

5
Did he not himself say to me, She is my sister? and she, even she herself, said, He is my brother: in the integrity of my heart and in the cleanness of my hands I have done this.
Said he not himself unto me, She is my sister? And she, even she herself said, He is my brother. In the integrity of my heart and the innocency of my hands have I done this.
הֲלֹ֨א ה֤וּא אָֽמַר־לִי֙ אֲחֹ֣תִי הִ֔וא וְהִֽיא־גַם־הִ֥וא אָֽמְרָ֖ה אָחִ֣י ה֑וּא בְּתָם־לְבָבִ֛י וּבְנִקְיֹ֥ן כַּפַּ֖י עָשִׂ֥יתִי זֹֽאת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

B-tom levavi u-v-niqyon kappai — in the integrity of my heart and in the cleanness of my hands. The doubled-noun moral-innocence formula (see b-tom-levav-u-v-niqyon-kappai-moral-innocence) is a fixed forensic-moral idiom. Cross-corpus parallels include Ps 24:4 neqi kappayim u-var levav (clean of hands and pure of heart), Ps 26:6 (washing of hands in innocence), Ps 73:13 (ach riq zikiti levavi va-erchatz b-niqayon kappai), and Job 22:30 (niqyon kappekha). The phrase recurs in shortened form (tom levav alone) at v 6 — Elohim's acknowledgment in the dream. ASV's integrity / cleanness register is preserved over modern smoothings (blamelessness / innocence) so that the doubled-construct legal form remains visible in English.

6
And the Elohim said to him in the dream, Yes, I know that in the integrity of your heart you have done this, and I also withheld you from sinning against me: therefore I did not let you touch her.
And God said unto him in the dream, Yea, I know that in the integrity of thy heart thou has done this, and I also withheld thee from sinning against me. Therefore suffered I thee not to touch her.
וַיֹּאמֶר֩ אֵלָ֨יו הָֽאֱלֹהִ֜ים בַּחֲלֹ֗ם גַּ֣ם אָנֹכִ֤י יָדַ֙עְתִּי֙ כִּ֤י בְתָם־לְבָבְךָ֙ עָשִׂ֣יתָ זֹּ֔את וָאֶחְשֹׂ֧ךְ גַּם־אָנֹכִ֛י אֽוֹתְךָ֖ מֵחֲטוֹ־לִ֑י עַל־כֵּ֥ן לֹא־נְתַתִּ֖יךָ לִנְגֹּ֥עַ אֵלֶֽיהָ׃
7
Now therefore restore the man's wife; for he is a prophet, and he shall pray for you, and you shall live: and if you do not restore her, know that you shall surely die, you, and all that are yours.
Now therefore restore the man`s wife. For he is a prophet, and he shall pray for thee, and thou shalt live. And if thou restore her not, know thou that thou shalt surely die, thou, and all that are thine.
וְעַתָּ֗ה הָשֵׁ֤ב אֵֽשֶׁת־הָאִישׁ֙ כִּֽי־נָבִ֣יא ה֔וּא וְיִתְפַּלֵּ֥ל בַּֽעַדְךָ֖ וֶֽחְיֵ֑ה וְאִם־אֵֽינְךָ֣ מֵשִׁ֗יב דַּ֚ע כִּי־מ֣וֹת תָּמ֔וּת אַתָּ֖ה וְכָל־אֲשֶׁר־לָֽךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

Ki-navi hu v-yitpallel ba-adkha — for he is a prophet, and he shall pray for you. The verse is one of the chapter's two foundational firsts: the first occurrence of navi (prophet) in the Hebrew Bible (see navi-first-occurrence-abraham), paired with the first occurrence of hithpallel (to pray, intercede) at v 17 (see hithpallel-first-prayer-occurrence). The two firsts coincide structurally — the prophetic role is named (v 7) alongside the prophetic function the role discharges (v 17).

The lexical-and-historical significance is substantial. (a) The navi designation places Abraham in the prophetic stratum before the Mosaic prophetic tradition begins (Deut 18:15-18 will frame Moses as the prophetic model, but the title itself is older within the canonical sequence). (b) The function-definition delivered here is intercessory, not predictive: v-yitpallel ba-adkha — and he shall pray on your behalf — defines the prophet as the figure who stands between the deity and the threatened party and prays for the latter's deliverance. The prophetic-intercession model recurs at Exod 32:11-14 (Moses for Israel after the golden calf), Num 14:13-19 (Moses for Israel at Kadesh), 1 Sam 7:5 and 12:23 (Samuel for Israel), Jer 7:16 and 11:14 and 14:11 (the prophetic-intercession prohibition formula — YHWH telling Jeremiah not to pray for the people), and Amos 7:1-6 (the prophet talks YHWH down from two of the three threatened plagues). (c) The mouth uttering the title is the Elohim addressing a foreign king — the title is not first heard from within the Israelite tradition but spoken to an outsider.

The modern scholarly literature distinguishes the navi (the one who is called, or the one who calls — both etymologies are defended) from the older ro'eh (seer, 1 Sam 9:9) and chozeh (visionary). Lindblom 1962 Prophecy in Ancient Israel, Wilson 1980 Prophecy and Society in Ancient Israel, Petersen 2002 The Prophetic Literature, and Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 all treat the Abraham-as-first-navi designation as canonically deliberate. Speiser (Anchor Bible Genesis, 1964) renders navi here as spokesman — preserving the called-and-sent etymology — but the ASV-baseline prophet is preserved here as the standard scholarly rendering; the function-definition is carried by the verse's own paratactic structure (navi hu v-yitpallel — he is a prophet, and he shall pray) rather than by the title's translation.

8
And Avimelekh rose early in the morning, and called all his servants, and spoke all these things in their ears: and the men were greatly afraid.
And Abimelech rose early in the morning, and called all his servants, and told all these things in their ear. And the men were sore afraid.
וַיַּשְׁכֵּ֨ם אֲבִימֶ֜לֶךְ בַּבֹּ֗קֶר וַיִּקְרָא֙ לְכָל־עֲבָדָ֔יו וַיְדַבֵּ֛ר אֶת־כָּל־הַדְּבָרִ֥ים הָאֵ֖לֶּה בְּאָזְנֵיהֶ֑ם וַיִּֽירְא֥וּ הָאֲנָשִׁ֖ים מְאֹֽד׃
9
Then Avimelekh called Abraham, and said to him, What have you done to us? and wherein have I sinned against you, that you have brought on me and on my kingdom a great sin? You have done deeds to me that ought not to be done.
Then Abimelech called Abraham, and said unto him, What hast thou done unto us? And wherein have I sinned against thee, that thou hast brought on me and on my kingdom a great sin? Thou hast done deeds unto me that ought not to be done.
וַיִּקְרָ֨א אֲבִימֶ֜לֶךְ לְאַבְרָהָ֗ם וַיֹּ֨אמֶר ל֜וֹ מֶֽה־עָשִׂ֤יתָ לָּ֙נוּ֙ וּמֶֽה־חָטָ֣אתִי לָ֔ךְ כִּֽי־הֵבֵ֧אתָ עָלַ֛י וְעַל־מַמְלַכְתִּ֖י חֲטָאָ֣ה גְדֹלָ֑ה מַעֲשִׂים֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר לֹא־יֵֽעָשׂ֔וּ עָשִׂ֖יתָ עִמָּדִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The king's rebuke echoes Pharaoh's rebuke at Gen 12:18 (mah-zot asita li — what is this you have done to me) and prefigures Abimelekh's later rebuke of Isaac at Gen 26:10 — the type-scene's recurring rebuke-of-the-patriarch element. The cumulative effect across the three episodes is to mark the patriarchs as morally compromised in their dealings with the foreign kings, and to give the foreign kings the moral voice in each scene.

10
And Avimelekh said to Abraham, What did you see, that you have done this thing?
And Abimelech said unto Abraham, What sawest thou, that thou hast done this thing?
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר אֲבִימֶ֖לֶךְ אֶל־אַבְרָהָ֑ם מָ֣ה רָאִ֔יתָ כִּ֥י עָשִׂ֖יתָ אֶת־הַדָּבָ֥ר הַזֶּֽה׃
11
And Abraham said, Because I thought, Surely the fear of the Elohim is not in this place; and they will slay me for my wife's sake.
And Abraham said, Because I thought, Surely the fear of God is not in this place. And they will slay me for my wife`s sake.
וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ אַבְרָהָ֔ם כִּ֣י אָמַ֗רְתִּי רַ֚ק אֵין־יִרְאַ֣ת אֱלֹהִ֔ים בַּמָּק֖וֹם הַזֶּ֑ה וַהֲרָג֖וּנִי עַל־דְּבַ֥ר אִשְׁתִּֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

Raq ein yir'at Elohim ba-maqom ha-ze — surely the fear of the Elohim is not in this place. The yir'at Elohim / yir'at YHWH motif is the Hebrew Bible's principal designation of moral restraint, prudent reverence, and covenantal-ethical conscience (see yirat-elohim-moral-restraint-motif). The cross-corpus chain is long and load-bearing: Gen 22:12 ata yadati ki yere Elohim ata — the Aqedah's closing word, now I know that you fear the Elohim; Gen 42:18 — Joseph to his brothers, et-ha-Elohim ani yare; Exod 1:17, 1:21 — the Hebrew midwives Shiphrah and Puah, whose fear-of-the-Elohim disobedience to Pharaoh saves Israelite infants; Exod 20:20 — at Sinai, in order that the fear of him may be before you; Job 1:1, 1:8, 2:3 — Job characterized as yere Elohim; and across Proverbs the wisdom-tradition axiom yir'at YHWH reshit da'at (Prov 1:7, 9:10).

The chapter's irony is sharp. Abraham assumed there was no fear-of-the-Elohim in Gerar, and acted to protect himself accordingly; the chapter has just demonstrated that in fact Avimelekh fears the Elohim (he refrains from touching Sarah on hearing the dream, vv 6-8) and the deception was unnecessary — and indeed harmful (it endangered Avimelekh and his household, v 18). The narrative's evaluation of Abraham's prudential calculation is implicit but pointed. Becker 1965 Gottesfurcht im Alten Testament is the standard treatment of the motif; Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 reads this verse as the chapter's central irony.

12
And moreover she is indeed my sister, the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother; and she became my wife.
And moreover she is indeed my sister, the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother; and she became my wife:
וְגַם־אָמְנָ֗ה אֲחֹתִ֤י בַת־אָבִי֙ הִ֔וא אַ֖ךְ לֹ֣א בַת־אִמִּ֑י וַתְּהִי־לִ֖י לְאִשָּֽׁה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

V-gam omnah achoti bat-avi hi, akh lo bat-imi — and moreover she is indeed my sister, the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother. The verse is the only place in the patriarchal narrative where the half-sister claim of the wife-sister deception is explicitly defended, and it is offered defensively under questioning. The claim makes Abraham's earlier statement at Gen 12:13 (imri-na achoti at) technically true — Sarah is, on this account, his half-sister; see the discussion at imri-na-achoti-at.

The modern critical-scholarship reading treats v 12 with caution: the half-sister datum appears only here, only after rebuke, and only in the chapter most plausibly assigned to the E source on the classical documentary hypothesis. Speiser 1964, Westermann 1985, Sarna 1989, and Hamilton 1990 all flag the verse as potentially apologetic redaction — material introduced precisely to mitigate the deception. The Mosaic-law prohibition of half-sister marriage (Lev 18:9, 20:17, Deut 27:22) post-dates the patriarchal period within the canonical sequence, so Abraham's claim does not on its face violate any law the narrative recognizes as binding on him; the harmonization works at the level of legal chronology. The rabbinic tradition (Gen Rab 41:2; Rashi) takes the verse at face value as authentic Abrahamic genealogy and treats Abraham's earlier deception as a partial truth under duress. The two readings are not mutually exclusive — both are documented in the imri-na-achoti-at entry.

13
And it came to pass, when the Elohim caused me to wander from my father's house, that I said to her, This is your kindness which you shall show to me: at every place where we shall come, say of me, He is my brother.
and it came to pass, when God caused me to wander from my father`s house, that I said unto her, This is thy kindness which thou shalt show unto me. At every place whither we shall come, say of me, He is my brother.
וַיְהִ֞י כַּאֲשֶׁ֧ר הִתְע֣וּ אֹתִ֗י אֱלֹהִים֮ מִבֵּ֣ית אָבִי֒ וָאֹמַ֣ר לָ֔הּ זֶ֣ה חַסְדֵּ֔ךְ אֲשֶׁ֥ר תַּעֲשִׂ֖י עִמָּדִ֑י אֶ֤ל כָּל־הַמָּקוֹם֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר נָב֣וֹא שָׁ֔מָּה אִמְרִי־לִ֖י אָחִ֥י הֽוּא׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

Hit'u oti Elohim mi-beit avi — the Elohim caused me to wander from my father's house. The verb hit'u (hiphil 3mp of t-ʿ-h, to err / wander) is morphologically plural — they caused me to wander — with Elohim as a plural-syntactic-agreement subject. The chapter contains one of the Hebrew Bible's clearest cases where Elohim takes a plural verb (other widely-cited cases: Gen 35:7 niglu elav ha-Elohim; Exod 32:4, 8 eleh elohekha Yisrael asher he'elukha me-eretz Mitzrayim; 2 Sam 7:23 / 1 Chron 17:21 halkhu Elohim lifdot lo l-am). The verse is editorially significant for the broader project policy on Elohim-with-plural-verbs, but the WoH translation here follows the ASV-baseline singular smoothing (the Elohim caused me) for English readability, and surfaces the morphological-plural datum in commentary rather than in the translation text.

The modern critical scholarship treats most such cases as singular-meaning despite plural form: NJPS, Speiser 1964, and the standard reference grammars (GKC §145i; Joüon-Muraoka §150f) read them as plural of majesty or as grammatical plural with semantic singular. The dissenting position — that the plural verb-agreement preserves an older polytheistic stratum — is associated with the History-of-Religions School (Gunkel; Wellhausen) and with the modern divine-council scholarship (Mullen 1980 The Divine Council; Smith 2001 The Origins of Biblical Monotheism). The WoH glossary's elohim-as-translation entry's article-convention does not preempt either reading; the chapter editor's note is to flag this verse for future Reviewer attention on whether a project-policy decision on plural-verb cases should be settled by a dedicated glossary entry.

14
And Avimelekh took sheep and oxen, and menservants and maidservants, and gave them to Abraham, and restored him Sarah his wife.
And Abimelech took sheep and oxen, and men-servants and women-servants, and gave them unto Abraham, and restored him Sarah his wife.
וַיִּקַּ֨ח אֲבִימֶ֜לֶךְ צֹ֣אן וּבָקָ֗ר וַעֲבָדִים֙ וּשְׁפָחֹ֔ת וַיִּתֵּ֖ן לְאַבְרָהָ֑ם וַיָּ֣שֶׁב ל֔וֹ אֵ֖ת שָׂרָ֥ה אִשְׁתּֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

The gift-and-restoration formula parallels Pharaoh's at Gen 12:16, 19-20 — same inventory items (sheep, oxen, servants, maidservants), same restoration of the wife. The type-scene's recurring gift-and-restoration element is the king's restitution after the deception is uncovered; Avimelekh adds (at v 15) the offer of the land itself, which Pharaoh did not.

15
And Avimelekh said, Behold, my land is before you: dwell where it pleases you.
And Abimelech said, Behold, my land is before thee. Dwell where it pleaseth thee.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֲבִימֶ֔לֶךְ הִנֵּ֥ה אַרְצִ֖י לְפָנֶ֑יךָ בַּטּ֥וֹב בְּעֵינֶ֖יךָ שֵֽׁב׃
16
And to Sarah he said, Behold, I have given a thousand pieces of silver to your brother: behold, it is for you a covering of the eyes to all that are with you; and in respect of all you are righted.
And unto Sarah he said, Behold, I have given thy brother a thousand pieces of silver. Behold, it is for thee a covering of the eyes to all that are with thee. And in respect of all thou art righted.
וּלְשָׂרָ֣ה אָמַ֗ר הִנֵּ֨ה נָתַ֜תִּי אֶ֤לֶף כֶּ֙סֶף֙ לְאָחִ֔יךְ הִנֵּ֤ה הוּא־לָךְ֙ כְּס֣וּת עֵינַ֔יִם לְכֹ֖ל אֲשֶׁ֣ר אִתָּ֑ךְ וְאֵ֥ת כֹּ֖ל וְנֹכָֽחַת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

Hineh hu lakh kesut eynayim — behold, it is for you a covering of the eyes. The phrase kesut eynayim is a hapax in the Hebrew Bible (see kesut-eynayim-vindication-formula) and its idiomatic sense is genuinely contested. Three principal modern readings: (a) vindication / public exoneration — the thousand pieces of silver function as a public sign that no one looking on can impute disgrace to Sarah; this is the dominant modern reading (Sarna JPS Genesis 1989, Speiser Anchor Bible Genesis 1964; cf. ASV a covering of the eyes); (b) veil / face-covering — a literal cloth purchased with the silver, as a marker of restored honorable-married-woman status (Rashi; some medieval commentators); (c) bribe / hush-money — a minority critical reading where the silver buys the silence of onlookers (some early 20th-c. critical commentators).

The closing phrase ve-et kol ve-nokhachat (and in respect of all you are righted) is also obscure — the verb yakach (niphal participle nokhachat) carries the sense of being-put-right / vindicated / judged-correct, but the syntax is difficult (Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 reads the text as possibly corrupt; NJPS hedges and you are cleared before everyone). The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline literal a covering of the eyes ... in respect of all you are righted, with the three-reading dispute carried in the apparatus.

17
And Abraham prayed to the Elohim; and the Elohim healed Avimelekh, and his wife, and his maidservants; and they bore children.
And Abraham prayed unto God. And God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his maid-servants. And they bare children.
וַיִּתְפַּלֵּ֥ל אַבְרָהָ֖ם אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִ֑ים וַיִּרְפָּ֨א אֱלֹהִ֜ים אֶת־אֲבִימֶ֧לֶךְ וְאֶת־אִשְׁתּ֛וֹ וְאַמְהֹתָ֖יו וַיֵּלֵֽדוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

Va-yitpallel Avraham el ha-Elohim — and Abraham prayed to the Elohim. The verse delivers the second of the chapter's two foundational firsts: the first occurrence in the Hebrew Bible of hithpallel (the hithpael of p-l-l, to pray, to intercede) — paired with the first occurrence of navi at v 7 (see hithpallel-first-prayer-occurrence and navi-first-occurrence-abraham). The two firsts coincide structurally: the prophet promised in v 7 now performs the prophetic act that v 7 defined. The chapter is the canonical Hebrew Bible function-definition of the prophet as intercessor.

The lexeme hithpallel (reflexive-iterative-hithpael of p-l-l) becomes the Hebrew Bible's principal verb for petitionary and intercessory prayer. The cross-corpus distribution is forward-loaded: Num 11:2, 21:7 (Moses for the people); 1 Sam 1:10, 1:12, 2:1 (Hannah); 1 Sam 7:5, 8:6, 12:19, 12:23 (Samuel, including the load-bearing chalilah li me-chato la-YHWH me-chadol l-hitpallel ba-adkhem — far be it from me to sin against YHWH by ceasing to pray for you); 1 Kgs 8:28-54 (Solomon's temple-dedication prayer, the longest hithpallel sequence in the corpus); 2 Kgs 19:15, 20:2, Isa 37:15, 38:2 (Hezekiah); Jer 7:16, 11:14, 14:11 (the prophetic-intercession-prohibition formula); Dan 9:4, 9:20; Neh 1:4, 1:6, 2:4. Greenberg 1983 Biblical Prose Prayer is the standard treatment; Miller 1994 They Cried to the Lord and Balentine 1993 Prayer in the Hebrew Bible extend the analysis. The etymology of hithpallel is debated — HALOT glosses to intercede; Greenberg argues for to assess oneself (taking the reflexive seriously); the broader West-Semitic root p-l-l (judge, intervene, mediate) underwrites both readings. The translation preserves the ASV-baseline prayed over the more lexically-precise interceded on the grounds that the standard rendering captures the function (and the function-definition is carried by the v 7 framing); the nuance is recorded in the entry.

18
For YHWH had fast closed up every womb of the house of Avimelekh, because of Sarah, Abraham's wife.
For Jehovah had fast closed up all the wombs of the house of Abimelech, because of Sarah, Abraham`s wife.
כִּֽי־עָצֹ֤ר עָצַר֙ יְהוָ֔ה בְּעַ֥ד כָּל־רֶ֖חֶם לְבֵ֣ית אֲבִימֶ֑לֶךְ עַל־דְּבַ֥ר שָׂרָ֖ה אֵ֥שֶׁת אַבְרָהָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

Ki-atzor atzar YHWH be-ad kol-rechem le-veit Avimelekh — for YHWH had fast closed up every womb of the house of Avimelekh. The doubled construction atzor atzar (infinitive absolute + finite verb of atzar — restrain, hold back, close up) is the Hebrew Bible's standard intensification pattern for emphatic verb-action; ASV's fast closed up preserves the doubling visibly. The chapter's structural movement — patriarch endangered → foreign king afflicted with closed wombs → patriarch intercedes (v 17) → wombs reopen — is reconstructed retrospectively at this closing verse, which functions as the chapter's etiological coda.

The closed-wombs-as-divine-sanction motif (see atzar-rechem-closed-wombs-divine-sanction) recurs across the matriarchal-barrenness pattern of the patriarchal and pre-monarchic corpus: Gen 11:30 (Sarai herself — the unresolved barrenness that hangs over the entire Abraham cycle and resolves only at Gen 21); Gen 25:21 (Rebekah, whose barrenness Isaac entreats YHWH for); Gen 29:31 (the Leah / Rachel inversion — va-yare YHWH ki s'nu'ah Leah va-yiftach et-rachmah v-Rachel aqarah — Leah's womb opened, Rachel's closed); Judg 13:2-3 (Manoah's wife, the mother of Samson); 1 Sam 1:5-6 (va-YHWH sagar rachmah — Hannah, with the same sagar-the-womb verbal collocation). Brichto 1973 HUCA 44 on the womb-theology of the matriarchal cycle is the standard treatment; Westermann Genesis 12-36 and Hamilton NICOT Genesis 18-50 extend the analysis. The chapter's irony recurs at the structural level: the wife-sister deception undertaken to protect the patriarchal couple has produced precisely the barrenness-on-the-foreign-household that the cycle ordinarily reserves for the matriarchs themselves.

Chapter 21

Genesis 21 — The birth of Yitzchaq, the expulsion of Hagar and Yishma'el, and the covenant at Be'er Sheva

1
And YHWH visited Sarah as he had said, and YHWH did to Sarah as he had spoken.
And Jehovah visited Sarah as he had said, and Jehovah did unto Sarah as he had spoken.
וַֽיהוָ֛ה פָּקַ֥ד אֶת־שָׂרָ֖ה כַּאֲשֶׁ֣ר אָמָ֑ר וַיַּ֧עַשׂ יְהוָ֛ה לְשָׂרָ֖ה כַּאֲשֶׁ֥ר דִּבֵּֽר׃
2
And Sarah conceived, and bore Avraham a son in his old age, at the appointed time of which Elohim had spoken to him.
And Sarah conceived, and bare Abraham a son in his old age, at the set time of which God had spoken to him.
וַתַּהַר֩ וַתֵּ֨לֶד שָׂרָ֧ה לְאַבְרָהָ֛ם בֵּ֖ן לִזְקֻנָ֑יו לַמּוֹעֵ֕ד אֲשֶׁר־דִּבֶּ֥ר אֹת֖וֹ אֱלֹהִֽים׃
3
And Avraham called the name of his son that was born to him, whom Sarah bore to him, Yitzchaq.
And Abraham called the name of his son that was born unto him, whom Sarah bare to him, Isaac.
וַיִּקְרָ֨א אַבְרָהָ֜ם אֶֽת־שֶׁם־בְּנ֧וֹ הַנּֽוֹלַד־ל֛וֹ אֲשֶׁר־יָלְדָה־לּ֥וֹ שָׂרָ֖ה יִצְחָֽק׃
4
And Avraham circumcised his son Yitzchaq when he was eight days old, as Elohim had commanded him.
And Abraham circumcised his son Isaac when he was eight days old, as God had commanded him.
וַיָּ֤מָל אַבְרָהָם֙ אֶת־יִצְחָ֣ק בְּנ֔וֹ בֶּן־שְׁמֹנַ֖ת יָמִ֑ים כַּאֲשֶׁ֛ר צִוָּ֥ה אֹת֖וֹ אֱלֹהִֽים׃
5
And Avraham was a hundred years old, when his son Yitzchaq was born to him.
And Abraham was a hundred years old, when his son Isaac was born unto him.
וְאַבְרָהָ֖ם בֶּן־מְאַ֣ת שָׁנָ֑ה בְּהִוָּ֣לֶד ל֔וֹ אֵ֖ת יִצְחָ֥ק בְּנֽוֹ׃
6
And Sarah said, Elohim has made me to laugh; everyone that hears will laugh with me.
And Sarah said, God hath made me to laugh. Every one that heareth will laugh with me.
וַתֹּ֣אמֶר שָׂרָ֔ה צְחֹ֕ק עָ֥שָׂה לִ֖י אֱלֹהִ֑ים כָּל־הַשֹּׁמֵ֖עַ יִֽצְחַק־לִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

Tzachoq asah li Elohim kol ha-shomea yitzchaq-li — the third and resolving instance of the Avraham-cycle's three-part laughter-Isaac play. Where Avraham's laugh at Gen 17:17 (va-yitzchaq) and Sarah's laugh at Gen 18:12-15 (va-titzchaq Sarah b-qirbah) were both internal disbelief-laughs at the announcement of conception, the v 6 laugh is the post-conception joy-laugh that closes the pattern. The verse compresses the densest verb-noun cluster of the tz-ch-q root in the entire chapter: the noun tzachoq (laughter) + the qal participle ha-shomea (the one who hears) + the qal imperfect yitzchaq (will laugh), with the name Yitzchaq itself naming the son.

The closing clause kol ha-shomea yitzchaq-li admits two readings: (i) everyone who hears will laugh with me (the joy-resolution reading; ASV, NJPS, Alter, Sarna JPS Genesis 1989, p. 146) — the standard modern critical default, reading the laughter as shared joy; (ii) everyone who hears will laugh at me (the mocking-Sarah reading; minority position with some rabbinic support) — the laughter as derisive. The WoH translation adopts the joy-resolution reading (ASV-baseline preserved), consistent with the chapter's three-part disbelief-to-joy inversion documented in the yitzchaq-name-etymology-laughter-pattern glossary entry.

The verse's name-and-verb cluster makes the etymology of Yitzchaq visible in a way that conventional English (Isaac) cannot reproduce; the transliteration preserves the play. Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985, pp. 333-336.

7
And she said, Who would have said to Avraham that Sarah should nurse children? For I have borne him a son in his old age.
And she said, Who would have said unto Abraham, that Sarah should give children suck? For I have borne him a son in his old age.
וַתֹּ֗אמֶר מִ֤י מִלֵּל֙ לְאַבְרָהָ֔ם הֵינִ֥יקָה בָנִ֖ים שָׂרָ֑ה כִּֽי־יָלַ֥דְתִּי בֵ֖ן לִזְקֻנָֽיו׃
8
And the child grew, and was weaned; and Avraham made a great feast on the day that Yitzchaq was weaned.
And the child grew, and was weaned. And Abraham made a great feast on the day that Isaac was weaned.
וַיִּגְדַּ֥ל הַיֶּ֖לֶד וַיִּגָּמַ֑ל וַיַּ֤עַשׂ אַבְרָהָם֙ מִשְׁתֶּ֣ה גָד֔וֹל בְּי֖וֹם הִגָּמֵ֥ל אֶת־יִצְחָֽק׃
9
And Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, whom she had borne to Avraham, playing.
And Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, whom she had borne unto Abraham, mocking.
וַתֵּ֨רֶא שָׂרָ֜ה אֶֽת־בֶּן־הָגָ֧ר הַמִּצְרִ֛ית אֲשֶׁר־יָלְדָ֥ה לְאַבְרָהָ֖ם מְצַחֵֽק׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The piel participle metzacheq is one of the most editorially contested verbs in the Avraham cycle, with four parallel readings each defended in the philological literature: (a) playing / sporting (the lexical default; Sarna, Alter, NJPS, Westermann); (b) playing-with-Isaac (the LXX expansion meta Isaak tou huiou autēs, followed by Vulgate, Peshitta, NRSV, and presupposed in Paul's diōkōn at Gal 4:29); (c) mocking / abusing (ASV/KJV, drawing on the piel's harsher uses at Gen 19:14, Gen 39:14-17, Exod 32:6); (d) the rabbinic-tradition maximal reading of idolatry / sexual immorality / bloodshed (Genesis Rabbah 53:11; b. Sotah 26b; Rashi). The WoH translation moves from ASV's mocking to playing, adopting the modern critical consensus default and reading the participle as the deliberate continuation of the chapter's tzachoq / yitzchaq / Yitzchaq name-and-verb play at vv 6-8.

The verse's syntax foregrounds Sarah's perception of the act, not its objective content: and Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian ... playing. Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985, pp. 338-339 reads the verb's semantic ambiguity as deliberate construction — the reader is left, like Avraham at v 11, uncertain how to weigh Sarah's reaction. The harsher readings are documented in the metzacheq-ishmael-debate glossary entry but not adopted; the editorial principle is to not import rabbinic-tradition harshness on Yishma'el where the MT does not itself carry it. Sarna JPS Genesis 1989, p. 146; Alter The Five Books of Moses 2004, pp. 103-104; Hamilton NICOT Genesis 18-50 1995, pp. 78-79.

10
And she said to Avraham, Cast out this handmaid and her son: for the son of this handmaid shall not be heir with my son, with Yitzchaq.
Wherefore she said unto Abraham, Cast out this handmaid and her son. For the son of this handmaid shall not be heir with my son, even with Isaac.
וַתֹּ֙אמֶר֙ לְאַבְרָהָ֔ם גָּרֵ֛שׁ הָאָמָ֥ה הַזֹּ֖את וְאֶת־בְּנָ֑הּ כִּ֣י לֹ֤א יִירַשׁ֙ בֶּן־הָאָמָ֣ה הַזֹּ֔את עִם־בְּנִ֖י עִם־יִצְחָֽק׃
11
And the thing was very grievous in Avraham's eyes on account of his son.
And the thing was very grievous in Abraham`s sight on account of his son.
וַיֵּ֧רַע הַדָּבָ֛ר מְאֹ֖ד בְּעֵינֵ֣י אַבְרָהָ֑ם עַ֖ל אוֹדֹ֥ת בְּנֽוֹ׃
12
And Elohim said to Avraham, Let it not be grievous in your eyes because of the lad, and because of your handmaid; in all that Sarah says to you, listen to her voice; for in Yitzchaq shall your seed be called.
And God said unto Abraham, Let it not be grievous in thy sight because of the lad, and because of thy handmaid. In all that Sarah saith unto thee, hearken unto her voice. For in Isaac shall thy seed be called.
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֜ים אֶל־אַבְרָהָ֗ם אַל־יֵרַ֤ע בְּעֵינֶ֙יךָ֙ עַל־הַנַּ֣עַר וְעַל־אֲמָתֶ֔ךָ כֹּל֩ אֲשֶׁ֨ר תֹּאמַ֥ר אֵלֶ֛יךָ שָׂרָ֖ה שְׁמַ֣ע בְּקֹלָ֑הּ כִּ֣י בְיִצְחָ֔ק יִקָּרֵ֥א לְךָ֖ זָֽרַע׃
13
And also of the son of the handmaid will I make a nation, because he is your seed.
And also of the son of the handmaid will I make a nation, because he is thy seed.
וְגַ֥ם אֶת־בֶּן־הָאָמָ֖ה לְג֣וֹי אֲשִׂימֶ֑נּוּ כִּ֥י זַרְעֲךָ֖ הֽוּא׃
14
And Avraham rose up early in the morning, and took bread and a skin of water, and gave it to Hagar, putting it on her shoulder, and the child, and sent her away; and she departed, and wandered in the wilderness of Be'er Sheva.
And Abraham rose up early in the morning, and took bread and a bottle of water, and gave it unto Hagar, putting it on her shoulder, and [gave her] the child, and sent her away. And she departed, and wandered in the wilderness of Beer-sheba.
וַיַּשְׁכֵּ֣ם אַבְרָהָ֣ם ׀ בַּבֹּ֡קֶר וַיִּֽקַּֽח־לֶחֶם֩ וְחֵ֨מַת מַ֜יִם וַיִּתֵּ֣ן אֶל־הָ֠גָר שָׂ֧ם עַל־שִׁכְמָ֛הּ וְאֶת־הַיֶּ֖לֶד וַֽיְשַׁלְּחֶ֑הָ וַתֵּ֣לֶךְ וַתֵּ֔תַע בְּמִדְבַּ֖ר בְּאֵ֥ר שָֽׁבַע׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

Chemat mayim — the chemet (חֵמֶת, H2573) is a leather water-skin, the load-bearing object of the expulsion scene and the chapter's three-verse arc (v 14 chemat mayim — a skin of water; v 15 va-yikhlu ha-mayim min ha-chemet — the water was spent from the skin; v 19 va-tmaleh et ha-chemet mayim — she filled the skin with water). The lexeme is a hapax-cluster in Genesis (recurring only at Hab 2:15 in the rest of the HB). ASV's bottle (KJV/RSV) is now misleading in modern English where bottle suggests a glass-or-plastic vessel; NRSV / NJPS / Alter render skin / waterskin, closer to the leather-vessel ANE reality. The WoH translation reads a skin of water, preserving the modernized lexeme without committing it to its own glossary entry (a minor lexical decision, recorded in commentary).

The geographical placement b-midbar Be'er Sheva (in the wilderness of Be'er Sheva) marks the chapter's first Be'er-Sheva reference and anticipates the v 31 in-text etiology of the place-name (see be-er-sheva-folk-etymology for the double-etymology sheva-as-seven / shava-as-oath). The wandering-in-the-wilderness register (va-tetа — and she wandered; the verb t-ʿ-h) anticipates the deeper expulsion-and-rescue narrative that the paqach Elohim et eyneha of v 19 will resolve.

15
And the water in the skin was spent, and she cast the child under one of the shrubs.
And the water in the bottle was spent, and she cast the child under one of the shrubs.
וַיִּכְל֥וּ הַמַּ֖יִם מִן־הַחֵ֑מֶת וַתַּשְׁלֵ֣ךְ אֶת־הַיֶּ֔לֶד תַּ֖חַת אַחַ֥ד הַשִּׂיחִֽם׃
16
And she went, and sat down opposite him a good way off, as it were a bowshot: for she said, Let me not see the death of the child. And she sat opposite him, and lifted up her voice, and wept.
And she went, and sat her down over against him a good way off, as it were a bowshot. For she said, Let me not look upon the death of the child. And she sat over against him, and lifted up her voice, and wept.
