וַתֵּלֶךְ֩ וַתֵּ֨שֶׁב לָ֜הּ מִנֶּ֗גֶד הַרְחֵק֙ כִּמְטַחֲוֵ֣י קֶ֔שֶׁת כִּ֣י אָֽמְרָ֔ה אַל־אֶרְאֶ֖ה בְּמ֣וֹת הַיָּ֑לֶד וַתֵּ֣שֶׁב מִנֶּ֔גֶד וַתִּשָּׂ֥א אֶת־קֹלָ֖הּ וַתֵּֽבְךְּ׃
17
And Elohim heard the voice of the lad; and the messenger of Elohim called to Hagar out of heaven, and said to her, What ails you, Hagar? Fear not; for Elohim has heard the voice of the lad where he is.
And God heard the voice of the lad. And the angel of God called to Hagar out of heaven, and said unto her, What aileth thee, Hagar? Fear not. For God hath heard the voice of the lad where he is.
וַיִּשְׁמַ֣ע אֱלֹהִים֮ אֶת־ק֣וֹל הַנַּעַר֒ וַיִּקְרָא֩ מַלְאַ֨ךְ אֱלֹהִ֤ים ׀ אֶל־הָגָר֙ מִן־הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וַיֹּ֥אמֶר לָ֖הּ מַה־לָּ֣ךְ הָגָ֑ר אַל־תִּ֣ירְאִ֔י כִּֽי־שָׁמַ֧ע אֱלֹהִ֛ים אֶל־ק֥וֹל הַנַּ֖עַר בַּאֲשֶׁ֥ר הוּא־שָֽׁם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

The verse contains the first Hebrew Bible occurrence of mal'akh Elohim — messenger of Elohim — and is editorially distinct from the mal'akh YHWH of the first Hagar-scene at Gen 16:7-13. The chapter's second Hagar-scene (vv 14-21) uses Elohim consistently throughout (vv 12, 17 twice, 19, 20) where Gen 16 used YHWH and mal'akh YHWH throughout. The divine-name register is consistently different across the two Hagar-scenes.

Modern critical scholarship reads the divine-name shift as the principal source-critical evidence for the documentary attribution of the two Hagar-scenes to different sources: Gen 16 to the J-document (the Yahwist), Gen 21:8-21 to the E-document (the Elohist), with the two scenes constituting a doublet — the same basic narrative (Hagar in the wilderness, divine encounter at a well, promise of Yishma'el's tribal future) attested in two parallel source-versions. Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985, pp. 339-340; Speiser Anchor Bible Genesis 1964, pp. 155-157; Friedman Who Wrote the Bible? 1987, pp. 246-258; Skinner ICC Genesis 1910, pp. 320-325. The post-documentary critique (Van Seters 1975, Carr 1996, Schmid 1999) preserves the doublet-recognition while questioning the four-source attribution; the source-critical reading is the standard modern-critical treatment without being uniquely consensus.

The min ha-shamayim (from heaven) qualifier marks a remoter mode of encounter than Gen 16's wilderness messenger who is found by Hagar at the spring and identified as YHWH at v 13. The Gen 21 messenger calls from heaven and is not physically present; the well of v 19 is revealed only by Elohim's opening of Hagar's eyes. The mediated-immanence register is consistent with the E-document's broader pattern (Gen 20:3 the dream-revelation to Avimelekh; Gen 28:12 the mal'akhei Elohim on the ladder; Gen 31:11 the mal'akh ha-Elohim in Ya'aqov's dream). See malakh-elohim-messenger for the full source-critical and cross-corpus treatment.

18
Arise, lift up the lad, and hold him by your hand; for I will make him a great nation.
Arise, lift up the lad, and hold him in thy hand. For I will make him a great nation.
ק֚וּמִי שְׂאִ֣י אֶת־הַנַּ֔עַר וְהַחֲזִ֥יקִי אֶת־יָדֵ֖ךְ בּ֑וֹ כִּֽי־לְג֥וֹי גָּד֖וֹל אֲשִׂימֶֽנּוּ׃
19
And Elohim opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water; and she went, and filled the skin with water, and gave the lad drink.
And God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water. And she went, and filled the bottle with water, and gave the lad drink.
וַיִּפְקַ֤ח אֱלֹהִים֙ אֶת־עֵינֶ֔יהָ וַתֵּ֖רֶא בְּאֵ֣ר מָ֑יִם וַתֵּ֜לֶךְ וַתְּמַלֵּ֤א אֶת־הַחֵ֙מֶת֙ מַ֔יִם וַתַּ֖שְׁקְ אֶת־הַנָּֽעַר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 19

Va-yifqach Elohim et-eyneha va-tere be'er mayim — the patriarchal corpus's first occurrence of the paqach et ha-eynayim (open the eyes) idiom in the revelatory-discovery register. The idiom names a developed Hebrew Bible figure for deity-induced perceptual revelation: a shift in the recipient's sight that brings a previously-hidden reality into view. The cross-corpus pattern is consistent: at Num 22:31 the deity uncovers Bil'am's eyes to see the mal'akh YHWH who had been standing in the road all along; at 2 Kgs 6:17 Elisha prays that his servant's eyes be opened to see the chariots of fire already on the mountain; at 2 Kgs 6:20 the verb operates in reverse on the Aramean army within Samaria. In every cross-corpus instance the deity opens the recipient's eyes to a reality that was already present but unseen.

The well of v 19 is editorially read in two main ways: (a) the well-was-always-there reading — the well is a pre-existing wilderness spring that Hagar had been unable to see; the divine action is Hagar's perception, not the well's creation. This is the modern critical default (Sarna JPS Genesis 1989, p. 147; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985, p. 343; NJPS; NRSV; Alter 2004, p. 105) and reflects the lexical core of the paqach-eynayim idiom across the HB. (b) The rabbinic-tradition well-created-at-the-moment reading (Genesis Rabbah 53:14; Pseudo-Jonathan; Midrash Tanchuma) — the well as ad hoc miracle, included in the list of created-at-twilight-of-the-sixth-day wells in the Mishnaic tradition. The WoH translation adopts the modern critical default; the rabbinic-tradition reading is documented in the paqach-eynayim-revelatory-perception glossary entry but not adopted.

Within the Avraham cycle, Hagar's pattern across the two scenes is editorially significant: at Gen 16:13 Hagar names the deity who saw her (El Ro'i — the El who sees; the first divine-name-naming by a woman in the HB); at Gen 21:19 the same deity opens her seeing. The foreign-Egyptian-woman-as-seer pattern is documented by Trible Texts of Terror 1984; Reis Reading the Lines 2002.

20
And Elohim was with the lad, and he grew; and he dwelt in the wilderness, and became, as he grew up, an archer.
And God was with the lad, and he grew. And he dwelt in the wilderness, and became, as he grew up, an archer.
וַיְהִ֧י אֱלֹהִ֛ים אֶת־הַנַּ֖עַר וַיִּגְדָּ֑ל וַיֵּ֙שֶׁב֙ בַּמִּדְבָּ֔ר וַיְהִ֖י רֹבֶ֥ה קַשָּֽׁת׃
21
And he dwelt in the wilderness of Paran; and his mother took him a wife out of the land of Mitsrayim.
And he dwelt in the wilderness of Paran. And his mother took him a wife out of the land of Egypt.
וַיֵּ֖שֶׁב בְּמִדְבַּ֣ר פָּארָ֑ן וַתִּֽקַּֽח־ל֥וֹ אִמּ֛וֹ אִשָּׁ֖ה מֵאֶ֥רֶץ מִצְרָֽיִם׃ (פ)
22
And it came to pass at that time, that Avimelekh and Phicol the captain of his host spoke to Avraham, saying, Elohim is with you in all that you do.
And it came to pass at that time, that Abimelech and Phicol the captain of his host spake unto Abraham, saying, God is with thee in all that thou doest.
וַֽיְהִי֙ בָּעֵ֣ת הַהִ֔וא וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֲבִימֶ֗לֶךְ וּפִיכֹל֙ שַׂר־צְבָא֔וֹ אֶל־אַבְרָהָ֖ם לֵאמֹ֑ר אֱלֹהִ֣ים עִמְּךָ֔ בְּכֹ֥ל אֲשֶׁר־אַתָּ֖ה עֹשֶֽׂה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

Elohim imkha b-khol asher atah oseh — the first patriarchal occurrence of the Elohim/YHWH im-X (Elohim/YHWH is with X) divine-presence formula, one of the most distributionally extensive recurring phrases in the Hebrew Bible. The formula names the deity's accompaniment-as-favor and serves across the corpus as the basis for inter-personal trust, treaty, and political alignment.

The verse is doubly editorially significant. First, it is the formula's first patriarchal occurrence — the inaugural deployment of what becomes the canonical HB divine-accompaniment phrase across Gen 26 (Yitzchaq), Gen 28-31 (Ya'aqov), Gen 39 (Yosef), Exod 3 (Moses), Deut 31 / Josh 1 (the Joshua-transition), and on through the Davidic narratives, the Solomonic temple-dedication, the prophetic-call inaugurations, and the post-exilic restoration material. Second, the formula appears on the lips of a non-Israelite king — Avimelekh of Gerar — recognizing the divine accompaniment of the patriarch from the outside. The external-recognition register is itself a developed HB pattern (the Yitzchaq doublet at Gen 26:28 uses the same formula on the same king's lips; cf. Pharaoh recognizing the ruach Elohim in Yosef at Gen 41:38; Bil'am recognizing YHWH's blessing of Israel at Num 22-24).

Within the chapter's structure, v 22 is the pivot from the Hagar-expulsion narrative (vv 1-21) to the Avimelekh-treaty narrative (vv 22-34). The pivot is editorially deliberate: the chapter has just demonstrated Elohim imkha through the Hagar-and-Yishma'el rescue (where Elohim appears five times across vv 12-20), and Avimelekh's recognition at v 22 is the external confirmation of the divine-accompaniment that the preceding pericope has just narratively established. H. D. Preuss Jahweglaube und Zukunftserwartung (BWANT 87) 1968 is the foundational monograph-treatment of the formula across the HB; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985, p. 343; Sarna JPS Genesis 1989, pp. 147-148.

23
Now therefore swear to me here by Elohim that you will not deal falsely with me, nor with my son, nor with my son's son: but according to the kindness that I have done to you, you shall do to me, and to the land in which you have sojourned.
Now therefore swear unto me here by God that thou wilt not deal falsely with me, nor with my son, nor with my son`s son. But according to the kindness that I have done unto thee, thou shalt do unto me, and to the land wherein thou hast sojourned.
וְעַתָּ֗ה הִשָּׁ֨בְעָה לִּ֤י בֵֽאלֹהִים֙ הֵ֔נָּה אִם־תִּשְׁקֹ֣ר לִ֔י וּלְנִינִ֖י וּלְנֶכְדִּ֑י כַּחֶ֜סֶד אֲשֶׁר־עָשִׂ֤יתִי עִמְּךָ֙ תַּעֲשֶׂ֣ה עִמָּדִ֔י וְעִם־הָאָ֖רֶץ אֲשֶׁר־גַּ֥רְתָּה בָּֽהּ׃
24
And Avraham said, I will swear.
And Abraham said, I will swear.
וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ אַבְרָהָ֔ם אָנֹכִ֖י אִשָּׁבֵֽעַ׃
25
And Avraham reproved Avimelekh on account of the well of water, which Avimelekh's servants had violently taken away.
And Abraham reproved Abimelech because of the well of water, which Abimelech`s servants had violently taken away.
וְהוֹכִ֥חַ אַבְרָהָ֖ם אֶת־אֲבִימֶ֑לֶךְ עַל־אֹדוֹת֙ בְּאֵ֣ר הַמַּ֔יִם אֲשֶׁ֥ר גָּזְל֖וּ עַבְדֵ֥י אֲבִימֶֽלֶךְ׃
26
And Avimelekh said, I know not who has done this thing; neither did you tell me, neither yet did I hear of it, except today.
And Abimelech said, I know not who hath done this thing. Neither didst thou tell me, neither yet heard I of it, but to-day.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֲבִימֶ֔לֶךְ לֹ֣א יָדַ֔עְתִּי מִ֥י עָשָׂ֖ה אֶת־הַדָּבָ֣ר הַזֶּ֑ה וְגַם־אַתָּ֞ה לֹא־הִגַּ֣דְתָּ לִּ֗י וְגַ֧ם אָנֹכִ֛י לֹ֥א שָׁמַ֖עְתִּי בִּלְתִּ֥י הַיּֽוֹם׃
27
And Avraham took sheep and oxen, and gave them to Avimelekh; and they two made a covenant.
And Abraham took sheep and oxen, and gave them unto Abimelech. And they two made a covenant.
וַיִּקַּ֤ח אַבְרָהָם֙ צֹ֣אן וּבָקָ֔ר וַיִּתֵּ֖ן לַאֲבִימֶ֑לֶךְ וַיִּכְרְת֥וּ שְׁנֵיהֶ֖ם בְּרִֽית׃
28
And Avraham set seven ewe-lambs of the flock by themselves.
And Abraham set seven ewe lambs of the flock by themselves.
וַיַּצֵּ֣ב אַבְרָהָ֗ם אֶת־שֶׁ֛בַע כִּבְשֹׂ֥ת הַצֹּ֖אן לְבַדְּהֶֽן׃
29
And Avimelekh said to Avraham, What do these seven ewe-lambs mean, which you have set by themselves?
And Abimelech said unto Abraham, What mean these seven ewe lambs which thou hast set by themselves?
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר אֲבִימֶ֖לֶךְ אֶל־אַבְרָהָ֑ם מָ֣ה הֵ֗נָּה שֶׁ֤בַע כְּבָשֹׂת֙ הָאֵ֔לֶּה אֲשֶׁ֥ר הִצַּ֖בְתָּ לְבַדָּֽנָה׃
30
And he said, These seven ewe-lambs you shall take from my hand, that it may be a witness to me, that I have dug this well.
And he said, These seven ewe lambs shalt thou take of my hand, that it may be a witness unto me, that I have digged this well.
וַיֹּ֕אמֶר כִּ֚י אֶת־שֶׁ֣בַע כְּבָשֹׂ֔ת תִּקַּ֖ח מִיָּדִ֑י בַּעֲבוּר֙ תִּֽהְיֶה־לִּ֣י לְעֵדָ֔ה כִּ֥י חָפַ֖רְתִּי אֶת־הַבְּאֵ֥ר הַזֹּֽאת׃
31
Therefore he called that place Be'er Sheva, because there they both swore an oath.
Wherefore he called that place Beer-sheba. Because there they sware both of them.
עַל־כֵּ֗ן קָרָ֛א לַמָּק֥וֹם הַה֖וּא בְּאֵ֣ר שָׁ֑בַע כִּ֛י שָׁ֥ם נִשְׁבְּע֖וּ שְׁנֵיהֶֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 31

Be'er Sheva — the chapter's in-text etiology of the place-name. The place-name is morphologically a construct chain be'er (well) + sheva, and the second element sheva sustains a deliberate double-etymology on the sh-b-ʿ root: sheva (שֶׁבַע — seven; the seven ewe-lambs of vv 28-30 set aside as the legal witness l-edah) and shava (שָׁבַע — to swear an oath; ki sham nishb'u sh'neihem — because there they both swore). The two senses are not alternatives but layered: the seven-ewe-lambs are the materials of the oath-swearing, and the oath-swearing ratifies the seven-ewe-lambs arrangement. The chapter compresses both the witness-object and the witness-act into a single phonological cluster.

The parallel etiology at Gen 26:33 activates only the sheva-as-oath sense (Yitzchaq's parallel-but-distinct Avimelekh covenant uses nishb'u / Shiv'ah without the seven-ewe-lambs object-arrangement), confirming the layered-vs-partial doublet structure of the two cycles. Sarna JPS Genesis 1989, p. 149; Wenham WBC Genesis 16-50 1994, pp. 92-95; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985, pp. 348-349.

The toponym becomes the canonical southern-boundary marker of the Israelite land in the Dan-to-Be'er-Sheva formula (Judg 20:1; 1 Sam 3:20; 2 Sam 3:10; etc.). The historical Be'er Sheva is securely identified with Tell es-Seba per the Aharoni 1973 / Herzog 1984/2016 excavation reports. The WoH translation preserves the transliteration Be'er Sheva, matching the WoH compound-place-name convention and preserving the double-etymology visibly; the Anglicized Beer-sheba (ASV) loses both the construct-chain structure and the sheva / shava play.

32
So they made a covenant at Be'er Sheva: and Avimelekh rose up, and Phicol the captain of his host, and they returned into the land of the Philistines.
So they made a covenant at Beer-sheba. And Abimelech rose up, and Phicol the captain of his host, and they returned into the land of the Philistines.
וַיִּכְרְת֥וּ בְרִ֖ית בִּבְאֵ֣ר שָׁ֑בַע וַיָּ֣קָם אֲבִימֶ֗לֶךְ וּפִיכֹל֙ שַׂר־צְבָא֔וֹ וַיָּשֻׁ֖בוּ אֶל־אֶ֥רֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּֽים׃
33
And Avraham planted a tamarisk in Be'er Sheva, and there called on the name of YHWH, the Everlasting El.
And [Abraham] planted a tamarisk tree in Beer-sheba, and called there on the name of Jehovah, the Everlasting God.
וַיִּטַּ֥ע אֶ֖שֶׁל בִּבְאֵ֣ר שָׁ֑בַע וַיִּ֨קְרָא־שָׁ֔ם בְּשֵׁ֥ם יְהוָ֖ה אֵ֥ל עוֹלָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 33

Va-yita eshel bi-Be'er Sheva va-yiqra-sham b-shem YHWH El Olam — the chapter's keystone cultic-site verse, naming the cult-site (Be'er Sheva), the cultic-marker (the eshel — tamarisk), the invocation-formula (qara b-shem YHWH), and the divine name (YHWH El Olam) in a single compact verse. This is the only formal Avraham-cultic-site-establishment in the Genesis cycle that combines all four elements.

YHWH El Olam. The verse contains the first occurrence of El Olam (the Everlasting El) in the Hebrew Bible and completes the four-fold patriarchal El-compound divine-naming pattern in the Avraham cycle: El Elyon at Gen 14:18-22 (Avram and Melchizedek); El Ro'i at Gen 16:13 (Hagar at the wilderness spring); El Shaddai at Gen 17:1 (the covenant-of-circumcision); and now El Olam at Gen 21:33. The four El-compounds across four distinct sanctuary-sites stage the patriarchal religious geography as a four-fold El-cult.

The religious-historical reconstruction reads the patriarchal El-compound pattern as preserving a West-Semitic El-religion locally diversified, with each sanctuary-site preserving a distinct El-compound divine name corresponding to that site's pre-Israelite high-god cult. The Yahwistic editor then harmonizes each El-compound with YHWH through the appositional construction — YHWH El Elyon at Gen 14:22 (Avram's oath to Melchizedek), now YHWH El Olam at Gen 21:33 (Avraham at his own cultic-site). The reading is grounded in Alt Der Gott der Väter 1929 (the foundational monograph) and developed in Cross Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic 1973, ch. 1 (the canonical modern statement), with reinforcement from the Ugaritic textual corpus, the Deir ʿAlla inscription, the Karatepe inscription's El qoneh ʾerets, and Philo of Byblos' Sanchuniathon-fragment. Smith The Origins of Biblical Monotheism 2001; Day Yahweh and the Gods and Goddesses of Canaan 2000; Köckert 1988.

The olam lexeme is read in the temporal-duration register (long duration, indefinite extent in time) rather than the abstract-eternity register (the latter is a later semantic development; HALOT ii.798-799; BDB 761-763; TDOT vol. 10 by H. D. Preuss; Jenni 1952-53). The WoH translation preserves ASV's Everlasting El, rejecting the NIV-style overreading to Eternal God and keeping the El-compound visible by substituting El for the generic God.

The eshel. Avraham plants (va-yita) an eshel — the Tamarix aphylla (the leafless tamarisk, native to the Negev). The lexeme is a botanical hapax-cluster: only three Hebrew Bible occurrences (Gen 21:33; 1 Sam 22:6 Saul under the tamarisk at Gibeah; 1 Sam 31:13 Saul's bones buried under the tamarisk at Yavesh-Gilead). The Avraham-tamarisk is the only patriarchal sacred-tree-site that is deliberately planted by the patriarch himself rather than pre-existing — in contrast with the elon Moreh at Shechem (Gen 12:6), the elonei Mamre (Gen 13:18, 18:1), and the wider sacred-tree-cultic-marker pattern. The plant + cult-site + invocation arrangement at v 33 founds the site as Avraham's own cultic-marker. See eshel-tamarisk-cultic-site for the sacred-tree-cultic-marker pattern across the wider HB lexeme-cluster (Pope Song of Songs 1985; Frevel 1995; Olyan 1988; Hadley 2000).

34
And Avraham sojourned in the land of the Philistines many days.
And Abraham sojourned in the land of the Philistines many days.
וַיָּ֧גָר אַבְרָהָ֛ם בְּאֶ֥רֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּ֖ים יָמִ֥ים רַבִּֽים׃ (פ)
Chapter 22

Genesis 22 — The binding of Yitzchaq and the oath at Moriah

1
And it came to pass after these things, that the Elohim tested Avraham, and said to him, Avraham. And he said, Here am I.
And it came to pass after these things, that God did prove Abraham, and said unto him, Abraham. And he said, Here am I.
וַיְהִ֗י אַחַר֙ הַדְּבָרִ֣ים הָאֵ֔לֶּה וְהָ֣אֱלֹהִ֔ים נִסָּ֖ה אֶת־אַבְרָהָ֑ם וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֵלָ֔יו אַבְרָהָ֖ם וַיֹּ֥אמֶר הִנֵּֽנִי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

Nisah — the piel of n-s-h, to test, try, put to the test — is the chapter's defining narrator-comment and one of the most theologically-discussed verses in the entire Hebrew Bible. The narrator preempts the reader's misreading of v 2 by naming the entire pericope a test before it begins; the dramatic-irony of the chapter is structurally generated by this opening (see nisah-divine-testing for the full lexeme-and-distribution survey).

Gen 22:1 is the first occurrence of the divine-testing piel in the Hebrew Bible and the only patriarchal-narrative instance; the motif is otherwise concentrated in the Mosaic and post-Mosaic corpus (Exod 15:25 the bitter-waters at Marah; Exod 16:4 the manna; Exod 20:20 Sinai post-theophany; Deut 8:2, 8:16, 13:4, 33:8; Judg 2:22, 3:1, 3:4; Ps 26:2; 2 Chr 32:31).

The theological-problem the verse generates — if the deity is omniscient, what does the test produce? — is one of the most-discussed cruxes of the Hebrew Bible reception-history. Four scholarly positions parallel: Maimonides (Guide III.24) reads the test as pedagogical-demonstration l-hodi'a (to make known) to humanity; Augustine and the patristic tradition read the test as disclosing the latent disposition in publicly-visible form; the modern philosophical-theology open theism position (Pinnock, Sanders, Boyd) reads the test as genuinely producing new divine knowledge; the modern critical default (Sarna, Westermann, Wenham, Hamilton, Brueggemann) reads the atah yadati of v 12 and the nisah of v 1 as narrative-anthropomorphism — the chapter is a story in the strict literary sense, and the story requires the test-and-discover narrative-grammar. The four positions are documented; none is preempted at the translation level. See nisah-divine-testing and ata-yadati-divine-learning-question for the full apparatus.

The article-bearing ha-Elohim (rather than bare Elohim or YHWH) is editorially significant: across the chapter the divine-name distribution is load-bearing — ha-Elohim at vv 1, 3, 9 for the test-commanding figure; bare Elohim at v 8 (Avraham's reply) and v 12 (yere Elohim certification); YHWH at vv 11, 14, 15, 16 for the staying-of-the-hand, the place-name, and the oath. The classical source-critical reading (Gunkel, von Rad, Westermann) treats this as an E-J redactional fingerprint with the J supplement at vv 11b-18 carrying the YHWH name. The WoH article-convention renders the form the Elohim per elohim-as-translation.

The first of the chapter's threefold hineni (vv 1, 7, 11) opens here — the prophetic-response formula in the call-narrative type-scene (Habel ZAW 1965; Brueggemann 1982). See hineni-prophetic-response-formula.

The wider philosophical reception (Kierkegaard Fear and Trembling 1843; Auerbach Mimesis 1946 ch. 1; Derrida The Gift of Death 1995) and the rabbinic-liturgical reception (Spiegel The Last Trial 1967; Levenson The Death and Resurrection of the Beloved Son 1993) are flagged as downstream-not-preempted.

2
And he said, Take now your son, your only son, whom you love, even Yitzchaq, and go forth to the land of Moriah; and offer him there for a burnt-offering upon one of the mountains which I will tell you of.
And he said, Take now thy son, thine only son, whom thou lovest, even Isaac, and get thee into the land of Moriah. And offer him there for a burnt-offering upon one of the mountains which I will tell thee of.
וַיֹּ֡אמֶר קַח־נָ֠א אֶת־בִּנְךָ֨ אֶת־יְחִֽידְךָ֤ אֲשֶׁר־אָהַ֙בְתָּ֙ אֶת־יִצְחָ֔ק וְלֶךְ־לְךָ֔ אֶל־אֶ֖רֶץ הַמֹּרִיָּ֑ה וְהַעֲלֵ֤הוּ שָׁם֙ לְעֹלָ֔ה עַ֚ל אַחַ֣ד הֶֽהָרִ֔ים אֲשֶׁ֖ר אֹמַ֥ר אֵלֶֽיךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The chapter's pivot-command. Four lexical-and-philological cruxes converge in a single verse, each of which carries reception-history beyond what the translation can preempt.

Qach-na et-binkha et-yechidkha asher ahavta et-Yitzchaq. The command's slow accumulation — take now your son, your only one, whom you love, [namely] Yitzchaq — is editorially deliberate. Rashi (citing Genesis Rabbah 39:9 and 55:7) reads the four-fold designation as a dramatic-prolongation: Avraham asks at each stage which son? — your son (I have two), your only one (each is the only one of his mother), whom you love (I love them both), until the final naming Yitzchaq leaves no escape. The midrashic reading captures the verse's deliberate-prolongation effect, which the modern critical literature (Sarna, Alter) reaches on literary-narrative grounds without the midrashic dramatization.

Yechidkha — your only one — generates a narrative-internal tension: Yitzchaq is not Avraham's only son. Yishma'el was born at Gen 16, expelled at Gen 21:8-21 but not dead. The modern critical readings — Yitzchaq as the only-one-of-the-promise-line per Gen 21:12 (Sarna 1989 p. 151; Wenham 1994 p. 105; Hamilton 1995 p. 105); yechid in the affective-emotional sense beloved-because-unique (Levenson 1993 pp. 28-34); the combined reading — are parallel; the verse does not require disambiguation. The downstream Christian (Vulgate unigenitum, the Johannine monogenes echo at John 3:16, Hebrews 11:17-19) and Islamic (Quran 37:101-113, with the early tafsir tradition divided on Yitzchaq vs. Yishma'el and the dominant Sunni Yishma'el-identification documented in Firestone 1990) receptions are flagged-not-preempted. See yechid-only-one-formula. The cross-corpus distribution (Judg 11:34 Jephthah's daughter; Ps 22:21, 35:17; Zech 12:10 the pierced-one with NT-reception at John 19:37) is also in the entry.

Lekh-lekha el eretz ha-Moriah. The doubled-imperative lekh-lekha (go forth) recurs here as the second and final use in the Hebrew Bible — the first being Gen 12:1, the call-of-Avram. The two uses form a deliberate inclusio across the Abraham-cycle (Sarna, Alter); see lekh-lekha for the full ethical-dative discussion.

Eretz ha-Moriah — the land of Moriah — is a Pentateuchal hapax (the noun recurs only once more in the entire Hebrew Bible, at 2 Chr 3:1, where the Chronicler explicitly identifies har ha-Moriah with the Jerusalem Temple Mount). The Chronicler's identification is the foundation of the Jewish tradition (Targum Pseudo-Jonathan; Rashi; Maimonides). Modern critical scholarship is divided: Sarna 1989 p. 151 accepts the 2 Chr 3:1 identification as preserving ancient tradition; Westermann 1985 p. 357 and Speiser 1964 p. 163 read the Chronicler as retrojecting. The textual variants are significant — LXX eis tēn gēn tēn hypsēlēn (into the high land) suggests a different Vorlage or interpretive gloss; Samaritan Pentateuch eretz ha-Moreh connects the site to the elon Moreh of Shechem and supports the Samaritan Mount-Gerizim identification. The MT ha-Moriah is preserved per WoH MT-fidelity policy; see eretz-ha-moriah-temple-mount-identification for the full text-critical and reception-historical apparatus.

V-ha'alehu sham l-olah. Olah — that-which-goes-up / whole-burnt-offering — is the cultic-technical term for the principal sacrifice-class of Lev 1, the sacrifice entirely consumed by fire on the altar with the smoke ascending to the deity. The chapter is the first sustained narrative use of olah in the Hebrew Bible (six occurrences across the chapter at vv 2, 3, 6, 7, 8, 13) and the first narrative use of olah with a human victim. The pre-Sinai pre-cultic-legislative status is editorially significant: the reader encounters olah here before the Sinai legislation that will codify the sacrificial system and explicitly prohibit human-sacrifice (Lev 18:21, 20:2-5; Deut 12:31, 18:10). On one mainstream reading (Levenson 1993; Sarna), the aqedah's pre-Sinai non-completion of the human-olah is the foundational textual repudiation of child-sacrifice. See olah-burnt-offering for the full cultic-technical-and-comparative apparatus including the ANE molk / mlk material (Day 1989; Heider 1985; Stager and Wolff 1984).

3
And Avraham rose early in the morning, and saddled his donkey, and took two of his young men with him, and Yitzchaq his son; and he split the wood for the burnt-offering, and rose up, and went to the place of which the Elohim had told him.
And Abraham rose early in the morning, and saddled his ass, and took two of his young men with him, and Isaac his son. And he clave the wood for the burnt-offering, and rose up, and went unto the place of which God had told him.
וַיַּשְׁכֵּ֨ם אַבְרָהָ֜ם בַּבֹּ֗קֶר וַֽיַּחֲבֹשׁ֙ אֶת־חֲמֹר֔וֹ וַיִּקַּ֞ח אֶת־שְׁנֵ֤י נְעָרָיו֙ אִתּ֔וֹ וְאֵ֖ת יִצְחָ֣ק בְּנ֑וֹ וַיְבַקַּע֙ עֲצֵ֣י עֹלָ֔ה וַיָּ֣קָם וַיֵּ֔לֶךְ אֶל־הַמָּק֖וֹם אֲשֶׁר־אָֽמַר־ל֥וֹ הָאֱלֹהִֽים׃
4
On the third day Avraham lifted up his eyes, and saw the place afar off.
On the third day Abraham lifted up his eyes, and saw the place afar off.
בַּיּ֣וֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁ֗י וַיִּשָּׂ֨א אַבְרָהָ֧ם אֶת־עֵינָ֛יו וַיַּ֥רְא אֶת־הַמָּק֖וֹם מֵרָחֹֽק׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

Ba-yom ha-shlishi — on the third day — is a Hebrew Bible stylized-formulaic marker for theophany, decisive-event, or transition. The modern critical literature (Sarna 1989 p. 152; Westermann 1985 p. 358; Wenham 1994 p. 107; Alter 2004 p. 110) reads it as a literary-narrative convention rather than as a literal chronometric datum.

The cross-corpus distribution makes the third-day a stylized topos: Exod 19:11, 15-16 (the Sinai theophany — the parallel-most-cited cross-reference for Gen 22); Hos 6:2 (on the third day he will raise us up — the keystone cross-corpus verse for the third-day-resurrection motif); Esth 5:1 (Esther's decisive intervention); Jonah 1:17 / MT 2:1 (the three-days-and-three-nights in the fish, cited at Matt 12:40); and Gen 31:22, 34:25, 40:20, 42:18 within the Genesis corpus itself.

The NT-resurrection-typology (1 Cor 15:4; Luke 24:7, 21, 46; Matt 12:40; Acts 10:40) reads the Gen 22 third-day prefiguratively; the patristic-typology (Origen, Augustine, Tertullian) treats Yitzchaq's bound-but-not-slain on the third day as the typological prefiguration of the crucified-but-raised on the third day (Daly 1978; Lerch 1950). The Jewish-liturgical reception is equally rich — the Akedah is read on Rosh Hashanah; the zechut avot (merit of the fathers) tradition treats the third-day-arrival as the foundational moment of merit-imputation (Spiegel 1967; Levenson 1993 chs 8-10). Both reception-traditions are flagged-not-preempted by ba-yom-ha-shlishi-third-day-formula.

The sa eynav va-yar (lifted up his eyes and saw) formula is the patriarchal seeing-and-perception formula (see sa-eynekha-lift-your-eyes). The chapter's first use of the formula; the second at v 13 (Avraham seeing the substitute ram) frames the chapter's structural seeing-arc.

5
And Avraham said to his young men, Stay here with the donkey, and I and the lad will go yonder; and we will worship, and come again to you.
And Abraham said unto his young men, Abide ye here with the ass, and I and the lad will go yonder; and we will worship, and come again to you.
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר אַבְרָהָ֜ם אֶל־נְעָרָ֗יו שְׁבוּ־לָכֶ֥ם פֹּה֙ עִֽם־הַחֲמ֔וֹר וַאֲנִ֣י וְהַנַּ֔עַר נֵלְכָ֖ה עַד־כֹּ֑ה וְנִֽשְׁתַּחֲוֶ֖ה וְנָשׁ֥וּבָה אֲלֵיכֶֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

V-nashuvah aleykhem — and we will come again to you — uses the plural verb (Avraham and Yitzchaq both subjects). The plural is grammatically explicit and editorially significant. Rashi (Bereshit Rabbah 56:2) reads it as Avraham's prophetic confidence that Yitzchaq will return — i.e., implicit faith in resurrection or substitution. Hebrews 11:19 makes the resurrection-typology explicit: logisamenos hoti kai ek nekrōn egeirein dynatos ho theos, hothen auton kai en parabolē ekomisato — reasoning that God was able to raise [him] even from the dead, from which he did receive him back as a parable.

Modern critical scholarship (Sarna 1989 p. 152; Wenham 1994 p. 108; Hamilton 1995 p. 110) parallel three readings: (a) deliberate dramatic-irony of the narrator; (b) Avraham's protective speech to the young men, concealing the test from them; (c) latent faith in a non-final outcome. The translation does not need to disambiguate — the plural is grammatically explicit. The Hebrews-typology is downstream-reception, flagged-not-preempted.

6
And Avraham took the wood of the burnt-offering, and laid it on Yitzchaq his son; and he took in his hand the fire and the knife; and they went both of them together.
And Abraham took the wood of the burnt-offering, and laid it upon Isaac his son. And he took in his hand the fire and the knife. And they went both of them together.
וַיִּקַּ֨ח אַבְרָהָ֜ם אֶת־עֲצֵ֣י הָעֹלָ֗ה וַיָּ֙שֶׂם֙ עַל־יִצְחָ֣ק בְּנ֔וֹ וַיִּקַּ֣ח בְּיָד֔וֹ אֶת־הָאֵ֖שׁ וְאֶת־הַֽמַּאֲכֶ֑לֶת וַיֵּלְכ֥וּ שְׁנֵיהֶ֖ם יַחְדָּֽו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

Ma'akhelet — the slaughter-knife / large-eating-knife — is one of the chapter's most marked-rare lexemes. The noun recurs only three times in the entire Hebrew Bible (here at Gen 22:6 + v 10; Judg 19:29 the Levite's dismembering-knife for the concubine; Prov 30:14 the metaphorical jaw-teeth as knives). The chapter's two occurrences (v 6 loading-of-implements; v 10 the narrative-climax) mark the chapter's vocabulary for the act of slaughter as specialized. Sarna 1989 p. 152: the rare term ma'akhelet, the slaughtering-knife, intensifies the dramatic horror. Alter 2004 p. 110: the rare slaughter-knife. Hamilton 1995 p. 110: the term often refers to a larger knife or even a sword. See ma-akhelet-sacrificial-knife.

The verse-closing va-yelkhu shneihem yachdav — and they went, the two of them, together — recurs at v 8b, framing the father-and-son walking-together as the chapter's most affectively-weighted refrain (Sarna 1989; Alter 2004 p. 110).

7
And Yitzchaq spoke to Avraham his father, and said, My father. And he said, Here am I, my son. And he said, Behold, the fire and the wood; but where is the lamb for a burnt-offering?
And Isaac spake unto Abraham his father, and said, My father. And he said, Here am I, my son. And he said, Behold, the fire and the wood. But where is the lamb for a burnt-offering?
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר יִצְחָ֜ק אֶל־אַבְרָהָ֤ם אָבִיו֙ וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אָבִ֔י וַיֹּ֖אמֶר הִנֶּ֣נִּֽי בְנִ֑י וַיֹּ֗אמֶר הִנֵּ֤ה הָאֵשׁ֙ וְהָ֣עֵצִ֔ים וְאַיֵּ֥ה הַשֶּׂ֖ה לְעֹלָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The chapter's middle hineni. Yitzchaq calls — Avi (my father) — and Avraham responds hineni v'ni (here am I, my son). The doubled-affective vocative (father–son; son–father) is the chapter's most affectively-weighted moment: Yitzchaq does not yet know what is happening, and Avraham's hineni to the son is grammatically the same response-formula he gave to the deity at v 1 and will give to the messenger at v 11.

The threefold-hineni structure (vv 1, 7, 11) is one of the chapter's most-cited compositional features. Sarna 1989 p. 150: the threefold hineni gives the chapter its structural-rhythmic backbone. Alter 2004 p. 109: the three hineni-responses, each in a different vocative direction, anchor the chapter's three-fold structure. See hineni-prophetic-response-formula for the cross-corpus prophetic-vocational distribution (Gen 31:11; 37:13; 46:2 — the Ya'aqov Ya'aqov doublet; Exod 3:4 — Moshe at the burning bush; 1 Sam 3 — Sh'mu'el's fourfold call; Isa 6:8 — Isaiah's here am I, send me).

Yitzchaq's question — v-ayeh ha-seh l-olah (but where is the lamb for a burnt-offering) — is the chapter's most-quoted line. The question is grammatically straightforward but narratively-devastating: the son names the missing element (the seh — the lamb / kid) that he himself, by the narrative's logic, is to become.

8
And Avraham said, Elohim will provide himself the lamb for a burnt-offering, my son: so they went both of them together.
And Abraham said, God will provide himself the lamb for a burnt-offering, my son. So they went both of them together.
וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ אַבְרָהָ֔ם אֱלֹהִ֞ים יִרְאֶה־לּ֥וֹ הַשֶּׂ֛ה לְעֹלָ֖ה בְּנִ֑י וַיֵּלְכ֥וּ שְׁנֵיהֶ֖ם יַחְדָּֽו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

Elohim yir'eh-lo ha-seh — Elohim will see for himself / provide for himself the lamb. The verb is the qal active 3ms imperfect of r-'-h (to see) with the reflexive lamed + 3ms suffix — will see for himself. The Hebrew preserves the seeing-equals-providing identity unambiguously; modern English does not (the etymological connection between provide and Latin pro-videre, to see in advance, is accidental — modern English readers do not hear see in provide).

The verse opens the chapter's wordplay-engine — the r-'-h root play that structures the chapter at vv 4, 8, 13, 14 (active yir'eh) and 14b (niphal passive yera'eh). The five-fold r-'-h axis is the chapter's deliberate compositional device (Sarna, Alter, Wenham, Hamilton — see yireh-yerah-hreh-wordplay).

Avraham's answer is grammatically ambiguous: it can be read as a faith-statement (Elohim will provide — Avraham trusts the deity to supply the substitute), as protective-deception of Yitzchaq (a cover for the test that Avraham has been forbidden to disclose), or as dramatic-irony (the narrator setting up the substitution-pivot at v 13 by foreshadowing the substitute-lamb in Avraham's own words). Sarna 1989 p. 152, Alter 2004 p. 110, and Wenham 1994 p. 108 parallel the three readings; the verse declines to disambiguate. The repeated refrain va-yelkhu shneihem yachdav (and they went, the two of them, together) closes the verse for the second time, framing the father-and-son's continued together-walking as the chapter's most affectively-weighted constant.

9
And they came to the place which the Elohim had told him of; and Avraham built the altar there, and laid the wood in order, and bound Yitzchaq his son, and laid him on the altar, upon the wood.
And they came to the place which God had told him of. And Abraham built the altar there, and laid the wood in order, and bound Isaac his son, and laid him on the altar, upon the wood.
וַיָּבֹ֗אוּ אֶֽל־הַמָּקוֹם֮ אֲשֶׁ֣ר אָֽמַר־ל֣וֹ הָאֱלֹהִים֒ וַיִּ֨בֶן שָׁ֤ם אַבְרָהָם֙ אֶת־הַמִּזְבֵּ֔חַ וַֽיַּעֲרֹ֖ךְ אֶת־הָעֵצִ֑ים וַֽיַּעֲקֹד֙ אֶת־יִצְחָ֣ק בְּנ֔וֹ וַיָּ֤שֶׂם אֹתוֹ֙ עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּ֔חַ מִמַּ֖עַל לָעֵצִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

Va-ya'aqod et-Yitzchaq — and he bound Yitzchaq. The verb is the qal of aqad (to bind, truss) — a Pentateuchal hapax in the verbal form. The morphologically-related noun aqudim (striped, ringed, banded) recurs in the Jacob-flock-breeding narrative of Gen 30-31, but the qal verbal sense to bind is unique to this verse in the entire Pentateuch (the wider verbal root is attested in post-biblical and Mishnaic Hebrew, where the verbal sense is standard).

The verb's uniqueness is editorially load-bearing: the entire pericope is named Aqedat Yitzchaq (the Binding of Yitzchaq) in the post-biblical Jewish tradition from this verb. Genesis Rabbah 56; b. Rosh Hashanah 16a, 26a; Mishnah Tamid 4:1 explicitly compares the daily tamid-offering's binding-procedure to Gen 22:9 — the front-right and rear-left legs bound together (forming the aqedah), the head-and-throat exposed for the shechita. The Mishnah cites Gen 22:9 as the precedent. Modern commentators (Sarna 1989 p. 153; Wenham 1994 p. 109; Hamilton 1995 p. 110) read the aqad verb as a technical-specialized sacrificial-binding term. See aqad-bind-aqedah-hapax for the full lexeme-and-reception apparatus including the wider Semitic-cognate distribution (Akkadian eqēdu, Arabic ʿaqada).

The chapter's narrative-vocabulary is densely cultic-technical at this verse: va-yiven sham Avraham et-ha-mizbeach (built the altar) — the first patriarchal-altar-construction at this site; va-ya'arokh et-ha-etzim (laid the wood in order) — the verb arakh is itself the cultic-technical wood-arranging term of Lev 1:7, 8, 12; 6:5 (English 6:12). The narrator's vocabulary at v 9 saturates the verse with sacrificial-cultic technical terms, intensifying the (averted) horror through specialized-cultic language.

10
And Avraham stretched forth his hand, and took the knife to slay his son.
And Abraham stretched forth his hand, and took the knife to slay his son.
וַיִּשְׁלַ֤ח אַבְרָהָם֙ אֶת־יָד֔וֹ וַיִּקַּ֖ח אֶת־הַֽמַּאֲכֶ֑לֶת לִשְׁחֹ֖ט אֶת־בְּנֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

The chapter's narrative-climax. The verse uses the verb shachat (to slaughter, slay) — the cultic-technical sacrificial-slaughter verb of Lev 1:5, 11; 3:2; 4:24; 14:13 etc. The vocabulary continues v 9's cultic-technical saturation: the act about to happen is named in the precise vocabulary of the Levitical sacrificial system.

The second of the chapter's two occurrences of ma'akhelet (the slaughter-knife — see ma-akhelet-sacrificial-knife). The cross-corpus pairing with Judg 19:29 (the Levite's dismembering of the concubine) is editorially load-bearing: both narratives use the rare ma'akhelet at moments of (potential or actual) human-victim slaughter (Sarna 1989; Trible Texts of Terror 1984 ch. 3).

Va-yishlach Avraham et-yado (and Avraham stretched forth his hand) is the action-verb that the messenger's intervention at v 12 will explicitly reverse — al-tishlach yadkha el-ha-na'ar (do not lay your hand upon the lad). The narrative is structured around the verb shalach yad: Avraham extends; the messenger forbids.

11
And the messenger of YHWH called to him out of heaven, and said, Avraham, Avraham. And he said, Here am I.
And the angel of Jehovah called unto him out of heaven, and said, Abraham, Abraham. And he said, Here I am.
וַיִּקְרָ֨א אֵלָ֜יו מַלְאַ֤ךְ יְהוָה֙ מִן־הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וַיֹּ֖אמֶר אַבְרָהָ֣ם ׀ אַבְרָהָ֑ם וַיֹּ֖אמֶר הִנֵּֽנִי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

The chapter's staying-of-the-hand. Two editorially-marked formulae converge at this verse: the doubled-name vocative Avraham Avraham, and the third hineni of the chapter's threefold structure.

Avraham Avraham. The doubled-name divine vocation-call recurs at exactly four moments in the Hebrew Bible — naming the four foundational vocational-calls of the canonical narrative: Gen 22:11 (Avraham, this verse); Gen 46:2 (Ya'aqov Ya'aqov, the night-vision before the descent to Egypt — the closest parallel-formulation, including the hineni response); Exod 3:4 (Moshe Moshe, the burning bush — the parallel-formulation in precise lexical parallel: va-yiqra elav + doubled name + hineni); 1 Sam 3:10 (Sh'mu'el Sh'mu'el, the call of the prophet). The four-fold pattern names the canonical figures of patriarchal foundation, Israel-name foundation, exodus foundation, and monarchy-transition foundation. The Masoretic paseq (the vertical-stroke separator visible in the MT at this verse — Avraham | Avraham) editorially marks the doubling. Genesis Rabbah 56:7 reads the doubled-vocative as l'shon chibah u-l'shon zeruz — an expression of affection and an expression of urgency. See doubled-name-calling-formula for the full cross-corpus apparatus and the NT-reception (Acts 9:4 / 22:7 / 26:14 — Saoul Saoul, the Damascus-road call of Paul; Luke 10:41 Martha Martha; Luke 22:31 Simon Simon; Luke 13:34 / Matt 23:37 Ierousalēm Ierousalēm).

Va-yomer hineni. The third hineni — the response to the messenger's call. The threefold structure (Elohim at v 1; Yitzchaq at v 7; the messenger at v 11) is the chapter's structural-rhythmic backbone (see hineni-prophetic-response-formula).

Mal'akh YHWH. The messenger of YHWH — first introduced in the Avraham cycle at Gen 16 — returns at this chapter's pivot. The modern critical literature presents three parallel positions on the messenger-identity question (Heidt 1949's distinct-envoy reading; Westermann 1985 / von Rad 1972's identity reading; Hamori 2008 / Sommer 2009 / MacDonald 2009's literary-theological-device reading); the WoH translation messenger of YHWH (preserving the Hebrew etymology over the conventional angel) is editorially neutral on the identity question. See malakh-yhwh-messenger. The figure recurs at v 15 (the second call); the chapter's deeper structural seam is the v 11 / v 15 messenger-bracketing of the substitution-and-oath sequence.

12
And he said, Do not lay your hand upon the lad, neither do anything to him; for now I know that you fear the Elohim, seeing you have not withheld your son, your only son, from me.
And he said, Lay not thy hand upon the lad, neither do thou anything unto him. For now I know that thou fearest God, seeing thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son, from me.
וַיֹּ֗אמֶר אַל־תִּשְׁלַ֤ח יָֽדְךָ֙ אֶל־הַנַּ֔עַר וְאַל־תַּ֥עַשׂ ל֖וֹ מְא֑וּמָּה כִּ֣י ׀ עַתָּ֣ה יָדַ֗עְתִּי כִּֽי־יְרֵ֤א אֱלֹהִים֙ אַ֔תָּה וְלֹ֥א חָשַׂ֛כְתָּ אֶת־בִּנְךָ֥ אֶת־יְחִידְךָ֖ מִמֶּֽנִּי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

The chapter's verdict and the theological-engine of the test. Three philological-and-theological cruxes converge.

Atah yadati — now I know. The phrase generates the classical theological-problem of divine testing: if the deity is omniscient, what does the test produce? Four scholarly positions parallel: (a) Maimonides Guide III.24 reads the test as pedagogical-demonstration l-hodi'a (to make known) to humanity, not for the deity's information; (b) the patristic / disclosure reading (Augustine, Aquinas ST I q.14, II-II q.97) distinguishes the deity's foreknowledge from the test's function of disclosing the latent disposition in publicly-visible form; (c) the open-theism position (Pinnock 1994; Sanders 1998; Boyd 2000) reads the atah yadati literally as a real epistemic event; (d) the modern critical default (Sarna 1989 p. 153; Westermann 1985 pp. 361-362; Wenham 1994 p. 110; Hamilton 1995 p. 113; Brueggemann 1982 pp. 187-194; von Rad 1972 pp. 244-245) reads the atah yadati as narrative-anthropomorphism — the chapter is a story in the strict literary sense, and the story requires the test-and-discover narrative-grammar. The four positions are documented; none is preempted at the translation level. See ata-yadati-divine-learning-question for the full apparatus, including the Heschel-pathos framework and the Kierkegaardian-existential and Levinasian-Derridean continental-philosophical receptions (flagged-downstream).

Yere Elohim atah — you fear the Elohim. The chapter's certification of Avraham as a yere Elohim — fearer of the Elohim — is the structural inversion of Gen 20:11, where Avraham himself had pre-judged Gerar as lacking the fear-of-the-Elohim. The patriarch who at Gen 20:11 had wrongly assumed the foreign land lacked the moral-restraint motif is here at Gen 22:12 himself certified as one who fears the Elohim, the certification coming from the deity at the climax of the aqedah. The motif's cross-corpus distribution (Exod 1:17, 1:21 the Hebrew midwives; Exod 18:21 Yitro's judges; Exod 20:20 post-Sinai; Job 1:1, 1:8, 2:3 Job's characterization; Prov 1:7, 9:10 the wisdom-tradition motto; Eccl 12:13 Qohelet's summa) is documented at yirat-elohim-moral-restraint-motif. Becker 1965 is the foundational monographic treatment.

V-lo chasakhta et-binkha et-yechidkha mimeni — the verb chasakh (to withhold) recurs at v 16 in the same clause, framing the chapter's verdict in repeated-key vocabulary. Yechidkha (your only one) is the chapter's recurring designation of Yitzchaq (vv 2, 12, 16); see yechid-only-one-formula for the narrative-internal Yishma'el-tension and the three modern critical readings (covenant-line, affective-emotional, combined).

13
And Avraham lifted up his eyes, and looked, and behold, behind him a ram caught in the thicket by his horns: and Avraham went and took the ram, and offered him up for a burnt-offering in the stead of his son.
And Abraham lifted up his eyes, and looked, and behold, behind [him] a ram caught in the thicket by his horns. And Abraham went and took the ram, and offered him up for a burnt-offering in the stead of his son.
וַיִּשָּׂ֨א אַבְרָהָ֜ם אֶת־עֵינָ֗יו וַיַּרְא֙ וְהִנֵּה־אַ֔יִל אַחַ֕ר נֶאֱחַ֥ז בַּסְּבַ֖ךְ בְּקַרְנָ֑יו וַיֵּ֤לֶךְ אַבְרָהָם֙ וַיִּקַּ֣ח אֶת־הָאַ֔יִל וַיַּעֲלֵ֥הוּ לְעֹלָ֖ה תַּ֥חַת בְּנֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

The chapter's substitution-pivot. The (averted) human-sacrifice is replaced by an animal-substitution — an ayil (ram), the standard olah-class sacrificial animal (Lev 1:10; 5:15, 18; 6:6; 9:2-4; 16:3-5 — the Yom Kippur ritual with two rams; 23:18; Ezek 43-46). The same sacrificial-class is preserved across the substitution-pivot: the substitute is itself an olah-class animal, marking the substitution as a class-preserving exchange.

The substitutionary-sacrifice theology. The substitution is the foundation of the rabbinic zecher aqedat Yitzchaq (memorial of the binding) tradition and the wider zechut avot (merit of the fathers) tradition (Genesis Rabbah 56:9; b. Rosh Hashanah 16a). The shofar (ram's-horn) of the Rosh Hashanah liturgy is connected to the qarnei ha-ayil (the ram's horns) of this verse — the shofar-blast invokes the aqedah-merit (Spiegel 1967; Levenson 1993 chs 7-10; Vermes 1973). The Christian vicarious-atonement reception reads the ram-substitution as the typological foundation of the doctrine of vicarious atonement (Hebrews 11:17-19; Origen Homilies on Genesis VIII; Augustine; the Reformation atonement-theology — Luther, Calvin; Daly 1978; Lerch 1950). The Islamic reception (Sura 37:107 dhibhin azim — a mighty sacrifice; the early tafsir tradition treats the ram as a special-divine-provision). All three reception-traditions are flagged-not-preempted. See ayil-ram-substitution-achar-echad-variant.

The textual variant achar / echad. The MT reads achar (אַחַר) — behind / after — supported by the Samaritan Pentateuch and Onkelos. The LXX (kai idou krios heis), Peshitta (chad), and some Vulgate manuscripts read echad (אֶחָד) — one. The variant rests on the visually-similar resh / dalet (ר/ד) confusion. Modern critical positions divide: Sarna, Wenham, Hamilton preserve MT achar as lectio difficilior; Speiser 1964 p. 163 and Westermann 1985 pp. 360-361 (cautiously) read LXX echad as original; Skinner and Driver treat the variant as unresolvable. The WoH translation preserves the MT achar (behind him) per established textual-conservative practice.

The second occurrence of the sa eynav va-yar (lifted up his eyes and saw) formula in the chapter (the first at v 4), framing Avraham's two seeings — the place from afar (v 4) and the substitute ram (v 13) — as the chapter's structural seeing-arc. See sa-eynekha-lift-your-eyes.

14
And Avraham called the name of that place YHWH-yir'eh: as it is said to this day, In the mount of YHWH it shall be provided.
And Abraham called the name of that place Jehovah-jireh. As it is said to this day, In the mount of Jehovah it shall be provided.
וַיִּקְרָ֧א אַבְרָהָ֛ם שֵֽׁם־הַמָּק֥וֹם הַה֖וּא יְהוָ֣ה ׀ יִרְאֶ֑ה אֲשֶׁר֙ יֵאָמֵ֣ר הַיּ֔וֹם בְּהַ֥ר יְהוָ֖ה יֵרָאֶֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

The chapter's etiological-closing-formula. The place-name YHWH-yir'eh (YHWH-will-see / YHWH-will-provide) preserves the chapter's r-'-h wordplay-axis in proper-name form. The verbal element yir'eh (qal active 3ms imperfect of r-'-h) retains the seeing-and-providing identity from Avraham's v 8 statement (Elohim yir'eh-lo ha-seh); the verse's first half is the place-name proper.

The closing-formula b-har YHWH yera'eh — on the mountain of YHWH it shall be seen / he shall appear / it shall be provided — introduces a deliberate verbal shift. Yera'eh is the niphal 3ms imperfect of r-'-h — the same form used in the patriarchal-theophany formula va-yera YHWH (see va-yera-yhwh-theophany). The shift from active yir'eh (v 14a) to passive yera'eh (v 14b) enacts the chapter's deepest theological move: the deity who sees-and-provides (active) is the same deity who will-appear / will-be-seen (passive) at the place. The seeing-and-being-seen identity is the engine of the chapter's wordplay-axis. See yireh-yerah-hreh-wordplay for the full active/passive analysis across the chapter's five-fold r-'-h distribution (vv 4, 8, 13, 14a, 14b).

The Chronicler's identification at 2 Chr 3:1 (va-yachel Shlomoh livnot et-beit YHWH bi-Yrushalayim b-har ha-Moriah asher nir'ah l-David aviv) explicitly reuses the same r-'-h root in the niphal (nir'ah — appeared / was-seen), marking the cross-reference between this verse's b-har YHWH yera'eh and the David-and-Temple-Mount appearance-tradition (2 Sam 24:16-25; 1 Chr 21:15-30). The temple-mount identification is foundational to the Jewish tradition (Targum Pseudo-Jonathan to Gen 22:14 explicitly identifies har ha-Moriah with the Temple-Mount). The Christian and Islamic reception-engagements are documented at eretz-ha-moriah-temple-mount-identification.

The etiological-formula asher ye'amer ha-yom (as it is said to this day) is a Hebrew Bible etiological marker (cf. 1 Sam 5:5; 2 Sam 18:18) indicating that the place-name was preserved as a continuing-tradition in the narrator's contemporary world. Sarna 1989 p. 154; Westermann 1985 pp. 363-364; Hamilton 1995 pp. 113-114.

The WoH translation preserves the transliterated place-name YHWH-yir'eh and reads it shall be provided at the closing-formula (ASV-baseline). The wordplay-axis is carried in the apparatus; the translation cannot preserve the see-equals-provide identity that Hebrew r-'-h carries.

15
And the messenger of YHWH called to Avraham a second time out of heaven,
And the angel of Jehovah called unto Abraham a second time out of heaven,
וַיִּקְרָ֛א מַלְאַ֥ךְ יְהוָ֖ה אֶל־אַבְרָהָ֑ם שֵׁנִ֖ית מִן־הַשָּׁמָֽיִם׃
16
and said, By myself I have sworn, says YHWH, because you have done this thing, and have not withheld your son, your only son,
and said, By myself have I sworn, saith Jehovah, because thou hast done this thing, and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son,
וַיֹּ֕אמֶר בִּ֥י נִשְׁבַּ֖עְתִּי נְאֻם־יְהוָ֑ה כִּ֗י יַ֚עַן אֲשֶׁ֤ר עָשִׂ֙יתָ֙ אֶת־הַדָּבָ֣ר הַזֶּ֔ה וְלֹ֥א חָשַׂ֖כְתָּ אֶת־בִּנְךָ֥ אֶת־יְחִידֶֽךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

The chapter's climactic divine-oath. Two editorially-marked formulae converge in a single clause: the divine-self-oath bi nishbati (by myself I have sworn), and the prophetic-utterance formula n'um YHWH (says YHWH / utterance of YHWH).

Bi nishbati — the divine-self-oath. The deity, having no superior by which to swear, swears by himself. The formula is rare and structurally-load-bearing across the canon. Gen 22:16 is the first occurrence of the formula in canonical narrative; the formula then recurs in the prophetic corpus: Isa 45:23 (every knee shall bow — cited at Rom 14:11 and Phil 2:10-11 as the foundational NT-text of the universal-confession); Jer 22:5, 49:13, 51:14; Amos 6:8 (with parallel b-nafsho — by his life); Isa 62:8. Hebrews 6:13-14 explicitly cites Gen 22:16-17 as the locus of the divine self-oath: epei kat' oudenos eichen meizonos omosai, ōmosen kath' heautou — since he had no one greater by whom to swear, he swore by himself. The Hebrews-reception is the foundational NT-treatment of the divine-self-oath as the theological-engine of the divine-promise-bond (Lane 1991; Attridge 1989; Ellingworth 1993). The reception is flagged-not-preempted; see bi-nishbati-divine-self-oath.

N'um YHWH — the prophetic-utterance formula. N'um YHWH (utterance of YHWH / declares YHWH) is the standard prophetic-utterance formula, recurring approximately 365 times across the Hebrew Bible, overwhelmingly in the prophetic corpus (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, the Twelve). Gen 22:16 is the chronological-canonical first occurrence in the Hebrew Bible — the formula's narrative-debut. The next occurrences (Num 14:28; 1 Sam 2:30; 2 Sam 23:1 — David's last words; 2 Kgs 9:26, 19:33) are concentrated in the early prophetic-narrative, before the formula's proliferation in the major and minor prophets. The lexeme n'um is morphologically a passive-participle of n-'-m (to utter), nominalized into a construct-noun; the lexeme is otherwise almost exclusively attested in the n'um YHWH / n'um Adonai YHWH formulae (HALOT ii.658-659; BDB p. 610). The first-canonical-occurrence at Gen 22:16 is therefore the foundational instance for the entire prophetic-utterance formula's HB distribution.

The bi nishbati n'um YHWH combination marks the chapter's blessing (vv 17-18) as a maximally-binding divine-pronouncement: the divine-self-oath (the strongest form of divine-commitment) combined with the prophetic-utterance-formula (the strongest form of divine-speech-act). The chapter's promise to Avraham is therefore the chapter's most-binding divine-statement — and its first-canonical-occurrence status marks the aqedah as the locus of the foundational divine-self-oath of the Hebrew Bible. Cross Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic 1973 ch. 11 on the divine-self-oath as covenantal-binding form; Hillers 1964 and Weinfeld 1972 on the wider ANE-and-Deuteronomic oath-and-treaty material.

The verbal echo of v 12 — v-lo chasakhta et-binkha et-yechidkha — recurs here at v 16 in slightly-varied form (v-lo chasakhta et-binkha et-yechidekha), framing the verdict-and-oath as a single semantic-unit with the certification of v 12. See yechid-only-one-formula.

17
that in blessing I will bless you, and in multiplying I will multiply your seed as the stars of the heavens, and as the sand which is upon the seashore; and your seed shall possess the gate of his enemies;
that in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed as the stars of the heavens, and as the sand which is upon the seashore. And thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies.
כִּֽי־בָרֵ֣ךְ אֲבָרֶכְךָ֗ וְהַרְבָּ֨ה אַרְבֶּ֤ה אֶֽת־זַרְעֲךָ֙ כְּכוֹכְבֵ֣י הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וְכַח֕וֹל אֲשֶׁ֖ר עַל־שְׂפַ֣ת הַיָּ֑ם וְיִרַ֣שׁ זַרְעֲךָ֔ אֵ֖ת שַׁ֥עַר אֹיְבָֽיו׃
18
and in your seed shall all the nations of the earth bless themselves; because you have obeyed my voice.
And in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed. Because thou hast obeyed my voice.
וְהִתְבָּרֲכ֣וּ בְזַרְעֲךָ֔ כֹּ֖ל גּוֹיֵ֣י הָאָ֑רֶץ עֵ֕קֶב אֲשֶׁ֥ר שָׁמַ֖עְתָּ בְּקֹלִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

V-hitbarakhu b-zarakha kol goyei ha-aretz — the universal-blessing formula in hithpael pointing (shall bless themselves). The MT-fidelity policy ratified at Gen 12 (glossary entry nivrakhu-bekha-niphal-hithpael) preserves the verse-by-verse niphal/hithpael distinction across the five universal-blessing-promise occurrences: niphal-pointed (passive shall be blessed) at Gen 12:3, 18:18, 28:14; hithpael-pointed (middle/reflexive shall bless themselves) at Gen 22:18 and 26:4.

Three positions parallel: (a) Traditional Christian / Pauline reading — the LXX renders all five verses with the passive eneulogēthēsontai; Paul cites the verse as straightforwardly passive in Galatians 3:8, reading the passive as the textual foundation of the gospel-to-the-gentiles; ASV/RSV/NRSV follow Paul. (b) Modern critical consensus (Speiser, Sarna, Westermann, NJPS) — the original idiom was middle/reflexive across all five verses (by you all the families shall bless themselves — i.e., invoke Avraham's name as a paradigm of blessing, cf. Gen 48:20). The niphal-pointings at 12:3 / 18:18 / 28:14 are Masoretic harmonization toward the theologically-richer passive. (c) MT-preserving reading (Wenham, Hamilton) — the distinction is meaningful and should be preserved verse-by-verse. The WoH translation follows position (c) — verse-by-verse, matching MT pointing at each occurrence.

The Pauline reception of Gal 3:8 (proeuangelisato tō Abraam hoti eneulogēthēsontai en soi panta ta ethnē — the scripture preached the gospel beforehand to Abraham, saying, In you shall all the nations be blessed) is flagged as downstream Greek-reception and is not preempted by the translation choice. See nivrakhu-bekha-niphal-hithpael for the full apparatus.

19
So Avraham returned to his young men, and they rose up and went together to Be'er Sheva; and Avraham dwelt at Be'er Sheva.
So Abraham returned unto his young men, and they rose up and went together to Beer-sheba. And Abraham dwelt at Beer-sheba.
וַיָּ֤שָׁב אַבְרָהָם֙ אֶל־נְעָרָ֔יו וַיָּקֻ֛מוּ וַיֵּלְכ֥וּ יַחְדָּ֖ו אֶל־בְּאֵ֣ר שָׁ֑בַע וַיֵּ֥שֶׁב אַבְרָהָ֖ם בִּבְאֵ֥ר שָֽׁבַע׃ (פ)
20
And it came to pass after these things, that it was told Avraham, saying, Behold, Milkah, she also has borne children to Nachor your brother:
And it came to pass after these things, that it was told Abraham, saying, Behold, Milcah, she also hath borne children unto they brother Nahor.
וַיְהִ֗י אַחֲרֵי֙ הַדְּבָרִ֣ים הָאֵ֔לֶּה וַיֻּגַּ֥ד לְאַבְרָהָ֖ם לֵאמֹ֑ר הִ֠נֵּה יָלְדָ֨ה מִלְכָּ֥ה גַם־הִ֛וא בָּנִ֖ים לְנָח֥וֹר אָחִֽיךָ׃
21
Uts his firstborn, and Buz his brother, and Kemu'el the father of Aram,
Uz his first-born, and Buz his brother, and Kemuel the father of Aram.
אֶת־ע֥וּץ בְּכֹר֖וֹ וְאֶת־בּ֣וּז אָחִ֑יו וְאֶת־קְמוּאֵ֖ל אֲבִ֥י אֲרָֽם׃
22
and Kesed, and Chazo, and Pildash, and Yidlaf, and Betu'el.
And Chesed, and Hazo, and Pildash, and Jidlaph, and Bethuel.
וְאֶת־כֶּ֣שֶׂד וְאֶת־חֲז֔וֹ וְאֶת־פִּלְדָּ֖שׁ וְאֶת־יִדְלָ֑ף וְאֵ֖ת בְּתוּאֵֽל׃
23
And Betu'el begat Rivkah: these eight Milkah bore to Nachor, Avraham's brother.
And Bethuel begat Rebekah. These eight did Milcah bear to Nahor, Abraham`s brother.
וּבְתוּאֵ֖ל יָלַ֣ד אֶת־רִבְקָ֑ה שְׁמֹנָ֥ה אֵ֙לֶּה֙ יָלְדָ֣ה מִלְכָּ֔ה לְנָח֖וֹר אֲחִ֥י אַבְרָהָֽם׃
24
And his concubine, whose name was Re'umah, she also bore Tevach, and Gacham, and Tachash, and Ma'akhah.
And his concubine, whose name was Reumah, she also bare Tebah, and Gaham, and Tahash, and Maacah.
וּפִֽילַגְשׁ֖וֹ וּשְׁמָ֣הּ רְאוּמָ֑ה וַתֵּ֤לֶד גַּם־הִוא֙ אֶת־טֶ֣בַח וְאֶת־גַּ֔חַם וְאֶת־תַּ֖חַשׁ וְאֶֽת־מַעֲכָֽה׃ (ס)
Chapter 23

Genesis 23 — The death of Sarah and the purchase of the Makhpelah cave

1
And the life of Sarah was a hundred years and twenty years and seven years; these were the years of the life of Sarah.
And the life of Sarah was a hundred and seven and twenty years. These were the years of the life of Sarah.
וַיִּהְיוּ֙ חַיֵּ֣י שָׂרָ֔ה מֵאָ֥ה שָׁנָ֛ה וְעֶשְׂרִ֥ים שָׁנָ֖ה וְשֶׁ֣בַע שָׁנִ֑ים שְׁנֵ֖י חַיֵּ֥י שָׂרָֽה׃
2
And Sarah died in Qiryat-Arba — the same is Hevron — in the land of Kena'an. And Avraham came to mourn for Sarah and to weep for her.
And Sarah died in Kiriath-arba (the same is Hebron), in the land of Canaan. And Abraham came to mourn for Sarah, and to weep for her.
וַתָּ֣מָת שָׂרָ֗ה בְּקִרְיַ֥ת אַרְבַּ֛ע הִ֥וא חֶבְר֖וֹן בְּאֶ֣רֶץ כְּנָ֑עַן וַיָּבֹא֙ אַבְרָהָ֔ם לִסְפֹּ֥ד לְשָׂרָ֖ה וְלִבְכֹּתָֽהּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The mourning-and-weeping doublet li-spod l-Sarah v-li-vkotah. The verb-pair distinguishes sapad (formal lament, public eulogy — the misped of the funeral-ritual; cf. Gen 50:10 Egyptian-period mourning for Ya'aqov; 2 Sam 1:12 David's lament for Sha'ul and Yehonatan; 2 Sam 11:26 Bat-Sheva's misped for Uriah; Jer 16:5-6 the prophetic-prohibition of formal lament; Ezek 24:16-17 Ezekiel forbidden to mourn his wife) from bakhah (private weeping, affective grief). The ASV-baseline to mourn for Sarah and to weep for her preserves the two-verb distinction; the verbs are not interchangeable, and the pairing is a stylized HB mourning-formula rather than a redundancy. No glossary entry is required for the doublet itself; the formula is recognized across the HB.

The va-yavo Avraham and the rabbinic whence? question. The verb va-yavo (and Avraham came) implies that Avraham was elsewhere when Sarah died, and the question of whence? has generated a long exegetical tradition. Rashi at v 2, drawing on Genesis Rabbah 58:5, reads va-yavo against the Be'er-Sheva of Gen 22:19 (the last patriarchal-residence-notice before this chapter) — Avraham came from Be'er-Sheva to Hebron upon Sarah's death. The modern critical readings divide: Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 156 reads the va-yavo as the patriarchal arrival-at-the-mourning-site (a standard mourning-formula); Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 p. 372 reads the verb as narrative-scaffolding without locative implication. The question is reception-level; the translation preserves the literal and Avraham came.

The Qiryat-Arba / Hevron toponym. The double-naming (b-Qiryat Arba hi Chevron) is the chapter's first editorial-gloss identifying the older toponym Qiryat Arba (city of Arba — etiologically connected at Josh 14:15 to the giant Arba, ha-adam ha-gadol ba-Anaqim) with the canonical Hevron. The double-naming recurs at v 19 (the closing) and at Gen 35:27 (Ya'aqov's return to Mamre Qiryat ha-Arba hi Chevron). The toponym-doubling is editorially-deliberate.

3
And Avraham rose up from before his dead, and spoke to the sons of Het, saying,
And Abraham rose up from before his dead, and spake unto the children of Heth, saying,
וַיָּ֙קָם֙ אַבְרָהָ֔ם מֵעַ֖ל פְּנֵ֣י מֵת֑וֹ וַיְדַבֵּ֥ר אֶל־בְּנֵי־חֵ֖ת לֵאמֹֽר׃
4
I am a stranger and a sojourner with you; give me a possession of a burying-place with you, that I may bury my dead out of my sight.
I am a stranger and a sojourner with you. Give me a possession of a burying-place with you, that I may bury my dead out of my sight.
גֵּר־וְתוֹשָׁ֥ב אָנֹכִ֖י עִמָּכֶ֑ם תְּנ֨וּ לִ֤י אֲחֻזַּת־קֶ֙בֶר֙ עִמָּכֶ֔ם וְאֶקְבְּרָ֥ה מֵתִ֖י מִלְּפָנָֽי׃
5
And the sons of Het answered Avraham, saying to him,
And the children of Heth answered Abraham, saying unto him,
וַיַּעֲנ֧וּ בְנֵי־חֵ֛ת אֶת־אַבְרָהָ֖ם לֵאמֹ֥ר לֽוֹ׃
6
Hear us, my lord. You are a prince of Elohim among us; in the choicest of our sepulchres bury your dead; none of us shall withhold from you his sepulchre, but that you may bury your dead.
Hear us, my lord. Thou art a prince of God among us. In the choice of our sepulchres bury thy dead. None of us shall withhold from thee his sepulchre, but that thou mayest bury thy dead.
שְׁמָעֵ֣נוּ ׀ אֲדֹנִ֗י נְשִׂ֨יא אֱלֹהִ֤ים אַתָּה֙ בְּתוֹכֵ֔נוּ בְּמִבְחַ֣ר קְבָרֵ֔ינוּ קְבֹ֖ר אֶת־מֵתֶ֑ךָ אִ֣ישׁ מִמֶּ֔נּוּ אֶת־קִבְר֛וֹ לֹֽא־יִכְלֶ֥ה מִמְּךָ֖ מִקְּבֹ֥ר מֵתֶֽךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

N'si Elohim atah b-tokhenu — the Hittites' formal honorific to Avraham. The construct phrase n'si Elohim admits four principal readings in the modern critical literature: (a) literal-theological — a prince of Elohim, one chosen or favored by the deity (Targum Onqelos rav min qodam YHWH, Vulgate princeps Dei, Peshitta ris min Aloho); (b) superlative-Elohim — a mighty / exalted prince, with Elohim as a Hebrew superlative-marker (the well-attested divine-name-superlative idiom — cf. harerei El Ps 36:7, arzei El Ps 80:11; D. W. Thomas VT 3 (1953) is the classical philological treatment); (c) polytheist-context — a Hittite-spoken designation in the speakers' own religious idiom; (d) diplomatic-honorific — a fixed-formula courtesy parallel to ANE diplomatic your excellency.

The modern critical default (Sarna, Westermann, NJPS, NRSV, ASV/KJV) is the superlative-reading (b): a mighty prince. The WoH translation preserves the literal a prince of Elohim (reading a), consistent with established WoH policy on Elohim-bearing phrases: where the MT preserves the lexeme Elohim, the WoH translation preserves it rather than collapsing to an English intensifier. The reading supports the patriarchal-narrative pattern of foreign-recognition of the patriarch's divine-standing (parallel to Avimelekh's Elohim imkha b-khol asher atah oseh at Gen 21:22).

The construct n'si Elohim is HB hapax — Gen 23:6 is its unique occurrence — which reinforces the editorial-distinctiveness of the honorific. See the glossary entry for the full cross-corpus distribution of nasi and the parallel divine-name-superlative constructions.

7
And Avraham rose up, and bowed himself to the people of the land, to the sons of Het.
And Abraham rose up, and bowed himself to the people of the land, even to the children of Heth.
וַיָּ֧קָם אַבְרָהָ֛ם וַיִּשְׁתַּ֥חוּ לְעַם־הָאָ֖רֶץ לִבְנֵי־חֵֽת׃
8
And he spoke with them, saying, If it be your desire that I should bury my dead out of my sight, hear me, and entreat for me to Ephron the son of Tsohar,
And he communed with them, saying, If it be your mind that I should bury my dead out of my sight, hear me, and entreat for me to Ephron the son of Zohar,
וַיְדַבֵּ֥ר אִתָּ֖ם לֵאמֹ֑ר אִם־יֵ֣שׁ אֶֽת־נַפְשְׁכֶ֗ם לִקְבֹּ֤ר אֶת־מֵתִי֙ מִלְּפָנַ֔י שְׁמָע֕וּנִי וּפִגְעוּ־לִ֖י בְּעֶפְר֥וֹן בֶּן־צֹֽחַר׃
9
that he may give me the cave of Makhpelah, which he has, which is in the end of his field; for the full price let him give it to me in the midst of you for a possession of a burying-place.
that he may give me the cave of Machpelah, which he hath, which is in the end of his field. For the full price let him give it to me in the midst of you for a possession of a burying-place.
וְיִתֶּן־לִ֗י אֶת־מְעָרַ֤ת הַמַּכְפֵּלָה֙ אֲשֶׁר־ל֔וֹ אֲשֶׁ֖ר בִּקְצֵ֣ה שָׂדֵ֑הוּ בְּכֶ֨סֶף מָלֵ֜א יִתְּנֶ֥נָּה לִ֛י בְּתוֹכְכֶ֖ם לַאֲחֻזַּת־קָֽבֶר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The cave-name me'arat ha-Makhpelah. The toponym carries the definite article ha- attached to what appears to be a proper-name — a feature shared with a small set of HB toponyms (ha-Negev, ha-Bashan, ha-Galil) — suggesting either an originally-descriptive term crystallized into a place-name, or a place-name retaining the article. The etymological dispute: (a) the LXX-rabbinic doubling etymology, reading Makhpelah from k-p-l (to double, fold) and rendered LXX to spēlaion to diploun (the double cave); the rabbinic tradition develops the reading into the double-chambered cave (b. Eruvin 53a) or the cave large enough for the doubled-couples of patriarchal burials (Genesis Rabbah 58:8 — Avraham + Sarah, Yitzchaq + Rivkah, Ya'aqov + Le'ah, with Adam + Chavah as the foundational-couple in some traditions); (b) the modern critical default (Sarna, Westermann, Wenham, Hamilton) treats ha-Makhpelah as a proper-name with lost etymology. The Hebron-tomb identification (the present-day Me'arat ha-Makhpelah / al-Haram al-Ibrahimi) is supported by Josephus Antiquities I.237 and War IV.532 (the Herodian-period structure already standing in the 1st century CE), by the still-extant Herodian-period enclosure-walls, and by uninterrupted Jewish, Christian, and Muslim tradition. The cross-corpus patriarchal-burial chain — Avraham (Gen 25:9-10), Ya'aqov's burial-instructions naming the previously-interred (Gen 49:29-32), Ya'aqov's own burial (Gen 50:13) — grounds the multi-generational tradition.

B-kesef male — the full-price legal-symbolic formula. Avraham's stipulation that he will purchase the cave for the full price is not mere politeness but legal-symbolic: by paying the full price (rather than accepting a gift, or paying a token), Avraham establishes an unambiguous and irrevocable transfer of title — one that cannot later be reclaimed by Efron's heirs or successors. The formula is the technical-legal seal of irrevocable transfer, with ANE-comparative parallels at Old Babylonian kaspu gamru (full silver) and Neo-Babylonian/Neo-Assyrian conveyance-formulae (Westbrook 1971 JSS 16, Tucker 1966 JBL 85, Petschow 1965 MVAG).

The cross-corpus parallel at 1 Chr 21:24-25. David, refusing Ornan's offer of the threshing-floor as a gift, insists on paying b-kesef male — verbally echoing Gen 23:9 — for the same legal-symbolic reason: cultically-significant land (in David's case, the future Temple-Mount) requires unambiguous title-transfer. Sarna 1989 p. 158 draws the Gen 23 / 1 Chr 21 parallel explicitly. Jer 32:9-12 preserves the most-elaborated HB legal-conveyance pericope — Jeremiah's symbolic field-purchase at Anatot with weighed-silver and witnessed-deed.

10
Now Ephron was sitting in the midst of the sons of Het. And Ephron the Hittite answered Avraham in the ears of the sons of Het, of all that went in at the gate of his city, saying,
Now Ephron was sitting in the midst of the children of Heth. And Ephron the Hittite answered Abraham in the audience of the children of Heth, even of all that went in at the gate of his city, saying,
וְעֶפְר֥וֹן יֹשֵׁ֖ב בְּת֣וֹךְ בְּנֵי־חֵ֑ת וַיַּעַן֩ עֶפְר֨וֹן הַחִתִּ֤י אֶת־אַבְרָהָם֙ בְּאָזְנֵ֣י בְנֵי־חֵ֔ת לְכֹ֛ל בָּאֵ֥י שַֽׁעַר־עִיר֖וֹ לֵאמֹֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

Efron ha-Chitti — the gentilic-adjective. v 10 introduces Efron with the explicit gentilic ha-Chitti (the Hittite) — distinct from the collective-noun-phrase bnei Het used elsewhere in the chapter. The WoH translation renders bnei Het as sons of Het (literal) and ha-Chitti as the Hittite (the standard English convention for the gentilic), preserving the lexical-form distinction in the MT.

The city-gate as juridical venue. The phrase l-khol ba'ei sha'ar iro (of all that went in at the gate of his city) names the venue: the city-gate is the standard HB formal-juridical venue for property-transactions and legal-cases (cf. Ruth 4:1-12 — Boaz's redemption-at-the-gate, the most-elaborated HB city-gate-transaction-scene; Deut 21:19, 22:15, 25:7 — the elders-at-the-gate). The phrase locates the negotiation in the formal-public legal-arena and identifies the bnei Het as the witnessing-population — the necessary precondition for the conferment-formula at vv 17-18, 20.

11
No, my lord, hear me. The field I give you, and the cave that is in it, I give it to you; in the presence of the sons of my people I give it to you; bury your dead.
Nay, my lord, hear me. The field give I thee, and the cave that is therein, I give it thee. In the presence of the children of my people give I it thee. Bury thy dead.
לֹֽא־אֲדֹנִ֣י שְׁמָעֵ֔נִי הַשָּׂדֶה֙ נָתַ֣תִּי לָ֔ךְ וְהַמְּעָרָ֥ה אֲשֶׁר־בּ֖וֹ לְךָ֣ נְתַתִּ֑יהָ לְעֵינֵ֧י בְנֵי־עַמִּ֛י נְתַתִּ֥יהָ לָּ֖ךְ קְבֹ֥ר מֵתֶֽךָ׃
12
And Avraham bowed himself down before the people of the land.
And Abraham bowed himself down before the people of the land.
וַיִּשְׁתַּ֙חוּ֙ אַבְרָהָ֔ם לִפְנֵ֖י עַ֥ם הָאָֽרֶץ׃
13
And he spoke to Ephron in the ears of the people of the land, saying, But if you will, I pray you, hear me: I will give the price of the field; take it from me, and I will bury my dead there.
And he spake unto Ephron in the audience of the people of the land, saying, But if thou wilt, I pray thee, hear me. I will give the price of the field. Take it of me, and I will bury my dead there.
וַיְדַבֵּ֨ר אֶל־עֶפְר֜וֹן בְּאָזְנֵ֤י עַם־הָאָ֙רֶץ֙ לֵאמֹ֔ר אַ֛ךְ אִם־אַתָּ֥ה ל֖וּ שְׁמָעֵ֑נִי נָתַ֜תִּי כֶּ֤סֶף הַשָּׂדֶה֙ קַ֣ח מִמֶּ֔נִּי וְאֶקְבְּרָ֥ה אֶת־מֵתִ֖י שָֽׁמָּה׃
14
And Ephron answered Avraham, saying to him,
And Ephron answered Abraham, saying unto him,
וַיַּ֧עַן עֶפְר֛וֹן אֶת־אַבְרָהָ֖ם לֵאמֹ֥ר לֽוֹ׃
15
My lord, hearken to me: a piece of land worth four hundred shekels of silver, what is that between me and you? Bury therefore your dead.
My lord, hearken unto me. A piece of land worth four hundred shekels of silver, what is that betwixt me and thee? Bury therefore thy dead.
אֲדֹנִ֣י שְׁמָעֵ֔נִי אֶרֶץ֩ אַרְבַּ֨ע מֵאֹ֧ת שֶֽׁקֶל־כֶּ֛סֶף בֵּינִ֥י וּבֵֽינְךָ֖ מַה־הִ֑וא וְאֶת־מֵתְךָ֖ קְבֹֽר׃
16
And Avraham hearkened to Ephron; and Avraham weighed out to Ephron the silver which he had named in the ears of the sons of Het, four hundred shekels of silver, current money with the merchant.
And Abraham hearkened unto Ephron. And Abraham weighed to Ephron the silver which he had named in the audience of the children of Heth, four hundred shekels of silver, current [money] with the merchant.
וַיִּשְׁמַ֣ע אַבְרָהָם֮ אֶל־עֶפְרוֹן֒ וַיִּשְׁקֹ֤ל אַבְרָהָם֙ לְעֶפְרֹ֔ן אֶת־הַכֶּ֕סֶף אֲשֶׁ֥ר דִּבֶּ֖ר בְּאָזְנֵ֣י בְנֵי־חֵ֑ת אַרְבַּ֤ע מֵאוֹת֙ שֶׁ֣קֶל כֶּ֔סֶף עֹבֵ֖ר לַסֹּחֵֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

Kesef over la-socher — the standardized-merchant-weight bullion-formula. The phrase over la-socher (passing for the merchant) identifies the silver as conforming to the inter-regional commercial-standard weight, as distinct from local or domestic weights. Through the entire patriarchal-period and Iron-Age HB chronology — coinage was not introduced in the ANE until the late 7th / early 6th century BCE (Lydia) — silver functioned as bullion-money, valued and exchanged by weight. The chapter's verb va-yishqol (and he weighed) is grammatically and lexically transparent in the pre-coinage system: silver was weighed out, not counted. The shekel itself derives from sh-q-l (to weigh).

The over la-socher formula is constrained in the HB — it recurs only at 2 Kgs 12:5 (the Jehoash temple-repair fund — kesef over, the abbreviated form), confirming the formula's commercial-currency function. The wider HB pre-coinage land-transaction parallels: Gen 33:19 (Ya'aqov's me'ah qesitah purchase at Shechem; cf. kesitah glossary entry from Job 42); 2 Sam 24:24 / 1 Chr 21:25 (David's threshing-floor purchase, with the explicit b-mishqal — by weight — at 1 Chr); Jer 32:9-12 (Jeremiah's elaborate-procedure purchase at Anatot).

The 400-shekel price. The price is not trivial — comparable to the 50 shekels David pays for the threshing-floor (2 Sam 24:24), the 17 shekels Jeremiah pays at Anatot (Jer 32:9), or the 200-shekel range of standard Iron-Age field-prices attested in the Neo-Assyrian and Neo-Babylonian corpora. Whether the 400-shekel figure represents (a) a genuinely-elevated price reflecting the cultic significance of the burial-site, or (b) a stylized-large-number reflecting Avraham's stature, or (c) the price Efron extracts after rhetorical-deflection, is editorially-undetermined; Sarna 1989 p. 162 notes the price as high but does not commit. The chapter's emphasis is on the full-payment (the legal-symbolic transfer) rather than on the price-as-bargain.

17
So the field of Ephron, which was in Makhpelah, which was before Mamre — the field, and the cave which was in it, and all the trees that were in the field, that were in all the border of it round about, were made sure
So the field of Ephron, which was in Machpelah, which was before Mamre, the field, and the cave which was therein, and all the trees that were in the field, that were in all the border thereof round about, were made sure
וַיָּ֣קָם ׀ שְׂדֵ֣ה עֶפְר֗וֹן אֲשֶׁר֙ בַּמַּכְפֵּלָ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֖ר לִפְנֵ֣י מַמְרֵ֑א הַשָּׂדֶה֙ וְהַמְּעָרָ֣ה אֲשֶׁר־בּ֔וֹ וְכָל־הָעֵץ֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר בַּשָּׂדֶ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר בְּכָל־גְּבֻל֖וֹ סָבִֽיב׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

The conferment-formula va-yaqem ... l-Avraham l-miqnah. vv 17-18 (continuing as a single legal-period across the verse-boundary) contain the chapter's formal legal-deed conferment-formula: the verb va-yaqem (qal of q-w-m with the inanimate-property subject — and-it-stood / was-established / was-confirmed) plus the l-Avraham l-miqnah construction (to Avraham as a possession-by-purchase) is the technical-juridical idiom of the conveyance-document genre. The chapter's careful enumeration here — the field, and the cave which was in it, and all the trees that were in the field, that were in all the border of it round about — is the standard ANE conveyance-document inventory-clause (the object-of-sale specification). The legal-formal weight is editorially-deliberate.

The chapter as legal-narrative. G. M. Tucker JBL 85 (1966) reads the entire chapter as a dialogue-deed — a literary-form derived from formal-conveyance documents, with the negotiation-dialogue replacing the document's witness-clause-and-stipulations and the conferment-formula at vv 17-18 (and the recapitulation at v 20) replacing the document's standing-clause. The classification has been broadly accepted (Westermann, Sarna, Wenham, Hamilton, Speiser). The ANE-comparative legal-deed parallels are well-documented (Old Babylonian sale-tablets, Neo-Babylonian/Neo-Assyrian conveyance-formulae, Aramaic Elephantine papyri, Hittite legal-deeds); the precise legal-tradition-of-origin is contested, with Westbrook 1971 JSS 16 arguing for the 1st-millennium Neo-Babylonian/Neo-Assyrian context as the closest formal-parallel and the older Lehmann 1953 BASOR 129 Hittite-law-parallel thesis substantially deflated by subsequent scholarship.

The Hebron-Mamre identification. The chapter locates the field lifnei Mamre (before Mamre) — the elonei Mamre (oaks/terebinths of Mamre) of Avraham's residence (Gen 13:18, 14:13, 18:1; see existing glossary entry). v 19 will make the identification explicit: al-pnei Mamre hi Chevron. The patriarchal-residence (the elonei Mamre of the three-visitors-theophany at Gen 18) and the patriarchal-burial-site are co-located at Hebron — the editorial-geography binds the Avraham-narrative's theophanic-peak (Gen 18) and its first formal land-acquisition (Gen 23) at a single site.

18
to Avraham for a possession, in the presence of the sons of Het, before all that went in at the gate of his city.
unto Abraham for a possession in the presence of the children of Heth, before all that went in at the gate of his city.
לְאַבְרָהָ֥ם לְמִקְנָ֖ה לְעֵינֵ֣י בְנֵי־חֵ֑ת בְּכֹ֖ל בָּאֵ֥י שַֽׁעַר־עִירֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

The witnessed-transfer clause l-eynei bnei Het b-khol ba'ei sha'ar iro. The conferment-formula's closing element names the witnesses — in the sight of the sons of Het, of all that went in at the gate of his city — and locates the transaction at the formal-juridical venue (the city-gate, cf. v 10 commentary). The witnessed-transfer is the necessary precondition for the title's irrevocability: the bnei Het as witnessing-population establish the public-record of the conferment. The closest HB formal-parallel is Ruth 4:1-12 — Boaz's redemption-and-marriage transaction at the city-gate of Bethlehem, with the witnessing-elders explicitly named (Ruth 4:9-11: edim atem ha-yom — you are witnesses this day) and the standing-clause pronounced. The Gen 23 and Ruth 4 parallels together constitute the HB's two most-elaborated formal-conveyance-narratives.

19
And after this, Avraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Makhpelah before Mamre — the same is Hevron — in the land of Kena'an.
And after this, Abraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Machpelah before Mamre (the same is Hebron), in the land of Canaan.
וְאַחֲרֵי־כֵן֩ קָבַ֨ר אַבְרָהָ֜ם אֶת־שָׂרָ֣ה אִשְׁתּ֗וֹ אֶל־מְעָרַ֞ת שְׂדֵ֧ה הַמַּכְפֵּלָ֛ה עַל־פְּנֵ֥י מַמְרֵ֖א הִ֣וא חֶבְר֑וֹן בְּאֶ֖רֶץ כְּנָֽעַן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 19

Sarah is the first interred in the Makhpelah cave — the foundational burial that grounds the multi-generational patriarchal-burial-tradition.
