The cross-corpus chain that follows from this verse: Avraham himself is buried in the cave by Yitzchaq and Yishma'el (Gen 25:9-10 — va-yiqberu oto Yitzchaq v-Yishma'el banav el-me'arat ha-Makhpelah... ha-sadeh asher qanah Avraham me-et bnei Het); Ya'aqov's burial-instructions on his deathbed name the previously-interred in the most extensive cross-reference (Gen 49:29-32 — Ya'aqov names Avraham + Sarah, Yitzchaq + Rivkah, and his own first wife Le'ah as already-buried at the site, instructing his sons to bury him there im-avotai); Ya'aqov is buried in the cave by his sons after the Egyptian-period mourning (Gen 50:13). The pattern: the cave is the first piece of the promised-land actually purchased and held in title by the patriarchs (the editorial-significance of the chapter's careful legal-formalization) and the burial-site for three patriarchal couples — Avraham + Sarah, Yitzchaq + Rivkah, Ya'aqov + Le'ah.

The verse's closing formula hi Chevron b-eretz Kena'an repeats the v 2 toponym-doubling and adds the land-of-Kena'an locator — closing the chapter's geographical frame and binding the burial explicitly to the patriarchal-promise land (cf. the eretz Kena'an of the Avrahamic land-promise, Gen 12:5-7, 13:14-17, 15:7-21, 17:8). The first patriarchal-burial is in the promised-land itself — editorially significant for the multi-generational return-and-burial pattern (Ya'aqov's deathbed insistence on burial im-avotai at Hebron rather than in Egypt, Gen 47:29-31, 49:29-32, 50:5-14).

20
And the field, and the cave that is in it, were made sure to Avraham for a possession of a burying-place by the sons of Het.
And the field, and the cave that is therein, were made sure unto Abraham for a possession of a burying-place by the children of Heth.
וַיָּ֨קָם הַשָּׂדֶ֜ה וְהַמְּעָרָ֧ה אֲשֶׁר־בּ֛וֹ לְאַבְרָהָ֖ם לַאֲחֻזַּת־קָ֑בֶר מֵאֵ֖ת בְּנֵי־חֵֽת׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 20

The chapter's recapitulating conferment-formula. v 20 repeats the va-yaqem ... l-Avraham la-achuzat qaver me-et bnei Het in a compact summary-form. The three-fold conferment-pattern across vv 17, 18, and 20 is editorially-deliberate: the chapter's legal-formalization is so important to the patriarchal-narratives' theological structure — the first piece of the promised-land actually held in title — that the conferment is restated for emphasis (Sarna 1989 pp. 162-163). The closing me-et bnei Het (from the sons of Het) names the conveying-party explicitly, sealing the title-transfer's documentary completeness.

The chapter's closing parashah-marker (ס). The petuhah/setumah closing-marker (ס for setumah, closed) marks the chapter as a self-contained narrative-unit in the Masoretic parashah-division. The next narrative-unit (Gen 24 — the betrothal-mission for Yitzchaq) presupposes Sarah's burial and the establishment of the patriarchal-tomb at Hebron, but is a distinct narrative-act.

Chapter 24

Genesis 24 — The servant's mission to Aram-Naharaim and the betrothal of Rivkah

1
And Avraham was old, well stricken in years; and YHWH had blessed Avraham in all things.
And Abraham was old, [and] well stricken in age. And Jehovah had blessed Abraham in all things.
וְאַבְרָהָ֣ם זָקֵ֔ן בָּ֖א בַּיָּמִ֑ים וַֽיהוָ֛ה בֵּרַ֥ךְ אֶת־אַבְרָהָ֖ם בַּכֹּֽל׃
2
And Avraham said to his servant, the elder of his house, who ruled over all that he had, Put, I pray you, your hand under my thigh,
And Abraham said unto his servant, the elder of his house, that ruled over all that he had, Put, I pray thee, thy hand under my thigh.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אַבְרָהָ֗ם אֶל־עַבְדּוֹ֙ זְקַ֣ן בֵּית֔וֹ הַמֹּשֵׁ֖ל בְּכָל־אֲשֶׁר־ל֑וֹ שִֽׂים־נָ֥א יָדְךָ֖ תַּ֥חַת יְרֵכִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

Sim-na yadkha tachat yereki — put, please, your hand under my thigh — is the chapter's first lexical-keystone moment. The gesture is the formal-juridical ANE oath-by-the-thigh; the standard ASV / KJV / NRSV / NJPS translations all preserve the literal thigh, and the WoH translation follows.

The verb yarekh (יָרֵךְ) means thigh in the strict anatomical sense (Gen 32:26 of Ya'aqov's wrestling-injury; Num 5:21 of the sotah's thigh), but in the legal-formulaic register of the two oath-passages it is widely read as a euphemism for the genital area — the seat of generative power, the source of the patriarchal seed-line whose continuation the oath is meant to secure. The reading is the modern critical consensus (Sarna JPS p. 163; Westermann Genesis 12-36 p. 384; Wenham WBC p. 141; Hamilton NICOT pp. 139-140; Alter p. 117; Speiser AB p. 178). Rashi at Gen 24:2 cites Pirqei de-Rabbi Eliezer 16 and reads the gesture as touching the circumcised member — because that is the first commandment that came upon him — connecting the oath to the brit milah covenantal-organ. The Hebrew euphemism is preserved in translation rather than disambiguated in the text.

The gesture's only other HB occurrence is Gen 47:29, where Ya'aqov commissions Yosef for the burial-oath using identical language (sim-na yadkha tachat yereki) paired with the chesed v-emet formula. The two HB instances share a structural pattern: a patriarchal father-figure at the end of his active life commissions an authorized agent on a matter the principal cannot personally accomplish — Avraham cannot travel to Aram-Naharaim; Ya'aqov cannot personally choose his burial-site in Kena'an. Malul 1985 (VT 35) develops the genital-touch reading and proposes the parallel to the pachad Yitzchaq (Thigh of Yitzchaq) at Gen 31:42, 53; Hill 1967 (JBL 86) treats the gesture in its formal-juridical register.

The ANE-comparative context: oath-on-fertility-symbol is widespread in the Mesopotamian, Hittite, Ugaritic, and Egyptian corpora — oaths by the cult-statue's genitals, by the king's sceptre, by the bull's testicles — but the specific gesture of touching another person's genital area to swear an oath is rare in the extant ANE texts. The two HB occurrences are the principal documentary attestation; the gesture's logic is comprehensible within the wider ANE pattern but its specific form is editorially distinctive to the patriarchal-narrative tradition.

3
and I will make you swear by YHWH, the Elohim of the heavens and the Elohim of the earth, that you will not take a wife for my son of the daughters of the Kena'ani, among whom I dwell;
And I will make thee swear by Jehovah, the God of heaven and the God of the earth, that thou wilt not take a wife for my son of the daughters of the Canaanites, among whom I dwell.
וְאַשְׁבִּ֣יעֲךָ֔ בַּֽיהוָה֙ אֱלֹהֵ֣י הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וֵֽאלֹהֵ֖י הָאָ֑רֶץ אֲשֶׁ֨ר לֹֽא־תִקַּ֤ח אִשָּׁה֙ לִבְנִ֔י מִבְּנוֹת֙ הַֽכְּנַעֲנִ֔י אֲשֶׁ֥ר אָנֹכִ֖י יוֹשֵׁ֥ב בְּקִרְבּֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

The verse contains the first occurrence in the Hebrew Bible of the formula YHWH Elohei ha-shamayim v-Elohei ha-aretz — YHWH, the Elohim of the heavens and the Elohim of the earth — and the first occurrence of the bare Elohei ha-shamayim construction. The doubled cosmographic-binitarian construct (heavens + earth under a single divine sovereignty) is the most-developed form of the formula in the patriarchal narratives; the bare Elohei ha-shamayim recurs at v 7 in Avraham's continued commissioning-speech, and then disappears from Genesis.

Across the rest of the canonical Hebrew Bible, the Elohei ha-shamayim phrase is concentrated in post-exilic and Persian-period literature: Jonah 1:9, Ezra 1:2, 5:11-12, 6:9-10, 7:12-23, Neh 1:4-5, 2:4-20, Dan 2:18-44, Ps 136:26. The cross-corpus distribution generates a well-known source-critical question. Westermann (Genesis 12-36 pp. 384-385) and Hamilton (NICOT pp. 138-139) read the Gen 24 occurrence as a Persian-period stock-phrase imported into the patriarchal narrative — evidence of late composition for the chapter as a whole. Sarna (JPS p. 164) reads it as a context-sensitive diplomatic-foreign-context idiom: Avraham is commissioning a journey into a foreign land (Aram-Naharaim) where the local cult is not YHWHistic, and the formula establishes YHWH's universal sovereignty in cosmographic-binitarian terms a foreign audience would recognize. The formula is concentrated in the foreign-context speeches (vv 3, 7), not in the narrative-prose, which supports the diplomatic-context reading.

The WoH translation preserves the article-bearing construct per established Elohim-policy: the Elohim of the heavens and the Elohim of the earth (rather than ASV's the God of heaven and the God of earth). The lens-relevant late-period-vs.-diplomatic-context question is held in this commentary; the translated text reads as standard scholarly English.

4
but you shall go to my land, and to my kindred, and take a wife for my son, for Yitzchaq.
But thou shalt go unto my country, and to my kindred, and take a wife for my son Isaac.
כִּ֧י אֶל־אַרְצִ֛י וְאֶל־מוֹלַדְתִּ֖י תֵּלֵ֑ךְ וְלָקַחְתָּ֥ אִשָּׁ֖ה לִבְנִ֥י לְיִצְחָֽק׃
5
And the servant said to him, Perhaps the woman will not be willing to follow me to this land. Must I needs bring your son back to the land from which you came?
And the servant said unto him, Peradventure the woman will not be willing to follow me unto this land. Must I needs bring thy son again unto the land from whence thou camest?
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֵלָיו֙ הָעֶ֔בֶד אוּלַי֙ לֹא־תֹאבֶ֣ה הָֽאִשָּׁ֔ה לָלֶ֥כֶת אַחֲרַ֖י אֶל־הָאָ֣רֶץ הַזֹּ֑את הֶֽהָשֵׁ֤ב אָשִׁיב֙ אֶת־בִּנְךָ֔ אֶל־הָאָ֖רֶץ אֲשֶׁר־יָצָ֥אתָ מִשָּֽׁם׃
6
And Avraham said to him, Beware that you do not bring my son back there.
And Abraham said unto him, Beware thou that thou bring not my son thither again.
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר אֵלָ֖יו אַבְרָהָ֑ם הִשָּׁ֣מֶר לְךָ֔ פֶּן־תָּשִׁ֥יב אֶת־בְּנִ֖י שָֽׁמָּה׃
7
YHWH, the Elohim of the heavens, who took me from my father's house, and from the land of my kindred, and who spoke to me, and who swore to me, saying, To your seed I will give this land — he will send his messenger before you, and you shall take a wife for my son from there.
Jehovah, the God of heaven, who took me from my father`s house, and from the land of my nativity, and who spake unto me, and who sware unto me, saying, Unto thy seed will I give this land. He will send his angel before thee, and thou shalt take a wife for my son from thence.
יְהוָ֣ה ׀ אֱלֹהֵ֣י הַשָּׁמַ֗יִם אֲשֶׁ֨ר לְקָחַ֜נִי מִבֵּ֣ית אָבִי֮ וּמֵאֶ֣רֶץ מֽוֹלַדְתִּי֒ וַאֲשֶׁ֨ר דִּבֶּר־לִ֜י וַאֲשֶׁ֤ר נִֽשְׁבַּֽע־לִי֙ לֵאמֹ֔ר לְזַ֨רְעֲךָ֔ אֶתֵּ֖ן אֶת־הָאָ֣רֶץ הַזֹּ֑את ה֗וּא יִשְׁלַ֤ח מַלְאָכוֹ֙ לְפָנֶ֔יךָ וְלָקַחְתָּ֥ אִשָּׁ֛ה לִבְנִ֖י מִשָּֽׁם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The bare Elohei ha-shamayim recurs without the v-Elohei ha-aretz second member, paired with an explicit thematic cross-reference to Gen 12:1-4 (lekh-lekha): YHWH ... asher l'qachani mi-beit avi u-me-eretz moladti — YHWH, who took me from my father's house and from the land of my kindred. The cross-reference is editorial-thematic rather than lexical (the lekh-lekha lexeme itself is not present at v 7), so the lekh-lekha glossary entry is not applied; the Abraham-from-Ur formula and the seed-land promise (l-zarakha eten et-ha-aretz ha-zot) are covered by zera-eretz-promise. The verse rehearses the chapter's diplomatic-context register: YHWH is named in his cosmographic-binitarian sovereignty (heavens) and his patriarchal-history specificity (the one who took Avraham from Aram) — the formula functioning as both universal-sovereignty claim and historical-anchoring.

8
And if the woman is not willing to follow you, then you shall be clear from this oath of mine; only you shall not bring my son back there.
And if the woman be not willing to follow thee, then thou shalt be clear from this my oath. Only thou shalt not bring my son thither again.
וְאִם־לֹ֨א תֹאבֶ֤ה הָֽאִשָּׁה֙ לָלֶ֣כֶת אַחֲרֶ֔יךָ וְנִקִּ֕יתָ מִשְּׁבֻעָתִ֖י זֹ֑את רַ֣ק אֶת־בְּנִ֔י לֹ֥א תָשֵׁ֖ב שָֽׁמָּה׃
9
And the servant put his hand under the thigh of Avraham his master, and swore to him concerning this matter.
And the servant put his hand under the thigh of Abraham his master, and sware to him concerning this matter.
וַיָּ֤שֶׂם הָעֶ֙בֶד֙ אֶת־יָד֔וֹ תַּ֛חַת יֶ֥רֶךְ אַבְרָהָ֖ם אֲדֹנָ֑יו וַיִּשָּׁ֣בַֽע ל֔וֹ עַל־הַדָּבָ֖ר הַזֶּֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The servant executes the oath-gesture commanded at v 2 — same lexeme, same euphemistic register; see the v 2 commentary.

10
And the servant took ten camels of the camels of his master, and departed, having all goodly things of his master's in his hand. And he arose, and went to Aram-Naharayim, to the city of Nachor.
And the servant took ten camels, of the camels of his master, and departed, having all goodly things of his master`s in his hand. And he arose, and went to Mesopotamia, unto the city of Nahor.
וַיִּקַּ֣ח הָ֠עֶבֶד עֲשָׂרָ֨ה גְמַלִּ֜ים מִגְּמַלֵּ֤י אֲדֹנָיו֙ וַיֵּ֔לֶךְ וְכָל־ט֥וּב אֲדֹנָ֖יו בְּיָד֑וֹ וַיָּ֗קָם וַיֵּ֛לֶךְ אֶל־אֲרַ֥ם נַֽהֲרַ֖יִם אֶל־עִ֥יר נָחֽוֹר׃
11
And he made the camels kneel down outside the city by the well of water at the time of evening, the time when women go out to draw water.
And he made the camels to kneel down without the city by the well of water at the time of evening, the time that women go out to draw water.
וַיַּבְרֵ֧ךְ הַגְּמַלִּ֛ים מִח֥וּץ לָעִ֖יר אֶל־בְּאֵ֣ר הַמָּ֑יִם לְעֵ֣ת עֶ֔רֶב לְעֵ֖ת צֵ֥את הַשֹּׁאֲבֹֽת׃
12
And he said, YHWH, the Elohim of my master Avraham, grant me good speed, I pray you, this day, and show chesed to my master Avraham.
And he said, O Jehovah, the God of my master Abraham, send me, I pray thee, good speed this day, and show kindness unto my master Abraham.
וַיֹּאמַ֓ר ׀ יְהוָ֗ה אֱלֹהֵי֙ אֲדֹנִ֣י אַבְרָהָ֔ם הַקְרֵה־נָ֥א לְפָנַ֖י הַיּ֑וֹם וַעֲשֵׂה־חֶ֕סֶד עִ֖ם אֲדֹנִ֥י אַבְרָהָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

The first direct prayer of a non-patriarch in the Hebrew Bible (Sarna JPS p. 165). The servant addresses YHWH directly with the four-element prose-prayer structure that Greenberg (Biblical Prose Prayer 1983 pp. 7-12) reads as foundational: (i) divine address-formula (YHWH); (ii) historical-anchoring phrase (Elohei adoni Avraham); (iii) petition (haqreh-na lefanai ha-yom — grant me good speed this day); (iv) appeal to divine virtue (va-aseh-chesed im adoni Avraham — show chesed to my master Avraham). Prior to this verse, direct divine address in Genesis is concentrated in patriarchal figures (Avraham at 15:2-3, 8; 17:18; 18:23-32) and in YHWH-initiated dialogue. The verse authorizes prayer-as-direct-address for figures outside the patriarchal-prophetic circle, establishing the general-servant prayer-access pattern standard in the later HB. Miller They Cried to the Lord 1994 pp. 100-105 extends the analysis to the sign-seeking sub-structure at vv 13-14.

The lexeme chesed is preserved untranslated per established WoH policy on theologically-loaded terms with no single English equivalent (parallel to nefesh, Elohim, YHWH); the apparatus carries the philological background. The full chesed v-emet virtue-pair appears at vv 27 and 49; see the entry chesed-v-emet-steadfast-love-and-truth.

The servant prays not as an individual-religious-subject but as an authorized-agent of Avraham's covenantal relation: he names YHWH as Elohei adoni Avraham (not as his own deity), petitions for chesed im adoni Avraham, and reads the test-result (v 14b) as kI-asita chesed im-adoni. The pattern is the agent-prayer sub-genre, recurring at Gen 32:10-13 (Ya'aqov at the Jabbok), Exod 32:11-14 and Num 14:13-19 (Moses), 1 Sam 12:23 (Samuel).

13
Behold, I am standing by the spring of water; and the daughters of the men of the city are coming out to draw water.
Behold, I am standing by the fountain of water. And the daughters of the men of the city are coming out to draw water.
הִנֵּ֛ה אָנֹכִ֥י נִצָּ֖ב עַל־עֵ֣ין הַמָּ֑יִם וּבְנוֹת֙ אַנְשֵׁ֣י הָעִ֔יר יֹצְאֹ֖ת לִשְׁאֹ֥ב מָֽיִם׃
14
Let it come to pass that the young woman to whom I shall say, Let down your jar, I pray you, that I may drink; and she shall say, Drink, and I will give your camels drink also — let her be the one whom you have appointed for your servant, for Yitzchaq; and by this I shall know that you have shown chesed to my master.
And let it come to pass, that the damsel to whom I shall say, Let down thy pitcher, I pray thee, that I may drink. And she shall say, Drink, and I will give thy camels drink also. Let the same be she that thou hast appointed for thy servant Isaac. And thereby shall I know that thou hast showed kindness unto my master.
וְהָיָ֣ה הַֽנַּעֲרָ֗ אֲשֶׁ֨ר אֹמַ֤ר אֵלֶ֙יהָ֙ הַטִּי־נָ֤א כַדֵּךְ֙ וְאֶשְׁתֶּ֔ה וְאָמְרָ֣ה שְׁתֵ֔ה וְגַם־גְּמַלֶּ֖יךָ אַשְׁקֶ֑ה אֹתָ֤הּ הֹכַ֙חְתָּ֙ לְעַבְדְּךָ֣ לְיִצְחָ֔ק וּבָ֣הּ אֵדַ֔ע כִּי־עָשִׂ֥יתָ חֶ֖סֶד עִם־אֲדֹנִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

The conditional structure v-haya ha-na'arah asher omar eleha ... v-amrah ... otah hokhachta l-avdekha is the chapter's divinatory sign-test: a future-conditional whose fulfillment will be read as direct divine designation. The sub-genre recurs in modified form at Judg 6:36-40 (Gideon's fleece-test — the canonical HB sign-seeking pericope) and at 1 Sam 14:8-10 (Jonathan's omen at Michmash). Gen 24:14 is the inaugural instance — earlier in the canonical sequence and providing the template for the later instances. The test's lexical core is the verb hokhicha (hiphil of y-k-ch — to determine, designate, appoint) which becomes the technical-juridical term for divine designation in the chapter (recurring at v 44 in the recitation-block).

15
And it came to pass, before he had finished speaking, that behold, Rivkah came out, who was born to Bethuel the son of Milkah, the wife of Nachor, Avraham's brother, with her jar upon her shoulder.
And it came to pass, before he had done speaking, that, behold, Rebekah came out, who was born to Bethuel the son of Milcah, the wife of Nahor, Abraham`s brother, with her pitcher upon her shoulder.
וַֽיְהִי־ה֗וּא טֶרֶם֮ כִּלָּ֣ה לְדַבֵּר֒ וְהִנֵּ֧ה רִבְקָ֣ה יֹצֵ֗את אֲשֶׁ֤ר יֻלְּדָה֙ לִבְתוּאֵ֣ל בֶּן־מִלְכָּ֔ה אֵ֥שֶׁת נָח֖וֹר אֲחִ֣י אַבְרָהָ֑ם וְכַדָּ֖הּ עַל־שִׁכְמָֽהּ׃
16
And the young woman was very fair to look upon, a virgin, and no man had known her; and she went down to the spring, and filled her jar, and came up.
And the damsel was very fair to look upon, a virgin, neither had any man known her. And she went down to the fountain, and filled her pitcher, and came up.
וְהַֽנַּעֲרָ֗ טֹבַ֤ת מַרְאֶה֙ מְאֹ֔ד בְּתוּלָ֕ה וְאִ֖ישׁ לֹ֣א יְדָעָ֑הּ וַתֵּ֣רֶד הָעַ֔יְנָה וַתְּמַלֵּ֥א כַדָּ֖הּ וַתָּֽעַל׃
17
And the servant ran to meet her, and said, Let me drink, I pray you, a little water from your jar.
And the servant ran to meet her, and said, Give me to drink, I pray thee, a little water from thy pitcher.
וַיָּ֥רָץ הָעֶ֖בֶד לִקְרָאתָ֑הּ וַיֹּ֕אמֶר הַגְמִיאִ֥ינִי נָ֛א מְעַט־מַ֖יִם מִכַּדֵּֽךְ׃
18
And she said, Drink, my lord; and she hastened, and let down her jar upon her hand, and gave him drink.
And she said, Drink, my lord. And she hasted, and let down her pitcher upon her hand, and gave him drink.
וַתֹּ֖אמֶר שְׁתֵ֣ה אֲדֹנִ֑י וַתְּמַהֵ֗ר וַתֹּ֧רֶד כַּדָּ֛הּ עַל־יָדָ֖הּ וַתַּשְׁקֵֽהוּ׃
19
And when she had finished giving him drink, she said, I will also draw for your camels, until they have finished drinking.
And when she had done giving him drink, she said, I will draw for thy camels also, until they have done drinking.
וַתְּכַ֖ל לְהַשְׁקֹת֑וֹ וַתֹּ֗אמֶר גַּ֤ם לִגְמַלֶּ֙יךָ֙ אֶשְׁאָ֔ב עַ֥ד אִם־כִּלּ֖וּ לִשְׁתֹּֽת׃
20
And she hastened, and emptied her jar into the trough, and ran again to the well to draw, and drew for all his camels.
And she hasted, and emptied her pitcher into the trough, and ran again unto the well to draw, and drew for all his camels.
וַתְּמַהֵ֗ר וַתְּעַ֤ר כַּדָּהּ֙ אֶל־הַשֹּׁ֔קֶת וַתָּ֥רָץ ע֛וֹד אֶֽל־הַבְּאֵ֖ר לִשְׁאֹ֑ב וַתִּשְׁאַ֖ב לְכָל־גְּמַלָּֽיו׃
21
And the man was gazing at her in silence, to know whether YHWH had prospered his journey or not.
And the man looked stedfastly on her, holding his peace, to know whether Jehovah had made his journey prosperous or not.
וְהָאִ֥ישׁ מִשְׁתָּאֵ֖ה לָ֑הּ מַחֲרִ֕ישׁ לָדַ֗עַת הַֽהִצְלִ֧יחַ יְהוָ֛ה דַּרְכּ֖וֹ אִם־לֹֽא׃
22
And it came to pass, when the camels had finished drinking, that the man took a golden nose-ring of half a shekel weight, and two bracelets for her hands of ten shekels weight of gold,
And it came to pass, as the camels had done drinking, that the man took a golden ring of half a shekel weight, and two bracelets for her hands of ten shekels weight of gold,
וַיְהִ֗י כַּאֲשֶׁ֨ר כִּלּ֤וּ הַגְּמַלִּים֙ לִשְׁתּ֔וֹת וַיִּקַּ֤ח הָאִישׁ֙ נֶ֣זֶם זָהָ֔ב בֶּ֖קַע מִשְׁקָל֑וֹ וּשְׁנֵ֤י צְמִידִים֙ עַל־יָדֶ֔יהָ עֲשָׂרָ֥ה זָהָ֖ב מִשְׁקָלָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The chapter's only weight-unit transaction. Beqa (half-shekel) is a rare HB lexeme — the only other clear occurrence is Exod 38:26, the half-shekel-per-head census-tax (beqa la-gulgolet machatzit ha-shekel b-shekel ha-qodesh), which explicitly identifies the beqa as machatzit ha-shekel. The standard-shekel mass is approximately 11.4 grams based on Iron Age weight-stone archaeology (Kletter 1998 Economic Keystones); inscribed weight-stones at Judahite sites preserve the BQA / NTzP / PYM unit-inventory. The HB economy operated by weighed bullion rather than by coinage until the Persian period.

The nose-ring + two bracelets total 20.5 shekels of gold ≈ 234 grams. In the betrothal-gift register, the weight-specification is formal-juridical content: the gift's value is recorded in weight-of-precious-metal because the gift is a quasi-contractual element of the marriage-negotiation (cf. Mesopotamian terhatu, Aramaic Elephantine marriage-contracts at TAD B2.6 / B3.8). The v 53 secondary gifts (silver vessels, gold vessels, garments, additional precious gifts to Lavan and the mother) escalate the value, but the v 22 initial gift establishes the formal opening of the negotiation.

23
and said, Whose daughter are you? Tell me, I pray you. Is there room in your father's house for us to lodge?
and said, Whose daughter art thou? Tell me, I pray thee. Is there room in thy father`s house for us to lodge in?
וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ בַּת־מִ֣י אַ֔תְּ הַגִּ֥ידִי נָ֖א לִ֑י הֲיֵ֧שׁ בֵּית־אָבִ֛יךְ מָק֥וֹם לָ֖נוּ לָלִֽין׃
24
And she said to him, I am the daughter of Bethuel the son of Milkah, whom she bore to Nachor.
And she said unto him, I am the daughter of Bethuel the son of Milcah, whom she bare unto Nahor.
וַתֹּ֣אמֶר אֵלָ֔יו בַּת־בְּתוּאֵ֖ל אָנֹ֑כִי בֶּן־מִלְכָּ֕ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר יָלְדָ֖ה לְנָחֽוֹר׃
25
She said moreover to him, We have both straw and fodder in plenty, and room to lodge in.
She said moreover unto him, We have both straw and provender enough, and room to lodge in.
וַתֹּ֣אמֶר אֵלָ֔יו גַּם־תֶּ֥בֶן גַּם־מִסְפּ֖וֹא רַ֣ב עִמָּ֑נוּ גַּם־מָק֖וֹם לָלֽוּן׃
26
And the man bowed his head, and prostrated himself before YHWH.
And the man bowed his head, and worshipped Jehovah.
וַיִּקֹּ֣ד הָאִ֔ישׁ וַיִּשְׁתַּ֖חוּ לַֽיהוָֽה׃
27
And he said, Blessed be YHWH, the Elohim of my master Avraham, who has not forsaken his chesed and his truth toward my master. As for me, YHWH has led me in the way to the house of my master's kin.
And he said, Blessed be Jehovah, the God of my master Abraham, who hath not forsaken his lovingkindness and his truth toward my master. As for me, Jehovah hath led me in the way to the house of my master`s brethren.
וַיֹּ֗אמֶר בָּר֤וּךְ יְהוָה֙ אֱלֹהֵי֙ אֲדֹנִ֣י אַבְרָהָ֔ם אֲ֠שֶׁר לֹֽא־עָזַ֥ב חַסְדּ֛וֹ וַאֲמִתּ֖וֹ מֵעִ֣ם אֲדֹנִ֑י אָנֹכִ֗י בַּדֶּ֙רֶךְ֙ נָחַ֣נִי יְהוָ֔ה בֵּ֖ית אֲחֵ֥י אֲדֹנִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 27

The first occurrence in the Hebrew Bible of the foundational virtue-pair chesed v-emet — chesed and truth. The pair recurs at v 49 in the servant's appeal to Lavan and Bethuel, and across the patriarchal narratives at Gen 32:11 (Ya'aqov at the Jabbok) and Gen 47:29 (Ya'aqov to Yosef, paired with the oath-by-the-thigh). The canonical theological keystone is at Exod 34:6 — the YHWH self-revelation at Sinai after the Golden Calf — where YHWH proclaims his attributes with rav-chesed v-emet at the structural center. The Gen 24:27 prayer thus addresses YHWH in the very terms YHWH will use to self-reveal at Sinai; the structural-thematic anticipation is editorially deliberate.

The lexeme chesed is preserved untranslated per established WoH policy on theologically-loaded terms with no single English equivalent. The standard English renderings — KJV lovingkindness (Coverdale's neologism), ASV mercy (narrower than the Hebrew), NRSV steadfast love (capturing only the loyal-love dimension), NJPS kindness — all flatten the lexeme's semantic core: loyal action toward a covenantal partner, gracious action exceeding what is owed, the disposition that grounds both. Sakenfeld The Meaning of Hesed in the Hebrew Bible (HSM 17, 1978) refines Glueck's foundational Das Wort Hesed (1927): chesed is the action of a more-powerful party on behalf of a less-powerful party in an established relationship, where the more-powerful party could have declined to act. Clark The Word Hesed in the Hebrew Bible (JSOTSup 157, 1993) extends the corpus-comprehensive analysis. The Sakenfeld refinement is the modern critical consensus.

The pair's cross-corpus reach is among the most extensive in the HB: Gen 24:27, 24:49, 32:11, 47:29; Exod 34:6; Josh 2:14; 2 Sam 2:6, 15:20; Ps 25:10, 40:11-12, 57:4, 61:8, 85:11, 86:15, 89:15, 138:2; Prov 3:3, 14:22, 16:6, 20:28; Hos 4:1; Mic 7:20. Twenty-plus principal occurrences across the canon.

28
And the young woman ran, and told her mother's house according to these words.
And the damsel ran, and told her mother`s house according to these words.
וַתָּ֙רָץ֙ הַֽנַּעֲרָ֔ וַתַּגֵּ֖ד לְבֵ֣ית אִמָּ֑הּ כַּדְּבָרִ֖ים הָאֵֽלֶּה׃
29
And Rivkah had a brother, and his name was Lavan; and Lavan ran out to the man, to the spring.
And Rebekah had a brother, and his name was Laban: and Laban ran out unto the man, unto the fountain.
וּלְרִבְקָ֥ה אָ֖ח וּשְׁמ֣וֹ לָבָ֑ן וַיָּ֨רָץ לָבָ֧ן אֶל־הָאִ֛ישׁ הַח֖וּצָה אֶל־הָעָֽיִן׃
30
And it came to pass, when he saw the nose-ring, and the bracelets upon his sister's hands, and when he heard the words of Rivkah his sister, saying, Thus the man spoke to me — that he came to the man; and behold, he was standing by the camels at the spring.
And it came to pass, when he saw the ring, and the bracelets upon his sister`s hands, and when he heard the words of Rebekah his sister, saying, Thus spake the man unto me. That he came unto the man. And, behold, he was standing by the camels at the fountain.
וַיְהִ֣י ׀ כִּרְאֹ֣ת אֶת־הַנֶּ֗זֶם וְֽאֶת־הַצְּמִדִים֮ עַל־יְדֵ֣י אֲחֹתוֹ֒ וּכְשָׁמְע֗וֹ אֶת־דִּבְרֵ֞י רִבְקָ֤ה אֲחֹתוֹ֙ לֵאמֹ֔ר כֹּֽה־דִבֶּ֥ר אֵלַ֖י הָאִ֑ישׁ וַיָּבֹא֙ אֶל־הָאִ֔ישׁ וְהִנֵּ֛ה עֹמֵ֥ד עַל־הַגְּמַלִּ֖ים עַל־הָעָֽיִן׃
31
And he said, Come in, you blessed of YHWH; why do you stand outside? For I have prepared the house, and a place for the camels.
And he said, Come in, thou blessed of Jehovah. Wherefore standest thou without? For I have prepared the house, and room for the camels.
וַיֹּ֕אמֶר בּ֖וֹא בְּר֣וּךְ יְהוָ֑ה לָ֤מָּה תַעֲמֹד֙ בַּח֔וּץ וְאָנֹכִי֙ פִּנִּ֣יתִי הַבַּ֔יִת וּמָק֖וֹם לַגְּמַלִּֽים׃
32
And the man came into the house, and he unloaded the camels; and he gave straw and fodder for the camels, and water to wash his feet and the feet of the men who were with him.
And the man came into the house, and he ungirded the camels. And he gave straw and provender for the camels, and water to wash his feet and the feet of the men that were with him.
וַיָּבֹ֤א הָאִישׁ֙ הַבַּ֔יְתָה וַיְפַתַּ֖ח הַגְּמַלִּ֑ים וַיִּתֵּ֨ן תֶּ֤בֶן וּמִסְפּוֹא֙ לַגְּמַלִּ֔ים וּמַ֙יִם֙ לִרְחֹ֣ץ רַגְלָ֔יו וְרַגְלֵ֥י הָאֲנָשִׁ֖ים אֲשֶׁ֥ר אִתּֽוֹ׃
33
And there was set before him food to eat; but he said, I will not eat until I have spoken my errand. And he said, Speak on.
And there was set food before him to eat. But he said, I will not eat, until I have told mine errand. And he said, Speak on.
ויישם [וַיּוּשַׂ֤ם] לְפָנָיו֙ לֶאֱכֹ֔ל וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ לֹ֣א אֹכַ֔ל עַ֥ד אִם־דִּבַּ֖רְתִּי דְּבָרָ֑י וַיֹּ֖אמֶר דַּבֵּֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 33

Ketiv/qere variant. The ketiv ויישם (vayisem / vayyisam — vague pointing, possibly a corrupted hophal or qal form) is replaced by the qere וַיּוּשַׂם (vayyusam — clear hophal: and-it-was-set). The WoH translation follows the qere per standard MT practice, matching ASV's And there was set. The variant is the chapter's only ketiv/qere divergence.

34
And he said, I am Avraham's servant.
And he said, I am Abraham`s servant.
וַיֹּאמַ֑ר עֶ֥בֶד אַבְרָהָ֖ם אָנֹֽכִי׃
35
And YHWH has blessed my master greatly, and he is become great; and he has given him flocks and herds, and silver and gold, and male and female slaves, and camels and donkeys.
And Jehovah hath blessed my master greatly. And he is become great. And he hath given him flocks and herds, and silver and gold, and men-servants and maid-servants, and camels and asses.
וַיהוָ֞ה בֵּרַ֧ךְ אֶת־אֲדֹנִ֛י מְאֹ֖ד וַיִּגְדָּ֑ל וַיִּתֶּן־ל֞וֹ צֹ֤אן וּבָקָר֙ וְכֶ֣סֶף וְזָהָ֔ב וַעֲבָדִם֙ וּשְׁפָחֹ֔ת וּגְמַלִּ֖ים וַחֲמֹרִֽים׃
36
And Sarah my master's wife bore a son to my master when she was old; and to him he has given all that he has.
And Sarah my master`s wife bare a son to my master when she was old. And unto him hath he given all that he hath.
וַתֵּ֡לֶד שָׂרָה֩ אֵ֨שֶׁת אֲדֹנִ֥י בֵן֙ לַֽאדֹנִ֔י אַחֲרֵ֖י זִקְנָתָ֑הּ וַיִּתֶּן־לּ֖וֹ אֶת־כָּל־אֲשֶׁר־לֽוֹ׃
37
And my master made me swear, saying, You shall not take a wife for my son of the daughters of the Kena'ani, in whose land I dwell;
And my master made me swear, saying, Thou shalt not take a wife for my son of the daughters of the Canaanites, in whose land I dwell.
וַיַּשְׁבִּעֵ֥נִי אֲדֹנִ֖י לֵאמֹ֑ר לֹא־תִקַּ֤ח אִשָּׁה֙ לִבְנִ֔י מִבְּנוֹת֙ הַֽכְּנַעֲנִ֔י אֲשֶׁ֥ר אָנֹכִ֖י יֹשֵׁ֥ב בְּאַרְצֽוֹ׃
38
but you shall go to my father's house, and to my kindred, and take a wife for my son.
But thou shalt go unto my father`s house, and to my kindred, and take a wife for my son.
אִם־לֹ֧א אֶל־בֵּית־אָבִ֛י תֵּלֵ֖ךְ וְאֶל־מִשְׁפַּחְתִּ֑י וְלָקַחְתָּ֥ אִשָּׁ֖ה לִבְנִֽי׃
39
And I said to my master, Perhaps the woman will not follow me.
And I said unto my master, Peradventure the woman will not follow me.
וָאֹמַ֖ר אֶל־אֲדֹנִ֑י אֻלַ֛י לֹא־תֵלֵ֥ךְ הָאִשָּׁ֖ה אַחֲרָֽי׃
40
And he said to me, YHWH, before whom I have walked, will send his messenger with you, and prosper your way; and you shall take a wife for my son of my kindred, and of my father's house.
And he said unto me, Jehovah, before whom I walk, will send his angel with thee, and prosper thy way. And thou shalt take a wife for my son of my kindred, and of my father`s house.
וַיֹּ֖אמֶר אֵלָ֑י יְהוָ֞ה אֲשֶׁר־הִתְהַלַּ֣כְתִּי לְפָנָ֗יו יִשְׁלַ֨ח מַלְאָכ֤וֹ אִתָּךְ֙ וְהִצְלִ֣יחַ דַּרְכֶּ֔ךָ וְלָקַחְתָּ֤ אִשָּׁה֙ לִבְנִ֔י מִמִּשְׁפַּחְתִּ֖י וּמִבֵּ֥ית אָבִֽי׃
41
Then you shall be clear from my oath, when you come to my kindred; and if they will not give her to you, you shall be clear from my oath.
Then shalt thou be clear from my oath, when thou comest to my kindred. And if they give her not to thee, thou shalt be clear from my oath.
אָ֤ז תִּנָּקֶה֙ מֵאָ֣לָתִ֔י כִּ֥י תָב֖וֹא אֶל־מִשְׁפַּחְתִּ֑י וְאִם־לֹ֤א יִתְּנוּ֙ לָ֔ךְ וְהָיִ֥יתָ נָקִ֖י מֵאָלָתִֽי׃
42
And I came this day to the spring, and said, YHWH, the Elohim of my master Avraham, if now you do prosper my way which I go,
And I came this day unto the fountain, and said, O Jehovah, the God of my master Abraham, if now thou do prosper my way which I go.
וָאָבֹ֥א הַיּ֖וֹם אֶל־הָעָ֑יִן וָאֹמַ֗ר יְהוָה֙ אֱלֹהֵי֙ אֲדֹנִ֣י אַבְרָהָ֔ם אִם־יֶשְׁךָ־נָּא֙ מַצְלִ֣יחַ דַּרְכִּ֔י אֲשֶׁ֥ר אָנֹכִ֖י הֹלֵ֥ךְ עָלֶֽיהָ׃
43
Behold, I am standing by the spring of water; and let it come to pass that the maiden who comes out to draw, to whom I shall say, Give me, I pray you, a little water from your jar to drink,
Behold, I am standing by the fountain of water. And let it come to pass, that the maiden that cometh forth to draw, to whom I shall say, Give me, I pray thee, a little water from thy pitcher to drink.
הִנֵּ֛ה אָנֹכִ֥י נִצָּ֖ב עַל־עֵ֣ין הַמָּ֑יִם וְהָיָ֤ה הָֽעַלְמָה֙ הַיֹּצֵ֣את לִשְׁאֹ֔ב וְאָמַרְתִּ֣י אֵלֶ֔יהָ הַשְׁקִֽינִי־נָ֥א מְעַט־מַ֖יִם מִכַּדֵּֽךְ׃
44
and she shall say to me, Drink, you also, and I will also draw for your camels — let her be the woman whom YHWH has appointed for my master's son.
And she shall say to me, Both drink thou, and I will also draw for thy camels. Let the same be the woman whom Jehovah hath appointed for my master`s son.
וְאָמְרָ֤ה אֵלַי֙ גַּם־אַתָּ֣ה שְׁתֵ֔ה וְגַ֥ם לִגְמַלֶּ֖יךָ אֶשְׁאָ֑ב הִ֣וא הָֽאִשָּׁ֔ה אֲשֶׁר־הֹכִ֥יחַ יְהוָ֖ה לְבֶן־אֲדֹנִֽי׃
45
Before I had finished speaking in my heart, behold, Rivkah came out, with her jar on her shoulder; and she went down to the spring, and drew. And I said to her, Let me drink, I pray you.
And before I had done speaking in my heart, behold, Rebekah came forth with her pitcher on her shoulder. And she went down unto the fountain, and drew. And I said unto her, Let me drink, I pray thee.
אֲנִי֩ טֶ֨רֶם אֲכַלֶּ֜ה לְדַבֵּ֣ר אֶל־לִבִּ֗י וְהִנֵּ֨ה רִבְקָ֤ה יֹצֵאת֙ וְכַדָּ֣הּ עַל־שִׁכְמָ֔הּ וַתֵּ֥רֶד הָעַ֖יְנָה וַתִּשְׁאָ֑ב וָאֹמַ֥ר אֵלֶ֖יהָ הַשְׁקִ֥ינִי נָֽא׃
46
And she hastened, and let down her jar from her shoulder, and said, Drink, and I will give your camels drink also; so I drank, and she made the camels drink also.
And she made haste, and let down her pitcher from her shoulder, and said, Drink, and I will give thy camels drink also. So I drank, and she made the camels drink also.
וַתְּמַהֵ֗ר וַתּ֤וֹרֶד כַּדָּהּ֙ מֵֽעָלֶ֔יהָ וַתֹּ֣אמֶר שְׁתֵ֔ה וְגַם־גְּמַלֶּ֖יךָ אַשְׁקֶ֑ה וָאֵ֕שְׁתְּ וְגַ֥ם הַגְּמַלִּ֖ים הִשְׁקָֽתָה׃
47
And I asked her, and said, Whose daughter are you? And she said, The daughter of Bethuel, Nachor's son, whom Milkah bore to him. And I put the nose-ring upon her nose, and the bracelets upon her hands.
And I asked her, and said, Whose daughter art thou? And she said, The daughter of Bethuel, Nahor`s son, whom Milcah bare unto him. And I put the ring upon her nose, and the bracelets upon her hands.
וָאֶשְׁאַ֣ל אֹתָ֗הּ וָאֹמַר֮ בַּת־מִ֣י אַתְּ֒ וַתֹּ֗אמֶר בַּת־בְּתוּאֵל֙ בֶּן־נָח֔וֹר אֲשֶׁ֥ר יָֽלְדָה־לּ֖וֹ מִלְכָּ֑ה וָאָשִׂ֤ם הַנֶּ֙זֶם֙ עַל־אַפָּ֔הּ וְהַצְּמִידִ֖ים עַל־יָדֶֽיהָ׃
48
And I bowed my head, and prostrated myself before YHWH, and blessed YHWH, the Elohim of my master Avraham, who had led me in the right way to take my master's brother's daughter for his son.
And I bowed my head, and worshipped Jehovah, and blessed Jehovah, the God of my master Abraham, who had led me in the right way to take my master`s brother`s daughter for his son.
וָאֶקֹּ֥ד וָֽאֶשְׁתַּחֲוֶ֖ה לַיהוָ֑ה וָאֲבָרֵ֗ךְ אֶת־יְהוָה֙ אֱלֹהֵי֙ אֲדֹנִ֣י אַבְרָהָ֔ם אֲשֶׁ֤ר הִנְחַ֙נִי֙ בְּדֶ֣רֶךְ אֱמֶ֔ת לָקַ֛חַת אֶת־בַּת־אֲחִ֥י אֲדֹנִ֖י לִבְנֽוֹ׃
49
And now if you will show chesed and truth to my master, tell me; and if not, tell me; that I may turn to the right hand, or to the left.
And now if ye will deal kindly and truly with my master, tell me. And if not, tell me. That I may turn to the right hand, or to the left.
וְ֠עַתָּה אִם־יֶשְׁכֶ֨ם עֹשִׂ֜ים חֶ֧סֶד וֶֽאֱמֶ֛ת אֶת־אֲדֹנִ֖י הַגִּ֣ידוּ לִ֑י וְאִם־לֹ֕א הַגִּ֣ידוּ לִ֔י וְאֶפְנֶ֥ה עַל־יָמִ֖ין א֥וֹ עַל־שְׂמֹֽאל׃
50
Then Lavan and Bethuel answered and said, The matter has come forth from YHWH; we cannot speak to you bad or good.
Then Laban and Bethuel answered and said, The thing proceedeth from Jehovah. We cannot speak unto thee bad or good.
וַיַּ֨עַן לָבָ֤ן וּבְתוּאֵל֙ וַיֹּ֣אמְר֔וּ מֵיְהוָ֖ה יָצָ֣א הַדָּבָ֑ר לֹ֥א נוּכַ֛ל דַּבֵּ֥ר אֵלֶ֖יךָ רַ֥ע אוֹ־טֽוֹב׃
Translation Commentary Verse 50

Me-YHWH yatza ha-davar — the matter has come forth from YHWH — is a divine-providence-acknowledgement formula. The family's role is reduced from active-choosing to ratification of an already-decreed outcome. The closest constructional parallel is Isa 45:23 (bi nishbati yatza mi-pi tzedakah davar v-lo yashuv — by myself I have sworn; the word has gone forth from my mouth in righteousness and shall not return), where the verb yatza + davar governs a divine speech-act. The HB pattern of non-Israelite-or-wavering-figure acknowledges YHWH's sovereignty over an outcome they cannot resist recurs at Gen 31:24 (Lavan's later dream-warning), Gen 41:38-39 (Pharaoh to Yosef), Num 23-24 (Balaam), Josh 2:9-11 (Rachav), 1 Sam 24:17-20 (Saul), Dan 2-6 (Nebuchadnezzar and Darius). Lavan and Bethuel's response is the patriarchal inaugural instance.

The Lavan-before-Bethuel order is editorially significant. Sarna (JPS p. 167) reads it as evidence of Lavan's primacy in the household-economy, comparing the Nuzi marriage-contracts (HSS V, JEN, AASOR 16) in which the bride's brother appears as the agnate-protector and the principal-agent in the marriage negotiation while the father plays a secondary role. The Nuzi parallels were extensively developed in mid-twentieth-century scholarship (Speiser 1955; Gordon 1937) and have been critically reviewed since (Greengus 1975 JAOS 95; Eichler 1989 JANES 18); the brother-as-agnate-protector reading remains defensible. An alternative critical reading (Westermann p. 387) treats the u-Bethuel as a textual interpolation, the v 50 response originally Lavan-only — Bethuel disappears from the chapter after this verse (vv 53, 55 name Lavan and the mother). The closing merism lo nukhal daber elekha ra o-tov — we cannot speak to you bad or good — is Lavan's idiom, recurring at Gen 31:24, 29 and at Num 24:13.

51
Behold, Rivkah is before you; take her, and go, and let her be your master's son's wife, as YHWH has spoken.
Behold, Rebekah is before thee, take her, and go, and let her be thy master`s son`s wife, as Jehovah hath spoken.
הִנֵּֽה־רִבְקָ֥ה לְפָנֶ֖יךָ קַ֣ח וָלֵ֑ךְ וּתְהִ֤י אִשָּׁה֙ לְבֶן־אֲדֹנֶ֔יךָ כַּאֲשֶׁ֖ר דִּבֶּ֥ר יְהוָֽה׃
52
And it came to pass, when Avraham's servant heard their words, that he prostrated himself to the ground before YHWH.
And it came to pass, that, when Abraham`s servant heard their words, he bowed himself down to the earth unto Jehovah.
וַיְהִ֕י כַּאֲשֶׁ֥ר שָׁמַ֛ע עֶ֥בֶד אַבְרָהָ֖ם אֶת־דִּבְרֵיהֶ֑ם וַיִּשְׁתַּ֥חוּ אַ֖רְצָה לַֽיהוָֽה׃
53
And the servant brought out vessels of silver, and vessels of gold, and garments, and gave them to Rivkah; he also gave precious gifts to her brother and to her mother.
And the servant brought forth jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment, and gave them to Rebekah. He gave also to her brother and to her mother precious things.
וַיּוֹצֵ֨א הָעֶ֜בֶד כְּלֵי־כֶ֨סֶף וּכְלֵ֤י זָהָב֙ וּבְגָדִ֔ים וַיִּתֵּ֖ן לְרִבְקָ֑ה וּמִ֨גְדָּנֹ֔ת נָתַ֥ן לְאָחִ֖יהָ וּלְאִמָּֽהּ׃
54
And they ate and drank, he and the men who were with him, and lodged there. And they rose up in the morning, and he said, Send me away to my master.
And they did eat and drink, he and the men that were with him, and tarried all night. And they rose up in the morning, and he said, Send me away unto my master.
וַיֹּאכְל֣וּ וַיִּשְׁתּ֗וּ ה֛וּא וְהָאֲנָשִׁ֥ים אֲשֶׁר־עִמּ֖וֹ וַיָּלִ֑ינוּ וַיָּק֣וּמוּ בַבֹּ֔קֶר וַיֹּ֖אמֶר שַׁלְּחֻ֥נִי לַֽאדֹנִֽי׃
55
And her brother and her mother said, Let the young woman stay with us a few days, at least ten; after that she shall go.
And her brother and her mother said, Let the damsel abide with us [a few] days, at the least ten. After that she shall go.
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אָחִ֙יהָ֙ וְאִמָּ֔הּ תֵּשֵׁ֨ב הַנַּעֲרָ֥ אִתָּ֛נוּ יָמִ֖ים א֣וֹ עָשׂ֑וֹר אַחַ֖ר תֵּלֵֽךְ׃
56
And he said to them, Do not delay me, since YHWH has prospered my way; send me away that I may go to my master.
And he said unto them, Hinder me not, seeing Jehovah hath prospered my way. Send me away that I may go to my master.
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֲלֵהֶם֙ אַל־תְּאַחֲר֣וּ אֹתִ֔י וַֽיהוָ֖ה הִצְלִ֣יחַ דַּרְכִּ֑י שַׁלְּח֕וּנִי וְאֵלְכָ֖ה לַֽאדֹנִֽי׃
57
And they said, We will call the young woman, and ask her own mouth.
And they said, We will call the damsel, and inquire at her mouth.
וַיֹּאמְר֖וּ נִקְרָ֣א לַֽנַּעֲרָ֑ וְנִשְׁאֲלָ֖ה אֶת־פִּֽיהָ׃
58
And they called Rivkah, and said to her, Will you go with this man? And she said, I will go.
And they called Rebekah, and said unto her, Wilt thou go with this man? And she said, I will go.
וַיִּקְרְא֤וּ לְרִבְקָה֙ וַיֹּאמְר֣וּ אֵלֶ֔יהָ הֲתֵלְכִ֖י עִם־הָאִ֣ישׁ הַזֶּ֑ה וַתֹּ֖אמֶר אֵלֵֽךְ׃
59
And they sent away Rivkah their sister, and her nurse, and Avraham's servant, and his men.
And they sent away Rebekah their sister, and her nurse, and Abraham`s servant, and his men.
וַֽיְשַׁלְּח֛וּ אֶת־רִבְקָ֥ה אֲחֹתָ֖ם וְאֶת־מֵנִקְתָּ֑הּ וְאֶת־עֶ֥בֶד אַבְרָהָ֖ם וְאֶת־אֲנָשָֽׁיו׃
60
And they blessed Rivkah, and said to her, Our sister, may you become thousands of ten thousands, and may your seed possess the gate of those who hate them.
And they blessed Rebekah, and said unto her, Our sister, be thou [the mother] of thousands of ten thousands, and let thy seed possess the gate of those that hate them.
וַיְבָרֲכ֤וּ אֶת־רִבְקָה֙ וַיֹּ֣אמְרוּ לָ֔הּ אֲחֹתֵ֕נוּ אַ֥תְּ הֲיִ֖י לְאַלְפֵ֣י רְבָבָ֑ה וְיִירַ֣שׁ זַרְעֵ֔ךְ אֵ֖ת שַׁ֥עַר שֹׂנְאָֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 60

The matriarchal-fertility blessing-formula — achotenu at hayi l-alfei revavah v-yiyrash zar'ekh et sha'ar son'av — and the chapter's most editorially-deliberate cross-reference to Gen 22:17. The second half of the verse (v-yiyrash zar'ekh et sha'ar son'av) verbally echoes Avraham's blessing at the aqedah: v-yiyrash zar'akha et sha'ar oyvav — may your seed possess the gate of his enemies — with the pronoun shifted from masculine (Avraham's seed) to feminine (Rivkah's seed). The verbal echo establishes a structural inclusio across the Abraham cycle: the bride enters the patriarchal family under a blessing that mirrors the patriarch's own foundational blessing.

The formula is the canonical matriarchal counterpart to the patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad (dust at Gen 13:16, stars at 15:5, sand at 22:17; see patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad). The masculine-patriarchal formulae use cosmographic-natural similes (dust, stars, sand); the matriarchal formula uses a numerical-arithmetic expression (alfei revavah — thousands of ten-thousands, equivalent to myriads of myriads / millions). The lexical distinction is editorially meaningful but the theological function is parallel. Trible (God and the Rhetoric of Sexuality 1978 pp. 27-31) reads the distinction as part of the HB's broader gendered-blessing rhetoric: masculine-patriarchal blessings frame in terms of cosmographic-spatial extension; matriarchal blessings frame in terms of numerical-generational descent.

The formula's matriarchal-canonical status is confirmed at Ruth 4:11-12 — the marriage of Boaz and Ruth — where the elders and people at the gate of Bethlehem bless Ruth with the explicit invocation of the matriarchs (k-Rachel u-k-Le'ah asher banu sh'tehem et-bet Yisrael) and extend the formula to Tamar (v-yhi vetkha k-veit-Peretz asher yaledah Tamar l-Yehudah), completing the four-matriarch + Tamar tableau that underwrites the Davidic genealogy. The Ruth recurrence is the canonical confirmation of the matriarchal-blessing as a HB literary form.

61
And Rivkah arose with her maidens, and they rode upon the camels, and followed the man; and the servant took Rivkah, and went his way.
And Rebekah arose, and her damsels, and they rode upon the camels, and followed the man. And the servant took Rebekah, and went his way.
וַתָּ֨קָם רִבְקָ֜ה וְנַעֲרֹתֶ֗יהָ וַתִּרְכַּ֙בְנָה֙ עַל־הַגְּמַלִּ֔ים וַתֵּלַ֖כְנָה אַחֲרֵ֣י הָאִ֑ישׁ וַיִּקַּ֥ח הָעֶ֛בֶד אֶת־רִבְקָ֖ה וַיֵּלַֽךְ׃
62
And Yitzchaq had come from the way of Be'er Lachai Ro'i; for he dwelt in the land of the Negev.
And Isaac came from the way of Beer-lahai-roi. For he dwelt in the land of the South.
וְיִצְחָק֙ בָּ֣א מִבּ֔וֹא בְּאֵ֥ר לַחַ֖י רֹאִ֑י וְה֥וּא יוֹשֵׁ֖ב בְּאֶ֥רֶץ הַנֶּֽגֶב׃
63
And Yitzchaq went out to meditate in the field at the turning of evening; and he lifted up his eyes, and saw, and behold, there were camels coming.
And Isaac went out to meditate in the field at the eventide. And he lifted up his eyes, and saw, and, behold, there were camels coming.
וַיֵּצֵ֥א יִצְחָ֛ק לָשׂ֥וּחַ בַּשָּׂדֶ֖ה לִפְנ֣וֹת עָ֑רֶב וַיִּשָּׂ֤א עֵינָיו֙ וַיַּ֔רְא וְהִנֵּ֥ה גְמַלִּ֖ים בָּאִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 63

La-suach is a HB hapax legomenon (qal infinitive of an uncertain root s-w-ch or s-y-ch) and one of the most-discussed lexical cruxes in the chapter. Six readings circulate across the ancient versions, the rabbinic tradition, and modern philology:

  1. 'to meditate' — Vulgate ad meditandum, ASV/KJV/ESV; the siach (meditation) cognate. Default modern-English-traditional.
  2. 'to converse / chatter / occupy oneself in thought' — LXX adoleschēsai; the siach (speech, talk) cognate.
  3. 'to pray' — Targum Onkelos (l-tzala'ah), Rashi. The rabbinic-canonical reading: b. Berakhot 26b reads the verse as the institution of the afternoon Minchah prayer-service — Yitzchaq tiken tefillat minchah — paired with Avraham instituting Shacharit (from Gen 19:27) and Ya'aqov instituting Ma'ariv (from Gen 28:11). The three-times-daily prayer-cycle's patriarchal-etiology rests on this verse.
  4. 'to commune / converse' — Speiser AB Genesis, some modern English.
  5. 'to complain / lament' — minority modern philological, connecting suach to siach in its lament-register (Ps 55:3, 64:2). Would frame Yitzchaq's evening-activity as continued mourning for Sarah (cf. v 67 va-yinacheim Yitzchaq acharei imo).
  6. 'to wander / walk about' — NRSV; modern philological treatment relating suach to sh-w-ch (with Akkadian and Aramaic cognates).

The WoH translation renders la-suach as to meditate — the ASV/Vulgate default, preserving the contemplative register closest to the siach-cluster semantic field, neutral on the meditate-vs.-pray-vs.-wander question. The rabbinic-canonical Minchah-institution reading is not preempted: the choice of meditate is editorially-conservative on the hapax, and the apparatus carries the six-reading documentation. The verse pairs with the sa-eynekha lift-your-eyes formula at the same verse (va-yisa eynav va-yar — he lifted up his eyes and saw) — the formula recurs at v 64 in Rivkah's parallel-action.

64
And Rivkah lifted up her eyes, and when she saw Yitzchaq, she alighted from the camel.
And Rebekah lifted up her eyes, and when she saw Isaac, she alighted from the camel.
וַתִּשָּׂ֤א רִבְקָה֙ אֶת־עֵינֶ֔יהָ וַתֵּ֖רֶא אֶת־יִצְחָ֑ק וַתִּפֹּ֖ל מֵעַ֥ל הַגָּמָֽל׃
65
And she said to the servant, Who is that man walking in the field to meet us? And the servant said, It is my master. And she took her veil, and covered herself.
And she said unto the servant, What man is this that walketh in the field to meet us? And the servant said, It is my master. And she took her veil, and covered herself.
וַתֹּ֣אמֶר אֶל־הָעֶ֗בֶד מִֽי־הָאִ֤ישׁ הַלָּזֶה֙ הַהֹלֵ֤ךְ בַּשָּׂדֶה֙ לִקְרָאתֵ֔נוּ וַיֹּ֥אמֶר הָעֶ֖בֶד ה֣וּא אֲדֹנִ֑י וַתִּקַּ֥ח הַצָּעִ֖יף וַתִּתְכָּֽס׃
66
And the servant told Yitzchaq all the things that he had done.
And the servant told Isaac all the things that he had done.
וַיְסַפֵּ֥ר הָעֶ֖בֶד לְיִצְחָ֑ק אֵ֥ת כָּל־הַדְּבָרִ֖ים אֲשֶׁ֥ר עָשָֽׂה׃
67
And Yitzchaq brought her into the tent of Sarah his mother, and took Rivkah, and she became his wife; and he loved her. And Yitzchaq was comforted after his mother.
And Isaac brought her into his mother Sarah`s tent, and took Rebekah, and she became his wife. And he loved her. And Isaac was comforted after his mother`s death.
וַיְבִאֶ֣הָ יִצְחָ֗ק הָאֹ֙הֱלָה֙ שָׂרָ֣ה אִמּ֔וֹ וַיִּקַּ֧ח אֶת־רִבְקָ֛ה וַתְּהִי־ל֥וֹ לְאִשָּׁ֖ה וַיֶּאֱהָבֶ֑הָ וַיִּנָּחֵ֥ם יִצְחָ֖ק אַחֲרֵ֥י אִמּֽוֹ׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 67

The Sarah-to-Rivkah generational-transition formula closes the chapter and the Abraham cycle's marriage-and-burial sequence. Sarah's death at Gen 23 sets up Rivkah's arrival at Gen 24:67: she enters the tent of Sarah his mother (ha-ohelah Sarah imo), the literal-locational succession from one matriarch to the next. The nifal va-yinacheim (was comforted) — from n-ch-m — is the HB's standard consolation-formula, recurring at major mourning-moments: Gen 37:35 (Ya'aqov refusing to be comforted for Yosef); 2 Sam 13:39 (David comforted for Amnon); Isa 40:1 (nachamu nachamu ami — the inaugural verse of Second Isaiah).

Avraham himself does not appear as an actor in the rest of the cycle: Gen 25:1-11 narrates his second marriage (to Keturah) and his death in summary. The chapter thus closes the Abraham cycle proper. The structural triptych of the cycle's closing — the aqedah at Gen 22, the death of Sarah at Gen 23, the bride-quest at Gen 24 — pairs the binding of the seed-child, the burial of the matriarch, and the arrival of the next matriarch as three movements of a single closing sequence.

Chapter 25

Genesis 25 — The death of Avraham, the Yishma'elite princes, and the birth and birthright of Ya'aqov and Esav

1
And Avraham took another wife, and her name was Keturah.
And Abraham took another wife, and her name was Keturah.
וַיֹּ֧סֶף אַבְרָהָ֛ם וַיִּקַּ֥ח אִשָּׁ֖ה וּשְׁמָ֥הּ קְטוּרָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

Vv 1-4 narrate Avraham's third domestic configuration: Keturah and her six sons, and the seven grandsons through Yokshan and Midyan. The Keturah-line is one of the HB's three collateral-Avrahamic-genealogies — paired with the Yishma'elite twelve (vv 12-18) and the Nahor twelve (Gen 22:20-24) — and assigns each to a tribal-political register peripheral to but related to the covenantal Yitzchaq-line.

The Keturah-line's tribal-onomastic content overlaps substantially with North-Arabian and Trans-Jordanian peoples attested elsewhere in the HB and in the ANE record. Midyan is the eponym of the Midianites — the wilderness-confederation attested at Exod 2:15-21 (Moses' Midianite father-in-law Jethro / Reuel), Num 22-24 (the Balaam-narrative), Num 25 (the Baal-Peor incident), and Judg 6-8 (the Gideon cycle); Knauf 1988 Midian and Mendenhall 1992 reconstruct the historical Midianite-confederation as an Iron-Age North-Arabian camel-pastoralist league. Sheva and Dedan recur at Gen 10:7 (via Cush — a different genealogical lineage, suggesting the names attach to peoples rather than to single ancestors), Isa 21:13 (the Dedanite caravans), Jer 25:23, Ezek 25:13 and 27:15 (Dedan in the Tyre lament), Ezek 38:13 (Sheva and Dedan among the Gog opponents); the toponyms map onto historical North-Arabian oasis-confederations attested in the Neo-Assyrian and Neo-Babylonian record. Ephah is attested in Isa 60:6 (with Midyan, in the caravan-imagery) and in the Tiglath-Pileser III annals (Ḫa-a-a-pa-a in the 8th-century North-Arabian campaign records). Knauf 1988 treats the Keturah-line as preserving real Late-Iron-Age North-Arabian tribal data, not ad hoc fiction.

The Keturah-narrative's editorial-function. The Keturah-genealogy at vv 1-4, paired with the inheritance-distribution at vv 5-6 (Yitzchaq receives all that he had; the concubine-sons receive gifts and are sent away eastward, to the east country), structurally closes the Avrahamic peripheral-line before the chapter pivots to the central Yitzchaq-line at v 19. The vv 5-6 inheritance-distribution functions in the same juridical register as the Gen 21:8-21 expulsion of Hagar and Yishma'el: the central covenantal line is reserved for Yitzchaq; the peripheral lines receive blessing-but-not-covenant.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 173-174; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 396-397; Knauf 1988 Midian; Mendenhall 1992 ABD IV: 'Midian'.

2
And she bore him Zimran, and Yokshan, and Medan, and Midyan, and Yishbak, and Shuach.
And she bare him Zimran, and Jokshan, and Medan, and Midian, and Ishbak, and Shuah.
וַתֵּ֣לֶד ל֗וֹ אֶת־זִמְרָן֙ וְאֶת־יָקְשָׁ֔ן וְאֶת־מְדָ֖ן וְאֶת־מִדְיָ֑ן וְאֶת־יִשְׁבָּ֖ק וְאֶת־שֽׁוּחַ׃
3
And Yokshan begot Sheva and Dedan. And the sons of Dedan were Ashurim and Letushim and Le'ummim.
And Jokshan begat Sheba, and Dedan. And the sons of Dedan were Asshurim, and Letushim, and Leummim.
וְיָקְשָׁ֣ן יָלַ֔ד אֶת־שְׁבָ֖א וְאֶת־דְּדָ֑ן וּבְנֵ֣י דְדָ֔ן הָי֛וּ אַשּׁוּרִ֥ם וּלְטוּשִׁ֖ים וּלְאֻמִּֽים׃
4
And the sons of Midyan: Ephah, and Epher, and Chanokh, and Avida, and Elda'ah. All these were the sons of Keturah.
And the sons of Midian: Ephah, and Epher, and Hanoch, and Abida, and Eldaah. All these were the children of Keturah.
וּבְנֵ֣י מִדְיָ֗ן עֵיפָ֤ה וָעֵ֙פֶר֙ וַחֲנֹ֔ךְ וַאֲבִידָ֖ע וְאֶלְדָּעָ֑ה כָּל־אֵ֖לֶּה בְּנֵ֥י קְטוּרָֽה׃
5
And Avraham gave all that he had to Yitzchaq.
And Abraham gave all that he had unto Isaac.
וַיִּתֵּ֧ן אַבְרָהָ֛ם אֶת־כָּל־אֲשֶׁר־ל֖וֹ לְיִצְחָֽק׃
6
But to the sons of the concubines that Avraham had, Avraham gave gifts; and he sent them away from Yitzchaq his son, while he yet lived, eastward, to the east country.
But unto the sons of the concubines, that Abraham had, Abraham gave gifts. And he sent them away from Isaac his son, while he yet lived, eastward, unto the east country.
וְלִבְנֵ֤י הַפִּֽילַגְשִׁים֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר לְאַבְרָהָ֔ם נָתַ֥ן אַבְרָהָ֖ם מַתָּנֹ֑ת וַֽיְשַׁלְּחֵ֞ם מֵעַ֨ל יִצְחָ֤ק בְּנוֹ֙ בְּעוֹדֶ֣נּוּ חַ֔י קֵ֖דְמָה אֶל־אֶ֥רֶץ קֶֽדֶם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

Vv 5-6 stage the inheritance-distribution: Yitzchaq receives kol-asher lo (all that he had); the bnei ha-pilagshim (sons of the concubines) receive mattanot (gifts) and are sent qedmah el-eretz qedem (eastward to the east country). The juridical pattern is identical to the Gen 21:8-21 expulsion of Hagar and Yishma'el: the covenantal line passes intact to the chosen son; the peripheral sons receive a partial-share and are geographically relocated outside the covenantal land.

The eastward-relocation is structurally significant. The eretz qedem (east country) is the HB's tropic of foreign-or-peripheral residence — Cain at Gen 4:16 vayyeshev b-eretz Nod qidmat-Eden (the land of Nod east of Eden); the Babel migrants at Gen 11:2 b-nasam mi-qedem (in their journey from the east); Lot's descendants at Gen 19:30-38 (the Ammonites and Moabites east of the Dead Sea); the Yishma'elites at Gen 25:18 (from Chavilah to Shur ... toward Ashur). The covenant-line dwells in the central land; the peripheral lines settle east. The pattern is editorially-deliberate.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 173-174; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 397-398.

7
And these are the days of the years of Avraham's life which he lived, a hundred and seventy-five years.
And these are the days of the years of Abraham`s life which he lived, a hundred threescore and fifteen years.
וְאֵ֗לֶּה יְמֵ֛י שְׁנֵֽי־חַיֵּ֥י אַבְרָהָ֖ם אֲשֶׁר־חָ֑י מְאַ֥ת שָׁנָ֛ה וְשִׁבְעִ֥ים שָׁנָ֖ה וְחָמֵ֥שׁ שָׁנִֽים׃
8
And Avraham expired, and died in a good old age, an old man, and full of years, and was gathered to his people.
And Abraham gave up the ghost, and died in a good old age, an old man, and full [of years], and was gathered to his people.
וַיִּגְוַ֨ע וַיָּ֧מָת אַבְרָהָ֛ם בְּשֵׂיבָ֥ה טוֹבָ֖ה זָקֵ֣ן וְשָׂבֵ֑עַ וַיֵּאָ֖סֶף אֶל־עַמָּֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

The patriarchal-good-death formula. The chapter's death-notice for Avraham — va-yamot Avraham b-seyvah tovah, zaqen v-savea, va-ye'asef el-amav (and Avraham died in a good old age, an old man and satisfied, and was gathered to his people) — deploys the HB's canonical zaqen u-s'va yamim formula in its short-form variant zaqen v-savea (old and satisfied, without the explicit yamim / days). The lexical-family is treated in the zaqen-u-s-va-yamim glossary entry, which lists Gen 25:8 in its appliesTo (set at v2.8.0) and treats the variant as part of the formula's reach.

The formula's structural function is to mark the figure as recipient of completed divine favor — the patriarchal mode of departure, death at the natural end of a long full life, in contrast to violent or premature death. Cross-corpus: Avraham (here), Yitzchaq (Gen 35:29), David (1 Chr 29:28), Jehoiada the priest (2 Chr 24:15), Job (42:17). The Avraham-instance is the canonical-first occurrence in the patriarchal narratives.

Two additional editorial-notes. The verb va-ye'asef el-amav (and was gathered to his people) is the canonical HB death-formula for familial-ancestral reunion — Avraham is gathered to his pre-Israelite ancestors (his father Terach died in Haran at Gen 11:32, but the broader amav includes the pre-Sumerian patriarchal-line). The formula is repeated for Yishma'el at v 17 — a structural pairing-by-formula that emphasizes the Avrahamic-paternity of both sons. The implicit pre-Sheol theology of the gathered-to-his-people formula is a pre-eschatological-conception of post-mortem familial-clan-reunion, not the developed Sheol-or-resurrection theology of later HB literature.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 174; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 p. 398; Alter Five Books of Moses 2004 p. 128.

9
And Yitzchaq and Yishma'el his sons buried him in the cave of Makhpelah, in the field of Efron the son of Tzochar the Chitti, which is before Mamre,
And Isaac and Ishmael his sons buried him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron the son of Zohar the Hittite, which is before Mamre.
וַיִּקְבְּר֨וּ אֹת֜וֹ יִצְחָ֤ק וְיִשְׁמָעֵאל֙ בָּנָ֔יו אֶל־מְעָרַ֖ת הַמַּכְפֵּלָ֑ה אֶל־שְׂדֵ֞ה עֶפְרֹ֤ן בֶּן־צֹ֙חַר֙ הַֽחִתִּ֔י אֲשֶׁ֖ר עַל־פְּנֵ֥י מַמְרֵֽא׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

Yitzchaq and Yishma'el bury Avraham together at Makhpelah — the only HB scene of the two brothers acting in concert after the Gen 21 expulsion. The reunion-at-burial is editorially-deliberate and is the structural-completion of the Avraham-cycle. The pre-expulsion Gen 16 conception of Yishma'el and the post-expulsion Gen 21 separation are resolved at the patriarchal-burial: the two sons stand together at the cave of Makhpelah where Sarah herself was buried (Gen 23:19), and where Avraham now joins her.

The naming-order Yitzchaq v-Yishma'el banav (Yitzchaq and Yishma'el his sons) places Yitzchaq first — chronologically the younger son but the covenantal-firstborn. The order is editorial: the covenantal-firstborn-priority is preserved in the burial-order, but the joint-action is preserved in the conjunction.

The reception traditions extend the reunion: Genesis Rabbah 38:12 reads the reunion as Yishma'el's teshuvah (repentance) — Yishma'el yields precedence to Yitzchaq at the burial as an act of acknowledgment of the covenantal hierarchy; Pirqei de-Rabbi Eliezer 30 similarly reads the reunion as a reconciliation-moment. The reunion-at-burial pattern recurs structurally at Gen 35:29 (Esav and Ya'aqov burying Yitzchaq together) and at Gen 50:13 (the twelve sons burying Ya'aqov), forming a HB-internal sub-theme of fraternal-reunion-at-patriarchal-burial.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 173; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 397-398; Genesis Rabbah 38:12; Pirqei de-Rabbi Eliezer 30.

10
the field which Avraham purchased from the sons of Chet. There was Avraham buried, and Sarah his wife.
The field which Abraham purchased of the children of Heth. There was Abraham buried, and Sarah his wife.
הַשָּׂדֶ֛ה אֲשֶׁר־קָנָ֥ה אַבְרָהָ֖ם מֵאֵ֣ת בְּנֵי־חֵ֑ת שָׁ֛מָּה קֻבַּ֥ר אַבְרָהָ֖ם וְשָׂרָ֥ה אִשְׁתּֽוֹ׃
11
And it came to pass after the death of Avraham, that the Elohim blessed Yitzchaq his son. And Yitzchaq dwelt by Be'er Lachai Ro'i.
And it came to pass after the death of Abraham, that God blessed Isaac his son. And Isaac dwelt by Beer-lahai-roi.
וַיְהִ֗י אַחֲרֵי֙ מ֣וֹת אַבְרָהָ֔ם וַיְבָ֥רֶךְ אֱלֹהִ֖ים אֶת־יִצְחָ֣ק בְּנ֑וֹ וַיֵּ֣שֶׁב יִצְחָ֔ק עִם־בְּאֵ֥ר לַחַ֖י רֹאִֽי׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 11

The verse closes the Avraham-cycle with the Elohim's blessing of Yitzchaq and Yitzchaq's settlement at Be'er Lachai Ro'i. The well-name is treated in the beer-lachai-roi glossary entry, which lists Gen 25:11 in its appliesTo (set at v2.13.0). The recurrence of the well at 25:11 is editorially-deliberate: Yitzchaq inherits not only the seed-promise but the residence-site of Hagar's foundational divine-encounter at Gen 16:13-14. The well of the El Ro'i (the God who sees Hagar) becomes Yitzchaq's dwelling-place after his father's death — a structural-thematic coupling of the Hagar-narrative and the Yitzchaq-narrative that the editor leaves implicit rather than commenting on.

The construction va-yeshev Yitzchaq im Be'er Lachai Ro'i uses the preposition im (with, alongside) rather than the more common b- (at, in) for residence-locale. The im-construction is unusual: residences are typically located at / in a place, not with a place. The unusual idiom is sometimes read (Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 174) as suggesting a more-than-locational relationship — Yitzchaq dwells in association with the well, treating the cultic-site as a fixed-point of his residence-pattern rather than as a transit-feature. The reading is plausible but not lexically-required.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 174; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 p. 398.

12
Now these are the generations of Yishma'el, Avraham's son, whom Hagar the Mitsri'it, Sarah's handmaid, bore to Avraham.
Now these are the generations of Ishmael, Abraham`s son, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah`s handmaid, bare unto Abraham.
וְאֵ֛לֶּה תֹּלְדֹ֥ת יִשְׁמָעֵ֖אל בֶּן־אַבְרָהָ֑ם אֲשֶׁ֨ר יָלְדָ֜ה הָגָ֧ר הַמִּצְרִ֛ית שִׁפְחַ֥ת שָׂרָ֖ה לְאַבְרָהָֽם׃
13
And these are the names of the sons of Yishma'el, by their names, according to their generations: the firstborn of Yishma'el, Nevayot; and Qedar, and Adbe'el, and Mivsam,
And these are the names of the sons of Ishmael, by their names, according to their generations: the first-born of Ishmael, Nebaioth, and Kedar, and Adbeel, and Mibsam,
וְאֵ֗לֶּה שְׁמוֹת֙ בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׁמָעֵ֔אל בִּשְׁמֹתָ֖ם לְתוֹלְדֹתָ֑ם בְּכֹ֤ר יִשְׁמָעֵאל֙ נְבָיֹ֔ת וְקֵדָ֥ר וְאַדְבְּאֵ֖ל וּמִבְשָֽׂם׃
14
and Mishma, and Dumah, and Massa,
and Mishma, and Dumah, and Massa,
וּמִשְׁמָ֥ע וְדוּמָ֖ה וּמַשָּֽׂא׃
15
Hadad, and Tema, Yetur, Nafish, and Qedmah.
Hadad, and Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.
חֲדַ֣ד וְתֵימָ֔א יְט֥וּר נָפִ֖ישׁ וָקֵֽדְמָה׃
16
These are the sons of Yishma'el, and these are their names, by their villages, and by their encampments; twelve princes according to their nations.
These are the sons of Ishmael, and these are their names, by their villages, and by their encampments. Twelve princes according to their nations.
אֵ֣לֶּה הֵ֞ם בְּנֵ֤י יִשְׁמָעֵאל֙ וְאֵ֣לֶּה שְׁמֹתָ֔ם בְּחַצְרֵיהֶ֖ם וּבְטִֽירֹתָ֑ם שְׁנֵים־עָשָׂ֥ר נְשִׂיאִ֖ם לְאֻמֹּתָֽם׃
17
And these are the years of the life of Yishma'el, a hundred and thirty-seven years; and he expired, and died, and was gathered to his people.
And these are the years of the life of Ishmael, a hundred and thirty and seven years. And he gave up the ghost and died, and was gathered unto his people.
וְאֵ֗לֶּה שְׁנֵי֙ חַיֵּ֣י יִשְׁמָעֵ֔אל מְאַ֥ת שָׁנָ֛ה וּשְׁלֹשִׁ֥ים שָׁנָ֖ה וְשֶׁ֣בַע שָׁנִ֑ים וַיִּגְוַ֣ע וַיָּ֔מָת וַיֵּאָ֖סֶף אֶל־עַמָּֽיו׃
18
And they dwelt from Chavilah to Shur, which is before Mitsrayim, as you go toward Ashur. He settled over against all his brethren.
And they dwelt from Havilah unto Shur that is before Egypt, as thou goest toward Assyria. He abode over against all his brethren.
וַיִּשְׁכְּנ֨וּ מֵֽחֲוִילָ֜ה עַד־שׁ֗וּר אֲשֶׁר֙ עַל־פְּנֵ֣י מִצְרַ֔יִם בֹּאֲכָ֖ה אַשּׁ֑וּרָה עַל־פְּנֵ֥י כָל־אֶחָ֖יו נָפָֽל׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 18

The verse closes the Yishma'elite-toledot by recalling the verb-frame of the Gen 16:12 pere adam prophecy. The two verses use a parallel idiom — al-pnei kol echav (over against all his brothers) — with different verbs: yishkon (shall dwell) at Gen 16:12; nafal (fell / settled / encamped) at Gen 25:18. The cross-reference is editorially-deliberate and forms a Yishma'elite-narrative inclusio: the verb-frame of the naming-prophecy is recalled at the closing-formula of the Yishma'elite-genealogy. The pere-adam-wild-ass-prophecy glossary entry (set at v2.13.0) lists both Gen 16:12 and Gen 25:18 in its appliesTo and treats the parallel as the foundational geographical-cultic placement of the Yishma'elite confederation east-and-south of the patriarchal-Israelite land.

The verb nafal (qal pf 3ms — fell / settled / encamped) is grammatically ambiguous: (a) ASV/KJV settled / fell (the ambiguity preserved); (b) NJPS settled (neutral residence-sense, parallel to Gen 16:12 yishkon); (c) some critical readings fell-apart-from or fell-as-by-the-sword (the standard fall register; the negative-encamping sense at Jer 7:20, Judg 7:12). The ASV's settled over against preserves the ambiguity. Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 175-176 reads the verb neutrally as settled; Alter Five Books of Moses 2004 pp. 128-129 reads the verb as encamped with the negative-encamping overtone; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 p. 399 reads the verse as paralleling Gen 16:12 with verb-variation. The translation preserves the ASV-baseline ambiguity.

The geographical placement — me-Chavilah ad-Shur asher al-pnei Mitsrayim bo'akhah Ashurah — locates the Yishma'elites along an arc from Chavilah (probably in the eastern Arabian peninsula) through Shur (in the northern Sinai) toward Ashur (the Neo-Assyrian-period geographical-pole). The placement matches the historical North-Arabian tribal-confederation reconstructed by Knauf 1989 Ismael; the Tiglath-Pileser III and Ashurbanipal annals attest the Yishma'elite confederation across precisely this arc in the 8th-7th centuries BCE.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 175-176; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 p. 399; Knauf 1989 Ismael; Eph'al 1982 The Ancient Arabs.

19
And these are the generations of Yitzchaq, Avraham's son: Avraham begot Yitzchaq.
And these are the generations of Isaac, Abraham`s son. Abraham begat Isaac.
וְאֵ֛לֶּה תּוֹלְדֹ֥ת יִצְחָ֖ק בֶּן־אַבְרָהָ֑ם אַבְרָהָ֖ם הוֹלִ֥יד אֶת־יִצְחָֽק׃
20
And Yitzchaq was forty years old when he took Rivkah, the daughter of Bethuel the Arammi of Paddan-Aram, the sister of Lavan the Arammi, to be his wife.
And Isaac was forty years old when he took Rebekah, the daughter of Bethuel the Syrian of Paddan-aram, the sister of Laban the Syrian, to be his wife.
וַיְהִ֤י יִצְחָק֙ בֶּן־אַרְבָּעִ֣ים שָׁנָ֔ה בְּקַחְתּ֣וֹ אֶת־רִבְקָ֗ה בַּת־בְּתוּאֵל֙ הָֽאֲרַמִּ֔י מִפַּדַּ֖ן אֲרָ֑ם אֲח֛וֹת לָבָ֥ן הָאֲרַמִּ֖י ל֥וֹ לְאִשָּֽׁה׃
21
And Yitzchaq entreated YHWH for his wife, because she was barren; and YHWH was entreated of him, and Rivkah his wife conceived.
And Isaac entreated Jehovah for his wife, because she was barren. And Jehovah was entreated of him, and Rebekah his wife conceived.
וַיֶּעְתַּ֨ר יִצְחָ֤ק לַֽיהוָה֙ לְנֹ֣כַח אִשְׁתּ֔וֹ כִּ֥י עֲקָרָ֖ה הִ֑וא וַיֵּעָ֤תֶר לוֹ֙ יְהוָ֔ה וַתַּ֖הַר רִבְקָ֥ה אִשְׁתּֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

The atar-intercession formula. The verse stages a stylistically-deliberate qal-niphal symmetry on the prayer-root atar (עָתַר): Yitzchaq va-ye'tar (qal — entreated) YHWH, and YHWH va-ye'ater (niphal — was entreated, allowed himself to be entreated) him. The qal-and-niphal pairing on the same root in adjacent clauses is a HB stylistic marker for the prayer-and-answer pattern: petition uttered, petition received. The lexeme and its cross-corpus reach are treated in the atar-l-yhwh-intercession glossary entry (new at v2.23.0).

Three editorial-notes. (i) Gen 25:21 is the first HB occurrence of the atar prayer-root. The lexeme is distinct from the broader HB prayer-vocabulary — hithpallel (the formal-petition root, see the existing hithpallel-first-prayer-occurrence entry at Gen 20:7,17), darash (the oracle-consultation root, see the companion darash-yhwh-seeking-oracle entry, Gen 25:22 immediately following), qara b-shem YHWH (the cultic-invocation formula). The atar register is specifically intercessory: petition offered for someone else, often in afflictive-context. The Yitzchaq-Rivkah barrenness-petition is the canonical-first instance and seeds the lexeme's later reach into the Mosaic plague-intercessions (Exod 8-10), Manoah's barrenness-petition for the future Samson (Judg 13:8 — a deliberate structural parallel to the Gen 25 scene), and the Manasseh-repentance (2 Chr 33:13,19).

(ii) The l-nokhach ishto clause (לְנֹכַח אִשְׁתּוֹ) is grammatically ambiguous. L-nokhach (from nokhach — over against, opposite, facing) can be read either (a) prepositionally (on behalf of / for his wife, the standard intercessory-direction; ASV/KJV/NRSV read this way) or (b) spatially (facing his wife, suggesting Yitzchaq prayed in Rivkah's physical presence; Alter 2004 reads this way). Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 179 prefers the spatial-presence sense: the petition is offered in shared-physical-presence, the patriarchal-intercession not a private act but a conjugal-shared petition. The translation preserves the ASV for his wife with the spatial-presence reading flagged in apparatus.

(iii) The Yitzchaq-Rivkah barrenness-pattern recapitulates the Sarah-Avraham barrenness-pattern of Gen 11:30-21:7 and prefigures the Rachel-Ya'aqov barrenness-pattern of Gen 29:31-30:24. The three-generation matriarchal-barrenness sequence is structurally deliberate. The chronological-detail at vv 20 and 26 (Yitzchaq at forty at marriage; Yitzchaq at sixty at the twins' birth) frames the atar-prayer as a twenty-year intercession.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 178-179; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 413-414; Greenberg Biblical Prose Prayer 1983 pp. 7-8, 19-21; Wagner Beten und Klagen 1971.

22
And the children struggled together within her; and she said, If it be so, why do I live? And she went to inquire of YHWH.
And the children struggled together within her. And she said, If it be so, wherefore do I live? And she went to inquire of Jehovah.
וַיִּתְרֹֽצֲצ֤וּ הַבָּנִים֙ בְּקִרְבָּ֔הּ וַתֹּ֣אמֶר אִם־כֵּ֔ן לָ֥מָּה זֶּ֖ה אָנֹ֑כִי וַתֵּ֖לֶךְ לִדְרֹ֥שׁ אֶת־יְהוָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The first HB occurrence of darash YHWH. Rivkah, distressed by the intrauterine struggle of the twins, va-telekh li-drosh et-YHWH — and she went to inquire of YHWH. The verse is the lexical-foundational instance of the HB's oracle-consultation lexeme, the verb-and-construction that names the act of seeking divine guidance through an authorized channel (priest, prophet, oracle-site). The lexeme and its cross-corpus reach are treated in the darash-yhwh-seeking-oracle glossary entry (new at v2.23.0).

The darash YHWH lexical-tradition is corpus-spanning. Principal occurrences after Gen 25:22: Exod 18:15 (Moses' Mosaic-judicial-oracle role); 1 Sam 9:9 (the canonical lexical-historical note on the ro'eh/navi shift — for he who is now called a Prophet was formerly called a Seer when a man went to inquire of God); 1 Sam 28:6-7 (Saul before Endor, the Yahwistic-frustrated-and-medium-substituted scene); 1 Kgs 14:5 (the Ahijah-Jeroboam oracle); 1 Kgs 22:5-8 (the Jehoshaphat-Ahab Ramoth-Gilead inquiry and the Micaiah-court-prophet tension); 2 Kgs 1:2-16 (the Ahaziah-Baal-Zebub scene with Elijah's three-fold rebuke on consultation-channel-legitimacy); 2 Kgs 22:13-18 (Josiah's reform Huldah-consultation, the second-named-female-prophetic-consultation of the HB and the structural-parallel to Rivkah here); Jer 21:2, 37:7 (the Zedekiah-Jeremiah inquiries); Ezek 14:1-11, 20:1-3 (the elders-of-Israel inquiries during exile).

Where did Rivkah go to inquire? The MT is editorially-silent. The interpretive-distribution: (a) the rabbinic-canonical reading (Targum Pseudo-Jonathan, Genesis Rabbah 63:6, Rashi) locates the consultation at the yeshivah of Shem (the patriarchal-pre-Levitical prophetic-channel in the rabbinic chronology); (b) the patriarchal-altar-site reading (Ibn Ezra, some modern conservative commentators) locates the consultation at one of the named altar-sites — Be'er Lachai Ro'i (v 11), Hebron / Mamre, or Beer-Sheva; (c) the modern critical reading (Sarna, Westermann) preserves the silence as deliberate — the patriarchal-period does not yet have an institutional consultation-channel; (d) the direct-divine-encounter reading takes v 23's va-yomer YHWH lah (and YHWH said to her) as the unmediated divine response. The textual silence is the editorial-point and the translation preserves it.

NT reception at Rom 9:10-13 reads the v 23 oracle (the divine response to Rivkah's darash) as the paradigm of divine election prior to action. The reception belongs to the v 23 commentary (see the rav-yaavod-tzair-younger-displaces-elder entry); the v 22 darash is the petition-act preceding the oracle.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 180; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 p. 415; Wagner Beten und Klagen 1971; Greenberg Biblical Prose Prayer 1983; Genesis Rabbah 63:6; Rashi at Gen 25:22.

23
And YHWH said to her, Two nations are in your womb, and two peoples shall be separated from your bowels; and the one people shall be stronger than the other people; and the elder shall serve the younger.
And Jehovah said unto her, Two nations are in thy womb, And two peoples shall be separated from thy bowels. And the one people shall be stronger than the other people. And the elder shall serve the younger.
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר יְהוָ֜ה לָ֗הּ שְׁנֵ֤י גיים [גוֹיִם֙] בְּבִטְנֵ֔ךְ וּשְׁנֵ֣י לְאֻמִּ֔ים מִמֵּעַ֖יִךְ יִפָּרֵ֑דוּ וּלְאֹם֙ מִלְאֹ֣ם יֶֽאֱמָ֔ץ וְרַ֖ב יַעֲבֹ֥ד צָעִֽיר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

The chapter's oracular keystone — and one of the HB's most theologically-load-bearing verses. Shnei goyim b-vitnekh u-shnei l'umim mi-me'ayikh yipparedu, u-l-om mi-l-om ye'ematz, v-rav ya'avod tza'ir — Two nations are in your womb, and two peoples shall be separated from your bowels; the one people shall be stronger than the other people; and the elder shall serve the younger. Three distinct issues at the verse, each treated in detail in the rav-yaavod-tzair-younger-displaces-elder glossary entry (new at v2.23.0).

(a) The ketiv/qere on goyim. The MT ketiv reads גיים (defective plural, ambiguous pointing); the qere reads גוֹיִם (full goyim). The qere is standard ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS practice and the translation follows it. The defective ketiv has generated multiple interpretive proposals — scribal-lapse, paronomasia with ge'im (proud-ones), the rabbinic b. Avodah Zarah 11a reading of the ketiv as hint at the proud-empires Rome and Persia.

(b) Subject-ambiguity at v-rav ya'avod tza'ir. The Hebrew word-order leaves the subject grammatically ambiguous. Reading 1 (dominant: ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS, LXX, rabbinic-canonical): the elder shall serve the younger (rav = subject, tza'ir = object). Reading 2 (minority: Speiser AB 1964; NJPS footnote): the elder, the younger shall serve (rav = object, tza'ir = subject). The translation follows R1 — supported by Hebrew word-order convention, the narrative outcome of Gen 25-36 (Esav loses the birthright; Ya'aqov receives both birthright and blessing), the LXX's unambiguous ho meizōn douleusei tō elassoni, and the rabbinic-canonical tradition. Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 181 documents both readings and presents R1 as dominant; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 p. 415 acknowledges the ambiguity as 'a real possibility in the Hebrew' but reads R1 as supported by the narrative outcome.

(c) Pauline reception at Rom 9:10-13. Paul cites the verse (LXX: ho meizōn douleusei tō elassoni) at Romans 9:12 as the proof-text for divine election prior to action: for the children being not yet born, neither having done any good or evil, that the purpose of God according to election might stand. The reception is foundational for two distinct interpretive traditions: (i) the Calvinist / Reformed reading takes the verse as evidence of double-predestination at the individual soteriological level (Calvin Institutes III.21-24; Westminster Confession III; canons of Dort); (ii) the Arminian / Wesleyan / modern Catholic reading reads the verse as election-of-corporate-peoples — the meizōn / elassoni naming the corporate destinies of Edom and Israel as nations rather than the individual-soteriological status of Esav and Ya'aqov as persons. Modern critical NT scholarship (Cranfield ICC, Dunn WBC, Käsemann) broadly sides with the corporate-peoples reading. The Pauline reception is flagged-not-preempted. The Gen 25:23 verse itself is editorially-confined to the patriarchal-political register (the two nations / two peoples opening clauses); the NT-reception belongs downstream and is documented in apparatus but not adjudicated here.

The younger-displaces-elder pattern. Gen 25:23 is the foundational HB instance of the younger-displaces-elder narrative pattern that recurs structurally across the Genesis arc: Abel over Cain (Gen 4); Yitzchaq over Yishma'el (Gen 17, 21); Ya'aqov over Esav (Gen 25-28, here); Yosef over the elder brothers (Gen 37, 41-50); Ephraim over Manasseh (Gen 48); Perez over Zerach (Gen 38:27-30, structurally restaging this verse's twin-birth). The pattern is editorially-coordinated and asserts the principle of election over primogeniture — divine selection operating by criteria other than birth-order. The HB extends the pattern into the post-Genesis material: Moses over Aaron, David over his elder brothers, Solomon over Adonijah.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 178-181; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 414-416; Wenham WBC Genesis 16-50 1994 pp. 176-177; Speiser AB Genesis 1964 pp. 195-196; Cranfield Romans 9-16 ICC 1979; Dunn Romans 9-16 WBC 1988; Käsemann Commentary on Romans 1980; Genesis Rabbah 63:7.

24
And when her days to be delivered were fulfilled, behold, there were twins in her womb.
And when her days to be delivered were fulfilled, behold, there were twins in her womb.
וַיִּמְלְא֥וּ יָמֶ֖יהָ לָלֶ֑דֶת וְהִנֵּ֥ה תוֹמִ֖ם בְּבִטְנָֽהּ׃
25
And the first came forth ruddy, all over like a hairy mantle; and they called his name Esav.
And the first came forth red, all over like a hairy garment. And they called his name Esau.
וַיֵּצֵ֤א הָרִאשׁוֹן֙ אַדְמוֹנִ֔י כֻּלּ֖וֹ כְּאַדֶּ֣רֶת שֵׂעָ֑ר וַיִּקְרְא֥וּ שְׁמ֖וֹ עֵשָֽׂו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

The elder twin's birth-description loads two paronomastic name-plays onto Esav. Va-yetze ha-rishon admoni, kulo k-aderet sear, va-yiqr'u shemo Esav — and the first came forth ruddy, all over like a hairy mantle; and they called his name Esav. Two name-plays are seeded at the birth and realized programmatically later:

(a) admoni (ruddy) → Edom (Red). The reddish coloration is paronomastically linked to adom (red), which becomes the explicit etymology of Esav's secondary-name Edom at v 30 (therefore was his name called Edom). The two-stage name-play — admoni at birth, adom at the lentil-stew — structurally brackets the Esav-naming-arc across vv 25-30.

(b) sear (hair) → Seir (the Edomite mountain-range). The hairy-mantle imagery is paronomastically linked to Seir (the proper-noun toponym of the Edomite mountains, identified with Esav's territory at Gen 32:4, 33:14-16, 36:8-9, Deut 2:4-5). The link is not made explicit at v 25 but is seeded for the later Esav-Edom-Seir compound-identity.

The name Esav itself is etymologically opaque — BDB derives it from an unattested root asah; HALOT marks it 'origin uncertain'. The v 25 narrative loads paronomastic foreshadows onto Esav's birth (admoni for Edom; sear for Seir) without grounding the name Esav itself. The editorial-effect is a multiple-name-etymology compression in which the elder twin's secondary names carry the paronomastic-etymological work. The cluster is treated in the esau-edom-name-etymology-double-play glossary entry (new at v2.23.0).

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 181; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 p. 416; Knauf 1988 Midian; Bartlett Edom and the Edomites 1989.

26
And after that came forth his brother, and his hand had hold on Esav's heel; and his name was called Ya'aqov. And Yitzchaq was sixty years old when she bore them.
And after that came forth his brother, and his hand had hold on Esau`s heel. And his name was called Jacob. And Isaac was threescore years old when she bare them.
וְאַֽחֲרֵי־כֵ֞ן יָצָ֣א אָחִ֗יו וְיָד֤וֹ אֹחֶ֙זֶת֙ בַּעֲקֵ֣ב עֵשָׂ֔ו וַיִּקְרָ֥א שְׁמ֖וֹ יַעֲקֹ֑ב וְיִצְחָ֛ק בֶּן־שִׁשִּׁ֥ים שָׁנָ֖ה בְּלֶ֥דֶת אֹתָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 26

The first explicit Ya'aqov-name-etymology. V-acharei-ken yatza achiv v-yado ochezet ba-aqev Esav va-yiqra shemo Ya'aqov — and after that came forth his brother, and his hand had hold on Esav's heel, and his name was called Ya'aqov. The name Ya'aqov (יַעֲקֹב) is paronomastically linked to aqev (עָקֵב — heel) via the shared consonantal root ʿ-q-b: Ya'aqov is the heel-grasper, named at the moment of birth from the gesture of grasping his elder twin's heel.
