The name's double-etymology — heel (the body-part at birth) and supplant (the denominative verb-action realized in subsequent narrative) — is editorially-load-bearing across the entire Ya'aqov-arc. The supplant-sense is not activated at v 26 (the verse uses only the neutral heel-imagery) but is HB-internal-explicitly derived at two later verses: Gen 27:36 — Esav's lament after the blessing-deception, ha-khi qara shmo Ya'aqov va-ya'aqveni zeh paamayim — is it not rightly that his name is called Ya'aqov, for he has supplanted me these two times; Hos 12:4 — ba-beten aqav et-achiv — in the womb he supplanted his brother. The lexical-family and the supplant-trajectory are treated in the yaakov-name-etymology-aqev-supplant glossary entry (new at v2.23.0).

The full Ya'aqov-arc is structurally framed by the aqev / aqav etymological-trajectory: from heel-grasp (here at birth) through serial-supplant-acts (the birthright-sale at vv 29-34, the blessing-deception at ch 27, the Lavan-flocks departure at ch 31) to the wrestling-rename at Penuel (Gen 32:25-33), where Ya'aqov is renamed Yisra'el (one who strives with El) and is left limping — the heel-injury inverts the heel-grasping birth-gesture, the supplanter is himself wounded in the kaf yerekh (socket-of-the-thigh) and walks with a limp thereafter.

Historical-onomastic context. The name Ya'aqov (or Ya'aqov-el) is attested in 2nd-millennium-BCE Egyptian and Akkadian sources outside the Hebrew Bible: the Hyksos king Ya'qub-har / Ya'qub-her (17th century BCE, preserved on scarabs and seals), the Thutmose III topographical lists at Karnak (15th century BCE; y-ʿ-q-b-ʾ-r, likely Ya'aqov-El), and Old Babylonian / Mari cuneiform attestations of Ia-aḫ-qu-ub-el and variants. The HB Ya'aqov-narrative is grounded in a real West-Semitic name-stock; the etymology (heel / supplant) is the HB-internal narrative-theological interpretation of an existing name.

Chronological note. The verse closes with v-Yitzchaq ben shishim shanah b-ledet otam — and Yitzchaq was sixty years old when she bore them. The detail frames the twin-birth as twenty years after Yitzchaq's marriage to Rivkah (Gen 25:20: forty at marriage), staging the atar-prayer of v 21 as a twenty-year intercession.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 181-182; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 416-417; Albright 1941 BASOR 81; Mendenhall 1958 BASOR 152; Hosea 12:4 commentary in Wolff Hosea Hermeneia 1974.

27
And the boys grew; and Esav was a man skillful in hunting, a man of the field; and Ya'aqov was a quiet man, dwelling in tents.
And the boys grew. And Esau was a skilful hunter, a man of the field. And Jacob was a quiet man, dwelling in tents.
וַֽיִּגְדְּלוּ֙ הַנְּעָרִ֔ים וַיְהִ֣י עֵשָׂ֗ו אִ֛ישׁ יֹדֵ֥עַ צַ֖יִד אִ֣ישׁ שָׂדֶ֑ה וְיַעֲקֹב֙ אִ֣ישׁ תָּ֔ם יֹשֵׁ֖ב אֹהָלִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 27

The twin-character-contrast formula at v 27 is the foundational characterization of Esav and Ya'aqov. Two parallel three-element formulae: Esav as ish yodea tzayid ish sadeh (a man knowing hunting, a man of the field); Ya'aqov as ish tam yoshev ohalim (an ish tam, dwelling in tents). The contrast is editorially-load-bearing — it justifies the parental-favoritism at v 28, establishes the narrative-grammar of all subsequent Esav-Ya'aqov interactions, and structurally aligns Esav with the Yishma'el / wilderness-pastoralist line and Ya'aqov with the Avraham-Yitzchaq / tent-dweller line.

The tam lexeme — the chapter's translation crux. Tam (תָּם) admits four principal readings, each lexically defensible. R1 (ASV/KJV-revised/NRSV/NJPS dominant): quiet, mild, peaceable — the family-character register; Ya'aqov the home-dwelling, mild-tempered, non-aggressive brother. R2 (KJV original): plain, simple, straightforward — the uncompounded-character register. R3 (modern critical / NRSV-adjusted): blameless, complete, integral — the moral-integrity register, aligning Ya'aqov's tam with Noah's tamim (Gen 6:9), Avraham's tamim (Gen 17:1, see the tamim-walk-before-god-covenant entry), and Job's tam v-yashar (Job 1:1, see the tom-v-yashar entry). R4 (Fokkelman 1975 minority): innocent, naive — ironic narrator-voice, Ya'aqov appearing uncomplicated while harboring the trickster-disposition.

The translation follows R1 (ASV-baseline quiet) as the dominant. The full four-reading apparatus is treated in the ish-tam-ish-sadeh-jacob-esau-contrast glossary entry (new at v2.23.0). The key editorial point is that R3 — the moral-integrity reading — creates an immediate narrative-tension with the immediately-following narrative (vv 29-34: Ya'aqov manipulates the famished Esav; ch 27: Ya'aqov deceives the blind Yitzchaq). The tension is the editorial-point: Ya'aqov is named tam at v 27 and then narrated as aqav (supplanter) at vv 29-34 and ch 27. The integrity register is in tension with the supplant-acts; the tension is the Ya'aqov-character's signature. Wenham WBC Genesis 16-50 1994 pp. 177-178 develops this reading explicitly; Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 180-181 and Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 416-417 acknowledge both R1 and R3.

The ish sadeh / yoshev ohalim contrast. The dwelling-locale-pair structurally aligns Esav with the wilderness-pastoralist-hunter register (cf. the pere adam / Yishma'el characterization at Gen 16:12 and Gen 25:18; see the existing pere-adam-wild-ass-prophecy entry) and Ya'aqov with the patriarchal-tent-dweller register (Avraham at Gen 18:1, yoshev petach ha-ohel; the patriarchal ohel as the dwelling-locale of the covenantal line). The contrast is not nomad-vs.-settled-agriculturalist — both brothers are pastoralist; the difference is where and how they dwell within the pastoralist register. The locale-contrast subordinates Esav to the Yishma'el / collateral-line type and aligns Ya'aqov with the Avraham-Yitzchaq / covenantal-line type. The chapter is structurally preparing the covenant-line transmission to Ya'aqov.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 180-181; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 416-417; Wenham WBC Genesis 16-50 1994 pp. 177-178; Fokkelman Narrative Art in Genesis 1975 pp. 86-87; Alter Five Books of Moses 2004 p. 131.

28
Now Yitzchaq loved Esav, because he ate of his venison; and Rivkah loved Ya'aqov.
Now Isaac loved Esau, because he did eat of his venison. And Rebekah loved Jacob.
וַיֶּאֱהַ֥ב יִצְחָ֛ק אֶת־עֵשָׂ֖ו כִּי־צַ֣יִד בְּפִ֑יו וְרִבְקָ֖ה אֹהֶ֥בֶת אֶֽת־יַעֲקֹֽב׃
29
And Ya'aqov boiled pottage. And Esav came in from the field, and he was faint.
And Jacob boiled pottage. And Esau came in from the field, and he was faint.
וַיָּ֥זֶד יַעֲקֹ֖ב נָזִ֑יד וַיָּבֹ֥א עֵשָׂ֛ו מִן־הַשָּׂדֶ֖ה וְה֥וּא עָיֵֽף׃
30
And Esav said to Ya'aqov, Feed me, I pray you, with that same red pottage; for I am faint. Therefore was his name called Edom.
And Esau said to Jacob, Feed me, I pray thee, with that same red [pottage]. For I am faint. Therefore was his name called Edom.
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר עֵשָׂ֜ו אֶֽל־יַעֲקֹ֗ב הַלְעִיטֵ֤נִי נָא֙ מִן־הָאָדֹ֤ם הָאָדֹם֙ הַזֶּ֔ה כִּ֥י עָיֵ֖ף אָנֹ֑כִי עַל־כֵּ֥ן קָרָֽא־שְׁמ֖וֹ אֱדֽוֹם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 30

The lentil-stew episode names the adom (red) connection that grounds Esav's secondary-name Edom. Hal'iteni-na min ha-adom ha-adom ha-zeh ki ayef anokhi al-ken qara shemo Edom — Feed me, I pray you, with that same red red stuff, for I am faint. Therefore was his name called Edom. Three philological items of editorial significance:

(a) The verb hal'iteni (הַלְעִיטֵנִי) is a HB hapax legomenon. The root l-ʿ-ṭ does not recur in the canonical Hebrew Bible. The verb is attested in Talmudic Aramaic and Mishnaic Hebrew in the force-feed register — especially of cattle being force-fed (cf. m. Shabbat 24:3; b. Shabbat 155b). The lexeme's force-feed-register at Gen 25:30 stages Esav's exhaustion as a desperate-quantity request: cram me with that red red stuff — an inelegant verb-choice that the narrator deploys to characterize Esav's appetite-driven character. The hapax-status is editorially-deliberate. The ASV smoothing feed me, I pray you loses the force-feed register but preserves the petition-form.

(b) The doubled ha-adom ha-adom is an emphatic-iterative redoubling. The construction conveys (i) intensification of the red-color description; (ii) the staccato-speech of a famished man; (iii) the adom paronomasia twice at the cusp of the Edom naming-formula. Alter Five Books of Moses 2004 p. 132 renders the doubling as that red red stuff; Speiser AB Genesis 1964 p. 196 as some of that red, red stuff. The WoH translation preserves ASV's smoother that same red pottage and surfaces the doubling in commentary.

(c) The explicit Edom-etymology al-ken qara shemo Edom. The closing-formula stages the second of the chapter's two Esav-naming-etymologies — paronomastic-foreshadowing at v 25 (admoni), explicit-naming-formula at v 30 (Edom). The two-stage etymology brackets the Esav-naming-arc. The Edom-etymology is independent of the Esav-etymology proper and stages Esav as the Edomite-progenitor figure for the wider Genesis arc (cf. Gen 36:8-9 Esav hu Edom — Esav is Edom).

The full philological apparatus and the Edom-historical-archaeological context (Knauf 1988, Bartlett 1989; the Edomite kingdom attested in the Neo-Assyrian record from the 9th century BCE forward; the archaeological sites at Buseirah, Tel el-Kheleifeh, Umm el-Biyara, Tawilan) are treated in the esau-edom-name-etymology-double-play glossary entry.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 181; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 417-418; Wenham WBC Genesis 16-50 1994 p. 178; Alter Five Books of Moses 2004 p. 132; Knauf 1988 Midian; Bartlett Edom and the Edomites 1989.

31
And Ya'aqov said, Sell me first your birthright.
And Jacob said, Sell me first thy birthright.
וַיֹּ֖אמֶר יַעֲקֹ֑ב מִכְרָ֥ה כַיּ֛וֹם אֶת־בְּכֹֽרָתְךָ֖ לִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 31

Vv 31-34 stage the birthright-sale — the foundational HB episode for the bekhorah (firstborn-status) institution and the canonical-narrative source-text for the inter-brother birthright-transfer legal-form. The bekhorah names the legal-juridical status of the firstborn son within the patriarchal-period inheritance-system: the double-portion of the paternal inheritance (formally codified at Deut 21:15-17), the patriarchal blessing (separately transferred at Gen 27), the headship-of-the-family-line, certain cultic-priestly responsibilities. The institution is legally-transferable between brothers under specified conditions, and the Gen 25 transaction is the canonical-HB instance of voluntary inter-brother birthright-transfer.

The ANE legal-form: the Nuzi parallels, with critical caution. The Nuzi tablets (Late-Bronze-Age Hurrian-Akkadian legal documents from northern Mesopotamia, 15th-14th centuries BCE) document multiple inter-brother inheritance-transfer transactions structurally parallel to the Gen 25 sale. The most-cited parallel is HSS V 67 (the Tupkitilla / Kurpazah tablet): a brother sells his inheritance-share to another brother for three sheep. Speiser AB Genesis 1964 pp. 195-196 develops the Nuzi-parallel argument extensively and treats Gen 25 as documenting a real ANE legal-institution-form. Critical caution: Selman 1976 Tyndale Bulletin and Van Seters 1975 Abraham in History and Tradition (Yale) review the Speiser-era Nuzi-parallel arguments and find that some of the early-mid-20th-century parallels were overdrawn. Greengus 1975 JAOS 95 and Eichler 1989 JANES 18 conclude that the basic legal-form of brother-to-brother inheritance-transfer is well-attested across the ANE legal corpora, but the specific Nuzi-Gen 25 parallels are less precise than the early-Speiser arguments suggested. The modern critical consensus: the legal-form is plausible-ANE-attested but the specific Nuzi-Hurrian context is not required; the institutional setting is plausible Late-Bronze / Iron-I West-Semitic legal-practice. The full apparatus is treated in the bekhorah-birthright-firstborn-status glossary entry (new at v2.23.0).

The four-step legal-juridical sequence at vv 31-33. (i) Ya'aqov's request — the legal-immediacy formula. Mikhrah ka-yom et-bekhoratkha li — sell me today your birthright. The phrase ka-yom is the legal-immediacy marker specifying immediate-effective transaction. (ii) Esav's response — the legal-justification clause (v 32). Hineh anokhi holekh la-mut, v-lamah zeh li b'khorah — Behold, I am about to die; and what profit shall the birthright do me?. The clause is a legal-justification for sale: Esav names a contingency (imminent death from hunger / from the wilderness-hunt) that renders the birthright valueless to him. The justification-clause is the legal-precondition for voluntary alienation-of-inheritance under standard ANE inheritance-law. (iii) Ya'aqov's oath-demand — the legal-binding instrument (v 33a). Hishav'ah li ka-yom — swear to me today. A sale-by-oath in the ANE legal-context is irrevocable. (iv) Esav's oath and the legal-completion (v 33b). Va-yishava lo va-yimkor et-bekhorato l-Ya'aqov — and he swore to him, and he sold his birthright to Ya'aqov. The legal-form is now complete.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 181-182; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 416-417; Speiser AB Genesis 1964 pp. 195-196; Selman 1976 Tyndale Bulletin; Van Seters 1975 Abraham in History and Tradition; Greengus 1975 JAOS 95; Eichler 1989 JANES 18.

32
And Esav said, Behold, I am about to die; and what profit shall the birthright do to me?
And Esau said, Behold, I am about to die. And what profit shall the birthright do to me?
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר עֵשָׂ֔ו הִנֵּ֛ה אָנֹכִ֥י הוֹלֵ֖ךְ לָמ֑וּת וְלָמָּה־זֶּ֥ה לִ֖י בְּכֹרָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 32

Esav's response — the legal-justification clause. Hineh anokhi holekh la-mut, v-lamah zeh li b'khorah — Behold, I am about to die; and what profit shall the birthright do me?. Two readings: (i) the literal hyperbolic reading — Esav exaggerates his hunger (he is exhausted from the field but not literally dying); the hyperbole names a valuation-mismatch between the immediate-need (food) and the long-term-asset (birthright). (ii) the wilderness-pastoralist-mortality reading — Esav's ish sadeh register exposes him to genuine mortality-risk from the hunt; the holekh la-mut names a real-and-ongoing condition rather than a hyperbolic moment. Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 182 reads the line as Esav's character-revealing self-rationalization: the rhetorical question and what profit shall the birthright do me? exposes Esav's preference for the immediate over the deferred. The clause functions as the legal-justification for sale under standard ANE inheritance-law: the seller must articulate a reason for voluntary alienation.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 182; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 416-417; Alter Five Books of Moses 2004 p. 132.

33
And Ya'aqov said, Swear to me first; and he swore to him; and he sold his birthright to Ya'aqov.
And Jacob said, Swear to me first. And he sware unto him. And he sold his birthright unto Jacob.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר יַעֲקֹ֗ב הִשָּׁ֤בְעָה לִּי֙ כַּיּ֔וֹם וַיִּשָּׁבַ֖ע ל֑וֹ וַיִּמְכֹּ֥ר אֶת־בְּכֹרָת֖וֹ לְיַעֲקֹֽב׃
Translation Commentary Verse 33

The oath-demand and the legal-completion. Hishav'ah li ka-yom va-yishava lo va-yimkor et-bekhorato l-Ya'aqov — Swear to me today; and he swore to him, and he sold his birthright to Ya'aqov. The oath-demand is the legal-binding instrument: a sale-by-oath in the ANE legal-context is irrevocable, the deity invoked as witness to the transaction. The verb makhar (qal imperfect with vav-consecutive) marks the legal-completion: the sale is now juridically-binding. The four-step sequence (request, justification, oath, completion) is now closed.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 182; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 416-417.

34
And Ya'aqov gave Esav bread and pottage of lentils; and he ate and drank, and rose up, and went his way: so Esav despised his birthright.
And Jacob gave Esau bread and pottage of lentils. And he did eat and drink, and rose up, and went his way. So Esau despised his birthright.
וְיַעֲקֹ֞ב נָתַ֣ן לְעֵשָׂ֗ו לֶ֚חֶם וּנְזִ֣יד עֲדָשִׁ֔ים וַיֹּ֣אכַל וַיֵּ֔שְׁתְּ וַיָּ֖קָם וַיֵּלַ֑ךְ וַיִּ֥בֶז עֵשָׂ֖ו אֶת־הַבְּכֹרָֽה׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 34

The narrator's editorial moral-verdict. V-Ya'aqov natan l-Esav lechem u-n-zid adashim va-yokhal va-yesht va-yaqam va-yelekh va-yivez Esav et-ha-bekhorah — and Ya'aqov gave Esav bread and pottage of lentils; and he ate, and drank, and rose up, and went his way: so Esav despised his birthright. The four staccato verbs (va-yokhal, va-yesht, va-yaqam, va-yelekh — and he ate, and drank, and rose, and went) deliberately stage Esav's animal-immediacy-of-appetite; Alter Five Books of Moses 2004 p. 132 notes the rhetorical effect. The closing verb va-yivez (qal impf 3ms of bazah — despise, scorn, hold in contempt) carries the narrator's editorial moral-verdict and is treated extensively in the va-yivez-esav-et-ha-bekhorah-despising-birthright glossary entry (new at v2.23.0).

The bazah lexeme is the HB's primary verb for despise, hold in contempt, treat as worthless. Cross-corpus: Num 15:31 (the apostate who despises the word of YHWH); 1 Sam 17:42 (Goliath despising the youth David); 2 Sam 6:16 (Michal despising David's dance); 2 Kgs 19:21 / Isa 37:22 (the virgin daughter of Zion despising Sennacherib); Mal 1:6-7 (the priests despising the name of YHWH); Isa 53:3 (the suffering servant nivzeh — despised). The verb's consistent register is strong-negative valuation. Its application to Esav at v 34 is the narrator's severe-verdict.

NT reception at Hebrews 12:16-17. Mē tis pornos ē bebēlos hōs Ēsau, hos anti brōseōs mias apedeto ta prōtotokia heautou — Lest there be any fornicator, or profane person (bebēlos), as Esau, who for one morsel of meat sold his own birthright. The Greek bebēlos (profane, unhallowed, irreligious) renders the va-yivez verdict into early-Christian theological vocabulary as profanity / unholiness. Esav becomes the type-figure of the profane-firstborn. The reception extends across the patristic and medieval Christian tradition: Origen Homilies on Genesis XII; Augustine De Civitate Dei XV-XVI (Esav as type of the earthly-city); Calvin Institutes III (Esav as evidence of reprobation). The Lectionary tradition pairs Gen 25:31-34 with Heb 12:16-17 across the Christian liturgical-year. The reception is flagged-not-preempted.

Rabbinic reception at Genesis Rabbah 63:11. The rabbinic tradition expands the va-yivez verdict into five sins committed by Esav on the day of the birthright-sale: (i) violation of a betrothed maiden; (ii) murder; (iii) denial of the resurrection of the dead; (iv) denial of divine providence; (v) despising the birthright. The midrashic-expansion amplifies the v 34 text into a comprehensive characterological condemnation. Rashi at Gen 25:34 cites Genesis Rabbah 63:11 and the comprehensive-contempt reading. The reception is flagged-not-preempted.

Modern critical reading. Modern critical scholarship (Sarna, Westermann, Wenham, Hamilton, Alter, Speiser) reads the va-yivez verdict more narrowly: the verse is the narrator's verdict on the immediacy-of-appetite-over-long-term-promise pattern. Esav values immediate satisfaction over deferred patriarchal blessing — the contempt-verdict targets this specific decision-making pattern, not a comprehensive moral failure.

Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 181-182; Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 414-418; Alter Five Books of Moses 2004 p. 132; Attridge Hebrews Hermeneia 1989 pp. 369-372; Bauckham Jude, 2 Peter WBC 1983; Genesis Rabbah 63:11; b. Bava Batra 16b.

Chapter 26

Genesis 26 — Yitzchaq at Gerar, the wife-sister episode, the wells, and the covenant at Be'er Sheva

1
And there was a famine in the land, besides the first famine that was in the days of Avraham. And Yitzchaq went to Avimelekh king of the Philistines, to Gerar.
And there was a famine in the land, besides the first famine that was in the days of Abraham. And Isaac went unto Abimelech king of the Philistines, unto Gerar.
וַיְהִ֤י רָעָב֙ בָּאָ֔רֶץ מִלְּבַד֙ הָרָעָ֣ב הָרִאשׁ֔וֹן אֲשֶׁ֥ר הָיָ֖ה בִּימֵ֣י אַבְרָהָ֑ם וַיֵּ֧לֶךְ יִצְחָ֛ק אֶל־אֲבִימֶּ֥לֶךְ מֶֽלֶךְ־פְּלִשְׁתִּ֖ים גְּרָֽרָה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The chapter opens with the second of two patriarchal famine-narratives: mi-l'vad ha-ra'av ha-rishon asher hayah bi-mei Avraham — besides the first famine that was in the days of Avraham — explicitly cross-referencing Gen 12:10's va-yhi ra'av ba-aretz and signalling the chapter's doublet relationship to the Avram-in-Mitsrayim narrative of Gen 12 and the Avraham-Avimelekh-of-Gerar narrative of Gen 20. Gen 26 is the third instance of the wife-sister type-scene (see wife-sister-type-scene; Alter The Art of Biblical Narrative 1981, ch. 3 is the canonical literary-critical treatment). The chapter does not introduce the type-scene; it closes it, replaying the inherited material with Yitzchaq and Rivkah in the patriarchal-couple roles and Avimelekh of Gerar in the foreign-king role (now structurally on his second appearance in the Genesis Avimelekh-cycle, after Gen 20-21).

2
And YHWH appeared to him, and said, Do not go down to Mitsrayim; dwell in the land of which I shall tell you.
And Jehovah appeared unto him, and said, Go not down into Egypt. Dwell in the land which I shall tell thee of.
וַיֵּרָ֤א אֵלָיו֙ יְהוָ֔ה וַיֹּ֖אמֶר אַל־תֵּרֵ֣ד מִצְרָ֑יְמָה שְׁכֹ֣ן בָּאָ֔רֶץ אֲשֶׁ֖ר אֹמַ֥ר אֵלֶֽיךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The chapter's first theophany — va-yera elav YHWH — is the editorial-pivot. The patriarchal-theophany formula (see va-yera-yhwh-theophany) marks the moment Yitzchaq, unlike his father at Gen 12:10, is explicitly forbidden to descend to Mitsrayim: al-tered Mitsraymah — do not go down to Mitsrayim. The prohibition is editorially loaded. Gen 12:10's Avram had descended without prior divine instruction; Gen 20:1's Avraham had moved south to Gerar without divine instruction; only Yitzchaq receives the explicit YHWH-given travel directive. Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 183 reads the prohibition as Yitzchaq-specific: the Yitzchaq-cycle distinguishes the second patriarch from the first by his geographical-confinement to the promised land (he is the only patriarch who never leaves it). The wider editorial pattern is the land-confinement register: Yitzchaq is the patriarch whose entire narrative-cycle is contained within the borders of the land that the Avrahamic-covenant has promised. The implication for the wife-sister-type-scene replay is that the Yitzchaq-cycle's version is structurally truncated: Yitzchaq does not descend to a foreign-imperial-court (Egypt at Gen 12; Gerar of an earlier Avimelekh-court at Gen 20) but stays within Gerar's nearer-Philistine-pastoral context.

3
Sojourn in this land, and I will be with you, and will bless you; for to you, and to your seed, I will give all these lands, and I will establish the oath which I swore to Avraham your father.
Sojourn in this land, and I will be with thee, and will bless thee. For unto thee, and unto thy seed, I will give all these lands, and I will establish the oath which I sware unto Abraham thy father.
גּ֚וּר בָּאָ֣רֶץ הַזֹּ֔את וְאֶֽהְיֶ֥ה עִמְּךָ֖ וַאֲבָרְכֶ֑ךָּ כִּֽי־לְךָ֣ וּֽלְזַרְעֲךָ֗ אֶתֵּן֙ אֶת־כָּל־הָֽאֲרָצֹ֣ת הָאֵ֔ל וַהֲקִֽמֹתִי֙ אֶת־הַשְּׁבֻעָ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר נִשְׁבַּ֖עְתִּי לְאַבְרָהָ֥ם אָבִֽיךָ׃
4
And I will multiply your seed as the stars of the heavens, and will give to your seed all these lands; and in your seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed.
And I will multiply thy seed as the stars of heaven, and will give unto thy seed all these lands. And in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed.
וְהִרְבֵּיתִ֤י אֶֽת־זַרְעֲךָ֙ כְּכוֹכְבֵ֣י הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וְנָתַתִּ֣י לְזַרְעֲךָ֔ אֵ֥ת כָּל־הָאֲרָצֹ֖ת הָאֵ֑ל וְהִתְבָּרֲכ֣וּ בְזַרְעֲךָ֔ כֹּ֖ל גּוֹיֵ֥י הָאָֽרֶץ׃
5
Because Avraham obeyed my voice, and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws.
Because that Abraham obeyed my voice, and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws.
עֵ֕קֶב אֲשֶׁר־שָׁמַ֥ע אַבְרָהָ֖ם בְּקֹלִ֑י וַיִּשְׁמֹר֙ מִשְׁמַרְתִּ֔י מִצְוֺתַ֖י חֻקּוֹתַ֥י וְתוֹרֹתָֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

The chapter's clearest source-critical signal. The four nouns mishmarti, mitzvotai, chuqotai, v-torotai (my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws) name the four primary categories of Pentateuchal legal-stipulation vocabulary — all of which properly belong to the Sinaitic / Mosaic legal-corpus (Exodus 19 onward) — and apply them to Avraham, who in the surrounding narrative receives covenant promises (Gen 12:1-3; 15; 17) but no enumerated law-code. Mishmeret is a Priestly cultic-duty term (Lev 8:35; Num 1:53; 3:7-8; 18:3-5); mitzvot is the Pentateuch's primary term for Sinaitic commandments (Exod 16:28, 20:6; the entire Deuteronomic passim-distribution); chuqot is the engraved-decree-law term, concentrated in the P and D strata; torot (plural — a comparatively rare form, 10 HB occurrences) names multiple distinct legal teachings. The four-noun cluster as a unit recurs only in late-Deuteronomistic and post-exilic contexts — Deut 11:1; 26:17; 28:1; 1 Kgs 2:3; 8:58; 9:6; 2 Kgs 17:13; 23:3; Mal 3:22 — making the Gen 26:5 application editorially anomalous.

Modern critical reading. Wellhausen Prolegomena 1885, Skinner ICC Genesis 1910 pp. 363-364, von Rad Genesis 1956 OTL, Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 425-426, Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 pp. 184-185, Wenham WBC 1994 pp. 189-190, Hamilton NICOT 1995 pp. 195-196, Alter 2004 p. 134, and Friedman Who Wrote the Bible? 1987 all read the verse as a late-Deuteronomistic or Priestly editorial gloss — a Sinaitic-retrojection imposing the Mosaic-legal vocabulary on the patriarchal narrative. The rabbinic-traditional alternative (m. Qidd. 4:14; b. Yoma 28b; Gen Rab 64:4; Rashi at Gen 26:5) harmonizes by holding that Avraham observed the entire Torah by anticipation. Both readings are documented in mishmeret-mitzvot-chuqim-torot-fourfold-covenant; the WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline four-noun rendering so that the source-critical anomaly is visible to the reader.

Structural function. The verse caps the YHWH-self-disclosure to Yitzchaq at vv 2-5 by providing the grounds for the promise-reissue: Yitzchaq receives the promise eqev asher shama Avraham b-qoli — because Avraham obeyed my voice. The grounds-clause-structure (gift-grounded-in-the-fathers'-merit) is a Deuteronomic signature, structurally parallel to Deut 7:8 and 9:5, reinforcing the late-stratum-attribution.

6
And Yitzchaq dwelt in Gerar.
And Isaac dwelt in Gerar.
וַיֵּ֥שֶׁב יִצְחָ֖ק בִּגְרָֽר׃
7
And the men of the place asked him about his wife; and he said, She is my sister; for he feared to say, My wife; lest, said he, the men of the place should kill me on account of Rivkah, because she was a woman beautiful of appearance.
And the men of the place asked him of his wife. And he said, She is my sister. For he feared to say, My wife. Lest, [said he], the men of the place should kill me for Rebekah. Because she was fair to look upon.
וַֽיִּשְׁאֲל֞וּ אַנְשֵׁ֤י הַמָּקוֹם֙ לְאִשְׁתּ֔וֹ וַיֹּ֖אמֶר אֲחֹ֣תִי הִ֑וא כִּ֤י יָרֵא֙ לֵאמֹ֣ר אִשְׁתִּ֔י פֶּן־יַֽהַרְגֻ֜נִי אַנְשֵׁ֤י הַמָּקוֹם֙ עַל־רִבְקָ֔ה כִּֽי־טוֹבַ֥ת מַרְאֶ֖ה הִֽיא׃
8
And it came to pass, when he had been there a long time, that Avimelekh king of the Philistines looked out through the window, and saw, and behold, Yitzchaq was sporting with Rivkah his wife.
And it came to pass, when he had been there a long time, that Abimelech king of the Philistines looked out at a window, and saw, and, behold, Isaac was sporting with Rebekah his wife.
וַיְהִ֗י כִּ֣י אָֽרְכוּ־ל֥וֹ שָׁם֙ הַיָּמִ֔ים וַיַּשְׁקֵ֗ף אֲבִימֶ֙לֶךְ֙ מֶ֣לֶךְ פְּלִשְׁתִּ֔ים בְּעַ֖ד הַֽחַלּ֑וֹן וַיַּ֗רְא וְהִנֵּ֤ה יִצְחָק֙ מְצַחֵ֔ק אֵ֖ת רִבְקָ֥ה אִשְׁתּֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

Va-yar v-hineh Yitzchaq m-tzacheq et Rivkah ishto — the piel participle m-tzacheq (from tz-ch-q, the root behind Yitzchaq's own name) here names conjugal-intimate play between husband and wife, visible enough through the window for Avimelekh to recognize the marriage. The conjugal sense is determined by the verse's grammatical and contextual disambiguators: (i) the explicit direct object et Rivkah ishto names the wife; (ii) the verb is observed through a window — a culturally-marked sight-line for intimate behavior; (iii) the observation is sufficient to disclose the marriage to Avimelekh, who immediately concludes hineh ishtekha hi (v 9). Mere playing would not have been disclosive. Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 185, Alter 2004 p. 134, Westermann 1985 p. 425, and the modern English versions (NJPS / NRSV / NIV-2011 fondling, caressing) all read the conjugal sense as determinative.

The same piel participle was used contestedly at Gen 21:9 (Sarah saw Yishma'el m-tzacheq — see metzacheq-ishmael-debate for the four-reading dispute). The two attestations are not in competition: Gen 26:8 is grammatically-determinate (the direct object disambiguates); Gen 21:9 is grammatically-indeterminate (no direct object in MT). The piel of tz-ch-q is deliberately polysemous across the corpus — playful-laughter (Gen 21:6); mocking-joking (Gen 19:14); erotic-love-play (Gen 26:8; Gen 39:14-17); ritual-cultic revelry (Exod 32:6); performance-spectacle (Judg 16:25) — and each attestation is disambiguated by context. See metzacheq-conjugal-vs-contested-disambiguation for the full polysemy treatment.

The Hebrew also activates the paronomastic name-play: Yitzchaq m-tzacheq — Yitzchaq Yitzchaq-ing / the-laugher laughing-erotically. The verb-name homophony continues the Gen 17-21 laughter-and-name-play arc (see yitzchaq-name-etymology-laughter-pattern). The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline sporting — a euphemistic-archaic register that preserves the conjugal sense while keeping the wordplay-with-the-name active. Modern explicit renderings (fondling, caressing) sharpen the conjugal sense at the cost of obscuring the name-play.

9
And Avimelekh called Yitzchaq, and said, Behold, of a surety she is your wife; and how did you say, She is my sister? And Yitzchaq said to him, Because I said, Lest I die because of her.
And Abimelech called Isaac, and said, Behold, of a surety she is thy wife. And how saidst thou, She is my sister? And Isaac said unto him, Because I said, Lest I die because of her.
וַיִּקְרָ֨א אֲבִימֶ֜לֶךְ לְיִצְחָ֗ק וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ אַ֣ךְ הִנֵּ֤ה אִשְׁתְּךָ֙ הִ֔וא וְאֵ֥יךְ אָמַ֖רְתָּ אֲחֹ֣תִי הִ֑וא וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֵלָיו֙ יִצְחָ֔ק כִּ֣י אָמַ֔רְתִּי פֶּן־אָמ֖וּת עָלֶֽיהָ׃
10
And Avimelekh said, What is this you have done to us? One of the people might easily have lain with your wife, and you would have brought guilt upon us.
And Abimelech said, What is this thou hast done unto us? One of the people might easily have lain with thy wife, and thou wouldest have brought guiltiness upon us.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֲבִימֶ֔לֶךְ מַה־זֹּ֖את עָשִׂ֣יתָ לָּ֑נוּ כִּ֠מְעַט שָׁכַ֞ב אַחַ֤ד הָעָם֙ אֶת־אִשְׁתֶּ֔ךָ וְהֵבֵאתָ֥ עָלֵ֖ינוּ אָשָֽׁם׃
11
And Avimelekh commanded all the people, saying, He that touches this man or his wife shall surely be put to death.
And Abimelech charged all the people, saying, He that toucheth this man or his wife shall surely be put to death.
וַיְצַ֣ו אֲבִימֶ֔לֶךְ אֶת־כָּל־הָעָ֖ם לֵאמֹ֑ר הַנֹּגֵ֜עַ בָּאִ֥ישׁ הַזֶּ֛ה וּבְאִשְׁתּ֖וֹ מ֥וֹת יוּמָֽת׃
12
And Yitzchaq sowed in that land, and found in the same year a hundredfold; and YHWH blessed him.
And Isaac sowed in that land, and found in the same year a hundredfold. And Jehovah blessed him.
וַיִּזְרַ֤ע יִצְחָק֙ בָּאָ֣רֶץ הַהִ֔וא וַיִּמְצָ֛א בַּשָּׁנָ֥ה הַהִ֖וא מֵאָ֣ה שְׁעָרִ֑ים וַֽיְבָרֲכֵ֖הוּ יְהוָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

Va-yizra Yitzchaq ba-aretz ha-hi va-yimtza ba-shanah ha-hi me'ah she'arim — Yitzchaq sowed in that land, and found in the same year a hundredfold. The verse is one of the patriarchal-narratives' only explicit agricultural-farming notices: Yitzchaq is depicted as a sedentary grain-farmer rather than as a pastoral-nomadic herder. The hundredfold-yield (me'ah she'arim) is an idealized-superabundant number; the in-land famine of v 1 has been reversed by divine-blessing. Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 184 reads the verse as the chapter's hinge between the wife-sister narrative (vv 1-11) and the Philistine-envy / well-conflict narrative (vv 12-22): Yitzchaq's prosperity is the cause of the Philistines' envy at v 14, and the well-stopping (v 15) and the Avimelekh-expulsion (v 16) are the consequence. The pastoral-and-agricultural compound character of Yitzchaq's wealth (miqneh-tzon u-miqneh baqar v-avudah rabbah at v 14 — flocks, herds, and a great household / cultivated land) is distinct from Avraham's pure-pastoral wealth (Gen 13:2) and Ya'aqov's later pure-pastoral wealth (Gen 30:43); Yitzchaq is the settled patriarch.

13
And the man grew great, and went on growing greater, until he became very great.
And the man waxed great, and grew more and more until he became very great.
וַיִּגְדַּ֖ל הָאִ֑ישׁ וַיֵּ֤לֶךְ הָלוֹךְ֙ וְגָדֵ֔ל עַ֥ד כִּֽי־גָדַ֖ל מְאֹֽד׃
14
And he had possessions of flocks, and possessions of herds, and a great household; and the Philistines envied him.
And he had possessions of flocks, and possessions of herds, and a great household. And the Philistines envied him.
וַֽיְהִי־ל֤וֹ מִקְנֵה־צֹאן֙ וּמִקְנֵ֣ה בָקָ֔ר וַעֲבֻדָּ֖ה רַבָּ֑ה וַיְקַנְא֥וּ אֹת֖וֹ פְּלִשְׁתִּֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

Va-yqan'u oto Pelishtim — and the Philistines envied him. The verse marks the structural-pivot to the well-conflict narrative. The Philistine envy is the chapter's depiction of inter-ethnic economic-territorial competition: Yitzchaq's prosperity in their land creates the political-economic tension that produces both the well-stopping (v 15) and the Avimelekh-expulsion (v 16). The pattern is a recurring HB type-scene: prosperity-of-the-resident-alien-provokes-host-population-envy (cf. Exod 1:7-10, the Pharaoh's response to Israelite multiplication in Mitsrayim; Sarna JPS Genesis p. 184 notes the cross-cycle parallel — the Yitzchaq-in-Gerar story prefigures the Israel-in-Mitsrayim story in compressed-narrative form).

15
Now all the wells which his father's servants had dug in the days of Avraham his father, the Philistines had stopped them, and filled them with earth.
Now all the wells which his father`s servants had digged in the days of Abraham his father, the Philistines had stopped, and filled with earth.
וְכָל־הַבְּאֵרֹ֗ת אֲשֶׁ֤ר חָֽפְרוּ֙ עַבְדֵ֣י אָבִ֔יו בִּימֵ֖י אַבְרָהָ֣ם אָבִ֑יו סִתְּמ֣וּם פְּלִשְׁתִּ֔ים וַיְמַלְא֖וּם עָפָֽר׃
16
And Avimelekh said to Yitzchaq, Go from us, for you are much mightier than we.
And Abimelech said unto Isaac, Go from us. For thou art much mightier than we.
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר אֲבִימֶ֖לֶךְ אֶל־יִצְחָ֑ק לֵ֚ךְ מֵֽעִמָּ֔נוּ כִּֽי־עָצַֽמְתָּ־מִמֶּ֖נּוּ מְאֹֽד׃
17
And Yitzchaq departed from there, and encamped in the wadi of Gerar, and dwelt there.
And Isaac departed thence, and encamped in the valley of Gerar, and dwelt there.
וַיֵּ֥לֶךְ מִשָּׁ֖ם יִצְחָ֑ק וַיִּ֥חַן בְּנַֽחַל־גְּרָ֖ר וַיֵּ֥שֶׁב שָֽׁם׃
18
And Yitzchaq dug again the wells of water, which they had dug in the days of Avraham his father; for the Philistines had stopped them after the death of Avraham. And he called their names after the names by which his father had called them.
And Isaac digged again the wells of water, which they had digged in the days of Abraham his father. For the Philistines had stopped them after the death of Abraham. And he called their names after the names by which his father had called them.
וַיָּ֨שָׁב יִצְחָ֜ק וַיַּחְפֹּ֣ר ׀ אֶת־בְּאֵרֹ֣ת הַמַּ֗יִם אֲשֶׁ֤ר חָֽפְרוּ֙ בִּימֵי֙ אַבְרָהָ֣ם אָבִ֔יו וַיְסַתְּמ֣וּם פְּלִשְׁתִּ֔ים אַחֲרֵ֖י מ֣וֹת אַבְרָהָ֑ם וַיִּקְרָ֤א לָהֶן֙ שֵׁמ֔וֹת כַּשֵּׁמֹ֕ת אֲשֶׁר־קָרָ֥א לָהֶ֖ן אָבִֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

Va-yiqra la-hen shemot ka-shemot asher qara la-hen aviv — and he called their names after the names by which his father had called them. The verse is an editorial-keystone for the Yitzchaq-cycle's continuity-with-Avraham character. Yitzchaq's distinctive narrative-feature across the chapter is paternal-continuation rather than independent innovation: he digs his father's wells, names them by his father's names, receives the same theophany-formulae (va-yera elav YHWH; the al-tira ki-itkha anokhi; the anokhi Elohei Avraham avikha), the same land-and-seed promises (vv 3-4), and the same Avimelekh-treaty (vv 26-31). The Yitzchaq-cycle is structurally the Avraham-cycle replayed — a deliberate editorial choice that places Yitzchaq as the patriarch of covenant-transmission rather than covenant-initiation. Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 423-424 reads the Yitzchaq-cycle's structural-truncation as editorial: the second patriarch is depicted not as a new patriarch but as the first heir — the one who receives, preserves, and transmits the Avrahamic-covenant without modification.

19
And Yitzchaq's servants dug in the wadi, and found there a well of living water.
And Isaac`s servants digged in the valley, and found there a well of springing water.
וַיַּחְפְּר֥וּ עַבְדֵֽי־יִצְחָ֖ק בַּנָּ֑חַל וַיִּ֨מְצְאוּ־שָׁ֔ם בְּאֵ֖ר מַ֥יִם חַיִּֽים׃
20
And the herdsmen of Gerar contended with Yitzchaq's herdsmen, saying, The water is ours. And he called the name of the well Esek, because they contended with him.
And the herdsmen of Gerar strove with Isaac`s herdsmen, saying, The water is ours. And he called the name of the well Esek, because they contended with him.
וַיָּרִ֜יבוּ רֹעֵ֣י גְרָ֗ר עִם־רֹעֵ֥י יִצְחָ֛ק לֵאמֹ֖ר לָ֣נוּ הַמָּ֑יִם וַיִּקְרָ֤א שֵֽׁם־הַבְּאֵר֙ עֵ֔שֶׂק כִּ֥י הִֽתְעַשְּׂק֖וּ עִמּֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

The first of three wells in the chapter's deliberate three-well place-name etymology triad — Esek, Sitnah, Rechovot. The name Esek (עֵשֶׂק — Contention) derives from the verb hit'as'qu in the in-text etiology: ki hit'as'qu imo — because they contended with him. The root a-s-q (contend, strive, dispute) is a hapax-or-near-hapax HB verbal-root; the noun Esek itself is a hapax outside this place-name. The contention-register is legal-economic — water-rights litigation — rather than military. See esek-sitnah-rechovot-well-name-triad for the triad's full structural treatment.

21
And they dug another well, and they contended for that also; and he called its name Sitnah.
And they digged another well, and they strove for that also. And he called the name of it Sitnah.
וַֽיַּחְפְּרוּ֙ בְּאֵ֣ר אַחֶ֔רֶת וַיָּרִ֖יבוּ גַּם־עָלֶ֑יהָ וַיִּקְרָ֥א שְׁמָ֖הּ שִׂטְנָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

The second well — Sitnah (שִׂטְנָה — Opposition / Hostility / Accusation). The name derives from the root s-t-n — the same root as ha-satan (הַשָּׂטָן, the adversary / the accuser) at Job 1-2, Zech 3:1-2, and 1 Chr 21:1. The root's primary register is hostile-opposition / adversarial-accusation. The abstract-noun sitnah has a sparse HB distribution: Gen 26:21 (here) and Ezra 4:6 (the sitnah al yoshvei Yehudah — the accusation sent against the inhabitants of Yehudah under Achashverosh). The root-connection to ha-satan is flagged but not conflated: the place-name and the later adversarial-figure share the root, but inhabit distinct semantic registers — the place-name is in the legal-or-territorial-contention register; the ha-satan is in the divine-council-prosecutor register. Sarna JPS Genesis 1989 p. 186 notes the root-connection without conflating; the WoH treatment follows. See esek-sitnah-rechovot-well-name-triad for the cross-corpus distribution and the not-conflated treatment.

22
And he removed from there, and dug another well; and they did not contend for it. And he called its name Rechovot; and he said, For now YHWH has made room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land.
And he removed from thence, and digged another well. And for that they strove not. And he called the name of it Rehoboth. And he said, For now Jehovah hath made room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land.
וַיַּעְתֵּ֣ק מִשָּׁ֗ם וַיַּחְפֹּר֙ בְּאֵ֣ר אַחֶ֔רֶת וְלֹ֥א רָב֖וּ עָלֶ֑יהָ וַיִּקְרָ֤א שְׁמָהּ֙ רְחֹב֔וֹת וַיֹּ֗אמֶר כִּֽי־עַתָּ֞ה הִרְחִ֧יב יְהוָ֛ה לָ֖נוּ וּפָרִ֥ינוּ בָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The third and resolving well — Rechovot (רְחֹבוֹת — Broad-Places / Room). The name derives from the root r-ch-v (be wide, be broad, make-room); the in-text etiology explicitly cites the verb hirchiv (hiphil — made-room): ki-atah hirchiv YHWH lanu u-farinu va-aretz — for now YHWH has made-room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land. The triad's structural progression — contention → opposition → room and fruitfulness — resolves at the third well, with the u-farinu va-aretz clause echoing the Gen 1:28 / 9:1 / 17:6 / 35:11 fruitfulness-formula. The pattern is a three-fold-progression-with-resolution: three iterations of conflict, the third yielding resolution. Yitzchaq's distinctive character as patriarch is editorially-encoded in the naming-pattern: he does not fight back at the wells; he moves on and digs another. Sarna p. 186: the names memorialize the conflict; Yitzchaq's pastoral-pacifist response is editorially-foregrounded.

23
And he went up from there to Be'er Sheva.
And he went up from thence to Beer-sheba.
וַיַּ֥עַל מִשָּׁ֖ם בְּאֵ֥ר שָֽׁבַע׃
24
And YHWH appeared to him the same night, and said, I am the Elohim of Avraham your father; do not fear, for I am with you, and will bless you, and multiply your seed for my servant Avraham's sake.
And Jehovah appeared unto him the same night, and said, I am the God of Abraham thy father. Fear not, for I am with thee, and will bless thee, and multiply thy seed for my servant Abraham`s sake.
וַיֵּרָ֨א אֵלָ֤יו יְהוָה֙ בַּלַּ֣יְלָה הַה֔וּא וַיֹּ֕אמֶר אָנֹכִ֕י אֱלֹהֵ֖י אַבְרָהָ֣ם אָבִ֑יךָ אַל־תִּירָא֙ כִּֽי־אִתְּךָ֣ אָנֹ֔כִי וּבֵֽרַכְתִּ֙יךָ֙ וְהִרְבֵּיתִ֣י אֶֽת־זַרְעֲךָ֔ בַּעֲב֖וּר אַבְרָהָ֥ם עַבְדִּֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 24

The chapter's keystone theophany — and one of the densest divine-self-disclosure verses in the patriarchal narratives. The verse compounds four canonical formulae into a single divine-speech: (i) the va-yera elav YHWH theophany-formula (see va-yera-yhwh-theophany); (ii) the anokhi Elohei Avraham avikha ancestral-god self-disclosure formula; (iii) the al-tira ki-itkha anokhi fear-not divine-encouragement formula; (iv) the u-verakhtikha v-hirbeiti et-zar'akha blessing-and-multiplication formula. The Yitzchaq-cycle's editorial signature is its formulaic density: Yitzchaq, the least-narratively-developed of the three patriarchs, receives the most-densely-formulaic divine-encouragement.

The al-tira ki-itkha anokhi formula — do not fear, for I am with you — is the foundational HB divine-encouragement-opener. It is one of the most distributionally extensive recurring formulae in the canon, deployed at moments of theophany or prophetic-call (Gen 15:1; Exod 3:6; Isa 6, 41:10; Jer 1:8; Ezek 2-3; Dan 10), at moments of military or political crisis (Exod 14:13; Num 14:9, 21:34; Josh 8:1; 2 Kgs 6:16), and at moments of eschatological-restoration prophecy (Isa 41-44; Jer 30, 46; Zech 8; Hag 2). The ANE context is well-documented: Conrad 1985 Fear Not Warrior (Brown Judaic Studies 75) distinguishes the warrior-encouragement form (recurring in Mari-letters and Neo-Assyrian prophecy-collections like the Esarhaddon-prophecies) from the patriarchal-encouragement form. Nissinen 2003 Prophets and Prophecy in the Ancient Near East (SBL WAW 12) documents the cross-cultural ANE distribution: the formula sits within a well-attested divine-encouragement-genre. See al-tira-fear-not-divine-encouragement-formula for the full cross-corpus treatment.

The anokhi Elohei Avraham avikha formula — I am the Elohim of Avraham your father — is the first canonical anokhi Elohei X avikha ancestral-god self-disclosure formula in the patriarchal narratives. The formula identifies the speaking deity through ancestral-relational genealogy rather than through self-naming or cosmological-predicate. The form is foundational for Albrecht Alt's 1929 Der Gott der Väter (The God of the Fathers) reconstruction of the patriarchal-period ancestral-god religion: the pre-Mosaic religious-form in which each patriarchal-lineage worshipped the deity who had appeared to the lineage's founding patriarch. The cross-corpus deployments — Gen 28:13 (Ya'aqov's ladder); Gen 31:5, 31:42, 31:53; Gen 32:10; Gen 46:3; Exod 3:6 (the burning bush — Anokhi Elohei avikha Elohei Avraham Elohei Yitzchaq v-Elohei Ya'aqov; the keystone deployment); Exod 3:13-16; 6:3 — make the formula one of the Pentateuch's foundational divine-name-disclosure clusters. The Alt 1929 reconstruction has been refined by Cross 1973 Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic ch. 1 (the Ugaritic-El-cult-identification), partly critiqued by Van Seters 1975 and Thompson 1974 (the patriarchal-narrative-historicity question), and held in modern-critical-consensus form by Miller 2000 and Smith 1990/2002. See anokhi-elohei-X-avikha-ancestral-god-formula for the full treatment.

25
And he built an altar there, and called on the name of YHWH, and pitched his tent there; and there Yitzchaq's servants dug a well.
And he builded an altar there, and called upon the name of Jehovah, and pitched his tent there. And there Isaac`s servants digged a well.
וַיִּ֧בֶן שָׁ֣ם מִזְבֵּ֗חַ וַיִּקְרָא֙ בְּשֵׁ֣ם יְהוָ֔ה וַיֶּט־שָׁ֖ם אָהֳל֑וֹ וַיִּכְרוּ־שָׁ֥ם עַבְדֵי־יִצְחָ֖ק בְּאֵֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

Yitzchaq builds an altar and calls on the name of YHWH — the patriarchal altar-formula (see qara-b-shem-yhwh). The formula's recurrences mark altar-sites along the Avrahamic-and-Yitzchaq itineraries: Gen 12:8 (Avram between Beth-El and Ai); Gen 13:4 (Avram returning); Gen 21:33 (Avraham at Be'er Sheva after planting a tamarisk); Gen 26:25 (here — Yitzchaq at Be'er Sheva). Yitzchaq's altar at Be'er Sheva is the structural continuation of his father's altar at the same place — another instance of the chapter's paternal-continuation signature (see commentary on v 18).

26
Then Avimelekh went to him from Gerar, and Achuzzat his friend, and Phicol the captain of his host.
Then Abimelech went to him from Gerar, and Ahuzzath his friend, and Phicol the captain of his host.
וַאֲבִימֶ֕לֶךְ הָלַ֥ךְ אֵלָ֖יו מִגְּרָ֑ר וַאֲחֻזַּת֙ מֵֽרֵעֵ֔הוּ וּפִיכֹ֖ל שַׂר־צְבָאֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 26

The Yitzchaq-Avimelekh covenant at Be'er Sheva (vv 26-31) is the structural doublet of the Avraham-Avimelekh covenant at Gen 21:22-32. The two narratives share four structural features: (i) the same Philistine king (named Avimelekh in both, though the two are nominally distinct rulers separated by a generation); (ii) the same Pichol sar tzva'o military-commander epithet (Gen 21:22 / Gen 26:26); (iii) the same Elohim/YHWH imkha divine-presence-recognition formula (Gen 21:22 / Gen 26:28 — see commentary on v 28); (iv) the same Be'er Sheva covenant-site etiology (Gen 21:31 / Gen 26:33). The doublet relationship is documented in wife-sister-type-scene (which treats the wife-sister doublet across Gen 12, 20, 26) and is mirrored by the wife-sister doublet's parallel three-fold structure. The Gen 26 covenant differs in two key respects: (i) Avimelekh initiates the covenant-approach (where at Gen 21 Avraham hosts; here Avimelekh travels from Gerar to Be'er Sheva); (ii) the covenant resolves a prior hostility (Avimelekh's expulsion of Yitzchaq at v 16) rather than a neutral mutual-recognition (Gen 21:22-23's setting).

27
And Yitzchaq said to them, Why have you come to me, seeing you hate me, and have sent me away from you?
And Isaac said unto them, Wherefore are ye come unto me, seeing ye hate me, and have sent me away from you?
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֲלֵהֶם֙ יִצְחָ֔ק מַדּ֖וּעַ בָּאתֶ֣ם אֵלָ֑י וְאַתֶּם֙ שְׂנֵאתֶ֣ם אֹתִ֔י וַתְּשַׁלְּח֖וּנִי מֵאִתְּכֶֽם׃
28
And they said, We saw plainly that YHWH was with you; and we said, Let there now be an oath between us, between us and you, and let us cut a covenant with you,
And they said, We saw plainly that Jehovah was with thee. And we said, Let there now be an oath betwixt us, even betwixt us and thee, and let us make a covenant with thee,
וַיֹּאמְר֗וּ רָא֣וֹ רָאִינוּ֮ כִּֽי־הָיָ֣ה יְהוָ֣ה ׀ עִמָּךְ֒ וַנֹּ֗אמֶר תְּהִ֨י נָ֥א אָלָ֛ה בֵּינוֹתֵ֖ינוּ בֵּינֵ֣ינוּ וּבֵינֶ֑ךָ וְנִכְרְתָ֥ה בְרִ֖ית עִמָּֽךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 28

Ra'o ra'inu ki-hayah YHWH imakh — we saw plainly that YHWH was with you. The verse is the structural near-doublet of Gen 21:22 (Avimelekh to Avraham: Elohim imkha b-khol asher atah oseh — Elohim is with you in all that you do; see elohim-imkha-divine-presence-formula). The doublet uses YHWH here where Gen 21:22 used Elohim — the divine-name shift is itself a documentary-source signal: the J-source (Yahwist) is generally associated with YHWH-use in patriarchal contexts, while the E-source (Elohist) is associated with Elohim-use; the Gen 21:22 / Gen 26:28 pair may reflect parallel J / E versions of the same narrative tradition. Westermann Genesis 12-36 1985 pp. 343, 428 and Friedman The Bible with Sources Revealed 2003 read the doublet as source-critically distinct. The external-recognition register is foundational across both occurrences: a non-Israelite king explicitly identifies the patriarch as divinely-accompanied. See the elohim-imkha-divine-presence-formula entry for the full cross-corpus treatment; appliesTo now includes both GEN-WOH-21:22 and GEN-WOH-26:28.

29
That you will do us no harm, as we have not touched you, and as we have done to you nothing but good, and have sent you away in peace. You are now the blessed of YHWH.
that thou wilt do us no hurt, as we have not touched thee, and as we have done unto thee nothing but good, and have sent thee away in peace. Thou art now the blessed of Jehovah.
אִם־תַּעֲשֵׂ֨ה עִמָּ֜נוּ רָעָ֗ה כַּאֲשֶׁר֙ לֹ֣א נְגַֽעֲנ֔וּךָ וְכַאֲשֶׁ֨ר עָשִׂ֤ינוּ עִמְּךָ֙ רַק־ט֔וֹב וַנְּשַׁלֵּֽחֲךָ֖ בְּשָׁל֑וֹם אַתָּ֥ה עַתָּ֖ה בְּר֥וּךְ יְהוָֽה׃
30
And he made them a feast, and they ate and drank.
And he made them a feast, and they did eat and drink.
וַיַּ֤עַשׂ לָהֶם֙ מִשְׁתֶּ֔ה וַיֹּאכְל֖וּ וַיִּשְׁתּֽוּ׃
31
And they rose up early in the morning, and swore one to another; and Yitzchaq sent them away, and they departed from him in peace.
And they rose up betimes in the morning, and sware one to another. And Isaac sent them away, and they departed from him in peace.
וַיַּשְׁכִּ֣ימוּ בַבֹּ֔קֶר וַיִּשָּׁבְע֖וּ אִ֣ישׁ לְאָחִ֑יו וַיְשַׁלְּחֵ֣ם יִצְחָ֔ק וַיֵּלְכ֥וּ מֵאִתּ֖וֹ בְּשָׁלֽוֹם׃
32
And it came to pass the same day, that Yitzchaq's servants came, and told him concerning the well which they had dug, and said to him, We have found water.
And it came to pass the same day, that Isaac`s servants came, and told him concerning the well which they had digged, and said unto him, We have found water.
וַיְהִ֣י ׀ בַּיּ֣וֹם הַה֗וּא וַיָּבֹ֙אוּ֙ עַבְדֵ֣י יִצְחָ֔ק וַיַּגִּ֣דוּ ל֔וֹ עַל־אֹד֥וֹת הַבְּאֵ֖ר אֲשֶׁ֣ר חָפָ֑רוּ וַיֹּ֥אמְרוּ ל֖וֹ מָצָ֥אנוּ מָֽיִם׃
33
And he called it Shiv'ah; therefore the name of the city is Be'er Sheva to this day.
And he called it Shibah. Therefore the name of the city is Beer-sheba unto this day.
וַיִּקְרָ֥א אֹתָ֖הּ שִׁבְעָ֑ה עַל־כֵּ֤ן שֵׁם־הָעִיר֙ בְּאֵ֣ר שֶׁ֔בַע עַ֖ד הַיּ֥וֹם הַזֶּֽה׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 33

The chapter's second Be'er Sheva etiology — Yitzchaq's well-name Shiv'ah (שִׁבְעָה) — is the partial-sense doublet of the Gen 21:31 layered double-etymology (where Avraham's Be'er Sheva was named under both the seven-ewe-lambs sheva sense and the swearing shava sense; see be-er-sheva-folk-etymology). The Gen 26:33 etiology activates only the sheva-as-oath sense (the swearing-clause at v 31 va-yishav'u ish l-achiv — the verb yishav'u, niphal of shava) with no parallel to the seven-ewe-lambs object-arrangement of Gen 21. The two etiologies are deliberately partial-vs-layered: Gen 26:33 is single-sense (oath only); Gen 21:31 is double-sense (seven + oath). The doublet is part of the wider wife-sister + Avimelekh + Be'er Sheva triad-doublet across the Avraham and Yitzchaq cycles. The appliesTo of be-er-sheva-folk-etymology now includes GEN-WOH-26:23 and GEN-WOH-26:33.

34
And when Esav was forty years old, he took to wife Yehudit the daughter of Be'eri the Chitti, and Basemat the daughter of Elon the Chitti.
And when Esau was forty years old he took to wife Judith the daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Basemath the daughter of Elon the Hittite.
וַיְהִ֤י עֵשָׂו֙ בֶּן־אַרְבָּעִ֣ים שָׁנָ֔ה וַיִּקַּ֤ח אִשָּׁה֙ אֶת־יְהוּדִ֔ית בַּת־בְּאֵרִ֖י הַֽחִתִּ֑י וְאֶת־בָּ֣שְׂמַ֔ת בַּת־אֵילֹ֖ן הַֽחִתִּֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 34

Esav at 40 takes two Chitti (Hittite) wives — Yehudit bat-Be'eri and Basemat bat-Elon. The wife-list is editorially-significant in three ways. (i) Chitti (Hittite) ethnonym — the patriarchal-narratives' Chittim are most plausibly the Canaanite-Hittite ethnonym rather than the Anatolian Late-Bronze-Age Hattusa-empire Hittites (the question is treated by the separate bnei-het-hittites-canaanite-anatolian-question entry, anchored at Gen 23's Hebron-Hittite trading-context; the Negev / Be'er Sheva locus of Gen 26:34 is consistent with the Canaanite-Hittite reading). (ii) Wife-list inconsistency — Esav's wife-list is given three different ways across Genesis: here (Yehudit bat-Be'eri, Basemat bat-Elon); Gen 28:9 (the Mahalat bat-Yishma'el addition after the Yitzchaq-blessing-stratagem); Gen 36:2-3 (the Esav-Edom-genealogy: Adah bat-Elon, Oholivamah bat-Anah, Basemat bat-Yishma'el). The three lists overlap partially but with different given-names and partly different ethnonyms (Chivvi vs Chitti). The rabbinic tradition (Gen Rab 65:2; Rashi at Gen 36:2) harmonizes by identifying the differently-named wives as the same persons; modern critical scholarship (Skinner ICC 1910 pp. 425-426; Westermann 1985 pp. 558-560; Friedman 2003) reads the lists as drawn from distinct documentary-sources. See morat-ruach-bitterness-of-spirit-esau-hittite-wives for the full treatment. (iii) Narrative-setup function — the wife-introduction at v 34 and the morat ruach notice at v 35 together establish the conditions for Rivkah's stratagem in Gen 27 (the Yitzchaq-deathbed-blessing deception).

35
And they were a bitterness of spirit to Yitzchaq and to Rivkah.
And they were a grief of mind unto Isaac and to Rebekah.
וַתִּהְיֶ֖יןָ מֹ֣רַת ר֑וּחַ לְיִצְחָ֖ק וּלְרִבְקָֽה׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 35

Va-tihyeinah morat ruach l-Yitzchaq u-l-Rivkah — and they were a bitterness of spirit to Yitzchaq and to Rivkah. The construct morat ruach (מֹרַת רוּחַ) is a fixed-phrase combining morah (bitterness, grief — from the root m-r-r) with ruach (spirit, breath, disposition). The phrase is virtually unique to Gen 26:35 in the HB. The choice of ruach (rather than nefesh) places the lexeme in the dispositional-temperament register — a persistent ongoing vexation — rather than the soul-level affliction register. The verse is the chapter's narrative-pivot toward Gen 27. Rivkah's complaint at Gen 27:46 — qatzti b-chayyai mi-pnei b-not Chet — I am weary of my life because of the daughters of Chet — is direct dialogue-quotation of this narrator's notice, and the wife-introduction-and-bitterness-notice together establish the motivation for Rivkah's stratagem to secure the Yitzchaq-deathbed-blessing for Ya'aqov. The chapter does not close with reconciliation; it closes with grievance — preparing the next chapter's deception.

Chapter 27

Genesis 27 — The blessing-deception, Yitzchaq's two blessings, and Ya'aqov's flight to Haran

1
And it came to pass, when Yitzchaq was old, and his eyes were dim from seeing, that he called Esav his elder son, and said to him, My son. And he said to him, Here am I.
And it came to pass, that when Isaac was old, and his eyes were dim, so that he could not see, he called Esau his elder son, and said unto him, My son. And he said unto him, Here am I.
וַיְהִי֙ כִּֽי־זָקֵ֣ן יִצְחָ֔ק וַתִּכְהֶ֥יןָ עֵינָ֖יו מֵרְאֹ֑ת וַיִּקְרָ֞א אֶת־עֵשָׂ֣ו ׀ בְּנ֣וֹ הַגָּדֹ֗ל וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֵלָיו֙ בְּנִ֔י וַיֹּ֥אמֶר אֵלָ֖יו הִנֵּֽנִי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

Va-tikheina eynav me-r'ot — "his eyes were dim from seeing." The verb kahah (קָהָה) covers a semantic range from "be dim, dull, weak" to "fail entirely" (BDB 462; HALOT s.v.). The Hebrew construction with privative-min (me-r'ot, "from seeing") is preserved here rather than smoothed to the ASV/KJV result-clause "so that he could not see" or NJPS's "too dim to see"; the literal preposition surfaces the lexical core.

The formula is one of the Hebrew Bible's narrative-trigger conventions for transferred-perception or transferred-authority episodes. Compare Mosheh at Deut 34:7 (negated — lo-khahatah eyno, "his eye had not dimmed," marking the absence of the trigger), Eli at 1 Sam 3:2, Achiyah at 1 Kgs 14:4: in each case patriarchal-blindness sets up a deception, a misidentification, or a generational hand-off. Yitzchaq's blindness is the mechanism that makes the chapter's blessing-deception possible. The glossary entry kahah-eynayim-patriarchal-blindness-narrative-trigger cross-references the cluster.

2
And he said, Behold now, I am old; I do not know the day of my death.
And he said, Behold now, I am old, I know not the day of my death.
וַיֹּ֕אמֶר הִנֵּה־נָ֖א זָקַ֑נְתִּי לֹ֥א יָדַ֖עְתִּי י֥וֹם מוֹתִֽי׃
3
Now therefore take, I pray you, your weapons, your quiver and your bow, and go out to the field, and hunt me venison.
Now therefore take, I pray thee, thy weapons, thy quiver and thy bow, and go out to the field, and take me venison.
וְעַתָּה֙ שָׂא־נָ֣א כֵלֶ֔יךָ תֶּלְיְךָ֖ וְקַשְׁתֶּ֑ךָ וְצֵא֙ הַשָּׂדֶ֔ה וְצ֥וּדָה לִּ֖י צידה [צָֽיִד׃]
4
And make me savory food, such as I love, and bring it to me, that I may eat; that my nefesh may bless you before I die.
And make me savory food, such as I love, and bring it to me, that I may eat. That my soul may bless thee before I die.
וַעֲשֵׂה־לִ֨י מַטְעַמִּ֜ים כַּאֲשֶׁ֥ר אָהַ֛בְתִּי וְהָבִ֥יאָה לִּ֖י וְאֹכֵ֑לָה בַּעֲב֛וּר תְּבָרֶכְךָ֥ נַפְשִׁ֖י בְּטֶ֥רֶם אָמֽוּת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

Two lexically loaded terms organize the chapter's blessing-mechanism. Matamim (מַטְעַמִּים), "savory food," is from the root ta'am (טעם, "taste"); BDB 381, HALOT s.v. KJV/ASV give "savoury meat"; NJPS smooths to "a dish." The term appears six times in this chapter (vv 4, 7, 9, 14, 17, 31) and only here in the Torah — a chapter-specific fixed phrase that names the blessing-transfer meal. See matamim-savory-food-blessing-meal.

Ba'avur t-varekhekha nafshi — "that my nefesh may bless you" — is the chapter's organizing-formula for what a blessing actually is. The construction recurs at vv 19, 25, 31: in each case the nefesh (not the man, not the mouth, not the hand) is the grammatical subject of the verb "bless." KJV's "my soul" carries post-Hellenistic baggage the Hebrew does not have; NJPS's "give you my innermost blessing" smooths the syntactic point away. Following Wolff (Anthropology of the Old Testament) and the project's prior nefesh-cluster entries, nefesh here is the patriarchal vital-self — the seat of the appetites and of the life-force whose transmission is the blessing. The translation transliterates per project convention. See nefesh-blessing-transfer.

5
And Rivkah was listening when Yitzchaq spoke to Esav his son. And Esav went to the field to hunt for venison, to bring it.
And Rebekah heard when Isaac spake to Esau his son. And Esau went to the field to hunt for venison, and to bring it.
וְרִבְקָ֣ה שֹׁמַ֔עַת בְּדַבֵּ֣ר יִצְחָ֔ק אֶל־עֵשָׂ֖ו בְּנ֑וֹ וַיֵּ֤לֶךְ עֵשָׂו֙ הַשָּׂדֶ֔ה לָצ֥וּד צַ֖יִד לְהָבִֽיא׃
6
And Rivkah spoke to Ya'aqov her son, saying, Behold, I heard your father speaking to Esav your brother, saying,
And Rebekah spake unto Jacob her son, saying, Behold, I heard thy father speak unto Esau thy brother, saying,
וְרִבְקָה֙ אָֽמְרָ֔ה אֶל־יַעֲקֹ֥ב בְּנָ֖הּ לֵאמֹ֑ר הִנֵּ֤ה שָׁמַ֙עְתִּי֙ אֶת־אָבִ֔יךָ מְדַבֵּ֛ר אֶל־עֵשָׂ֥ו אָחִ֖יךָ לֵאמֹֽר׃
7
Bring me venison, and make me savory food, that I may eat, and bless you before YHWH before my death.
Bring me venison, and make me savory food, that I may eat, and bless thee before Jehovah before my death.
הָבִ֨יאָה לִּ֥י צַ֛יִד וַעֲשֵׂה־לִ֥י מַטְעַמִּ֖ים וְאֹכֵ֑לָה וַאֲבָרֶכְכָ֛ה לִפְנֵ֥י יְהוָ֖ה לִפְנֵ֥י מוֹתִֽי׃
8
Now therefore, my son, obey my voice in that which I command you.
Now therefore, my son, obey my voice according to that which I command thee.
וְעַתָּ֥ה בְנִ֖י שְׁמַ֣ע בְּקֹלִ֑י לַאֲשֶׁ֥ר אֲנִ֖י מְצַוָּ֥ה אֹתָֽךְ׃
9
Go now to the flock, and bring me from there two good kids of the goats; and I will make of them savory food for your father, such as he loves.
Go now to the flock, and fetch me from thence two good kids of the goats. And I will make them savory food for thy father, such as he loveth.
לֶךְ־נָא֙ אֶל־הַצֹּ֔אן וְקַֽח־לִ֣י מִשָּׁ֗ם שְׁנֵ֛י גְּדָיֵ֥י עִזִּ֖ים טֹבִ֑ים וְאֶֽעֱשֶׂ֨ה אֹתָ֧ם מַטְעַמִּ֛ים לְאָבִ֖יךָ כַּאֲשֶׁ֥ר אָהֵֽב׃
10
And you shall bring it to your father, that he may eat, so that he may bless you before his death.
And thou shalt bring it to thy father, that he may eat, so that he may bless thee before his death.
וְהֵבֵאתָ֥ לְאָבִ֖יךָ וְאָכָ֑ל בַּעֲבֻ֛ר אֲשֶׁ֥ר יְבָרֶכְךָ֖ לִפְנֵ֥י מוֹתֽוֹ׃
11
And Ya'aqov said to Rivkah his mother, Behold, Esav my brother is a hairy man, and I am a smooth man.
And Jacob said to Rebekah his mother, Behold, Esau my brother is a hairy man, and I am a smooth man.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר יַעֲקֹ֔ב אֶל־רִבְקָ֖ה אִמּ֑וֹ הֵ֣ן עֵשָׂ֤ו אָחִי֙ אִ֣ישׁ שָׂעִ֔ר וְאָנֹכִ֖י אִ֥ישׁ חָלָֽק׃
12
Perhaps my father will feel me, and I shall be in his eyes as a deceiver; and I shall bring a curse upon myself, and not a blessing.
My father peradventure will feel me, and I shall seem to him as a deceiver. And I shall bring a curse upon me, and not a blessing.
אוּלַ֤י יְמֻשֵּׁ֙נִי֙ אָבִ֔י וְהָיִ֥יתִי בְעֵינָ֖יו כִּמְתַעְתֵּ֑עַ וְהֵבֵאתִ֥י עָלַ֛י קְלָלָ֖ה וְלֹ֥א בְרָכָֽה׃
13
And his mother said to him, Upon me be your curse, my son; only obey my voice, and go, fetch them for me.
And his mother said unto him, Upon me be thy curse, my son. Only obey my voice, and go fetch me them.
וַתֹּ֤אמֶר לוֹ֙ אִמּ֔וֹ עָלַ֥י קִלְלָתְךָ֖ בְּנִ֑י אַ֛ךְ שְׁמַ֥ע בְּקֹלִ֖י וְלֵ֥ךְ קַֽח־לִֽי׃
14
And he went, and took, and brought them to his mother; and his mother made savory food, such as his father loved.
And he went, and fetched, and brought them to his mother. And his mother made savory food, such as his father loved.
וַיֵּ֙לֶךְ֙ וַיִּקַּ֔ח וַיָּבֵ֖א לְאִמּ֑וֹ וַתַּ֤עַשׂ אִמּוֹ֙ מַטְעַמִּ֔ים כַּאֲשֶׁ֖ר אָהֵ֥ב אָבִֽיו׃
15
And Rivkah took the goodly garments of Esav her elder son, which were with her in the house, and clothed Ya'aqov her younger son.
And Rebekah took the goodly garments of Esau her elder son, which were with her in the house, and put them upon Jacob her younger son.
וַתִּקַּ֣ח רִ֠בְקָה אֶת־בִּגְדֵ֨י עֵשָׂ֜ו בְּנָ֤הּ הַגָּדֹל֙ הַחֲמֻדֹ֔ת אֲשֶׁ֥ר אִתָּ֖הּ בַּבָּ֑יִת וַתַּלְבֵּ֥שׁ אֶֽת־יַעֲקֹ֖ב בְּנָ֥הּ הַקָּטָֽן׃
16
And she put the skins of the kids of the goats upon his hands, and upon the smooth of his neck.
And she put the skins of the kids of the goats upon his hands, and upon the smooth of his neck.
וְאֵ֗ת עֹרֹת֙ גְּדָיֵ֣י הָֽעִזִּ֔ים הִלְבִּ֖ישָׁה עַל־יָדָ֑יו וְעַ֖ל חֶלְקַ֥ת צַוָּארָֽיו׃
17
And she gave the savory food and the bread, which she had prepared, into the hand of Ya'aqov her son.
And she gave the savory food and the bread, which she had prepared, into the hand of her son Jacob.
וַתִּתֵּ֧ן אֶת־הַמַּטְעַמִּ֛ים וְאֶת־הַלֶּ֖חֶם אֲשֶׁ֣ר עָשָׂ֑תָה בְּיַ֖ד יַעֲקֹ֥ב בְּנָֽהּ׃
18
And he came to his father, and said, My father. And he said, Here am I; who are you, my son?
And he came unto his father, and said, My father. And he said, Here am I. Who art thou, my son?
וַיָּבֹ֥א אֶל־אָבִ֖יו וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אָבִ֑י וַיֹּ֣אמֶר הִנֶּ֔נִּי מִ֥י אַתָּ֖ה בְּנִֽי׃
19
And Ya'aqov said to his father, I am Esav your firstborn; I have done as you spoke to me. Arise, I pray you, sit and eat of my venison, that your nefesh may bless me.
And Jacob said unto his father, I am Esau thy first-born; I have done according as thou badest me: arise, I pray thee, sit and eat of my venison, that thy soul may bless me.
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר יַעֲקֹ֜ב אֶל־אָבִ֗יו אָנֹכִי֙ עֵשָׂ֣ו בְּכֹרֶ֔ךָ עָשִׂ֕יתִי כַּאֲשֶׁ֥ר דִּבַּ֖רְתָּ אֵלָ֑י קֽוּם־נָ֣א שְׁבָ֗ה וְאָכְלָה֙ מִצֵּידִ֔י בַּעֲב֖וּר תְּבָרֲכַ֥נִּי נַפְשֶֽׁךָ׃
20
And Yitzchaq said to his son, How is it that you have found it so quickly, my son? And he said, Because YHWH your Elohim caused it to come to me.
And Isaac said unto his son, How is it that thou hast found it so quickly, my son? And he said, Because Jehovah thy God sent me good speed.
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר יִצְחָק֙ אֶל־בְּנ֔וֹ מַה־זֶּ֛ה מִהַ֥רְתָּ לִמְצֹ֖א בְּנִ֑י וַיֹּ֕אמֶר כִּ֥י הִקְרָ֛ה יְהוָ֥ה אֱלֹהֶ֖יךָ לְפָנָֽי׃
21
And Yitzchaq said to Ya'aqov, Come near, I pray you, that I may feel you, my son, whether you are really my son Esav or not.
And Isaac said unto Jacob, Come near, I pray thee, that I may feel thee, my son, whether thou be my very son Esau or not.
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר יִצְחָק֙ אֶֽל־יַעֲקֹ֔ב גְּשָׁה־נָּ֥א וַאֲמֻֽשְׁךָ֖ בְּנִ֑י הַֽאַתָּ֥ה זֶ֛ה בְּנִ֥י עֵשָׂ֖ו אִם־לֹֽא׃
22
And Ya'aqov came near to Yitzchaq his father; and he felt him, and said, The voice is the voice of Ya'aqov, but the hands are the hands of Esav.
And Jacob went near unto Isaac his father. And he felt him, and said, The voice is Jacob`s voice, but the hands are the hands of Esau.
וַיִּגַּ֧שׁ יַעֲקֹ֛ב אֶל־יִצְחָ֥ק אָבִ֖יו וַיְמֻשֵּׁ֑הוּ וַיֹּ֗אמֶר הַקֹּל֙ ק֣וֹל יַעֲקֹ֔ב וְהַיָּדַ֖יִם יְדֵ֥י עֵשָֽׂו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

Ha-qol qol Ya'aqov v-ha-yadayim y'dei Esav — "the voice is the voice of Ya'aqov, but the hands are the hands of Esav." The verse is the chapter's narrative-rhetorical fulcrum and one of the Hebrew Bible's most-cited chiastic doublets. The two-clause structure with definite article on each subject and construct-state on each predicate (ha-qol qol-X / ha-yadayim y'dei-Y) is editorially marked; the translation preserves the article-construct repetition rather than smoothing to "this is Jacob's voice, but these are Esau's hands." ASV is the baseline; Alter (The Five Books of Moses) reads the doubling as deliberate verbal mirroring of the chapter's identity-confusion.

The rabbinic reception is dense. Genesis Rabbah 65:20 reads the qol as Torah-study and the yadayim as Esav-violence — a hermeneutic that recurs in patristic, medieval Jewish, and contemporary homiletic exegesis. The clause is also the verse from which Yitzchaq is conventionally said to have "sensed but not acted," reading the contradiction without resolving it — a feature the narrative exploits when it has him bless Ya'aqov anyway at v 23.

The Wheel of Heaven lens neither sharpens nor blunts the irony here; the chiastic-doublet is a literary feature of the Hebrew, and the translation's job is to preserve it rather than rewrite it. See ha-qol-qol-yaakov-deception-formula.

23
And he did not recognize him, because his hands were hairy, as his brother Esav's hands; so he blessed him.
And he discerned him not, because his hands were hairy, as his brother Esau`s hands. So he blessed him.
וְלֹ֣א הִכִּיר֔וֹ כִּֽי־הָי֣וּ יָדָ֗יו כִּידֵ֛י עֵשָׂ֥ו אָחִ֖יו שְׂעִרֹ֑ת וַֽיְבָרְכֵֽהוּ׃
24
And he said, Are you really my son Esav? And he said, I am.
And he said, Art thou my very son Esau? And he said, I am.
וַיֹּ֕אמֶר אַתָּ֥ה זֶ֖ה בְּנִ֣י עֵשָׂ֑ו וַיֹּ֖אמֶר אָֽנִי׃
25
And he said, Bring it near to me, and I will eat of my son's venison, that my nefesh may bless you. And he brought it near to him, and he ate; and he brought him wine, and he drank.
And he said, Bring it near to me, and I will eat of my son`s venison, that my soul may bless thee. And he brought it near to him, and he did eat. And he brought him wine, and he drank.
וַיֹּ֗אמֶר הַגִּ֤שָׁה לִּי֙ וְאֹֽכְלָה֙ מִצֵּ֣יד בְּנִ֔י לְמַ֥עַן תְּבָֽרֶכְךָ֖ נַפְשִׁ֑י וַיַּגֶּשׁ־לוֹ֙ וַיֹּאכַ֔ל וַיָּ֧בֵא ל֦וֹ יַ֖יִן וַיֵּֽשְׁתְּ׃
26
And Yitzchaq his father said to him, Come near now, and kiss me, my son.
And his father Isaac said unto him, Come near now, and kiss me, my son.
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר אֵלָ֖יו יִצְחָ֣ק אָבִ֑יו גְּשָׁה־נָּ֥א וּשְׁקָה־לִּ֖י בְּנִֽי׃
27
And he came near, and kissed him; and he smelled the smell of his garments, and blessed him, and said, See, the smell of my son is as the smell of a field which YHWH has blessed.
And he came near, and kissed him. And he smelled the smell of his raiment, and blessed him, and said, See, the smell of my son Is as the smell of a field which Jehovah hath blessed.
וַיִּגַּשׁ֙ וַיִּשַּׁק־ל֔וֹ וַיָּ֛רַח אֶת־רֵ֥יחַ בְּגָדָ֖יו וַֽיְבָרֲכֵ֑הוּ וַיֹּ֗אמֶר רְאֵה֙ רֵ֣יחַ בְּנִ֔י כְּרֵ֣יחַ שָׂדֶ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר בֵּרֲכ֖וֹ יְהוָֽה׃
28
And may the Elohim give you of the dew of the skies, and of the fatness of the land, and abundance of grain and new wine.
And God five thee of the dew of heaven, And of the fatness of the earth, And plenty of grain and new wine.
וְיִֽתֶּן־לְךָ֙ הָאֱלֹהִ֔ים מִטַּל֙ הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וּמִשְׁמַנֵּ֖י הָאָ֑רֶץ וְרֹ֥ב דָּגָ֖ן וְתִירֹֽשׁ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 28

The blessing-formula proper. Mi-tal ha-shamayim u-mi-shemanei ha-aretz — "of the dew of the skies and of the fatness of the land." The mi- preposition is partitive here, fixed by the parallel construction with v-rov dagan v-tirosh ("and abundance of grain and new wine") which is unambiguously partitive in sense. The same preposition is famously ambiguous at vv 39–40 where Yitzchaq counter-blesses Esav with formally near-identical wording — the contrast between definite-partitive at v 28 and ambiguous-mi- at v 39 is editorially load-bearing (see commentary at v 39).

Dagan v-tirosh — "grain and new wine" — is one of the canonical agricultural-blessing word-pairs of the corpus (Deut 7:13, 11:14, 28:51; Hos 2:10; Joel 1:10). Tirosh is specifically the new wine / must (HALOT 1727), not aged yayin.

The articulated ha-Elohim — "the Elohim" — is preserved per project convention against KJV/ASV's flat "God." The article in the Hebrew is real: Yitzchaq's blessing addresses ha-Elohim (the Elohim, plural collective with article), distinct from v 20's unarticulated YHWH Eloheikha. See tal-shamayim-shemanei-aretz-blessing-formula and the standing elohim-as-translation entry.

29
Let peoples serve you, and nations bow down to you; be lord over your brothers, and let your mother's sons bow down to you. Those who curse you, cursed; and those who bless you, blessed.
Let peoples serve thee, And nations bow down to thee. Be lord over thy brethren, And let thy mother`s sons bow down to thee. Cursed be every one that curseth thee, And blessed be every one that blesseth thee.
יַֽעַבְד֣וּךָ עַמִּ֗ים וישתחו [וְיִֽשְׁתַּחֲו֤וּ] לְךָ֙ לְאֻמִּ֔ים הֱוֵ֤ה גְבִיר֙ לְאַחֶ֔יךָ וְיִשְׁתַּחֲוּ֥וּ לְךָ֖ בְּנֵ֣י אִמֶּ֑ךָ אֹרְרֶ֣יךָ אָר֔וּר וּֽמְבָרֲכֶ֖יךָ בָּרֽוּךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 29

Two interlocking blessing-formulas. Ya'avdukha amim v-yishtachavu lekha le'umim — "let peoples serve you, and nations bow down to you." The amim/le'umim pair is a fixed-pair in Hebrew Bible blessing-and-oracle contexts (Gen 25:23, shnei goyim/shnei le'umim; Num 24:7; Isa 17:12–13; Ps 47:4); a qere at yishtachavu supplies the standard hishtaphel form against the defective ketiv yishtachu. The clause assigns Ya'aqov the peoples-subjugation half of the patriarchal-blessing chain.

Orerekha arur u-m'varakhekha barukh — "those who curse you, cursed; and those who bless you, blessed." This is an explicit reverse-echo of the curse-blessing-protection clause first issued to Avraham at Gen 12:3 (u-m'varakhekha avarekha u-m'qallelekha a'or). The Gen 12:3 form was the direct YHWH-theophany to Avraham; the Gen 27:29 form transmits the same clause through Yitzchaq to Ya'aqov — a transfer that ratifies the Avrahamic-blessing-chain (Avraham → Yitzchaq → Ya'aqov) one generation further. Note that v 29 uses the curse-blessing-protection clause only, without the universal-blessing nivrakhu bekha niphal/hithpael clause that follows it at Gen 12:3; the existing nivrakhu-bekha-niphal-hithpael entry covers the universal-blessing clause, while cursed-cursers-blessed-blessers-echo covers the protection clause and its transmission to Ya'aqov here.

30
And it came to pass, as soon as Yitzchaq had finished blessing Ya'aqov, and Ya'aqov was just gone out from before Yitzchaq his father, that Esav his brother came in from his hunting.
And it came to pass, as soon as Isaac had made an end of blessing Jacob, and Jacob was yet scarce gone out from the presence of Isaac his father, that Esau his brother came in from his hunting.
וַיְהִ֗י כַּאֲשֶׁ֨ר כִּלָּ֣ה יִצְחָק֮ לְבָרֵ֣ךְ אֶֽת־יַעֲקֹב֒ וַיְהִ֗י אַ֣ךְ יָצֹ֤א יָצָא֙ יַעֲקֹ֔ב מֵאֵ֥ת פְּנֵ֖י יִצְחָ֣ק אָבִ֑יו וְעֵשָׂ֣ו אָחִ֔יו בָּ֖א מִצֵּידֽוֹ׃
31
And he also made savory food, and brought it to his father; and he said to his father, Let my father arise, and eat of his son's venison, that your nefesh may bless me.
And he also made savory food, and brought it unto his father. And he said unto his father, Let my father arise, and eat of his son`s venison, that thy soul may bless me.
וַיַּ֤עַשׂ גַּם־הוּא֙ מַטְעַמִּ֔ים וַיָּבֵ֖א לְאָבִ֑יו וַיֹּ֣אמֶר לְאָבִ֗יו יָקֻ֤ם אָבִי֙ וְיֹאכַל֙ מִצֵּ֣יד בְּנ֔וֹ בַּעֲב֖וּר תְּבָרֲכַ֥נִּי נַפְשֶֽׁךָ׃
32
And Yitzchaq his father said to him, Who are you? And he said, I am your son, your firstborn, Esav.
And Isaac his father said unto him, Who art thou? And he said, I am thy son, thy first-born, Esau.
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר ל֛וֹ יִצְחָ֥ק אָבִ֖יו מִי־אָ֑תָּה וַיֹּ֕אמֶר אֲנִ֛י בִּנְךָ֥ בְכֹֽרְךָ֖ עֵשָֽׂו׃
33
And Yitzchaq trembled a great trembling exceedingly, and said, Who then is he who has hunted venison, and brought it to me, and I ate of all before you came, and I blessed him? Indeed, he shall be blessed.
And Isaac trembled very exceedingly, and said, Who then is he that hath taken venison, and brought it me, and I have eaten of all before thou camest, and have blessed him? Yea, [and] he shall be blessed.
וַיֶּחֱרַ֨ד יִצְחָ֣ק חֲרָדָה֮ גְּדֹלָ֣ה עַד־מְאֹד֒ וַיֹּ֡אמֶר מִֽי־אֵפ֡וֹא ה֣וּא הַצָּֽד־צַיִד֩ וַיָּ֨בֵא לִ֜י וָאֹכַ֥ל מִכֹּ֛ל בְּטֶ֥רֶם תָּב֖וֹא וָאֲבָרֲכֵ֑הוּ גַּם־בָּר֖וּךְ יִהְיֶֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 33

Two clauses, both editorially load-bearing.

Va-yecherad Yitzchaq charadah gedolah ad-me'od — "and Yitzchaq trembled a great trembling exceedingly." The construction is a Hebrew cognate-accusative (verb + noun from the same root) intensified by the adjective gedolah and the marker ad-me'od — three intensifiers stacked on a single emotional event. The charad/charadah root names the intense-fear/agitation lexical cluster (Exod 19:16 of Sinai's charad at the theophany; Isa 32:11; Ezek 26:18); the deployment here is the strongest narrative-emotion-marking in the patriarchal cycle. The translation preserves the cognate-accusative literally rather than ASV's smoothed "trembled very exceedingly." See va-yecherad-charadah-trembling-formula.

Gam-barukh yihye — "indeed, he shall be blessed." The clause is the blessing-irrevocability-declaration: once pronounced, the patriarchal blessing cannot be revoked even when extracted by deception. The reading is the source-text for the rabbinic and medieval doctrine of blessing-irrevocability and for the Heb 12:17 topon metanoias ouk heuren reception ("Esau found no place for repentance, though he sought it diligently with tears"). Yitzchaq's recognition of the deception does not undo the words; the blessing once spoken belongs to whomever received it. The Wheel of Heaven lens reads the clause not theologically but performatively — a blessing is a transfer of nefesh (see v 4 commentary), and what is transferred cannot be un-transferred.

34
When Esav heard the words of his father, he cried out a great and bitter cry exceedingly, and said to his father, Bless me, even me also, my father.
When Esau heard the words of his father, he cried with an exceeding great and bitter cry, and said unto his father, Bless me, even me also, O my father.
כִּשְׁמֹ֤עַ עֵשָׂו֙ אֶת־דִּבְרֵ֣י אָבִ֔יו וַיִּצְעַ֣ק צְעָקָ֔ה גְּדֹלָ֥ה וּמָרָ֖ה עַד־מְאֹ֑ד וַיֹּ֣אמֶר לְאָבִ֔יו בָּרֲכֵ֥נִי גַם־אָ֖נִי אָבִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 34

Va-yitz'aq tza'aqah gedolah u-marah ad-me'od — "he cried out a great and bitter cry exceedingly." Another cognate-accusative (tza'aq tza'aqah), now doubled by the adjective-pair gedolah u-marah (great and bitter) and the intensifier ad-me'od. The bitter-cry formula functions as a marker of irreversible-loss in the corpus's narrative-emotional repertoire.

The phrase recurs cross-corpus most famously at Esth 4:1 of Mordecai upon learning of the destruction-decree: va-yiz'aq ze'aqah gedolah u-marah ad-me'od — a near-verbatim echo widely treated in the literary-critical literature as deliberate (Berlin, Esther JPS, 44–45; Sarna, Genesis JPS, 192; Levenson, Esther OTL, 78). The Esther echo is the load-bearing one: Mordecai's cry mirrors Esav's, marking Mordecai's situation as Esav-like and (in the lines of rabbinic tradition that read Haman as an Amalekite-Edomite descendant) closing a long-running narrative-arc. See tzaaqah-gedolah-u-marah-bitter-cry.

35
And he said, Your brother came with deceit, and has taken your blessing.
And he said, Thy brother came with guile, and hath taken away thy blessing.
וַיֹּ֕אמֶר בָּ֥א אָחִ֖יךָ בְּמִרְמָ֑ה וַיִּקַּ֖ח בִּרְכָתֶֽךָ׃
36
And he said, Is he not rightly named Ya'aqov? For he has supplanted me these two times: he took my birthright, and behold, now he has taken my blessing. And he said, Have you not reserved a blessing for me?
And he said, Is not he rightly name Jacob? for he hath supplanted me these two time. He took away my birthright. And, behold, now he hath taken away my blessing. And he said, Hast thou not reserved a blessing for me?
וַיֹּ֡אמֶר הֲכִי֩ קָרָ֨א שְׁמ֜וֹ יַעֲקֹ֗ב וַֽיַּעְקְבֵ֙נִי֙ זֶ֣ה פַעֲמַ֔יִם אֶת־בְּכֹרָתִ֣י לָקָ֔ח וְהִנֵּ֥ה עַתָּ֖ה לָקַ֣ח בִּרְכָתִ֑י וַיֹּאמַ֕ר הֲלֹא־אָצַ֥לְתָּ לִּ֖י בְּרָכָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 36

Esav's bitter pun on the Ya'aqov / ʿqb root. Ha-khi qara shemo Ya'aqov va-y'aqveni zeh fa'amayim — "is he not rightly named Ya'aqov, for he has supplanted me these two times?" The denominal verb va-y'aqveni (qal from the supplant / heel-trip root ʿqb) makes Esav's complaint the second of the two Ya'aqov-name-aetiologies in the patriarchal cycle — the first at Gen 25:26 (v-yado ochezet ba-aqev, his hand grasping the heel), the second here. Hosea 12:4 picks up the same etymology later as theological complaint. The standing entry yaakov-name-etymology-aqev-supplant supplies the lexical baseline and renders the verb "supplanted" per ASV; the literal heel-trip etymology is surfaced in the rationale rather than in the running text.

The fa'amayim ("two times") is enumerated in Esav's own next breath: et-bekhorati laqach v-hineh atah laqach birkhati — "he took my birthright, and behold, now he has taken my blessing." The pair bekhorah / berakhah is consonantally near-identical (ב־כ־ר / ב־ר־כ), transposing the middle radicals — a deliberate sound-play that marks the two losses as paired. The standing entry bekhorah-birthright-firstborn-status cross-references the Gen 25 birthright sale; the chapter's coda binds the two stories into a single Esav-loss narrative.

37
And Yitzchaq answered and said to Esav, Behold, I have made him lord over you, and all his brothers I have given to him for servants; and with grain and new wine I have sustained him; and what then shall I do for you, my son?
And Isaac answered and said unto Esau, Behold, I have made him thy lord, and all his brethren have I given to him for servants. And with grain and new wine have I sustained him. And what then shall I do for thee, my son?
וַיַּ֨עַן יִצְחָ֜ק וַיֹּ֣אמֶר לְעֵשָׂ֗ו הֵ֣ן גְּבִ֞יר שַׂמְתִּ֥יו לָךְ֙ וְאֶת־כָּל־אֶחָ֗יו נָתַ֤תִּי לוֹ֙ לַעֲבָדִ֔ים וְדָגָ֥ן וְתִירֹ֖שׁ סְמַכְתִּ֑יו וּלְכָ֣ה אֵפ֔וֹא מָ֥ה אֶֽעֱשֶׂ֖ה בְּנִֽי׃
38
And Esav said to his father, Have you but one blessing, my father? Bless me, even me also, my father. And Esav lifted up his voice, and wept.
