And Esau said unto his father, Hast thou but one blessing, my father? Bless me, even me also, O my father. And Esau lifted up his voice, and wept.
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר עֵשָׂ֜ו אֶל־אָבִ֗יו הַֽבְרָכָ֨ה אַחַ֤ת הִֽוא־לְךָ֙ אָבִ֔י בָּרֲכֵ֥נִי גַם־אָ֖נִי אָבִ֑י וַיִּשָּׂ֥א עֵשָׂ֛ו קֹל֖וֹ וַיֵּֽבְךְּ׃
39
And Yitzchaq his father answered and said to him, Behold, away from the fatness of the land shall be your dwelling, and away from the dew of the skies from above.
And Isaac his father answered and said unto him, Behold, of the fatness of the earth shall be thy dwelling, And of the dew of heaven from above.
וַיַּ֛עַן יִצְחָ֥ק אָבִ֖יו וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֵלָ֑יו הִנֵּ֞ה מִשְׁמַנֵּ֤י הָאָ֙רֶץ֙ יִהְיֶ֣ה מֽוֹשָׁבֶ֔ךָ וּמִטַּ֥ל הַשָּׁמַ֖יִם מֵעָֽל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 39

The famous mi- ambiguity at the Esav counter-blessing. Hineh mi-shemanei ha-aretz yihye moshavekha u-mi-tal ha-shamayim me-al — verbally near-identical to v 28's Ya'aqov-blessing but with the mi- preposition open to two diametrically opposite readings.

Partitive reading (ASV, KJV, rabbinic-harmonizing): "of the fatness of the land shall be your dwelling, and of the dew of the skies from above" — Esav receives a parallel blessing, sharing the same land-fertility and dew-from-above as Ya'aqov.

Separative reading (Westermann, Genesis 12–36; Speiser, Genesis AB; Sarna, Genesis JPS with discussion; NRSV; NJPS in its apparatus): "away from the fatness of the land shall be your dwelling, and away from the dew of the skies from above" — Esav is exiled from fertile-land and dew-region into desert-marginality, a reading that fits the geographical setting of the Edomite-Seir-mountain region (the southern desert-and-mountain Esav's descendants will inhabit, per Gen 36).

The two readings yield diametrically opposite blessings. The mi- preposition does both jobs in Biblical Hebrew, and the immediate context fixes neither: v 28's mi- is forced partitive by the parallel v-rov dagan v-tirosh, but v 39 lacks the disambiguating partitive parallel and the surrounding clauses (the sword-life and brother-service of v 40) lean separative.

The Wheel of Heaven translation adopts the separative reading at the running text. The context-favoring argument is decisive: Esav is being given the lesser blessing of the two; the partitive reading produces a near-duplicate of Ya'aqov's v 28 blessing, which loses the chapter's central irony (the stolen blessing was the inheritable one; Esav gets its inverse). The geographical reading — that Edomite territory is arid/marginal relative to Canaan — is supported by Gen 36's Edomite-genealogy and the cherev tichye ("by your sword you shall live") that follows immediately at v 40. Modern critical consensus (Westermann, Speiser, Sarna apparatus, NRSV) reads separative; ASV/KJV/NJPS main text preserve partitive. WoH chose with the modern critical consensus here over the older PD translation, departing from the nivrakhu-precedent because in this case modern critical scholarship is broadly aligned (not split as at Gen 12:3). See mi-shemanei-ha-aretz-partitive-vs-separative.

40
And by your sword shall you live, and your brother shall you serve; and it shall come to pass, when you break loose, that you shall break his yoke from off your neck.
And by thy sword shalt thou live, and thou shalt serve thy brother. And it shall come to pass, when thou shalt break loose, That thou shalt shake his yoke from off thy neck.
וְעַל־חַרְבְּךָ֣ תִֽחְיֶ֔ה וְאֶת־אָחִ֖יךָ תַּעֲבֹ֑ד וְהָיָה֙ כַּאֲשֶׁ֣ר תָּרִ֔יד וּפָרַקְתָּ֥ עֻלּ֖וֹ מֵעַ֥ל צַוָּארֶֽךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 40

Two clauses, both load-bearing for the Edomite-tradition setup.

V-al-charbekha tichye v-et achikha ta'avod — "by your sword shall you live, and your brother shall you serve." The sword-and-service couplet is the structural opposite of Ya'aqov's g'vir l-acheikha ("lord over your brothers") at v 29. The pair marks Esav / Edom as the warrior-vassal foil to Ya'aqov / Israel — a typology developed across the corpus (2 Kgs 8:20–22 on Edom's revolt under Yehoram; Mal 1:2–5 "Esav I hated"; Obadiah's entire 21-verse oracle against Edom; 1 Macc 5:3 on Judah's campaign against the Idumeans).

V-haya ka-asher tarid u-faraqta ulo me-al tzavarekha — "and it shall come to pass, when you break loose, that you shall break his yoke from off your neck." The verb tarid (תָּרִיד) is cognate-difficult: possibly (a) from rud — "wander, rove, become restless" (ASV's "when thou shalt break loose"), (b) linked to radah — "rule" (some medieval Jewish exegesis, KJV-Targum "have the dominion"), or (c) an alternative philological reconstruction toward "grow aggrieved" (NJPS "when you grow restive"). The translation follows ASV's break loose per the project's ASV-baseline convention. The clause is the prophetic counterpart to the v 39 blessing-ambiguity: Esav serves Ya'aqov for a time, then throws off the yoke — historically realized in the 2 Kgs 8 Edomite revolt and developed in the Edom-typology through Obadiah and the Hasmonean period. See cherev-tichye-by-the-sword-edom-tradition.

41
And Esav hated Ya'aqov on account of the blessing with which his father had blessed him; and Esav said in his heart, The days of mourning for my father are at hand; then I will kill Ya'aqov my brother.
And Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing wherewith his father blessed him. And Esau said in his heart, The days of mourning for my father are at hand. Then will I slay my brother Jacob.
וַיִּשְׂטֹ֤ם עֵשָׂו֙ אֶֽת־יַעֲקֹ֔ב עַל־הַ֨בְּרָכָ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר בֵּרֲכ֖וֹ אָבִ֑יו וַיֹּ֨אמֶר עֵשָׂ֜ו בְּלִבּ֗וֹ יִקְרְבוּ֙ יְמֵי֙ אֵ֣בֶל אָבִ֔י וְאַֽהַרְגָ֖ה אֶת־יַעֲקֹ֥ב אָחִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 41

Va-yistom Esav et-Ya'aqov — "and Esav hated Ya'aqov." The verb satam (שָׂטַם) is a near-synonym of the standard sane (שָׂנֵא, "hate") but with the distinctive nuance of "bear a grudge, harbor sustained resentment, lie in wait for" (BDB 966; HALOT s.v.). The cross-corpus distribution (Gen 49:23 of Joseph's brothers; 50:15 again of Joseph's brothers; Job 16:9 and 30:21 of God; Ps 55:4) supports the sustained-grudge / ambush-readiness reading rather than the flat-hate of sane.

The verb-choice is plausibly editorial: it foreshadows the explicit kill-plan in the verse's continuation (v-aharga et-Ya'aqov achi, "then I will kill Ya'aqov my brother"). ASV's flat "hated" is preserved as the running translation, but the nuance is the satam-grudge, not the sane-hatred. The chapter's last hostile word before Ya'aqov's twenty-year exile is lexically the grudge-verb, not the hate-verb — a calibration the narrator makes deliberately.

42
And the words of Esav her elder son were told to Rivkah; and she sent and called Ya'aqov her younger son, and said to him, Behold, your brother Esav is consoling himself concerning you, by planning to kill you.
And the words of Esau her elder son were told to Rebekah. And she sent and called Jacob her younger son, and said unto him, Behold, thy brother Esau, as touching thee, doth comfort himself, [purposing] to kill thee.
וַיֻּגַּ֣ד לְרִבְקָ֔ה אֶת־דִּבְרֵ֥י עֵשָׂ֖ו בְּנָ֣הּ הַגָּדֹ֑ל וַתִּשְׁלַ֞ח וַתִּקְרָ֤א לְיַעֲקֹב֙ בְּנָ֣הּ הַקָּטָ֔ן וַתֹּ֣אמֶר אֵלָ֔יו הִנֵּה֙ עֵשָׂ֣ו אָחִ֔יךָ מִתְנַחֵ֥ם לְךָ֖ לְהָרְגֶֽךָ׃
43
Now therefore, my son, obey my voice; and arise, flee to Lavan my brother, to Haran;
Now therefore, my son, obey my voice. And arise, flee thou to Laban my brother to Haran.
וְעַתָּ֥ה בְנִ֖י שְׁמַ֣ע בְּקֹלִ֑י וְק֧וּם בְּרַח־לְךָ֛ אֶל־לָבָ֥ן אָחִ֖י חָרָֽנָה׃
44
And dwell with him a few days, until your brother's fury turns away;
And tarry with him a few days, until thy brother`s fury turn away.
וְיָשַׁבְתָּ֥ עִמּ֖וֹ יָמִ֣ים אֲחָדִ֑ים עַ֥ד אֲשֶׁר־תָּשׁ֖וּב חֲמַ֥ת אָחִֽיךָ׃
45
Until your brother's anger turns away from you, and he forgets what you have done to him; then I will send and fetch you from there. Why should I be bereaved of you both in one day?
Until thy brother`s anger turn away from thee, and he forget that which thou hast done to him. Then I will send, and fetch thee from thence. Why should I be bereaved of you both in one day?
עַד־שׁ֨וּב אַף־אָחִ֜יךָ מִמְּךָ֗ וְשָׁכַח֙ אֵ֣ת אֲשֶׁר־עָשִׂ֣יתָ לּ֔וֹ וְשָׁלַחְתִּ֖י וּלְקַחְתִּ֣יךָ מִשָּׁ֑ם לָמָ֥ה אֶשְׁכַּ֛ל גַּם־שְׁנֵיכֶ֖ם י֥וֹם אֶחָֽד׃
46
And Rivkah said to Yitzchaq, I am weary of my life because of the daughters of Het; if Ya'aqov takes a wife of the daughters of Het, such as these, of the daughters of the land, what good shall my life be to me?
And Rebekah said to Isaac, I am weary of my life because of the daughters of Heth. If Jacob take a wife of the daughters of Heth, such as these, of the daughters of the land, what good shall my life do me?
וַתֹּ֤אמֶר רִבְקָה֙ אֶל־יִצְחָ֔ק קַ֣צְתִּי בְחַיַּ֔י מִפְּנֵ֖י בְּנ֣וֹת חֵ֑ת אִם־לֹקֵ֣חַ יַ֠עֲקֹב אִשָּׁ֨ה מִבְּנֽוֹת־חֵ֤ת כָּאֵ֙לֶּה֙ מִבְּנ֣וֹת הָאָ֔רֶץ לָ֥מָּה לִּ֖י חַיִּֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 46

Qatzti b-chayyai mi-p'nei b'not Chet — "I am weary of my life because of the daughters of Het." Rivkah's closing speech recycles the Hittite-wives complaint from Gen 26:34–35 (Esav's marriage to Yehudit bat-Be'eri and Basemat bat-Elon, morat ruach to Yitzchaq and Rivkah — see morat-ruach-bitterness-of-spirit-esau-hittite-wives) and deploys it as the pretext for her recommendation that Ya'aqov be sent away to Lavan in Aram-Naharaim.

The narrative-logical relationship is editorially load-bearing. The real reason for Ya'aqov's departure — Esav's death-threat at vv 41–45 — is told only to Rivkah and Ya'aqov; Yitzchaq is not informed. What Rivkah brings to Yitzchaq at v 46 is a decoy reason, a topic she knows he shares concern about (per Gen 26:35), so the dispatch reads to Yitzchaq as bride-search rather than as flight. The rhetorical-cover is one of the chapter's most-studied narrative-strategic moves (Alter, The Art of Biblical Narrative ch. 6 on information-asymmetry in the Genesis narrator; Sternberg, The Poetics of Biblical Narrative on multi-layered dialogue-pragmatics).

The Wheel of Heaven lens reads the chapter as already showing what the rest of the corpus will repeatedly model: human characters operate the larger plan through partial knowledge, pretexts, and information-asymmetry, not through transparent shared deliberation. Rivkah's pretext is not a flaw in the narrative — it is the narrative's working assumption about how providential outcomes get realized through ordinary human cunning. See rebekah-pretext-hittite-wives-cover.

Back · Library

Contents

    Wheel of Heaven is an open, multilingual reading of the ancient world's creation traditions — Raëlian, biblical, Mesopotamian, and others — through a single working hypothesis: that the beings called Elohim were a small advanced human civilization that came here, designed life on Earth, and left a record in the religious texts. The reading runs across twelve ages of the precessional cycle, from the project's beginning twenty-two thousand years ago to the present age of disclosure and into the long future the next Great Year is opening. The site is free, source-aware, and built so the work can be checked.
